Original_Search_Title: Governor Promises to Create More Local Governments in Ebonyi
Headline: Nigeria: Governor Promises to Create More Local Governments in Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Umuahia — Ebonyi state governor, Sam Egwu, has said that the public acceptance of the newly created 21 local government areas in the state has provided the encouragement for the creation of additional councils before the end of his tenure even as controversy continues to trail the exercise.
Egwu, who made this declaration at a civic reception during the week at Ekoh Edda Afikpo South Local Government Area of the state, affirmed that the newly created councils have fulfilled the obligation of transforming the rural areas.
Apart from the transformatory inputs of the new councils, Egwu maintained, "the success of the exercise has encouraged other states in the country to follow in the footsteps of his administration" assuring that under the new exercise Ekoli-Edda will surely become a new council area.
He commended the people of the area for their contribution towards the stability of his administration assuring that the area will always benefit from his laudable developmental programmes in appreciation of the support for him by the people.
Egwu assured the people that the problem of a motorable road linking the area especially the Owutu-Ekuta-Ekoli road will be addressed by next January adding that the state government will soon embarked on the construction of the Ekoli-Ibi-Ofu-Okinywe road to alleviate the transportation problems between the various communities along that road.
Earlier, the General President of Ekoh-Edda Band of Hope Union, Chief Ude I Ude, praised Egwu for his efforts in transforming the state assuring that the people of the area will support his re-election in 2003.
The high point of the occasion was the conferment of the "Uzo Ka Enyi" of Ekoli Edda title and also the decoration with a hoe and cutlass to signify his admission into the Edda traditional society and some yam seedling.
The occasion also witnessed the launching of a N30 million hospital and regional market development fund where the state government announced a donation in support of the launch.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo And the Igbo Race
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo And the Igbo Race
Body_Text:
The position of the Igbos in this democratic dispensation is comparable to a dedicated housemaid who was prevented from partaking in a meal of pounded yam she prepared for the family. She was inelegantly accused of having taken her share in the food aroma she perceived while cooking. The poor maid expectedly became confused and worried but did not see anybody to discuss her problem with. She goes about lamenting, sulking, frening, and raining curses at those who caused her problem. For her to be sidelined is criminal. Yes it is criminal. What moral justification does the head of the family have to starve this maid after all she has done for the family. It is nothing other than executive wickedness, according to top senator.
We have heard of generals who lost wars because they ignored popular counsel. We have equally seen generals who were stopped by God because they deviated from His original plan for His people.
The cry of marginalisation by the Easterners has so far been ignored or sometimes treated lacadastically. To the head of state, the Nigeria-Biafra war has ended nevertheless but there is still a mopping up operation that is on-going or call it war of attrition. The Igbos on the other hand as is usual of them have demonstrated on a very clear and unmistakable terms their determined resolve to pus this country forward irrespective of the amount of sacrifice required to achieve this.
lt is true that Obasanjo played a daring key role in bringing the war to an end and later by sheer providence became the military head of state of this great country. He was applauded in most instances and castigated in yet others. This is to say that he tried his best considering the prevailing circumstances at that time. The political suppression of the Igbos by the youthful head of state and the transited nation that was emerging from a 30 month fratricidal war was well understood by everyone including painfully the Igbos to certain extent. Looking back to that era, Obasanjo as a person pushed aside the popular slogan of no victor, no vanquished, which was decleared by General Yakubu Gowon at the end of the war and went about implementing his programs. He was, however forgiven on the ground of the youth [actor and his atheistic disposition then. (He is now a self-proclaimed born-again).
lgbos religiously believed and rightly too that the grand old man (now old) following his humiliation in the hands of general Sanni Abacha and his eventual incarceration had changed enough to be trusted. But Doom his attitude and wild pro in response to Igbo demands in recent times it is apparently clear that the leopard will never change its colours,instead of' changing, he has added more colours and grown more fearsome. He has deliberately failed to understand the problems of the people by trying to explain away everything in the most infantile dimension with the aim of knocking the heads of various ethnic groups together. ln other words, he has succeeded in a way in poison~ the minds of other hitherto peaceful ethnic groups to regard the lgbos as a race that is most difficult to satisfy politically. But the generality of Nigerians know better.
Just as the masses of this country are not asking the government to create paradise on earth for them other than the provision of basic amenities such as water, electricity, security, basic health care and an enabling environment for the acualisation of the various aspiration of the people, the Igbos in tum so much believe in the adage that says, "let the Eagle perch and let the hawk also perch". They believe more than any other does in the peaceful co-existence of all. They cry for fair representation in governance and equitable citing and provision of necessary amenities in their zone and be recognised as fellow passengers in the same ship heading an (un) known destination and nothing more than that.. The Ighos would honestly like to remember Obasanjo as the man who in his first rule committed a lot of available blunders to both his own people, the Yorubas and the East but came back as a democratic head of state to put things right. But from the way the general is going will this wish ever come to fruition. It will, if he ever malces a detour with the aim of restoring this people's confidence, battered personality and in so doing change their general attitude towards themselves and the government alike. It takes very little to rnake a chilLd happy, I believe. Of course nobody gives his child bones in place of flesh.
But if the wanton decay, the dearth of infrastfucture, the ravaging natural disasters, absence of federal government's presence in all spheres make the head of state happy, let it be so. He is experienced enough to know that as long as a whestler continues to pin his opponent to the canvas he too remains on the floor. As Nza the bird that perches on Ngige is dancing, Ngige is also dancing.
In the spirit of unity, let us sing the song of galvanization and not of balkanisation. The almighty God has been very merciful to us and the nation this past 2 years plus. We can not afford to offend Him this time around by going back to our evil days. Igbos are not actually crying in order that their captors may leave them but for the entire world to hear them. We have unfortunately realised lately that in Nigeria amenities are distributed according to how loud you can cry. The people of Niger Delta are subtly receiving attention because of the amount of destruction being carried out by the youths of that region. The same with the Yoruba race whose hobby now is to frighten the nation by their incessant threat of secession. Although their method has been that of hit and run. Odua Peoples Congress and area boys have been the tool deployed to test the waters. Those who still cry marginalisation are those who have gotten 'something' but want more according to the president in his interview on CNN. The Igbos sadly fall into this group.
The Yoruba race for now is not crying for they have gotten enough and more than enough ( I however dough my hat for FALANA, the pro-democracy activist an d co, who have b een crying foul). It will be in human for a single race to eat the flesh and go ahead to eat the bones.
There is no doubt that there are some promiment Igbo sons and daughters who shamelessly benefit from this confusion, they are the descendants of those who sold their brothers, sisters, mothers and fathers into slavery in exchange of as little as a bottle of brandy, whisky or even a wrap pf dry tobacco leaf. They are those who have every opprotunity to meet and dine with the president either in Ota or Abuja and feel satisfied by that alone, rather than use the opportunity to confront him with the problems of their race. They are those who embezzle even the meagre amount allocated to the various state in the East by the federal government and thereafter embark on mere grading of the roads thereby exposing them to the vagaries of weather. It is partly the cause of the gully erosion we have today in that zone.We are witnesses to what happened in Anambra State where the contractors were dictating the proceedings in the state hosue of assembly and have noctural meetings with the rebel legislators where huge sums of money were distributed so that their past sins would never be unearthed.They could run and run but for how long.Let them come out in all sincerity and work with the governor and all for the overall development of that backward state aptly regarded as the state that has one of the best human resources but unfortunately the leat developed.It is a sad commentary.This is why sometimes I intend to believe with those who ask for how long should we continue to ascribe our woes to external forces.
I would not want to join issues with the critics or cynics who posit that the denial of certain basic political benefits to the Igbos was aimed at whittling down and demystifying Chief Ekwueme's fast growing political image. By so doing, the sources revealed, the Igbos would regard Ekwueme as weak hence he could not attract any meaningful office to his people as well as his zone.The same reasons were alleged why all his nominees for ministerial appointmnet were not considered. Could the exclusion of the Igbos until recently from the National Council be attributed to an oversight or was it intentional.That until now nobody has given any meaningful explanation to this tend to reinforce the position of the cynical analysts who also believe nothing good would come out of Egypt.
What we are saying nevertheless is that the federal government should genuinely play their expected role while we take care of the hawks, wolves and leopards we have within the house.
Chizoba Ugo, wrote in from Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Point of Order
Headline: Ghana: Point of Order
Body_Text:
I cannot imagine whether any honourable member of the opposite side of our august house (Minority) would ever again say that Presidents Kufuor's government has done nothing to reduce cost of foodstuff on our local market knowing very well that when cassava, plantain, cocoyam, yam or maize farm is cultivated, it takes months before produce could be harvested before getting onto the market.
I would as such pray for God's forgiveness to whoever had the guts to blame the NPP government sometime in July this year on high cost of food in the country.
When Colonel Bernasko launched the "Operation Feed Yourself" programme, we saw how our local market was flooded with rice and maize. The then government built silos for storage but this laudable programme was short-lived because of another military adventurer's intervention in government.
Whether the late President Hilla Limann would have re-generated the idea of rice or maize production or not, his PNP government was booted out of office. And for almost two decades, revolutionaries who claimed to know much of our problems did not do anything in that sector apart from what has come to be known as "Aveyime Grain Saga" in which some people are being tried in court. And whether or not the numerous gari, palm kennel and palm oil processing factories that were commissioned by Madam Konadu Rawlings of 31st December Movement fame made the cost of gari, the student's companion cheap, should be left to historians in Ghana and in the Diaspora.
For twenty years or more, cocoa, the nation's foreign exchange earner has not seen any governmental action plan. We have allowed neighbouring Coe d'Ivoire to lead in cocoa production and export on the World Market. If today, government for the first time is undertaking disease and pest control spraying exercise in cocoa growing areas that has generated in a way jobs for over 21,000 jobless Ghanaians, should we rather not thank God?
The NPP government being sensitive to the plight of our dear farmers, has increased the producer price of cocoa by 35 per cent and NDCist say it is inadequate, though they know that they have caused the emptiness of even the consolidated fund.
Here, I would beg to pray that the good Lord holds us, fashions us, raises into pure and holy atmosphere where the rich currents of His love may flow through our souls and in turn bless those we encountered in this land of yours. Amen!
President Kufuor has launched a special initiative on Agribusiness, particularly cassava. One can imagine that should we all embrace it, instead of allowing our young boys and girls to engage in selling dog chains, "nkatee burger", video cassettes on our roads, over 20,000 able-bodied Ghanaians would have jobs, as about 14,000 or more farmers in selected districts where the initiative is being piloted, benefited immensely. And should the scheme be successful, which I am sure it would by the Grace of God (Allah), and be expanded to cover more districts in the years ahead, would the nation not earn some amount of foreign exchange as starch is going to be extracted for export?
This is assurance in the foresight of our dignified presidency. He has taken the initiative; let's allow time before coming out with criticisms.
This is my Point of Order.

Original_Search_Title: Anyim, Atiku, Babangida And 2003
Headline: Nigeria: Anyim, Atiku, Babangida And 2003
Body_Text:
It was a most shameful night when elected representatives of the people at the highest level would congregate to lie so brazenly. But I had told myself that no matter the chicanery at the event I would neither comment nor ask question. My position had been formed after the two previous nights, one in Minna, the other in Abuja. I had resolved within me that no matter the perfidy from politicians this is the time the media should rise to save this democracy in our own interest.
Before we editors left Lagos for Abuja for the fourth media roundtable with the Senate President, Chief Anyim Pius Anyim, at the weekend, we knew it was going to turn out a disaster for the man because of the Electoral Act and we were not disappointed.
After a long speech, the Senate President, who was flanked by several of his colleagues, said he would want all questions pertaining to the electoral bill to be asked first so that he could take them together. The import of that was not lost on us, he did not want any follow-up question. Everything had been pre-planned by the law makers, the same way they rig elections at source. Incidentally, on the high table was the Senate Chairman on Information, Senator Jonathan Zwingina, who only last week told us President Olusegun Obasanjo, as the 'Chief law maker' of the federation, reserves the power to re-write the Electoral law. Of course he would reverse himself by equivocating that what he meant was that the President initiates bill and signs it into law as if we did not know that before. But I know, as most people also know, that if our democracy derails tomorrow and soldiers come back, God forbid, Zwingina is a potential Information Minister, because such characters are good salesmen for the military, simply because they have the talent for rationalizing anything, including why democracy failed, if we help them to scuttle the process. It is for this reason I believe those of us in the media would have to restrain ourselves in times like this so that we do not play into the hands of opportunists who personally have nothing to lose.
Because I had decided not to ask the Senate President any question, I felt pained when, rather than answer the simple question about how the contentious proviso in section 80 entered the electoral bill, Anyim was shouting on us, even calling us "ignorant". We, however, bore all the indignities with philosophical calmness, and my uncle, Mr. Segun Babatope, the Managing Director of Anchor Newspaper made it very clear to the Senate President that our politicians still have a lot to learn, because insult would not take anything from us and any public official that indulges in such habit only demeans himself and the office he occupies. That message will be instructive for some other people too. What I found particularly painful about the session was that Professor Osunbor, a Senator and eminent lawyer for whom I have respect, would want to call white black in trying to justify how his Harmonisation Committee allowed itself to be teleguided to rewrite the electoral law without a recourse to the entire house when the issue involved is so fundamental. But as I said it is time we took this matter away from the law maker, we have to save this process.
My paradigm shift on this Electoral Act began to take shape on Monday night at a dinner party we in the THISDAY Board of Editors held for six national honours recipients who, in our estimation, have distinguished themselves and are therefore role models not only for our profession but indeed our generation. They are Mr. Sam Amuka, (known to many as Uncle Sam or Sad Sam) the self evasing publisher of the Vanguard; Mallam Wada Maida, the extremely humble but powerful Editor-in-chief of News Agency of Nigeria(NAN); Mr. Olisa Agbakoba, SAN, the fearless lawyer who pioneered the civil rights community as an institution in Nigeria; Mr. Akin Kekere Ekun, the Managing Director of Habib Bank who has shown that a bank does not have to deal in dirty money through round tripping or 'free funds' and other sharp practices to ensure a healthy balance sheet that add value to the economy; Mr. Nasiru el-Rufai, the dimunitive Mr. Privatization who has very tall and bright ideas when it comes to economic management and of course, my egbon, Mr. Gbenga Oyebode, the high flying international lawyer who has his hands in almost every big business pie from telecoms to energy.
It was a quiet dinner at NICON-Hilton with nice highlife music but as it most often happens in any of our events, there was drama. First, Chief Molade Okoya Thomas, "invited" himself into the dinner because, as he said, he is a member of our generation, having resolved that he would not allow anyone to "push him upstairs". The highly respected business man and Lagos high chief was to add a lot of fun to the night. Then as we were about to really get going, another uninvited guest, Vice President Alhaji Atiku Abubakar, breezed in. A very practical man without any air of self-importance, the V.P. said he had come to the hotel to cut his hair only to learn about the party and he decided to come in to honour our Chairman, Mr. Nduka Obaigbena even when he was not invited. His presence, as it would not turn out, not only added meaning and essence to the night, it marked the turning point for me about this electoral act. While giving the vote of thanks for the guests, Mr. Amuka had reminded us all that whatever may be our misgivings on issues, we must never forget one fact: Nigeria is the only country we have. When it was his turn to propose a toast to the federal republic of Nigeria, Vice President Atiku Abubakar waxed philosophical even as he enjoined us all to do all within our capacity to make this country great. He reiterated Uncle Sam's position, as he called on everyone present to ensure we rise above our differences in the effrot to build an enduring democracy. I can now no longer recall the exact words he said but I felt touched by it all and I left the party with a resolve that I will be part of those who will help Nigeria get out of its problems, any problem, including this electoral act palaver. Incidentally, I had spent the previous night in Minna with General Ibrahim Babangida and in his own way, perhaps without knowing it, he made me to realise that political issues are not always black and white hence the reason we must learn to accommodate each other's differences in times like this. My trip to Minna was a gamble that paid off because I spent quality time discussing, sometimes arguing, (at both multilateral and bilateral level ) with the former President.
Given that General Babangida's name had dominated several issues (Oputa Panel, 2003, new political party, Nitel sale etc) in the last couple of months, I had wanted to speak with him but all efforts, either on phone or through the usual contact, failed. That was why I was surprised two weeks ago when I got home from office and my wife told me he had called to congratulate her on our new baby. That emboldened me to go to Minna last Sunday uninvited and when the security men politely turned me back because there was no message that anybody with my name was coming, all I did was to cleverly wave my two mobile phones (none of which was working anyway) to show that I too belong (in Nigeria, such habit still works) and muttered "but the General himself called me yesterday." I also pretended as if I was angry that he failed to inform his people that I was coming. It worked. One of the security men asked for my complimentary card, telling me to wait for him to go and confirm. I knew the General would not turn me back and I was not disappointed. It turned out that he was going to the Mosque as I was coming in. As usual he greeted me warmly with banters, telling the people following him that they would have to pray for my conversion to Islam inside the Mosque and I retorted that I was going inside to be praying for his conversion to Christianity. Incidentally, when they came out one of the men told me that they actually prayed for me. If truly they did, they must have been praying amiss.
Since it was lunch time we all went to the dinning together and as I descended on the pounded yam, the discussion which had commenced continued. Of course it was on the Electoral Act. With several politicians and a senior member of the House of Representatives in attendance it was not surprising. It was a most revealing session and everybody had his say, predictably most of them expressed anger at President Obasanjo for closing the door on new parties but the high point for me was the one-on-one session I had with the General. From Oputa to Nitel (on which he said he was not in anyway involved) to 2003, we talked though it was not an interview but rather an informal session, just for him to provide insight into several issues which would benefit me as a reporter and most of which I will find useful one day when I decide to write on Nigeria. The one that is relevant for this piece though is our discussion of the electoral act and the role of the media. But since I know many people will crucify me if I do not say what we discussed on 2003 I need to state that he merely reiterated what he said earlier this year that he has no interest. Even while I am aware many people are goading him into what may turn out a misadventure should he try, I doubt if he would take the gamble. He is not a fool. What I, however, consider tragic is that in a nation of 120 million people, with several internationally reputed professionals, whenever we talk about leadership, all that we can think of are former players. Is it really not shameful for us as a nation that a man who left office almost nine years ago after spending eight years in office is the same person some people would be making cold calculations to recycle for Nigeria's presidency in the 21st century? But we shall come back to this some day. Of course some people will say "what about Obasanjo?" but it is the same problem.
In the course of our discussing the Electoral Act, General Babangida, while agreeing that he saw no reason why a democratic government would want to exclude new parties from elections told me that the media have been over dramatising the issue. "I read your column ('Here We Go Again..' published last week) and I noticed how hard you were on the President. One thing you don't know is that there is enormous pressure on the man. I have been there before so I can understand what he is going through. It is not easy, with several contending interests. Besides, is he not entitled to make mistakes? I have also noticed one trend in the media, you people are already creating scares in the minds of the people. Two days ago, I read one article in the Comet newspaper where the writer made allusion to the crisis that always happen at every ten-year interval, talking about 1963, 1973, 1983, 1993 and now making predictions about 2003. Such analysis based on superstitions is dangerous because people may begin to believe it". I told him it was no superstition and we had arguments on some of the positions but I quite agree that it is time the media began to be more circumspect about our positions on some of these political issues, beginning from the electoral act.
Now, what are the contentious issues in the Act? The first is the tenure of local government while the second is the issue of allowing new parties to contest the coming 2003 general elections.
In practical terms, these are the issues: the Governors want to conduct council elections next year so that they can rig their cronies in as council chairmen. The National Assembly members want to checkmate the Governors by postponing the Council poll till 2003 when there would be no opportunity to install surrogates through a manipulated process. The Chairmen of the 774 looting centres called local government do not want to go having been awarded an additional year free of charge (?)by the lawmakers. The President does not want new parties because he is afraid of some imaginary candidate that could come from the so-called new parties to dislodge him. Yet everybody is right in his own eyes and can use the constitution to buttress his position.
From all these we can see that none of our elected public officials making noise can actually speak from any moral high ground. Now, to talk about the new parties. The only one with any measure of seriousness is the United Nigeria Democratic Party (UNDP) but what manner of party is that one too? It is just an amalgam of ex-this, ex-that of the Babangida regime. From what we have seen of them thus far, they are not in any way different from the ruling PDP or the All Peoples Party (APP) or the Alliance for Democracy(AD). Perhaps the only defining character is that UNDP is a congregation of the displaced, the disgruntled and the malcontents of the PDP but is that what makes a political party?
But they have a point when they argue we should not put unnecessary hurdles on their way and I believe it is time we began to look at the way forward. The first is for all the stakeholders, especially the politicians to realise we dare not fail this time around and the best way to ensure that is for us to begin to test our institutions, especially the judiciary. There are several contentious provisions in the constitution, why are our politicians afraid of asking for the interpretation by the courts? Anyim, for instance, gave himself away when he was talking about the issue of whether some governors are qualified to contest 2003 polls. He repeatedly talked about their "Third Term" which means chief Olusegun Osoba and the three others, as far as the National Assembly is concerned, cannot contest the 2003 polls. Is that the position of the law? Only the court can determine that but nobody is willing to bell the cat.
Much more significantly, however, I want to appeal to my colleagues in the media that even as we report the controversy and the drama, we should be careful not to give the impression that this is the end of the world. We should encourage the political class to come out with a solution and if at the end of the day the status quo remains we should learn to live with it. We have a very good example to learn from. Almost every American journalist was aware that the Presidential election in Florida was rigged in favour of President George Bush but he would not say it. Nobody also talked about the military as a solution. As it was in America, so it should be here. The last option should be the court. This electoral act is our own Florida and we should handle it the same way.

Original_Search_Title: This Old Man Takes Us For Granted
Headline: Zimbabwe: This Old Man Takes Us For Granted
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT Robert Mugabe at the weekend summed up what Zimbabweans should brace for in the run-up to next year's presidential elections - war.
Therefore no sane Zimbabwean should ever doubt that as far as Mugabe is concerned, he is not going for an election but for war: a war which he will wage for the sole purpose of clinging to power by whatever means.
Zimbabweans should not get bogged down in semantics on what Mugabe actually meant or what type of war he is talking about because by now everyone should be clear on how the man thinks and what war he is talking about.
The war that Mugabe is talking about is the one to keep himself in power at any cost, even if this means he alone remains the sole person running this country. This is Mugabe for you; you can only dismiss his declaration of war at your own peril.
Mugabe's pronouncements at the ZANU PF conference in the resort town of Victoria Falls clearly demonstrate the type of leadership we have in this country.
The statement, apart from inciting violence, is most irresponsible from a person who is a head of state.
As was rightly predicted before the conference, the bottom line of the ZANU PF meeting in Victoria Falls was to give Mugabe a platform that he desperately misses these days to create a war psychosis in the country ahead of the March election.
The declaration of war was the only message which Mugabe managed to put across, unconcerned about offering solutions on the crumbling economy. Pointedly, he failed to speak against political violence ahead of such a major election.
Over and above everything else, the ageing President showed that he has mastered the art of taking docile Zimbabweans for granted.
In this day and age when Zimbabweans are wallowing in abject poverty, Mugabe still had the guts to say the country is not collapsing and that Zimbabweans are mostly rural and simple people who do not live on potatoes but on maize and groundnuts.
What an insult when he and his cronies were wining and dining in Victoria Falls at the taxpayers' expense.
In Victoria Falls, he asked: "Are people living in towns and cities Zimbabweans? Why do they behave contrary to their parents in rural areas?"
This was in obvious reference to the voting patterns that have reflected a rural-urban divide between ZANU PF and the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC), with the new opposition scoring heavily in the towns and cities.
Mugabe has on numerous occasions said Zimbabweans are a resilient people that will always tighten their belts.
Some of his senior advisers have even said Zimbabweans can make do with yams while he himself has branded those living in Mbare as totem-less people.
Such is the disdain with which Mugabe treats ordinary Zimbabweans and yet we have stood akimbo and allowed him to get away with this. No, enough is enough.
If I were to ask: does Mugabe think that the cowed rural folk represent the benchmark of voting behaviour and political thinking in this country?
People in the towns are faced with daily escalating food prices and shortages, some have to walk to work while others can hardly afford a meal.
Hundreds are dying in their homes every day because they cannot afford medication and hospital fees.
Some in towns have gone for years without seeing their parents in rural areas because bus fares are now out of their reach.
A multitude of school leavers - many of them from the rural areas - armed with school certificates and diplomas have descended on urban areas trying to find a job but to no avail.
University and college students have been reduced to jaywalkers in towns while the more daring ones have resorted to crime or become "dealers" to survive.
All these problems are a result of Mugabe's failed policies because the man has single-handedly presided over the ruination of this country's once thriving economy.
Yet with all these problems, Mugabe still wants to ask people who live in urban areas whether they are Zimbabweans or not?
Mr President, do you think people living in urban areas are Mongolians or Libyans or even Afghans?
We would like to remind you Mr President, lest you forget, people who live in urban areas are poverty-stricken Zimbabweans who are unlike you, your family and sycophantic Cabinet ministers who live like angels.
Mr President, let me remind you once more, by the same token that people living in rural areas are also suffering Zimbabweans who have borne the brunt of years of state neglect but have come to accept their intolerable condition as normal.
Don't you believe, Mr President, that you have abused Zimbabweans enough?
If Mugabe thinks about Zimbabweans in these simplistic terms, then for once I agree with the ZAPU idea of federalism that would result in Mugabe being sent packing and becoming a president in Uzumba-Maramba-Pfungwe, deep down in Mashonaland East where the rural folk has been cowed stiff to allow ZANU PF to rule forever.
There Mugabe could rule until Amen.
According to Mugabe's reasoning, in Uzumba-Maramba-Pfungwe he will be ruling Zimbabweans and not in Harare.
This is precisely the problem which I have with Zimbabweans who have allowed Mugabe to get away with this rubbish talk as if he owes us a living.
Sometimes I wish we could hire Zambians or South Africans, even for a day, to help us put an end to this madness by whatever legal means.
As the New Year beckons and we celebrate Christmas, let all progressive Zimbabweans reflect and renew themselves and gather their energies and willpower to tame a man who has wrecked our lives.
Pray for peace and not Mugabe's war during next year's presidential election and, above all, pray to God to help deliver us from evil as we walk the last mile.

Original_Search_Title: Song: Little Haven in Adamawa State
Headline: Nigeria: Song: Little Haven in Adamawa State
Body_Text:
Song local government in Adamawa State is the home town of Professor Jubril Aminu the renowned surgeon; former vice chancellor; former secretary of the National Universities Commission, former education Minister, former Petroleum-Resource Minister, and present Ambassador to the United States of America! On this merit alone; this is a place to dig its name and fame. Professor Jubril Aminu is a big name.
According to Abdurahman Maina yaji, Mutawwadi Michika, and information officer of the local government who exchanged views with newsmen recently in Yola, apart from this, Song, which is about 80 kilometres from Yola the Adamawa State Capital, is noted in tourist circles for the three-sisters rock, a natural phenomenal whose curvature is a beauty to behold.
On the trunk A road, to Maiduguri, Song is known for its peaceful countryside. The green lush grass, the rich savannah grassland, the potentials for investment, the rice, beans, legumes, tomato, spinach guava, lemon, oranges, banana, groundnut, maize, coco yam, and all the agricultural products you can think of, flourish there.
With the undulating land, the serene environment is a symbol of peace that eases the mind. The quietness and calmness of the area alone is a blessing, and is very good for studies, reflection and up date on former events.
Additionally it is one local government that has sizable number of cattle, sheep, and goats. Besides, the rich humus soil across the local government, is an indication of the economic buoyancy of the area.
This is why, Abdurahman Maina Yaji, the information officer emphasises that Ruma River can be used for purposes similar to the Argungu Fishing Festival in Kebbi State.
Five Kilometres wide, and seven Kilometres long, the Ruma River has millions of fish. With research experts who inspected the river from the Chad Basin Development Authority, and a visit from officials in Abuja-Ruma river is 15 kilometres from Song, the headquarters of the same council. This is food for throught for National Tourism Board.
On top of the green scenery, research indicates that each farming season earns more than a billion Naira annually from the crops that are grown there.
Therefore; not only is Song considered a tourist haven from this perspective, but efforts have been put in place by Alhaji Umaru Hamajam, the executive chairman of Song Local Government area in Adamawa State to give investors and tourists a delightful conducive atmosphere and environment when there.
A shopping complex has been built which is at this moment utilised by business men. There are 36 shops operational and quarters for rent, plus 8 chalets for guests and an administraion block.
Information gathered reveal that all roads linking the villages and towns including Gulatabal Salasa, Zumo, Paikin, Loko, Garin -Madugu, Dumne are graded to make users, visitors, Investors and guests accessible with tractors worth more than seven million Naira acquired by the chairman. The roads to Dumne to Dirma, winding to Salasa cost the local government endlessly N3 Million.
Alhaji Umaru Hammajam is the Chief Exeuctive and Chairman Song Local Govenrment Area in Adamawa State.
He is one who would not dip his hands into the treasury of the council.
Therefore this chairman was able to stablise workers salaries consistently too. He has been able to work on the welfare of staff carefully with due attention.
He has re-furbished the local government Secretariat and bought chairs, tables, curtains, air conditioners and has settled leave grants and typewriters have been bought for clerks.
To this end, he has been given various awards for industry, hard work, commitment to duty and to tolerance.
His relationship with the National Union of Local Government Employees is said to be harmonious and cordial.
The Song Local government area in Adamawa State dug 3 wells in each of the eleven wards and consitutencies and rehabilitated 2 each in the same places, which makes a total of 5 per 11 wards.
Forty three motorcycles were bought and distributed to unemployed Secondary School leavers on Loan to lessen hardship. Some have proved worthy ambassadors, and have been able to pay back the loans, and have purchased more on their own to generate lump sum revenue and independent self -sustenance.
These are apart from the buses that have been bought to ply Mubi Gombi, Biu, Maiduguri, Kano, Jos, Bauchi, Gombe, Jalingo, Yola, and surrounding market towns opening up business channels and swelling government purse in terms of revenue.
As the hometown of Professor Jubril Aminu, the medical Surgeon, this local government scored one of the best marks in the primary health care drive. It has conducted the National Programme on Immunisation (NPI) the National Immunisation Days (NID), kick Out Poliomyelitis, Roll Back Malaria have been successful here.
Portraits of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, Atiku Abubakar Vice-President, and Governor Boni Haruna adorn bill boards and residential buildings calling on parents to immunize their children. These are translated from English language, into Hausa and Fulfulde so that the messages are passed across. Song Local Government Area in Adamawa State has made a distinction going by current eftorts to construct drainages to alleviate the flood menace that engages the headquarter in a battle.
A little fact finding on the Universal Primary Education in the area is instructive. Since the teachers are provided necessary logistics and the improvement in rehabilitating of schools, a lot of water has passed under the bridge.
In the morning on week days it is nnpressive to watch pupils going to school, in green, white, yellow, brown uniforms in large groups with their bags and books. The Umaru Hammajam administration in Song LGA of Adamawa State has resuscitated the fallen glory of education; at most or at least in Song as a council and area.
Still, the National Poverty Education Programme (NAPEP) has taken off here. At the time of writing this report; graduates professionals, school certificate holders and Diploma holders are busy, requesting for the signature of the chairman; Alhaji Umaru Hammajam and endorsement by various councilors in the eleven wards for onward delivery to Yola.
Infact this is one aspect, the Obasanjo administration has to be commended for. This programme has lessened theft, idleness of the youth, and has gingered and stimulated acquisition of skills. In Song, the joy is palpable as the forms are available without passing through hurdles. The talk is PDP and NAPEP only, especially and particularly, the large troops of the employed, one saw at the secretarial that day.
But one thing that is interesting in Song local government area of Adamawa State is the innovation, creativity , inclination) inventiveness) and resourcefulness of Alhaji Umaru Hammajam, the chairman.
He has built a viewing centre in each of the eleven wards of the local government. He provided a television set each in the viewing centres. He provided a video set and an antennae too. Then, he provided a generator, a guardsman who is the operator to link the Adamawa Television in Yola and NT A Yola when they start transmission.
As from four O'clock every day; the neighbourhood gathers to watch programmes. This has enlightened them fairly on activities of the government and they are abreast of developments in Nigeria to the extent that they are no longer suspicious of visitors or journalists in particular. They do volunteer information and talk with confidence unlike during military rule.
Alhaji Umam Hammajam, the executive chairman, who is a learned Islamic scholar uses this to advantage by producing quality documentary programmes on his tremendous and remarkable achievements, clinics, roads, primary schools, unveiling plaque, delivering speeches and what have you. These are normally beamed to them on other weeks or when an occasion occurs.
I saw reason in bringing information dissemination closer and nearer to the people in this strategy which takes note of government activities.
Finally, he requested the newspaper distributor in Yola to establish an area office in Song headquarters, for proper accessibility to dailies, newspapers, magazines and publications.
In all, Song local government area in Adamawa State is a delightful place to visit The people are innocent and very ready to give you a helping hand with your broken down car or when in need of pure water, after an exhaustible energy journey from Salasa to Dirrna, Satellite towns of the area. There is no doubt an individual who thirsts for fresh air, a quiet countryside to watch nature in its true form and beauty will have a blissfully rich visit.

Original_Search_Title: Display Maturity, Ibori Advises Opponents
Headline: Nigeria: Display Maturity, Ibori Advises Opponents
Body_Text:
Oleh, Delta — GOVERNOR James Ibori of Delta State yesterday counselled his opponent
in the race towards 2003 gubernatorial election to display political
maturity in their campaign strategies.
Ibori who spoke at the rally of the People's Democratic Party (PDP)
in the Delta South Senatorial District at Oleh, in Isoko North Local
Government Council of the state, advised those opposing his second
term bid that only God install leaders.
The governor who was adopted as the gubernatorial candidate of the
ruling People's Democratic party in the 2003 election in the state by
all the local government councils in the senatorial district,
enjoined his opponents to shun campaign of calumny and be guided by
his myriads of achievements in their actions and utterances.
His words: You see it, is a shame that we don't learn from our
mistakes, it's a great shame, if you look at the country today, there
are 36 states, if you look at the section of the country where this
unusual drumbeat are emanating from, it is our area, is it right to
say that our brothers elsewhere, people do not contest governorship
election there? There are those who want to contest there, but you
don't hear them, everyday, everyday, we fight ourselves, but you
don't hear from other people."
"So we must learn, we must get sense, our people must get sense, if
we don't get sense, it is only God that can help us."
Ibori accused those challenging his second term bid of causing
confusion for no just cause, adding that his achievements in the just
30 months in office stand him out as the only candidate to beat in
the 2003 gubernatorial election.
The governor also spoke about the need for continuity in the
governance of the state by the PDP led government stressing that,"
it's the only meaningful and consistent way to bring some development
to our long suffering people."
He added "this continuity we are talking about make a lot of sense
and I want to tell you about continuity, because if you go to farm,
you pluck yam, the yam has not germenated, you run away, the next
person would come, how will he find where you hid the yam, he won't
see it."
"We are tired of how they used our people, for experiment, today
experiment, tomorrow experiment, it's people that suffers from
experiment, so that is why we are all in support of continuity."
He however, implored all PDP faithfuls to remain firm and keep faith
with the party, "respect the respect since the party is supreme."
The governor debunked the claims about the existence of a parallel
state executive council in the state PDP and enjoined the PDP
faithfuls to ignore "totally and treat these rascals with ignominy
because the more you talk about them, the more you think about them,
the more you make them prominent, they are no where in this party."
"If they want to contest for power, they should come in and contest
for vote, but I doubt if they can make it.'
On the new entrants into PDP fold, Ibori assured that they would be
treated as "if they have joined many years ago."

Original_Search_Title: High Interest Rate Worries Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: High Interest Rate Worries Obasanjo
Body_Text:
President Olusegun Obasanjo yesterday in Abuja expressed concern over high interest rates charged by banks in the country even as he stated plans by his administration to bring the rate down.
Speaking when a delegation of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) visited him, the president said his commitment towards bringing down the rate was informed by the fact that it was counter productive.
"As long as the rate of interest is that high, we cannot have a productive economy. It is a problem I will continue to fight until we get a solution. It is one thing we must get right," he said.
The president debunked the claim of commercial banks that they charged high interest rates as a result of inflation, saying, it was a wrong solution.
"Today, if we have any inflation at all, it is very small, the price of gari, yam and beans have come down. Those of rice, petrol and diesel are stable. So where does the inflation come from?" he asked.
the president then directed that an immediate action be taken to ascertain the true rate of inflation in the country.
He explained that a more correct choice of the item and weighting used by the Federal Office of Statistics to determine the rate of inflation would result in a downward review of the rate currently put at 19 per cent.
"There are many things we have to do to the economy. If all other things are right, we will not have violent fluctuations in the exchange rate," he said.
He warned that government would impose punitive sanctions on any bank that  took "morality out of business and still engaged in "round tripping to the detriment of the nation's economy."
He allayed fears raised by the MAN delegation over the multiplicity of taxes imposed by state and local governments on investors.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Accuses Banks of Sabotaging Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Accuses Banks of Sabotaging Economy
Body_Text:
Abuja — PRESIDENT Olusegun Obasanjo has accused banks in the country of
sabotaging the economy through the high interest rate charged on
loans.
President Obasanjo condemned the attitude of the banks while
receiving a delegation of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria
(MAN) in Abuja.
According to the President, there was no justifications for the high
interest rate of the banks.
He said that the inflation rate on which the banks based their
interest rates was not high enough to warrant the posture of the
banks.
He said: "Today, if we have any inflation at all. It is very small.
The prices of garri, yam and beans have come down, those of rice,
petrol and diesel are stable, so where does the inflation come from?"
He, however, directed that proper action be taken to determine the
true rate of inflation in the country.
He added that as long as the interest rate remained high, the nation
could not have a productive economy.
"It is a problem I will continue to fight until we get a solution, it
is one thing we must get right."
He conceded, however, that there were some things to be done to make
the economy more stable at the macro level, adding that this would
eliminate violent fluctuation in the exchange rate.
He warned that his administration might impose punitive action on
banks which engaged in round-tripping.
"I will not allow people to ruin this country for their little
conveniences and I will not get tired of sanctioning those who clog
the wheels of progress," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Ruler Decries Dependence On Oil
Headline: Nigeria: Traditional Ruler Decries Dependence On Oil
Body_Text:
Port-Harcourt — The Chairman of the Rivers State Council of Traditional Rulers and the Amayanbo of Grand Bonny, King Edward Asimini Dappa Pepple, Perekule XI, has criticised Nigeria's dependence on revenue from petroleum to the detriment of agriculture.
Speaking at the NNPC/Shell Farmers' Day celebrations in Port Harcourt , Rivers State at the weekend, Pepple said " The importance of encouraging and motivating our numerous farmers who more often than not go unrecognised, whose efforts go unsung all due to emphasis and misguided dependence of our economy on oil should not be understated".
The royal father, who commended the management of the Shell Petroleum Development Company (SPDC) for organising the yearly celeberation, said the event should be properly publicised to the grassroots farmers to encourage them to grow more food.
Not much, he said, had been acheived by the Federal Government in the agricultural sector in spite of spirited efforts to enhance food production.
The General Manager (Production) East, SPDC, Mr Samuel Inyang, said the Niger Delta region was blessed with more than oil and gas. The region, Inyang said, could boast of abundance of food crops such as cassava, plantain, rice, yam and oil palm.
He disclosed that SPDC organised the farmers' day to identify with, motivate and encourage the Nigerian farmer to produce more food. "The Farmers Day also provides the opportunity to recognise outstanding farmers through the award of prizes as encouragement towards greater achievement".
Inyang disclosed that N11.3 million had been disbursed to 29 co-operative societies under the Micro Credit for Agricultural Development Scheme (MISCAD).
"The scheme, which is facilitated by the SPDC in partnership with the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), the First Bank of Nigeria Plc and Co-operative Development Bank will be enlarged to accommodate new groups", he said.
Five states, namely Akwa-Ibom, Imo, Rivers, Bayelsa and Abia participated in the celebration which also witnessed the participation of research institutes.

Original_Search_Title: Dr Freddistein's Monster
Headline: Zambia: Dr Freddistein's Monster
Body_Text:
IT was ghastly hot and humid in Cha Cha Cha Road, with storm clouds
gathering overhead. To escape the heat, I took a short cut down a
shady alley, round the back of the old Ambassador Funeral Parlour,
now long deceased.
Or so I thought. For halfway down the alley I came to a sign saying
Kafupi Freddistein, Funeral Director. Curiosity opened the front door
for me, so I stepped inside.
'Ah, Spectator Kalaki!' said a little fellow in a white suit, as he
got up from his desk to shake my hand. 'I'm Freddistein. I've been
expecting you.' 'Just a bit of rigor mortis in the joints,' I said.
'I could linger on for years.' 'Don't waste money on the pills,' he
laughed, 'you'll have nothing left for the funeral.' 'So
comfortable,' I said, as I lowered myself into a silk lined coffin
and closed my eyes. 'Outside there are people fighting like rats over
the smallest morsel, but in here its so peaceful. The government has
really brought peace to Zambia.' 'And prosperity,' said Freddistein.
'Funerals are now very big business.
With people dying at the rate of 300,000 a year, its created
employment for thousands.
Just imagine, ten years of coffins, all put in a line, would be 6,000
kilometres long! The line would stretch all the way from here to the
World Bank in Washington.' 'Then they would really be able to see our
progress,' I laughed, as I lay back comfortably in my coffin. 'But
can this government really be re-elected after sending so many to the
grave?' 'To heaven! That's why we were declared a Christian country!'
chortled Freddistein. 'The government has saved all these people from
earthly torment, and given them heavenly peace!' 'But if all the
satisfied voters have gone to heaven!' I said, sitting up straight in
my coffin and wagging my finger, 'only dissatisfied voters remain!'
'You're right!' he admitted. 'So I'll let you into the secret. The
Movement for Mobilising the Departed is going to get the vote out
from the graveyard. Three million dead voters! It's an inbuilt
majority! The secret of re-election is resurrection!' 'I thought your
job was to put them in the grave, not take them out!' 'Come with me,'
he said, taking my hand to help me out of my coffin, and leading me
into the mortuary. He pointed to all the smiling corpses resting
happily in their coffins. 'You have now departed from Zambia, and
entered Zombia, where election success depends mostly on timing. We
shall be holding the election during Halloween, when all the zombies
rise up from the grave. All we need is a leader for the Movement for
Mobilising the Departed.' 'How about you?' 'Good gracious no!'
laughed Freddistein. 'Only a zombie can lead other zombies!' 'So how
will you find him?' 'Not find him! Make him! Not many people realise
that I have a Monster Making Doctorate from the University of
Blantyre,' he declared as he flung open the lid of a huge coffin.
There lay a monstrous ungainly corpse, with swollen belly and limbs
stitched clumsily together. Two rusty metal electrodes were attached
to each side of its head.
'You really think this thing can become a great leader?' 'Only if he
is truly representative of all zombies. So I have stitched together
parts from zombies of every tribe, from different graveyards all over
Zambia.' 'But is this leader a truly indigenous Zambian Zombie?' 'I
have taken parts only from those bodies which were buried in Zambia
more than two hundred years ago, before any foreigner arrived.' 'Two
hundred years! Did you find a brain still in good condition?' 'That's
been a problem,' sighed Freddistein, 'the brain is too tasty, and the
worms go for it first. For the moment, I've had to substitute with
cauliflower.' 'And what are the two electrodes for?' 'They are
connected to a huge lightening conductor high on the roof. When the
first storm comes, a massive spark of electricity will surge down
into the body and then...' As he spoke there was a huge crash and
flash of light. Both Freddistein and myself were thrown backwards as
the monster's coffin was blown apart. Then, as we watched, the
monster slowly sat up with a creaky rasping sound, and opened its
eyes.
'Er er, um um ah,' it said, with a terrible moan.
Freddiestein put his arm around the monster. 'There there,' he said
kindly, 'you're amongst friends. You are going to be our great
leader.' 'Owa glate reader,' said the monster slowly, trying to mimic
its master.
'Er, um, so I yam notty a cabbagy.' 'Of course not,' said
Fleddistein, 'you're a cauliflower.' 'Aaaghh,' cried the monster, as
tears poured down its stitched and battered face, 'ah yused tuh have
a glate blain.' 'Don't worry about that,' snapped Freddistein, 'you
can use mine!'

Original_Search_Title: Traders Return To Giri Onion Market
Headline: Nigeria: Traders Return To Giri Onion Market
Body_Text:
The Giri onion market along the Gwagwalada-Airport road junction destroyed
during the president's tour of the FCT is springing up again as traders have
begun reconstruction.
When Abuja Trust called at the market over the weekend, the usually busy
market noted for cheap supplies of onions, local and Irish potatoes, fruits
and yams was a shadow of itself.
Apart from few traders displaying their wares mostly onions on tables in the
middle of the ruins of their shanty shades, the ever busy market would have
passed for a ghost town.
Some of the traders with members of their families were seen gathering
sticks for the reconstruction of their shades.
Some of then who spoke with Abuja Trust described the incident as a blow
especially during their festive period.
"This is a very big blow," one of the traders said. "They burnt everything
here to ashes just because President Obasanjo was passing by.  Who told them
that Obasanjo was not aware of road side markets," he queried.
Abuja Trust gathered that men of the Abuja Environmental Protection Board
had destroyed the Giri market in readiness for President Obasanjo's visit
last week.
In a related development, owners of road structures destroyed in preparation
for the visit are returning to base.  Some of them affected in Berger, Bwari
and Dutse are mobilising to site, Abuja Trust gathered.

Original_Search_Title: Bone of Contention
Headline: Nigeria: Bone of Contention
Body_Text:
Do traditional healers do a better job of healing broken bones? That is the bone of contention
Godwin Ijakoli, 42, a yam trader at Ketu,   Lagos   fractured his ankle in a motor accident along   Ore/Benin Road  . Doctors, on examination of his case at the   National  Orthopaedic  Hospital  , Igbobi,   Lagos   told him that his leg was badly damaged. His chances of walking again, they said, were slim. The doctors recommended amputation. Ijakoli did not want to lose his leg. In his desperation, he opted for native treatment at Akaeze village, Ivo local government area of   Ebonyi  State  . Two months after he began his treatment Ijakoli could walk again.
More and more people with similar cases are going to traditional healers.
Richard Imhansuen, a prominent traditional bone healer at Old Army Barracks, Abule Egba,   Lagos  , said people preferred traditional treatment because modern medicine had failed many others. He told Newswatch that he had successfully treated people who could not get healed from the   National  Orthopaedic  Hospital  , Igbobi,   Lagos  .
Imhansuen told Newswatch that bone treatment methods varied from one healing home to the other. Some traditional healers use herbs for treatment. Some use what is loosely known as "chicken approach." Before the treatment begins, the leg of a live chicken is broken. "As the broken leg of the chicken gets healed, so also is the person," Imhausuen said.
Leonard Mbamara, medical director, Albina Mejeel Clinic, Iba, Ojo,   Lagos   and an orthopaedic surgeon said amputation would be necessary only when the damaged leg becomes a threat to the entire body.
He said the attraction for the traditional operators was because of its accessibility in the rural areas coupled with the cheap cost of treament.
A doctor at NOH, Igbobi, who didn't want his name in print said native bone healers were doing what they knew little or nothing about. He told Newswatch that as far as bone treatment was concerned, traditionalists only engaged themselves in trial-and-error, thereby complicating problems for ignorant patients. He said if it worked out for them, they claimed credit, believing that they had "superior forces" working for them. But when it fails, patients always bear the brunt and go to hospital as a last resort.
Sylvanus N. Omeonu, medical director and orthopaedic surgeon,   Hopeville  Hospital   and Maternity, Gboko,   Benue  State  , however, did not rule out the potentials of traditional bone healers completely. He said some native doctors have acquired the skills of 'reduction and fixation' through their traditional means of massaging the affected area with herbs and other means.
Omeonu noted that the method works for them in some cases, especially when there is no open wound. He said that patients were at the mercy of these practitioners when there was open wound and it was infected. He said the obvious handicap of traditionalists was ignorance. "They know little or nothing about the body anatomy, but are concerned only about the bones. So, any other problem affecting the body system, the patients are left to their fate," Omeonu stated.
He said the advantage doctors have over traditional healers was that they were specially trained and understood the body anatomy better. "Happily, however, many traditional bone healers are now improving on their practice by attaching medical personnel to their healing homes," he explained.

Original_Search_Title: The Killings Are Premeditated - Chaha
Headline: Nigeria: The Killings Are Premeditated - Chaha
Body_Text:
Zaki-Biam — Chief Benjamin Chaha is a distinguished Tiv man. A Zaki-Biam indigene, Chaha
was speaker of the House of Representatives in the Second Republic. He spoke
to Weekly Trust in Zaki-Biam
Weekly Trust: Sir, what's your impression of the recent crisis in Zaki-Biam?
Chaha: Well, I wouldn't say I have any impression because this is a useless
thing, senseless. And it's done out of sentiments.
WT: Sentiments?
Chaha: Yes, sentiments. Because the talk of soldiers being killed miles away
from this place... and one would wonder why this place should be affected as
a result of the killing of this soldiers. The killing happened in Gbeji...
40-50 miles away from this place and they went and killed about 300 people
there, and they the soldiers were still not satisfied. Why should they come
to Zaki-Biam? And then they were headed for Katsina Ala. And along the line
they destroyed Malu's house. Why? It's a premeditated action.
WT: Why do you think so?
Chaha: I feel they think Zaki-Biam is the pride of this place. That
Zaki-Biam is the cradle of food in this place, Tivland. They wouldn't say
that though. But from every indication, it shows that they are against the
economic factor of Zaki-Biam. Otherwise, why should they have destroyed this
place?
WT: Are you saying some people don't like the fact that Zaki-Biam is a
significant trading post for produce?
Chaha: That's right. Because when you talk of 'food basket' of the nation,
you are essentially referring to Zaki-Biam.
WT: When you say food basket, in practical terms, what do you mean?
Chaha: I mean this is where you get food in abundance. Go to the yam market
in Zaki-Biam, you'll see what I mean. Whereas there are other big produce
areas in Plateau and Adamawa, none however compares to Zaki-Biam. Farmers
come with their stuff from all parts of Nigeria to sell here. See, we are
proud of it.
WT: Against the backdrop of the age-long conflict between Tivs and  Jukuns
around this area, how do you rationalise the current round of crisis? What
does it tell you?
Chaha: This is a difficult question. Because from time immemorial Jukuns
have been trying to claim this area. Once they were said to rule this area,
so to say, not conquered. They were a little bit organised than Tiv people.
And so we were just wanderers while they had some settlements. . . . So they
feel this area is theirs. But how far that is true, I don't know because I
have not delved into it to know the true picture, to know exactly what
happened, what took place that they could claim this area. You see, we have
Ogoja people fighting with us but our own fight with Jukun is more serious.
It's a kind of planned fight and there's no going back. What we'll do, they
are sceptical. But they hire people, pay fighters, mercenaries... you kill
them all and tomorrow you'll find the number is doubled (he laughs).
WT: Sir, were you around when the soldiers were first invited to keep the
peace here?
Chaha: That's right, I was around here. I will like to single out myself. I
was so impressed at first. Why? Because I know the state government doesn't
have an army but we need people to protect us.
First of all, Jukuns started bringing in soldiers without telling our side
here that they are keeping soldiers for peace. We couldn't know and that is
why that incident took place.
You see, our fighters couldn't differentiate, they couldn't know who was
who. So they went to Bakor to try and keep peace. When those people rejected
them, they went to Gbeji.
WT: So are you saying it was a case of mistaken identity that the initial
soldiers that were killed were Jukuns in army uniform?
Chaha: That is right. Yes, I think so. Because as Tivs were asking for
identity, they had no numbers, nothing. The vehicle was not an army vehicle
and so on and so forth. So you see, the whole thing is under heavy cloud.
But I wouldn't say anything because I was not directly involved. But I think
there is something that was fishy about the whole thing.
WT: Sir, already there is a problem identified. If you are to provide a
solution, what would be your solution?
Chaha: What problem? The fighting between the Tivs and Jukuns? You see the
fighting has got a very deep root. But as we are saying, they've not come to
me or come together to decide what to do.
The Jukun people feel Tiv people are not giving them support politically.
Because a few years back when a Jukun was nominated to be the chairman of a
local government there, the Tiv people approached him that they would like
to produce the deputy and he refused. After further  consultation, they
still  refused. Then the Tivs felt they were not wanted because a Hausa man,
Bello was made the chairman. He was the one who made a Tiv man his deputy
and started the whole matter.
WT: What year was that?
Chaha: That  was about four years ago because that was the last regime about
four years now. So the trouble now is that the Jukun people do not want  to
see Tiv people in their own area.
WT: Sir, you did say you were not in the picture and as an indigene of this
place, I don't think it would be better to fold your hands to see things
getting out of control. Can you now give a solution, suggestion or advice?
Chaha: But you see, in politics, no enemy is permanent, that I know very
well. The only trouble is that the Jukun people are completely inaccessible.
If they see a Tiv man, they'll kill him now. Where do we seat together to
decide what to do? Otherwise I say I would prefer that Tiv people living in
Taraba should come together with the Jukuns and decide. You see, when they
turned down the request to make a Tiv man the deputy chairman of a local
government, we warned them seriously that that was not the way to live
together. The population of the Tiv people in Taraba is in tens. But their
understanding is that the Tiv people are settlers, they've come to farm. And
I think that is ridiculous. But the solution can be found if they can pipe
down and try to understand one another.
WT: Do you think government at federal level is capable of bringing any
solution to these crises?
Chaha: I am afraid not. That is my fear.
WT: Perhaps you share the view of a majority of Tivs that the federal
government is partisan in the conflict between Tivs and Jukuns, and that
that is why it deliberately ordered soldiers in Tivland to carry out
retaliatory killings after the 19 soldiers were killed?
Chaha: I do partially, I don't know for sure. But from every indication, one
would believe something fishy is happening at the federal quarters.
First of all, our people started accusing Danjuma and then Atiku and
Obasanjo. That why should Obasanjo as the head of state go quietly to sign
that so many weapons be removed from the armoury to fight Tiv people. That
is, in a way go and destroy them. Is that a solution? It can never be a
solution.
WT: So then sir, who do you think  gave the orders for soldiers to come and
carry out the destructions here?
Chaha: But we have a head of state. Danjuma is just a minister of defence.
He can use his own initiatives, yes. But Obasanjo is the overall leader and
commander-in-chief of the armed forces and I am quite sure that he appended
his own signature on the collection of these weapons.
WT: It appears there's a dwindling fortune for the Tiv tribe in Nigeria. A
short while ago, their sons headed the Nigerian army and the ruling party in
the country. Now both positions are gone, coupled with rampaging soldiers on
their land which culminated in the destruction of Zaki-Biam and the killing
of large numbers of Tivs. Won't you say it's a critical period for your
people?
Chaha: No, no, no. I know the things that happened to Victor Malu, yes. We
would like to condemn what happened to Malu. He's a nice person. Maybe I am
wrong but Malu is a man who failed to use his own position while he was on
the seat. As for Gemade, I don't know what he was doing. He was there and
the Tiv people, many of us couldn't feel his impact. I am sorry to say this.
Maybe I am too frank to say this but that is how it was. Their removal would
not affect the Tiv man on the streets, because how many of us were relying
on  them? While they were there in their positions, what did we get? Without
fear I say to them. I wish I could have them to stand as you are doing now
to say this.  Ask them what they did. So that is a wrong notion.
WT: As we can see sir, your property has been destroyed. Is this the only
property you have here in town that was torched?
Chaha: No. All my small buildings around the town have been destroyed.
WT: Have you been able to estimate the value of what you have lost?
Chaha: No, I have not started that yet. I want the authority if at all they
care to do something. Really, I will sit down quietly and decide.
WT: Was there anyone in the house when it was burnt or everyone deserted the
house?
Chaha: Who could be in the house at that time? I don't know what they used
here. Bomb or what, I don't know. But I think they used about three, can we
say one, two, three to destroy this house.
WT: They knew this was your house?
Chaha: They saw it. But on Wednesday, this was the first house they started
destroying. I was within earshot, I was just up here. And when they started,
they said yes, that is my house.
WT: Are you still in politics, sir?
Chaha: Well, for as long as I live.
WT: So what role are you playing right now?
Chaha: Advisory. I advise my people, like decide for them what to do. I feel
I am not too old to do these things. I feel I am still doing fine. But when
they feel that I am getting deranged, then they can stop me.
WT: Finally sir, can you compare politics as you played it in the Second
Republic  with what is happening now?
Chaha: You know I don't know what is happening now. No. You see, the thing
is that we had our own elections and they were accepted because the army had
stayed too long in power. Otherwise, it was nothing to write home about.

Original_Search_Title: Soldiers in Tivland; Peace Keepers Or Law Breakers?
Headline: Nigeria: Soldiers in Tivland; Peace Keepers Or Law Breakers?
Body_Text:
Zaki-Bam — It's a season of harvest for soldiers on peace mission in Tivland. But their
'barn' is brimming with cash and not with 'yam tubers' - main produce of the
area. At each of the 10 checkpoints straddling the Katsina-Ala - Zaki-Biam
road, commercial vehicle drivers must pay soldiers before they proceed. It
doesn't matter to the gun-wielding military men that a driver has just paid
some money two kilometres back to soldiers and will have to part yet again
with the standard fare, N20 for cars at the next checkpoint, which will be
another kilometre ahead. Lorries pay as much as N100 depending on the size
of their carriage.
Surprisingly, the drivers obey the unwritten rule almost religiously. They
approach a checkpoint in a near solemn manner, like a hearse arriving a
cemetery, and then they will obediently park off the main road; once in a
while they put off their engines, otherwise a soldier haughtily walks by the
driver's side, driver and soldier shake hands, that's it. One buys his
passage, the other sells his honour.
At one checkpoint, the soldier would not accept N20 having inspected the car
and found out that it was heavy with goods. He shouted, "Driver, ya money na
two leaf." No argument, the driver obeyed by adding another 'green leaf',
making it N40. The next 'cash point' (read: checkpoint) was more dramatic.
The car approached and the soldiers ignored its presence. Driver parked and
honked. One of the soldiers turned and immediately directed his newly issued
Kalashnikov at the car. No one needed to tell the driver the appropriate
thing to do. He went out to meet them, clutching a 'green leaf,' and on
reaching shook hands.
However, no luck this time it appeared. The soldier unveiled the squeezed
note and frowned; from the distance he could see some hefty socks barely
sitting conveniently in the car's trunk. He promptly marched the driver to
the tail of the car, pointing at the goods as he turned to go, he said to
the driver: "When you're ready to go, bring five leaf." Well, the driver
gathered three N20 notes, and they accepted. Vroom we moved on.
At the end of the journey, having survived the 10 checkpoints, the driver,
Terhemba (not real name as he pleaded to remain anonymous) said the soldiers
collectively took away from him the sum of N260 which is about 15 per cent
of the particular trip's revenue.
Most likely, the soldiers do not know that their taxing goods and services
that ply the Katsina-Ala - Zaki-Biam road is triggering an inflationary
trend in the prices of goods in the area. Each trader that is taxed tries to
recoup what he has spent from the cost of his goods or services.
Which is why the principal demands of the Tivs - rural and urban - is for
the federal government to withdraw soldiers from their land. A prominent Tiv
and indigene of Zaki-Biam, Chief Benjamin Chaha, who is also one-time
speaker of the House of Representatives in the Second Republic told Weekly
Trust that the only recipe for peace at the moment is the withdrawal of
soldiers from Tivland.
The paramount ruler of Tiv, Dr Alfred Akawe Torkula, Tor Tiv IV said in a
local Makurdi paper, raised an unusual alarm, saying soldiers were engaging
in acts of indiscipline including the killing of his people indiscriminately
at Gbeji, Tse A door, Zaki-Biam and Anyiin. But perhaps the most indicting
accusation by the Tor Tiv is that the soldiers were raping Tiv women. "The
soldiers are violating our wives and female children. Our men are beaten up,
killed and our farmlands are plundered by soldiers," he said.
The Tor Tiv may be referring to incidents in Katsina-Ala, where health
workers have embarked on an indefinite strike to protest against cases of
sexual harassment and rape against Tiv women by men of the Nigerian army.
The news, first reported by a western media, said the nurses were demanding
for the unconditional withdrawal of soldiers before they can resume work.
They said they are afraid to venture into the streets at night for fear of
harassment and rape.
Zaki-Biam, however, is a different case altogether. After soldiers killed
more than 200 people and demolished houses there, including those of the
former chief of army staff, General Victor Malu and former speaker, Benjamin
Chaha, all government agencies have deserted the town which prides itself as
the "food basket of Benue State." Of course, Benue State as is well- known
by its slogan is the "food basket of the nation."
At present, public health services in Ukum Local Government Area, which
embodies Zaki-Biam, do not exist. Meaning there's not a single government
hospital running services since the invasion by soldiers. Likewise, there's
no law enforcement agency of any kind; the police station is in rubbles,
flattened by military bombs. There's also no administrative services as the
local government secretariat does not function. As for the chairman, nobody
has seen him around the secretariat for more than a month. According to
Isaac Iorver, a local government education authority official in Ukum, of
the 150 government-owned schools in the area, none is open because "parents
in the first place will not send the children to school in the face of what
is happening. And again, teachers are afraid to come out so that they don't
get shot at by soldiers."
Therefore, in the absence of any government services in Zaki-Biam, the
people can be seen wandering about, and those in business, mainly motorcycle
transporters and food vendors are not making enough. The population of the
town has reduced to only about a quarter of its actual number.
Former speaker, Chaha's rationalising of the situation is that there's a
deliberate attempt to destroy Zaki-Biam, which according to him is a
significant economic zone not only in Benue but also in the country. "When
you talk of food basket of the nation, you're essentially referring to
Zaki-Biam. I mean this is where you get food in Nigeria," said Chief Chaha.
An official of the Zaki-Biam Yam Market Traders Association, Uchoh Aguta
Uchoh, shed more light on the importance of the city as a produce trading
post. Uchoh, who's the association's secretary, said before the crisis,
there used to be an average of 100 lorries coming into the yam market to
load yams to different destinations in the country. Now, however, not more
than one or two come around on a normal day.
"There's no other yam depot. Maybe even in Africa, that can compare with the
Zaki-Biam yam market," said Uchoh, adding that the crisis has affected the
market more than the outside world knows. "Many trucks were burnt and so
were thousands of tubers of yams destroyed by the soldiers. Almost the
entire market was destroyed. Again about 23 people were killed in the
market."
He disclosed also that collectively, members of the Yam Traders Association
have lost more than a billion naira as a result of the soldiers' raid. "Our
trucks, yam tubers, sheds and cash are worth more than a billion naira,"
Uchoh said.
The traders, however, according to Uchoh, are not expecting any compensation
from government in spite of the fact that the vice president, Atiku
Abubakar, House of Representatives speaker, Ghali Na'Abba and Senator Chuba
Okadigbo have all visited the market separately.
Again, because there's a virtual lack of government presence in the city,
public health service is now only affordable by a few who can afford charges
