at private hospitals.
Thus one of the private clinics in Zaki-Biam, JIDDS hospital teems with
patients daily who ordinarily would have gone to government-owned hospitals
were they operating. The medical director, Dr Ityozenda Jinawa Dzeen told
Weekly Trust that suddenly, things became dull to the extent that there are
no public health facilities functioning.
However, even without the law in place, to occasionally arrest the thief or
keep order, the "Zaki-Biam community is going rather very well," said Dr
Dzeen.

Original_Search_Title: Managing The Engulfing Filth
Headline: Ghana: Managing The Engulfing Filth
Body_Text:
Cape Coast — ASSUMING THAT everybody living in your town drinks three sachets of
iced water a day, be the water "Opanin de panin" or pure water;"
assuming that all of them throw the polythene containers in the
neighbourhood - you could have 418,110 bags a day in such a town as
Koforidua with a population of 139,370.
In a week, 2,926,770; in a month, 11,707,080 and in a year
1,404,849,600 bags would be littering your town. Supposing that the
waste management system of your area breaks down or gets so bad that
the "rubbers," as the bags are wrongly called, are not cleared - in
just one year the whole place could be engulfed in disused polythene.
That is the real threat to such a small district as New Juaben with a
population density of 1,066 persons/Km2. That is, in fact, the threat
to other urban areas like Suhum, Nsawam, Akyem Oda, Nkawkaw and even
smaller towns to which garbage build-up was alien until recently.
And if the menace of polythene littering is accepted as an issue of
public concern, then the larger issue of all waste - both liquid and
solid waste - must be even of graver concern.
Though one may argue that the municipal, district, urban and town
councils will not sit down for rubbish to engulf their towns their
capacities to clean our towns remain in serious doubt.
THE CASE OF NEW JUABEN, AKYEM ODA
Answers to questionnaire served the New Juaben Municipal Assembly
(NJMA) showed that every single day 36 tonnes of solid waste is
lifted from the municipality for disposal. In a month, that is 1,080
tonnes.
The administration also lifts 200 tonnes of liquid waste a day or
6,000 tonnes every month. This is at a considerable cost to the NJMA.
Running and maintenance cost on vehicle use etc., took ¢460 million
of its budget estimates this year. Cumulative actual amount spent as
at the end of November was ¢219,768,635. Birim North whose biggest
town is Oda spent ¢104,291,600 last year on waste management and
estimates spending ¢110,774,309 this year.
Though exact figures could not be obtained from Akuapem South
(Nsawam) and Suhum, it was learnt that each spends over ¢100 million,
managing waste.
One common challenge is the lack of disposal sites. So the question
that follows is whether these considerable expenditures are able to
maintain our towns and villages neat, sweet-scented and safe for
habitation.
GROSSLY-INADEQUATE RESOURCES
In spite of New Juaben's high figures and the fact that the
authorities do a lot to keep the Eastern Regional capital neater than
the district capitals, the logistics and their effect remain woefully
inadequate. In use for lifting at the moment are 24 skip or small
refuse containers and eight roll on/roll off or large refuse
containers.
Over 50 plastic buckets had been placed at vantage points in the
municipality since year 2000 for people to dump solid waste in them.
But "we have 48 electoral areas and even if we allocate five
containers to each, that would be 240. This implies that the
containers at hand are not enough." Mr. Avvah, the co-ordinating
director admitted.
Are all the assemblies able to lift the containers after emptying the
small receptacles every day? No. In actual fact, far more rubbish
remains in the towns than is carted for disposal. More than the local
authorities, it is rain that occasionally cleans our streets and
gutters, thoroughly. As one cynic observed "God is our tankass."
Cholera outbreaks, mosquito breeding, malaria spread and dozens of
health hazards spawned by our worsening sanitation all cost our local
authorities, government and families a lot of money, pain and
unnecessary deaths.
The story of malaria and cholera insurgence, as a result of waste
mismanagement, at Nsawam has already received considerable reportage.
EFFECTIVE WASTE MANAGEMENT?
That is not a mirage! But that can only come about if the key players
quite understand the unfortunate Ghanaian way of life that churns out
tonnes of waste every hour into the environment. You need to visit
Koforidua "Bode" to see the grass dead, yam plants, sand and other
waste carted alongside the tubers all the way from Afram Plains,
Bamboi and the North to the municipality.
When fresh maize appear on the market between June and August and
November and December, the husk and cobs are thrown about with wanton
abandon. From mid-December through February citrus fruits take the
place of maize and our market places and streets are littered like
orchards.
In Easter the garbage that mangoes and cane sugar bring, and the
houseflies too, make you wish the season never comes again. If you
can cast your mind to the gutter near you, the charcoal market in
your area, the coconut seller's point, the public toilet area and the
slaughter house nearby, you can picture how filthy we can be.
Where dustbins are provided some people prefer to throw the waste
elsewhere or at the base of the receptacle. Looks like what has
recently been described as "Pure Madness." We need also to know the
extent to which our people appreciate the effect of their actions.
Osagyefo Amoatia Ofori Panin has observed that when you talk about
the environment in this country, people do not fully appreciate
because it is not something whose impact they can see in the short
run. Avvah adds that the cultural practices of the Ghanaian remains a
major drawback on waste reduction and management.
To reverse the trend, therefore, the people will have to be led from
the negative attitudes, perceptions and practices that encourage them
to spew out so much garbage into the neighbourhood. The Ghana Medical
Association (GMA) at the end of its 43rd AGM urged the government to
evolve effective waste management systems.
RECYCLING
Prof Agyeman Badu Akosa, the GMA's immediate past president, said
glass particles should be separated from wood and other biodegradable
garbage.
"Separate the plastic, polythene and the metals as well right from
the home or unit area before they get to the refuse dump."
Once they are so separated, find the industrial use for each and
recycle them into exactly those. That will ensure effective waste
management that provides jobs and creates wealth. It may interest
some readers to hear that the most valuable car seat cushions are
made from the fibre of coconuts; the same coconut waste that choke
our gutters and grow into speed rumps on our streets.
Major Courage Quashiga lamented at Old Akrade only two months ago
that now that the population of Accra has virtually exploded, a
compost-making plant built in the First Republic has broken down. Why
don't we think of establishing compost-making plants at Koforidua,
Nsawam, Oda, Nkawkaw, etc., etc.?
EDUCATION
The first three necessities in all these would be education,
education and education. Education of the farmer/middleman who carts
the foodstuff to the town to discard as much garbage as possible;
education of the consumer to dispose the waste strategically or
responsibly and education of the waste manager or private businessman
to turn the huge garbage into goods and cash.
Private sector participation will be needed and those clamouring for
road and building contracts need to be re-oriented. LAW But above
all, the education should go hand-in-hand with the bold enforcement
of sanitation bye-laws. Each of the 15 districts has a code of
sanitation bye-law; few, however, has mustered the will power to
enforce them.
New Juaben, this year, prosecuted 18 people who were fined various
sums for breaking such laws but failed to sustain the exercise. As we
step into the new year, 2002, and as Ghana attains the mature age of
45 years, I dare all the assemblies in our dear region to wipe off
the dust from their sanitation bye-laws and implement them.
Let them solicit the co-operation of the local courts and prosecute
offenders. They need not be keen on fines. Let the courts commit
offenders to community service - making them clean streets and
gutters. Next year by this time, we would have a cleaner environment.

Original_Search_Title: Two Chiefs Clash Over 'Role of the Family in Customary Marriage'
Headline: Ghana: Two Chiefs Clash Over 'Role of the Family in Customary Marriage'
Body_Text:
Accra — Kofi and Ama were living happily together for 20 years and had four
children. On a rather cold afternoon Kofi passed away after a few
weeks of illness.
His family asked Ama to leave the house with her children because
they claim they did not know her. That they never performed any
marriage rites for her on behalf of Kofi and as such, she was only a
girlfriend.
Not satisfied, Ama took a daring step forward by filing a suit in one
of the courts preventing the family from collecting the letters of
administration to administer her spouse's estate.
As far as Ama was concerned, customary rites or no customary rites,
she lived with the man and between the two of them, they were husband
and wife.
This is where Ama gets the support of law lecturer, Dr. Kofi Adinkra.
Dr. Adinkra who was speaking at a one day meeting on the "Rudiments
of customary marriage; A new approach" organised by the Legal
Pluralism and Gender Project of the German development organisation,
(GTZ) said the absence of documents evidencing a customary marriage
presents the courts with difficult problems when they are faced with
the question whether or not customary marriage exists.
He said such questions arise most often in matters relating to
inheritance and suits to establish paternity.
"The approach of the court has invariably been to test the assertion
of marriage against certain so-called essentials of customary
marriages," Dr. Adinkra explained adding, " It often turns out that
the so-called essentials are nothing more than the stages procedures
and phases of customary law processes."
The law lecturer observed that doubts have always existed among some
skeptical minds about the legal requirements of a customary marriage
as enunciated by the courts.
He said agreement by parties to live together as man and wife and
consent of family of the man that he should have the woman as his
wife have often been used as baseline for establishing whether there
is marriage.
The man's family may indicate such consent by acknowledging the woman
as his wife.
Another indicator that the courts use to determine whether there is a
marriage in any such case as Ama's is the consent of the woman's
family that she should be joined in marriage to the man, according to
Dr. Adinkra.
Again that consent should be indicated by the acceptance of drinks
from the man or his family or by the family of the woman merely
acknowledging the man as the wife of the woman.
The last indicator used is whether the marriage has been consummated
at all.
Adinkra said it is evident that new directions are needed to
determine whether there is a marriage, since parents may object to
their children marrying some one due to ethnicity, religion and other
factors.
Equally important is the murky issue of whether families are part of
marriages contracted.
"Attending funerals are cited as reasons why families are part of the
system, but can there be a legal action against some one who refuses
to attend funeral in the spouses family?" Dr. Adinkra asked.
He suggested that what the new approach should focus on is the man
and woman, leaving out the families, important as they might be.
"Consent of a family is not a legal requirement in marriages, consent
if handed to parents would be a dangerous weapon."
Dr. Adinkra recommends that the courts in resolving marital disputes
involving inheritance, succession of establishing the paternity of a
child should consider the length of time the couples have lived
together and have held themselves out as husband and wife.
This he claimed is the equivalent of the common law repute ad
cohabitation.
Again, Dr. Adinkra who is also a traditional ruler in the Drobo
Traditional Area in the Brong-Ahafo Region said the view of the
community where the couple lives should also be considered: if
members of the community regard them as husband and wife then so be
it.
He said the number of children a couple has had could also be used in
establishing whether they are married or not. Beyond this, the courts
should also consider common ventures such as farming or trading
enterprises undertaken in the belief that they are married.
Another issue Dr. Adinkra recommended the court considers in
resolving marital disputes is how much they save their monies and
property, whether they operate joint savings or have a common budget.
Whether couples have agreed to attend and pay funeral expenses in
each other's family is an another indicator that should be considered
in determining whether a marriage is viable, Adinkra emphasised.
"Among the more enlightened and westernised, it should be
investigated if they submit income tax returns claiming for
themselves benefits to which only the married are entitled to," Dr.
Adinkra pointed out.
He said also if they hold bonds, and shares in their joint names, and
if they name each other as beneficiaries in their individual wills
and any other death benefits which each may be entitled to in the
absence of one of them should be used as an indicator of marriage.
This factor is a more reliable indicator that the couple intended a
permanent relationship than ordinary concubinage, the traditional
ruler said.
"Society is not static, customary law changes with the passage of
time so if an institution looks like one, let the courts say it," he
concluded.
But other traditional rulers at the meeting disagreed with Dr.
Adinkra. They insisted that cultural norms and traditions pertaining
to marriage should be fully performed before couples are considered
as married.
Mawerehene of Otumfuo Osei Tutu, Nana Baffour Osei Brantuo said there
was nothing customary about what Dr. Adinkra said. "Our customs and
our laws on marriage are so clear and if the courts should use them
in resolving such disputes they would not be having problems at all,"
Nana Brantuo said.
The Mawerehene who described Ashanti marriage custom as "simple"
explained that a man who qualifies to marry in Ashanti's matrilineal
system of inheritance has to be an adult, gainfully employed, come
from a good home and not suffering from any disease such as insanity.
A woman should also pass puberty, have a good character and a good
family background. "If your family is noted to live among a gang of
thieves, you will not be accepted," he pointed out.
Nana Brantuo also said marriages in the Ashanti Traditional Area are
strictly dealt at the family level. "For us marriage is contracted
between two families and not two individuals," he said.
It is also the belief of the people that the man in the marriage
provides the spirit of the offsprings while the woman provides the
blood.
Because our daughters are not for sale, items collected include
Ntirinsa or drinks, essentially two schnapps, or two pots of palm
wine in the olden days, Danta or loin cloth for the father of the
girl and Akonta gye sekan for the brothers of the bride.
He said in the past days, the Akonta gye sekan (Cutlass for the
brother in-law) was to enable the in-law protect the woman till she
lives the family house for the husband's home.
Nana Brantuo said until the man performs all the rights, he is not
considered as married to the woman as Dr. Adinkra sought to suggest.
In a contribution, the queen mother of Peki Traditional Area in the
Volta Region, Mamaga Amega Kofi Bra (I) said in their patrilineal
system of inheritance, a man who wants to marry in the area needs to
see the prospective father in law with two bottles of schnapps as
knocking rum.
Followed with marriage consultation of four bottles overseas schnapps
or two bottles of wine as well as two more bottles of schnapps.
The prospective husband also has to give the father-in-law one piece
of cloth, one pair of native sandals, an umbrella and a suitable
amount of money.
For the mother in law, tradition requires that she is given a half
piece of cloth, one pair of native sandals, two handkerchiefs and a
presentable amount of money.
The bride also receives six half pieces of cloth, two pairs of shoes,
three handkerchiefs, one big suit case or trunk containing a set of
gold trinkets, comb, mirror, powder, perfume beads, and underwear,
Mamaga Bra (I) added. A Bible and a ring are added to the items.
The family of the bride also receives six pots of palm wine, six
tubers of yam and one box of biscuits.
Until all these stages are completed a couple cannot hold themselves
out as married.

Original_Search_Title: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Body_Text:
Four truckloads of looted yam impounded by the police in Taraba State about two weeks ago have been sold to the public.
The yam tubers were sold members of the public on Tuesday at the command headquarters, Jalingo, where people appeared in large number to buy the commodity. Ten tubers of yam were sold for N150 as against the market price of N750.
The police public relations officer, Mr Clement Robert, said it was necessary to dispose the perishable commodity as the owners failed to show up.
Robert said that the police impounded another three truckloads of looted yam, adding that proceeds from the sale would be deposited into government treasury.
The PPRO said that some of the yam would be bought by the state government for distribution to refugees in various camps in the state.
Commenting on the latest conflict between the Tiv and the Jukuns in Dongah Local Government, Robert said policemen had been dispatched to the area to restore peace.

Original_Search_Title: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Police Sell Impounded Yam
Body_Text:
Four-truck load of looted yam impounded by the police in Taraba State about
two weeks ago have been sold at a paltry sum.
The yam tubers were sold to members of the public on Tuesday at the command
headquarters, Jalingo, where people rushed in droves to buy the commodity.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reported that 10 tubers of yam were sold at
N150 as against the market price of N750.
The Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO), Mr. Clement Robert, said it was
necessary to dispose of the perishable commodity as the owners had failed to
show up.
Robert said that the police had impounded another three-truck load of looted
yam, adding that proceeds from the sale would be deposited in government
treasury.
The PPRO said that some of the yam would be bought by the state government
for distribution to refugees in various camps in the state.
Commenting on the latest conflict between the Tiv and the Jukuns in Donga
Local Government Area, Robert said policemen had been dispatched to the area
to contain the situation.

Original_Search_Title: Special Tax Imports Should Be Increased, Not Wiped Out
Headline: Ghana: Special Tax Imports Should Be Increased, Not Wiped Out
Body_Text:
Cantonments, Accra — A senior economics lecturer of the University of Ghana, Legon has
advocated for the abolishing of the special tax imposed on some
imported items.
According to Dr Fritz Gockel, the special tax. which was imposed last
year has not achieved its purpose. He argued that the policy rather
hurts consumers by increasing prices of the affected imported items
and it has also increased indirect taxes to the government.
I find it difficult to understand why the above argument is being
used as the reason to call for the special tax to be abolished.
Perhaps because I am not an economist, but it does not quite make
economic sense to me. Just take a look at the items attracting the
special tax - poultry products, milk, human hair, potatoes, grapes,
apples, flour, edible oil, cocoa beverages, chocolates and biscuits.
Others are potato chips. peanuts. fruit juice, soups and broth, ice
cream, mineral water, beer, paint, gas cylinders, toilet paper and
soap.
Among the items listed, the only ones that cannot be produced in this
country are flour, grapes, apples, potatoes and white women's hair
In any case there are perfect local substitutes for these items like
mangoes, oranges, yam, cocoyam, and the beautiful hair of our own
women.
LUXURY GOODS
Because Ghanaians can do without most of these items and be no worse
off, it is correct to class them as luxury goods. Dr. Gockel's
argument that tax on these luxury goods is hurting consumers is
difficult to understand.
Which consumers is he referring to? Is it those driving in
air-conditioned luxury vehicles, who stop at traffic lights and roll
down their car windows to buy apples? Or is he rather referring to
those who pop into Koala supermarket after a meal in Dynasty
restaurant just to buy after meal snacks and treats like imported
chocolates, potato chips and peanuts?
Come again Dr. Gockels. Are you seriously saying that it is wrong for
the government to raise revenue from the consumption of luxury goods
so that the money could be used for important sectors like health and
education, when even essentials like petrol is heavily taxed in most
countries? The tax on petrol in Britain is more than 65% of the pump
price
VOLUNTARY DECISION
As an economist, I am sure Dr. Gockels will agree with me that an
indirect tax is only paid by those who make the conscious and
voluntary decision to buy the goods attracting such tax. The argument
that the special tax on these items hurt consumers, is therefore,
false since the vast majority of Ghanaians have no need for the items
listed above or they could make do with local substitutes.
I would rather spend money to buy oranges, bananas, pawpaws or
mangoes grown by our local farmers than buy apples and avoid paying
any special taxes. It makes a lot of economic sense to me for the
government to raise money for development by imposing a special tax
on frivolous items like human hair, apples, potato chips and Mars
Bars, which are imported with scarce foreign exchange.
It is rather unfortunate that the government bowed to pressure and
reduced the special tax from 20% to I0%. That decision has not helped
this country in any way unless the Golden Age of Business that we
hear about has to do with buying anything from abroad and selling in
Ghana. It is true to argue that overprotected industries stand the
danger of remaining inefficient.
But it is unfair to conclude that the policy of special taxes has
been a failure when it has barely been in operation for two years,
one year of which was under a government noted for impeding the
progress of local industry just because the owners were viewed as
political opponents. It will also be pertinent to point out that the
observation by Dr. Gockel that the trend in imports since the
introduction of the special tax remains the same has to do more with
the lack of political will to make it effective rather than an
inherent weakness of the tax.
It was a weakness on the part of the government to bow to pressure
and reduce the rate of the special tax. It also doesn't help when we
pay lip-service to the slogan 'buy Ghanaian' and continues to allow
the importation of goods we can produce in our own country
There is a law against the importation of over-aged vehicles into the
country, yet it appears that law is not worth the paper it is written
on.
When European Union or United States Iaw states that an item is
prohibited from being imported into their territory, they mean just
that and there is no way, apart from illegal concealment, to get such
items into those countries. If government seriously wants to run a
successful economy, then the first step it has to take is to enforce
economic laws.
The distress action embarked upon by the VAT Service to recover
unpaid taxes is a good example of economic law enforcement. The
courts must deal drastically with any person or group that attempts
to prevent, or worse still, to assault the VAT officials carrying out
their duties. An assault on these officials is an assault on the law.
CAPONE'S PREDICAMENT
There is a story about the infamous Chicago mobster, Al Capone, who
had trouble with the American government and sought assistance from
his colleagues in the Mafia. When his friends realized Al Capone's
predicament had to do with tax evasion they gave him this advice.
'Al'. they said. 'if you had murdered somebody, we would have gladly
come to your aid. But since it is Uncle Sam's money you tried to
keep, there is nothing we can do to help. Face the music alone'.
Al Capone was jailed for tax evasion and died in prison. This is just
one example of how some countries take their economic laws very
seriously
Let me suggest a way for the government to stop the importation of
over-age vehicles. A heavy fine should be imposed on any shipping or
clearing agent that handles such prohibited vehicles. That way the
government will have the money to pay for stripping the vehicles
before they are sent to the smelter. Any ship that brings such
vehicles into our country should also be fined. If the Captain of the
ship refuses to pay the fine he can keep the vehicle or drop it in
the sea. Too many lives are being needlessly lost in accidents
involving over-age and poorly maintained vehicles plying our roads.
A final comment on Dr.Gockel's point that the aim of the special tax
was not for government to increase revenue but to assist struggling
firms. The two are inextricably bound. The government cannot assist
struggling firms with empty coffers. So the point does not carry much
weight.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Sellers Threaten to Boycott Transport Company
Headline: Ghana: Yam Sellers Threaten to Boycott Transport Company
Body_Text:
Akosombo — A 500-Member yam sellers association drawn from Kete-Krachi, Dambai,
Accra, Krachi Asaman and some outlying villages have threatened to
boycott the Volta Lake Transport Company Limited (VLTC) unless
positive steps are taken to improve their service.
To this end, the members have called on the Volta River Authority
(VRA) to take serious view of the matter so as to avoid any
embarrassment.
The members decision was made known to the press after an emergency
meeting with the commercial director, Mr. Erzuah Miezah and Mr.
Baiden, both of VLTC at Akosombo over the weekend.
Mr. Frank Agyekum, chairman of the association, said poor service by
VLTC has plunged most members into serious financial crisis resulting
in majority of them abandoning the yam trade.
According to Agyekum, management's interest supersedes that of the
numerous customers on board the vessel, adding that in spite of the
numerous appeals to management to improve operation of the pantoon,
their request had not been heeded to.
He stated that VLTC has failed to make forklift operators do overtime
to discharge all perishable goods knowing very well that the company
has only one forklift machine to discharge several tonnes of goods on
the boat.
The chairman mentioned that for the past 30 years that yam sellers
had engaged in business of transporting yams and other things by
river, VLTC has never given them any kind of comfort, besides the
cheating and fraudulent means to live.
Agyekum cited an instance where Miss Faustina Sagoe, a toll collector
issued a ¢40,000 ticket but paid ¢17,000 to the company and revealed
that almost all ticket and toll collectors have built houses out of
their nefarious activities.
"We can no longer sit down for personnel of VLTC to continue playing
around us only for us to swim in the pool of debt", Agyekum
emphasised.

Original_Search_Title: Muscles Muscles And More Muscles
Headline: Uganda: Muscles Muscles And More Muscles
Body_Text:
Isaac Nsumba says the sport is not for the low class but a sport for all to keep fit
In case you have not met Nsumba, he is the young man who appears on your screen before WBS television sports news time. That man with tensed muscles, shinny skin carrying a TV set with a WBS TV logo.
To the lay person its called body building to a body builder its weight lifting, body building, and power lifting.
Isaac Nsumba says he starts his day with training at any gym, one of the benefits of his achievement. "National body builders don't pay any fee to do their training in any gym around the country," he says.
Nsumba recites the times he started the sport in 1992. "I first got inspired by the movies I watched when I was young like Commando," an action packed movie produced early 1990s starring Arnold Schwarzneger. "Schwarzneger was my idol. I wondered how he got his big body, which I admired," he narrates.
He says that when he inquired about the sporting facilities from friends, 'They told me to go to sporting gym centres and begin training."
This led him to the Nakivubo Power Gym, where upon he took his interest seriously. "I seriously begun body building in my senior one 1995 while studying at Kampala Grammar Secondary School." He then looks at me in the eye, as though asking me, is that enough? And continues, "I was 16 years old by then, weighing 40 or 48 kgs," and looks up as though the answers were pegged somewhere up on the iron roof. "Hmm...48!".
During his ordinary level he would leave school at 4:00 p.m. and run to the gym to train for two hours. At 6:00 p.m. he would leave for home. "I used to watch my colleagues compete." He never got a chance because of his under weight.
"When I made 68kgs I qualified for the first time for Mr. Kampala Lightweight Bodybuilding Competitions," in a low tone. "Though I was not so used I only got experience, this helped me gain confidence in standing and posing on stage" adds. "I did not meet the standards," meaning he didn't get a trophy. So he trained so hard also gaining more appetite in the process.
He again attended the Mr. Kampala Bodybuilding Competitions 1996 and walked away with a trophy. "I later went for Mr. Carnivore competitions-1996 in Nairobi, Kenya and returned with a trophy."
It was quite a challenge for the team of six muscular men he says, tilting his head forward, and gesturing with his right arm.
The biggest show ever came in 1999, he traveled to Egypt for the first East and Central Africa Bodybuilding Championships. Here he came face to face with the greatest challenge of his life. After a stiff competition with different participants from other African countries, he managed to return with a trophy.
"I think I have about seven or eight trophies," he tries to recollect, slides his left hand into the pocket of his deep blue jeans, draws out a wallet and searches inside for more records, fails to find them and mutters to himself.
"I think they are not here, I shall check at home later, but I know they are seven," he reaffirms. True he got seven trophies in his course of competitions.
Isaac, now 25 years says he has benefited a lot from the exercises "if you do a lot of exercises it improves your health and prolongs your life." he explains with an aura of authority and good faith.
As more body builders pour into the gym the noise also increases, the music blares. He stares at the coach who carries weights at short intervals while conversing in a staccato like voice to another trainer.
"I got to know a lot of people, got trophies, money and offers from big companies to do adverts, for their products." Not only has he gained popularity from the sport but he has also travelled to many countries and places that he confesses he never expected to set sight on. At the moment, "I have an offer from Health and Performance Products (U) ltd to do them promotions, which I think is good achievement".
From the numerous prizes and awards he got Nsumba has been able to pay his school fees. "Since the death of my father I have been able to maintain my self in school from what I earn," he says in a melancholic tone.
At noon, he starts another part of his day as a steward at Cineplex/DV 8 Bar and Bistro. He pulls out an identity card to confirm this point. "Now I weigh 76 kilogrammes, which is middleweight. I have won three trophies and other prizes with this weight."
"It took me only six months, to gain a weight of 64kgs which means my body responds quickly to food stuffs and supplements."
He says, other people's bodies may not quickly respond to the food required to build it in a shorter time. However, he quickly adds that may not mean that one's body cannot be built. His diet is a full pack of the necessary foodstuffs like egg white and Soya flour, two yam tubers or cassava plus five raw eggs, for lunch. After two hours, he takes a pint of milk and fruit salad. He also takes food supplements.
"At dinner I eat five boiled eggs, with passion fruit juice or fresh water mixed with muscle recovery powder. But at breakfast a cup of milk, four pieces of bread plus two fingers of ripe bananas," he says. All these make his daily diet.
The celebrated body builder dismisses the idea that bodybuilding is for the lowly and the naive.
"Body building is good for everyone, the young and adults alike but many people are not aware. Women also enjoy the sport unfortunately we've not had any woman participating, maybe because they don't have time," he says while gazing at a couple of women selling food.
"But in developed countries and the countries I travelled to like Egypt many people love bodybuilding and a lot of women participate in the competitions," Despite his popularity, Nsumba is not yet married but says, "I look forward to getting married when the right time comes. I had a girl friend, Matilda, we had misunderstandings and broke up."
Some girls say they are scared of his muscles they think he can hurt them if they annoy him but others are not. "They make fun and always desire to befriend me," he shyly reveals.
The bodybuilder says he does not fight people, despite his massive size. Though he fought once to prevent intruders from entering a show.
Isaac Nsumba, five feet four inch was born on the May 15, 1976 to the late Saul Richard Nsumba and Ruth Nsumba a secretary at the faculty of Arts Makerere University. His sporting career, has earned him a couple of trophies which include:
Mr. Carnivore-1996, East and Central African Body building Championships-1999; Best Sportsman of the Year, Body Builder 2000, Mr. Kampala 2000 and 2001, Mr. Hercules Bodybuilding Competitions 2001.
His coach, Sali Salongo, speaks well of him, "Since I started training him, Isaac has always been so hardworking trying to learn everything I teach him. Though it was not easy for him to catch up very fast."
Pauses and runs back to train and instruct then returns, "He's doing a great job now," he adds while nodding his head.
Nsumba is a darling of fans of bodybuilding. Peter a bodybuilder and a fan says Isaac is a dedicated body builder. "He's respectful and humble." Some young ladies, who admitted they are Isaac's fans, expressed their love for him. "He looks a hunk on stage", one lady said; "I admire his body size", another interjected.
In Uganda, the sport though popular among men of a certain class has a long history of existence as Coach Sali Salongo says. It was founded by a group of Indians then living in Jinja.
James Onyango 1972 took the interest and teamed up with a few youth like Rashid Kawayida, Agong, and Kwaha Sali among others. After the expulsion of Asians from Uganda.
Onyango and his colleagues moved to Kampala for a wider audience. Then President Idi Amin Dada loved and supported the sport, which was mixed with boxing and wrestling, and it gained more support.
Salongo Ssali the coach heads a team of over 250 body builders, weight lifters and power lifters at the Nakivubo Sports Club.
To ensure the best outcome in their competitions a selected number of committee members sit to pick out abler people or those fit for the competitions.
The jovial coach says between pauses as he runs back to exercise. "We have travelled a lot with this precious Sport, since we started, in 1997 for the first time we went for Mr. Universe Championship in Jordan which revealed that we were really serious."
Salongo the coach heads a team of over 250 body builders, weight lifters and power lifters at the Nakivubo Sports Club. To ensure the best outcome in their competitions a selected number of committee members sit to pick out abler people or those fit for the competitions.
Their greatest obstacle, the coach says, is getting sponsorship for their shows. "Though bodybuilding companies can promote their products, If companies could come out to sponsor body building activities, it will be well perceived in the whole country like football," the 100kg, large chested and healthy looking , Salongo reveals inbetween gestures.

Original_Search_Title: Northern Volta And The Wind of Change
Headline: Ghana: Northern Volta And The Wind of Change
Body_Text:
NKWANTA AND Kete-Krachie districts in the Northern part of the Volta
Region are the nation's Yam Basket forgotten by previous governments.
The two districts together produce the bulk (about 60%) of the
country's total yam consumption, yet, in terms of development, they
can lucidly be described as casualties of political neglect. It is a
pity that Nkwanta and Kete-Krachie are cut off from the rest of the
country as far as telecommunication is concerned.
Radio and Television receptions are not good. In fact, the two
districts are being administered incommunicado.
It was not surprising when the chiefs and their people expressed
unmeasurable joy over the wind of change that the Kufuor
administration has brought there.
At a durbar in honour of the Volta Regional Minister, Mr. Kwasi Owusu
Yeboa, during his five-day inspection tour of the area, the chiefs of
Nkwanta expressed keen interest and admiration over "the government's
efforts to put into concrete terms practical measures, plans and
programmes aimed at improving the nation's economy, especially the
Emergency Social Relief Programme and the enabling environment for
the private sector."
ROAD
They were full of praises for the government's efforts at reshaping
the once immotorable Kadjebi-Nkwanta-Damanko-Bimbilla road and
appealed for it to be macadamized.
Owing to the vast nature of the district, agriculture towns like
Kpasa, Damanko, Kecheibi and Abrewanko are not connected to the
national grid, though some other villages are enjoying solar energy.
The Danish International Development Agency (DANIDA) and other NGOs
have helped the people with potable water. In addition to that, the
GTZ/KFW consultants have drilled three wells into which pipes will be
connected to serve the capital towns with water.
Relatively, Nkwanta is enjoying peace, unity and stability which are
the basic ingredients for develoment.
SHIARE
At Shiare, a serene and beautiful natural environment, hidden in a
mountain range, the Osuwule Wura (Paramount Chief) of the area, Nana
Oberko Agyei, said the visit of the Hon. Minister was the first in a
very long time by any high-ranking government official to the capital
town of our traditional area" adding "This demonstrates the wind of
positive change blowing across the country."
"We the people of Shiare and the entire Atwodes are very proud to
share this victory because for over a decade my people have been
branded as opposition elements in high political corridors because of
our search for the truth and genuine development based on freedom and
justice."
He recounted his humiliating "imprisonment" in police cells "on my
own land without any charge."
GARI PROCESSING
Shiare is noted for its high grade gari, starch and cassava chips
thus becoming a tremendous potential support for the President's
initiative launched a couple of weeks ago to produce cassava starch
for export and also increase the income earnings of farmers and as
well offer employment opportunities for the youth.
The Osuwule Wura appealed to President Kufuor to remember the Atwodes
in the appointment to high government offices to atone for the
political neglect as a result of their past political victimisations
emanating from their sympathy for the NPP when they were in
opposition.
KRACHIE-WURA
The Krachie-Wura, Nana Mprah Besemuna II confirmed his support for
the NPP government.
"Today, the chiefs and people of Krachi Traditional Area wish to
assure you and His Excellency President J.A. Kufuor and his
administration of our unflinching support and loyalty. We shall
always help the government as would be expected of us in the task of
nation building."
He was filled with nostalgia as he talked about Krachie as it was in
the 1950 saying the town roads were tarred and given district capital
status with social amenities.
"But today, after being used as a sacrificial lamb for the formation
of the Volta Lake, the town has been reduced to a state of anguish
and frustration."
He went on: "Honestly, we deserve every right to continue crying for
attention in terms of needs and concern. We, therefore, consider this
as an opportune time to repeat some of our needs to enable Krachie
come out of its deprivation."
TRAINING COLLEGE
The Dambai Teacher Training College is the one institution serving
the two districts of Krachie and Nkwanta. It has a population of 435
students.
Apart from water problems, the college is always invaded by stray
cattle and other animals.
Students are often given extra work of collecting cow-dropping in
order to keep the compound clean.
Thieves have taken undue advantage of the large unfenced compound to
be having a fields day. Louvre blades and students' belongings are
not spared.
The acting principal of the college, Joel Stephen Amewotey, expressed
regret that the police at Dambai are adamant to their complaints and
reports.
Yeboa suggested the formation of a Task Force in conjunction with the
District Assembly to arrest the situation and admonish the school
authorities to demarcate the boundaries of the college in order to
secure life and property.
The five-day tour took the minister and his entourage to towns and
villages as Kpasa, Damanko, Gyanekrom, Twereso, Shiare, and Osramanae
where he inspected on-going projects and those initiated by the NPP
government.
They include water projects, schools, markets, roads, offices and
KVIP places of convenience.
He assured the people of the government's determination to improve
their lots and urged them to eschew all negative tendencies that
might divide them and impede the development of their communities.
Yeboa was accompanied on the tour by the Head of the Ghana Highway
Authority, Kodwo Aboagye, Regional Director of the Department of
Urban Roads, Edward Koranteng, the Regional Economic Planning
Officer, Rueben Amegashiti and the Regional Co-ordinting Director,
Woeli K. Kemevor and some NPP officials.

Original_Search_Title: Reprisal Attacks: Miseries of Displaced Persons
Headline: Nigeria: Focus On Displaced People in Central Region
Body_Text:
This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations
Twelve year old Terna and his 10 year-old brother Sev- both orphans- are among the latest arrivals at Daudu camp for displaced people on the outskirts of the Benue State capital, Makurdi, in central Nigeria.
They had fled into the bush with their mother when soldiers attacked their village, Gbeji, a month earlier and killed their father along with scores of other men, they told IRIN.
After two weeks of hiding in the bush, they said, their mother was bitten by a snake.
They watched horrified and helpless as she suffered the agony and died the next day. Not knowing what to do, they kept vigil over her body for two days until two men found them and buried the body. The men took them along and after another two weeks of walking through bush paths and avoiding checkpoints mounted by soldiers on the main roads, they finally arrived at the Daudu camp.
More than one month after Nigerian soldiers attacked and destroyed several communities inhabited by the Tiv people of Benue State, victims who include men, women and children are still making their way to camps and safe places set up by the state government. The attacks launched by soldiers in armoured vehicles were in apparent reprisal for the killing by a Tiv militia of 19
soldiers sent by the federal authorities to quell fighting between the Tiv and their Jukun
neighbours on the border between Benue and Taraba states.
In the camps at Agasha, Daudu, Gbajimba, Mutum Biyu, Sankara and Torkula, new arrivals join old camp inmates who fled earlier, not only the Tiv and Jukun clashes, but also clashes in Nasarawa State to the east of Benue that occurred in June and July between the Tiv and Hausa-speaking Azare people.
The camps, most of which were set up on primary school compounds, are now overflowing with people. Thatched huts and tents have been put up on every available space while large numbers of people huddle underneath trees to get away from scorching sunshine and daytime temperatures averaging 38 degrees centigrade.
Benue State government officials say there are currently more than 550,000 displaced people in the state. "The whole of Benue State is now a displaced peoples' camp," John Chokwe, an aide to the governor George Akume, told IRIN. "It is only a fraction that you can find in places designated officially as camps because many have been accommodated in neighbouring villages or taken in by relatives in other towns."
Taking care of the urgent needs of such large numbers of people has become a heavy burden for the state government. Not even the federal government of President Olusegun Obasanjo, which ordered in the troops, has provided any relief assistance for the victims.
"We have not got any assistance from anyone except the Red Cross," Shima Ayati, the state government official in charge of a committee for displaced people, said. "Even food items worth over 10 million naira we put in a central warehouse at Sankara were destroyed by the army."
(1 Naira = US $ 0.008)
Nigerian Red Cross officials said they have so far conducted needs assessment surveys in 15 camps in both Benue and Taraba states and found more than 30,000 people in urgent need of both food and non-food assistance.
"We finished distributing non-food materials last week and plan to start food distribution this week," Patrick Bawa, Red Cross spokesman, told IRIN. But, according to the humanitarian agency, the number of people in need of assistance was continually increasing as more displaced people arrive at the camps or other safe locations.
Conditions in the camps so far remain very harsh. Food assistance was  initially provided by the Benue and Taraba state governments when the camps first opened late last month. But this has now stopped. Most people living in the camps now go out daily to search for food, often opting to work as farm labourers in order to earn some money to provide food and other urgent
needs.
And because they spent weeks in the bush, most camp residents are in poor health. Women and children were particularly at risk, with some women reported to have given birth while staying
in the bush. In the camps, the lack of health facilities coupled with poor sanitary conditions make matters worse. Water supply is generally inadequate and although some pit toilets have been provided, they remain grossly inadequate for the number of people that need them. According to the Nigerian Red Cross many cases of diarrhoea, malaria and conjunctivitis have been reported in the camps.
Among the displaced as well are scores of injured people. Those with the most serious cases, such as gunshot injuries or whose limbs were cut off during the ethnic fighting, have been evacuated with Red Cross assistance to hospitals. Most of were taken to the Federal Medical Centre in Makurdi; the Referral Hospital in Mutum Biyu, or Hossanah Hospital, Agasha.
Many Nigerians expect the upheavals in the central region to undermine local and national food security both in the short and long run. The central region, one of Nigeria's food baskets, accounts for most root crop foods, such as yam and cassava, as well as cowpea and grains including maize, millet and sorghum. The town of Zaki Biam, which bore the main brunt of the army attacks, was the biggest yam market in the country.
"Whatever we do now to ameliorate this very bad situation, the fact remains
that it will take these ordinary people, who are mainly farmers, a long time
to pick up the pieces and recover what they have lost," said Chokwe. "And
while we wait for that to happen, the damage done to both them and the
country will remain immeasurable."

Original_Search_Title: Atiku's Trouble-Shooting Mission to Benue State
Headline: Nigeria: Atiku's Trouble-Shooting Mission to Benue State
Body_Text:
Vice President Abubakars' trip to Makurdi and many of the communities affected by recent hostilities between military personnel and the Tiv people in Benue State was a bold move to finally resolve the lingering crisis that had afflicted the four neighbouring states of Benue, Plateau, Nassarawa and Taraba States for sometime.
The visit was one lacking in the usual presidential reception. In the morning of Wednesday'31 October,5.the' Makurdi Airport bore testimony to the reception that awaited Vice President Abubakar. Desolate, lifeless and bushy, it played host to the cream of Tiv elders and top Benue; State Government functionaries who came to receive Vice President Abubakar. However, when Abubakar finally; arrived at 8.58 a.m., the reception was; devoid of the customary revelries of such visits.
There was no staccato of cultural drums electrifying the dancing steps of traditional dancers. The melody's of the ubiquitous police band was absent. The beauty of gaily dressed and elegant flower- bearing children were not in sight. The reception was a reflection of the heavy laden mind of the guest, Vice President Abubakar and histories. The Governor of Benue State, George Akume captured the mood hours later when he addressed the elders of Benue State at that Benue Government House. His' words: "The Bible says for everything, there is a season, A season to laugh and a season to cry.
It was indeed a season of mourning. The preceding weeks had recorded heightened violence in Tiv communities in the state leading to two instances of gruesomeimurders that 'nation could not- ignore. Twenty two soldiers deployed to ' keep peace among the Tiv of Benue State: and the Jukuny of Taraba State were(abducted and murdered by some yet unidentified persons. The mutilated corpses of 19 of them were later recovered injected community, Zaki Biam Two weeks after the incident, some military personnel invade all comrnunities and killed many of the wrecked damaze on properties. lt was a new political dimension in the orgy~ of violence between the Tivs and Juluns over sundry issues like land disputes, political authority, fears of marginalisation ancl do~nination.
"Today, we have to manage two wrongs", Vice President Abubakar summarised the latest sequence of events in the lingering crisis that had pitched the 'Ivies and Jukuns against one.
His visit to the: areas affected by the recently hostilities was a continuation of efforts to resolve the crisis. immediately he arrived the Makurdi airport, he had a short stop over at the Government l- louse where he was brieed by the governor. The Vice President later led a kilomelre long convoy in a two hour drive to the trouble spots. His first point of call was the ruin of the residence of the former Chief of Army Staff, Major General Victor Malu in Agoor. He was conducted around the ruin by Mr Tydakoso Ageeyina, Chairman of Ukum Local government area of the state. From there, the convoy moved and stopped-over at Kyado, Zaki Ibiam Gbaaji, Vaase, Adoor e.t.c. to inspect the damage of the military attack. ln Zaki Biam which recorded the most devastating damage among the arcas visited, the Vice president and Governor Akurne led the delegation in 'a walk of about 30 minutes across the town, stopping to inspect houses, shops and vehicles raized down or destroyed during the attack. The Vice President also visited the Yam Market to which people of the town had reportedly been invited by soldiers for a meeting before the attack. He was also at the Headquarters of the Ukum Local Government which also bore evidence of attack. When he was informed that some remote villages were affected in the attack, Vice president Abubakar decided to inspect them with the helicopter which had been provided by the Nigerian Army to give surveillance cover for the visit.
Abubakar, who earlier in the year was installed the Zege Mule Tiv bared his impressions at a meeting with the cream of Tiv elders and functionaries of the Benue State Government at the Banquet hall of the Government house, Makurdi.
At the occasion, governor Akume expressed gratitude for the visit of the vice president and expressed the hope that the visit will open new vistas for the return of peace to the trouble areas.
He affirmed that the Tiv and the people of Benue State in general had utmost respect for the federal government and recalled, that they have equal respect for the military institution which many of its indigenes had helped to build. The attack on Tiv communities by military personnel was an indication that the problem of the Tiv in Tarab, Nassarawa and Plateau States have to be immediately resolved, he said.
Vice president Abubakar began his address with a request for the observation of a minute's silence in memory of those who died in the crises.
For the Vice President, the occasion was auspicious to reiterate the capability of the federal Government to project Nigerians wherever they choose to live within the country. He also admonished the leaders of Benue State on the imperatives of peaceful co-existence anti respect for constituted authority.
Speaking on the abduction and killing of 22 soldiers in a Tiv community, he said it was an invitation to anarchy. "Let me make it clear that where you send a policeman and he is apprehended and killed, it is the most reprehensible thing to do in a civilised world", he said. He continues: "when the last line of defence - soldiers - are deployed and they are abducted and killed, it is an extremely bad situation. We should respect law and order. We should respect constituted authority and have faith in the institutions for the enforcement of our rights".
Speaking on the attack by the soldiers, he drew the attention of governor Akume to the fears he expressed over the involvement of soldiers in maintaining order among civilians at an earlier meeting. He expressed the hope that the experience in Benue State will make Nigerians learn to resolve their differences in civilised manner.
The vice president also disclosed that President Obasanjo had scheduled to host a retreat with the governors of Benue, Taraba, Nassarawa and Plateau States which had been bedeviled by recurring boundary disputes and bloodletting. He also revealed to the applause of the gathering that the recommendations of the Benue people to the federal government on the resolution of the crisis across these states are receiving adequate attention of president Obasanjo.
Adeolu Akande is the deputy chief press secretary to vice president Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Hon. Leo Welfare Foundation Launched
Headline: Nigeria: Hon. Leo Welfare Foundation Launched
Body_Text:
Lagos — At an historic ceremony that drew thousands of chieftains and
faithful of Alliance for Democracy (AD), Honourable Leo Chioma
Welfare Foundation was launched in Lagos at the Ward F3, Taiwo
Street, Okota Secretariat of the party.
Speaking at the occasion, Special Adviser to Governor Bola Ahmed
Tinubu on Legislative Matters, Honourable (Chief) Ajomale described
the effort of Honourable Leo Chioma, the councillor representing Ward
F3 in the Oshodi/Isolo Local Government as a "pace setting' one.
Chief Ajomale, who represented Senator Bola Ahmed Tinubu at the
occasion, urged members of AD to begin serious grassroots
mobilisation and to be on guard so as to thwart the efforts of the
rival party, the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) from rigging the
forthcoming elections in the state.
Said he: "Lagos State has been programmed for rigging by the PDP. We
have the facts." He assured that when new local governments are
created in the state, Isolo Local Government would be one of them.
Earlier in his speech, Hon. Leo Chioma said his concern for the
people made him to set up the foundation. He described democracy as a
collective responsibility, adding that since he was elected to
represent the Okota community, he has put up a number of projects,
including road construction and rehabilitation.
Honourable Niyi Fabikun of the Lagos State House of Assembly
representing Oshodi-Isolo noted that Honourable Chioma's effort was a
challenge to other councillors in the state. He called on Lagosians
to vote massively for Governor Tinubu come 2003.
In an exclusive chat at the occasion, Chairman of the party in the
ward, Mr. Wahab A. Raji lauded Honourable Chioma for his initiative
and expressed the readiness of the party to retain the seat in the
next election.
Over 200 beneficiaries of the welfare scheme went home with
quantities of rice and tubers of yam. They included Mr. Kayode
Adebayo and Mrs. Deborah Adebiyi.
Chieftains of AD who were also at the occasion include Chief A.B.
Taiwo, Engineer Alade, Mrs. Yinka Taiwo and Elder Oguntoye.

Original_Search_Title: Jukun-Tiv Clash: Lawmaker Seeks Permanent Army Base
Headline: Nigeria: Jukun-Tiv Clash: Lawmaker Seeks Permanent Army Base
Body_Text:
As the Federal Government strives to restore lasting peace to the Benue-Taraba boundary, a legislator yesterday cautioned the government against hasty withdrawal of soldiers from the affected areas.
The Arewa Consultative Forum had recently brokered a peace talk between Tiv and Jukun leaders, with a communique calling on the government to relax military presence in the warring zones of the two states.
Mr Abdulazeez Tonku, house of representatives member for Donga/Takum/Ussa federal constituency in Taraba, told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja that the call was designed to create opportunity for the Tiv to exterminate his people.
"We in Taraba State say no to the call for the withdrawal of the troops, because it was Governor George Akume of Benue State who asked for them from the president, and he can call for the withdrawal of soldiers from his state," he noted.
He argued that the establishment of a permanent military barracks along the boundary was the only antidote to the perennial rifts between the two ethnic communities.
The lawmaker, who reviewed the recent visit to the affected areas in Benue by the house of representatives speaker, Ghali Na'abba, said that his people were waiting for the speaker to verify the damage caused to lives and property in Taraba.
"As I am talking to you, dead bodies still litter the Akwana and Aruo villages after a recent attack by the Tiv
people, and we want Na'abba to see them before we arrange their burial," he said.
He said that Jukuns in Taraba had suffered more losses than the Tiv in the crisis which, he opined, was sponsored by retired military officers in Benue "who provide the attackers with sophisticated weapons".
He also accused prominent Tiv leaders of making provocative comments on the conflict, saying that before the murder of 19 soldiers in Benue, hundreds of Jukuns had been killed in the fracas.
On the prospect of a lasting peace in the area, Tonku enjoined Tiv farmers to embrace modern farming techniques to minimise migration to other states.
"They grow their yams in Taraba and sell them in Zaki Biam, but they foment trouble by laying claims of ownership to the land leased to them by owners of the land," he argued.

Original_Search_Title: We've Done Well, Says Tinubu
Headline: Nigeria: We've Done Well, Says Tinubu
Body_Text:
The quarterly summit of Governors of Southern States may be the hand shake across the Niger that has been spoken of for a long time by politician from the South west, south east and south-south but which had never really materialised until governor Bola Tinubu of Lagos State initiated it. Waheed Odusile was at Owerri Imo State last week to cover the 5th summit where he met Tinubu. His report
When he mooted the idea of a summit of all southern states governors last year, Bola Ahmed Tinubu of Lagos State might not have anticipated the huge success the quarterly meeting has recorded within one year. Twelve months after the gathering is waxing stronger and Tinubu is happy.
At the 5th summit hosted by Imo state last week, Tinubu spoke with reporters in Owerri on the journey so far for the summit of southern states governors, why he initiated and hosted the first summit in October 2000 and other issues peculiar to the south.
Against the backdrop of criticism that the summit is a mere talk shop and had achieved nothing, Tinubu said on the contrary, the main objective had been achieved.
When he conceived of the idea he said he thought of those issues such as true federalism, fiscal federalism and others which he had consistently talked about as a governor but without much support and success. " It looked like only one voice in the wilderness. It was like just making issues out of nothing", he said. Not allowing himself to be weighed down by this seemingly lack of success, he thought of involving others, hence his recourse to the southern governors summit.
"One day I was just looking at the issues put together, what I went through during the struggle for democracy and sustenance of democracy and I learnt from it that it takes a collective voice to make a serious impact. If you look at the political history of the country you would find out that we have a lot in common in the south; we have common vegetation, we have similar culture, we have a whole lot in common. And we are inter marrying and seriously co-existing, our trading and economic activities are so integrated and inter woven. When you look at the population of other ethnic nationalities in Lagos you have to think of the way to harmonize issues.
"Besides, there is the question of constitutional democracy, the socio- economic aspect of our life as well. How can we achieve an enduring democracy as a principle and equally political stability that would translate into economic development. It is like we are working for a re-birth of our nation, through our region. And that is crucially important. There was the question of security as well. I started talking of state police. It used to be a disjointed lone voice but we need the whole to sensitize, agitate and dissect those issues to be able to have a common understanding and accommodate one another. We did equally wanted to show Nigerians that regardless of your political affiliation you can always get together to articulate issues," Tinubu said.
And as a result of the governors summit, he says " I understand my brother in the eastern part of the country better today than ever before. I do understand and appreciate the views of the governors Cross River, Edo, Delta and indeed all the governors in the south east and south-south states better now than ever before. Now they are just one telephone call away from me. They respect me and I respect them. These are some of the things that have been achieved, accomplished since the southern governors forum came into being. It is much better now, better cooperation and united voice. We now behave as if we are brothers and in fact we play the role of brothers", Tinubu explained.
But has it been all rosy since the forum began, considering the fact that it has ruffled some feathers? Or has there been any cause for regret or reason to continue with the forum? Tinubu was asked and he took his time to explain that in fact recent events in the country have made the body an imperative. According to him the various ethnic/religious crises in the country and the zeal and seriousness with which the governors handled them were some of the reasons why the forum should continue. "The visit of the Bayelsa state governor during the Yoruba and Ijaw crisis was an evidence that such cooperation is necessary, such association is more than protection of interest. The forum has for instance placed the qustion of resource control in proper perspective . These are what reinforces the point that the forum is necessary."
Even on national issues Tinubu said the forum has proved itself more than necessary. "For instance on revenue allocation. When we were trying to get the differential between the projected oil price and the actual price, when there was a wage crisis in the country, we came together to re-examine the issues. Today we are discussing the creation of local government. How could we have done that if we are in disarray. But now we get together and discuss such issues because we are closer to one another. This has never been possible in this country before. This is what our founding fathers didn't achieve other than political debate and antagonism among themselves".
But is it not necessary to carry along the other governors from the north at least in the spirit of togetherness? Tinubu explained that this has been done at the much larger forum for all the 36 state governors. At the forum he said "we are able to educate one another ; explain and debate the issues involved and you know it is getting better than before"
But on resource control the people in the North are saying are saying they have none to control
"I don't believe so. There are various minerals unexploited. And until you even do geo-physical analysis of what is in the ground you never know some of the minerals that are available to most states. I don't believe that. Because you don't have the natural resources now does not mean you can not develop such facilities in the future. In any case it is a question of right", he said. And while allaying the fears that the states want to take everything Tinubu explained that "we are not saying absolute, but let's put it on the table and discuss it. Let's discuss." Speaking specifically about Lagos state, he asked why the state should be denied the revenue of the activities of the ports. If those area of water were to be an open land, who would plant cassava in there, who would plant yam in there, who would build the shanty in there. So if God gave me the water now and deny me the land and the opportunities therein, but you are now exploiting the water for the economic activities of this country, shouldn't I have a large share of the revenue accruing to it. Now let's go back to that again. Now the ports activities are there, all the several thousands of workers in the ports, who clear their refuse? Lagos State. Can you imagine the level of their water consumption and other items, who takes care of their housing requirement, and their security, the school their children attend - Lagos State.
Now if Lagos State is not taking anything out of the ports, in terms of revenue is that not a gross denial. That is one.
Two, you look at the number of the haulage vehicles that are moving goods in the port to other part of the country, who cares for those roads? Who would take care of the place? Can you compare the level of usage and the required maintenance of these roads to some roads elsewhere that only four or five similar vehicles would pass in a week, can you imagine that! probably we need to do some research as to what happened in New York, in Germany, what happened when they changed their capital. Can you go back and do a research on the statement of the former Head of State, late General Murtala Mohammed, what did he say when they launched the creation of the Federal Capital Territory in Abuja, what did he say about Lagos State? Then when you look at that you will come back to the question of onshore. I ask you a question if those deep area of the ocean were to be a parcel of land, who would cultivate it. Who other than the people of the area that goes fishing in those areas? Delineation of territorial waters is an issue between nations. A nation can not declare a boundary dispute between it and a component part. How can a government say this is the boundary of a state because you want to exploit its resources. Is that equitable? Is that fair? Naturally, they have been denied the opportunity of using their land area, the opportunity to exploit the agricultural rights in those area, so you cannot but give them the resources, to compensate for that.
It is their God given resources, what is happening in other states (countries), we should look at that. We have blue prints that. We can copy from other states we should not manipulate the constitution you can not declare a boundary dispute on territorial waters within a country.
The issue of territorial waters can only come up between countries and not between a country and a component part. Between Nigeria and Cameroun fine! the federal government can not because of its constitutional responsibility of providing security confiscate the property and resources of its citizenry.
If the issue of resource control is peculiar to the Southern states, not so with that of local government administration as all the 36 states are solidly united in their resolve to go ahead with council elections next April, even when the National Assembly is insisting on a four year tenure for council chiefs which therefore push their re-election to 2003. Tinubu said the furore generated by this is good for democracy.
"It is the beauty of democracy we will solve that problem when we get there. I believe that we will solve it one way or the other. But one thing is that the positions of the governors in this matter should be followed correctly we are not being emotional we are not unreasonable we are saying this is the constitution. If you want to change it go through the established procedure. You don't start to amend the constitutional through the back door. You (journalists) are all masters of languages, particularly English. If you remove comma in a sentence, you have changed the texture, when you change the comma to semi-colon it means a different thing. If you put a full stop it is another. The same is also true for a country's constitution. That is one. Two in constitutional democracy there are prescribed rules for each members, the roles of the governors are clearly stated, that of the members of the National Assembly are also clear, ditto the role of 'council chairman, the role of judiciary are also stated in the constitution. The National Assembly shall make law for the good governance, of the people. You don't make laws for personalities, similarly, they have the right to amend the constitution, but it has to be done through the accepted procedures.
But if you now want to interpret one section of it to mean something else, then you are thinking of taking over a responsibility not conferred on you by the constitution. So if in their own wisdom, the members of the National Assembly are now playing the role of the judiciary, we as a group or individual or any citizen for that matter, we'll challenge them. You don't usurp responsibility. The constitution is specific about these things. You go and look at it you can not exercise a right you don't have. I am not a lawyer but even from my layman point of view the issues are clear to me. Three years is three years which ever way you look at it. Again, the constitution has a provision for state electoral commission, and so will you not be abrogating such provision if you now say all elections should take place in one day. Or are you saying that while the state electoral commission will be conducting the council election the INEC will be conducting the other elections what have you created?
In 1983, 1983 what did you have, it was chaos and premature termination of that regime. The president is the only unique person in Africa that I know, that came back in the manner he handed over in 1979. When he had the opportunity of being a candidate in a constitutional environment what process and order got him there? It was the same order; from bottom to the top. So how can it be in his own era that people are talking about reversing the order, it will be a dangerous precedent. To me it is a matter of legacy. He left a worthy legacy when he was retiring as a military dictator, he is now running a constitutional democracy, so he has the onerous responsibility to not only stir the ship of the nation but to chart the way forward.

Original_Search_Title: FCT Residents React to Bank's Proposed Coins
Headline: Nigeria: FCT Residents React to Bank's Proposed Coins
Body_Text:
A cross section of residents of the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) have
expressed reservations over plans by the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) to
introduce N5, N10 and N20 coins in the country.
Speaking with Abuja Trust on Monday, the respondents described the plan as a
further step to increase inflation, and make things more difficult for the
poor.
According to Mrs. Gloria Onyiuke who sells yam in the New Wuse and Karmo
markets, government has no business tampering with the existing currency
notes as they are.  She expressed a lot of anger with the proposed move by
the apex bank, saying "it is another step to push the poor masses deeper
into the abyss."
"I cannot understand," she fumed, "or, may be, they know better, but on my
part, and in the minds of most market women like me, the plan is most
unacceptable."
Asked why she does not support the proposal, Mrs. Onyiuke who spoke in Igbo
reverberated; "I have told you it makes things costlier, for instance, where
are the one naira and fifty kobo coins today?  Have they been phased out?
Answer me! Then, after that, how any things can you buy for one naira and
fifty kobo now? Answer me!"
Another woman, Mrs. Idenyi Asogwa, who runs a canteen in Garki, told Abuja
Trust that if the government were to conduct a survey to ascertain the
feelings of the people before implementing that proposal, they would drop
the idea because according to her, the plan is only popular among the very
rich.
"We are the people who suffer most of the difficult decisions taken on the
top.  If for instance I have N3,000, imagine the coins I would carry.  If
you are saying that I should avoid carrying so many coins, and other people
avoid carrying coins in large amount, it means the currency units which
appear in coins are gradually fading out, do you imagine the consequence of
that?"
Idenyi said if she had the means, she would mobilise all women in her
category to embark on a protest march against the proposal.
In her own reaction, Mr. Candidus Idehen, a commercial bus driver, said the
plan would die a "natural death" because according to him, it is not in the
interest of the poor, who are in the majority.
"If I had my way, I would reactivate the N1,50 kobo and other coins.  In the
first place, I do not support the minting of N1 coin, not to talk of N5 and
N10 coins.  In that case, we would be carrying so many coins per trip!"
On his part, Mr Ibrahim Musa, a student, appealed to government and the CBN
to shelve the plan.  According him, the income level of majority of
Nigerians cannot conveniently accommodate such adjustments at the moment
"Its like they base their plans or decisions on what obtains in the cities.
How convenient do they think this change would be to the villagers?
Gradually, the lower denominations of the currency would cease to attract
any purchasing power," he said.
Several other respondents who obliged to speak to Abuja Trust, condemned the
move, saying it is not acceptable by the masses, who they said, have more
need for the smaller denominations than the rich.  A banker, who spoke on
the condition of anonymity, told Abuja Trust that he and many of his
colleagues were not in tune with the plan.
"But we are just like sheep, where the shepherd shoves them, they move; who
am I to challenge a CBN decision?" he queried.

Original_Search_Title: Fears of Fresh Troop Deployment Spur Returnees' Flight
Headline: Nigeria: Fears of Fresh Troop Deployment Spur Returnees' Flight
Body_Text:
There are strong indications that the Federal Government has deployed more troops to the war ravaged town of Zaki-Biam, sending palpable fear into the returnees and forcing them to once again flee for fear of a fresh attack by the military.
The development which comes barely 48 hours after the Speaker of the Federal House of Representative, Alhaji Ghali Umar Na'Abba joined other Nigerians in the persistent call on the Federal Government to withdraw soldiers deployed to trouble spots along the Benue/Taraba border.
A visit by THISDAY to Zaki-Biam town confirmed that soldiers armed with armoured tanks also used an army truck to drive into the rubble of the once vibrant and biggest yam market in Nigeria.
The presence of more soldiers using armoured vehicles has halted the striking number of returnees who inspite of strong fear were returning into the area out of sheer hopelessness for survival if they continued staying in the bush.
THISDAY also learnt that since the October 20 army reprisal attack on Zaki-Biam and 19 other villages in Benue State the area has been reduced to a jungle with no administrative leadership, no police and no form of security against further attacks.
The council chairman Hon. Ityoakoso has fled . So have the police who a police source said have been directed to stay out of the town. The PPRO Benue State Police Command however said the police have abandoned the area due to lack of office.
A young man who identified himself as Terwsetold THISDAY, that they are once again fleeing because they fear the arrival of more military man with heavy weapons who attack the returnees.
The returnees fear that with such utterances coming out of the nations Defence Minister Lt. General Theophilus Y. Danjuma and Senator representing Taraba South Senatorial district, Senator Sangari, and the inability of the Federal Government to restrain them the role of even the federal Government is suspect in the circumstance.
Only last Tuesday the Speaker, House of Representative, Alhaji Ghali Umar Na'Abba had during his diplomatic visit to Benue State, stopped short to years when he visited the affected areas where he called on the Federal Government to withdraw the army without further delay.
Na'Abba who had also insisted that the Federal Government should never again use of soldiers of quell any civil unrest saying "I don't support the use of the military in any civil situation because it can not be a panacea", was speaking the minds of many others.
Benue State governor, Mr. George Akume had also called on the Federal Government to withdraw the soldiers from Benue State.
Apart from governor Akume, the Tor Tiv IV Dr. Alfred Akawe Torkula has also called on the Federal Government to withdraw the military men who he said rather then provide a solution to the problem they have constituted themselves into another problem, rapping women and committing other atrocities.
Already soldiers stationed along the Katsina-Ala /Zaki-Biam road have resorted to extorting money from motorists as well as food stuff as yams and animals being taken to market.

Original_Search_Title: Identity of Slain Soldiers Doubtful, Senator Alleges
Headline: Nigeria: Identity of Slain Soldiers Doubtful, Senator Alleges
Body_Text:
