Government Guest House, the council chairman's official residence,
and the store where food items for refugees worth over N10 million
were stored.
If the operation in Sankera, according to an eyewitness, was targeted
at crumbling opposition in Ukum Local Government, then that of
Zak-Ibiam, the attack on the biggest yam market in the state, was
intended to kill the commercial activities in the area and the state.
Before the troops destroyed Malu's house, they had razed the
Zik-Ibiam yam market, killing 19 people in the process. But at Gebiji
village, more than 100 people were killed by the soldiers in the
armoured tanks and two personnel carriers, according to Shina Ayati,
special assistant to Governor George Akume on political affairs.
Sylvester Kane, chairman of Logo Local Government, another council,
sharing border with Taraba State, said the army had sacked Anyi,
Torza, Chimbe and Adedeme villages, in vengeance for their slain
colleagues. Kane who has been taking refuge at the Government House,
Makurdi, since Monday, 22 October, said, "I cannot give you the
number of people killed so far, but many have been killed by the
soldiers and the tension at Ugba, the Logo Local Government
headquarters is still high." The soldiers' invasion has complicated
the condition of thousands of refugees, who were resettled in some
camps in Ukum Local Government. No fewer than 50 of them were killed
by the soldiers and the surviving ones will have to battle with
hunger, as the warehouse where their foodstuffs are kept was burnt
down.
The military operation has increased the tension in the state,
further creating suspicion between Tivs and other ethnic groups. It
has also added additional responsibility to Governor Akume, who has
been using the lean resources of the state to cater for the over
300,000 refugees from the Nasarawa and Taraba crises, currently in
the state. Gov. Akume will now have additional mouths to feed. For
the very first time since the refugee influx into Benue last June,
the governor will be receiving refugees of Benue State origin from
Ukum, Katsina-Ala, and Rogo, who have been displaced by soldiers and
not the Jukuns, their traditional enemies.
The news of the destruction of Gen. Malu's house and the devastation
of three local governments by soldiers, sparked off violent
demonstrations by students of Benue State University on Wednesday, 24
October. The demonstrators started at about 8 am blocking highways
and streets in the town.
Some motorists and commercial motor cyclists were harassed, while
their vehicles were burnt. The demonstrations culminated in the
indefinite closure of the Benue State University at about 2 p.m on
Wednesday.
As at press time, the Benue State Government was yet to make any
official statement on the military attacks on the state, but members
of the Benue State executive council, met in Government House, with
Governor Akume. Members of the State's House of Assembly were also
drafted by the state government to appeal to the demonstrating
students not to take the law into their hands.
Attempts by the speaker of the Benue State House of Assembly, Mrs.
Margaret Icheen and some members of the House, to persuade Governor
Akume, to come out and talk to the students because there was the
fear that the governor might be attacked. Opponents of the governor
used his N5 million compensation to the Hausa community in Gboko, who
were attacked last week by some Tiv youths as proof of his lack of
support for his own people.
The youths burnt down some houses and a mosque in an apparent
reaction to the attack on Dan Anacha, a Tiv settlement in Taraba
State, last week. The sight of emaciated bodies of the refugees from
the area, who were brought in lorries into Gboko, the traditional
headquarters of the Tivs, infuriated the youths, who took to the
streets and started attacked non-indigenes from the North.
There has been widespread allegation that the minister of defence,
General Theophilus Danjuma (retd), is behind the use of soldiers in
attacking the Tivs. Though Mr. Paul Unongo stopped short of
mentioning names in his last December press conference, Mr. Mathias
Ohungwa, a member of the Benue State House of Assembly, pointedly
accused the Defence minister of using the army to kill the Tivs, in
their fight with his kinsmen, the Jukuns.
Meanwhile, Abinsi, a predominantly Jukun settlement, a few kilometres
away from Makurdi, is now at the mercy of the Tivs. The Tivs and
Jukuns in Abimsi, clashed a few years back, over dispute on the
relocation of a market. There are fears that the Tivs may attack the
Jukun people in the village, as hostilities between the two ethnic
groups continue to worsen.
Since June, the Benue State government has been tackling the in-flux
of Tiv refugees from other states to Benue. The governor released N20
million as relief package for Nasarawa crisis refugees, while N10
million was given to the Taraba Crisis Committee, to provide
temporary accommodation and feeding for the displaced people. Now he
has thousands of mouths of his own people to feed. Akume's political
opponents, sources said, have been waiting by the side, hoping that
the lingering communal crises will derail his re-election bid and his
administration.
The military invasion has compounded his problems, just like it has
compounded the Tiv-Jukun crisis. When will peace come? This is the
question concerned observers are asking.

Original_Search_Title: Military Campaigns Against the Tiv People
Headline: Nigeria: Military Campaigns Against the Tiv People
Body_Text:
Recently, soldiers went on the rampage in Katsina-Ala, Logo and Ukum local
government areas of Benue State. Zaki Biam in particular seems to have been
razed down, and the entire population utterly devastated. Simon Imobo-Tswam,
our Assistant news Editor, travelled down to Benue State, and investigated
the matter. Among others, he interviewed General Victor Samuel Leo Malu
(Rtd) whose house was completely destroyed in the attack.
Makurdi, the Benue State capital, is just returning to its rhythm after the
riots that engulfed the city last Tuesday, and the dusk to dawn curfew that
followed.
Even though it is not a boisterous state capital in the sense that
Benin-city is, Makurdi has its own dynamic vibrancy. However, the riots,
which a Red Cross Official in Makurdi said claimed up to 10 lives, forced
the city into a coma. For much of last week, many shops remained closed and
usually busy precincts like Wurukum Round- about and rowdy streets like
Katsina-Ala and Vandeikya were deserted. In the place of residents were
ubiquitous heavily-armed soldiers and military tanks. And as Makurdi was
quiet, so was Gboko, the traditional headquarters of the Tiv nation, where
about two weeks ago, a mosque was destroyed by an irate mob.
The riots were reactions to the invasion of Logo, Ukum and Katsina-Ala local
government areas of Benue by a detachment of soldiers from the 31 Airborne
Brigade based in Yola, Adamawa State.
If Makurdi and Gboko, which were comatose while the heat was on, are
regaining their relative dynamism, towns like Zaki-Biam, Gbeji, Iorja,
Anyiin and Vase, where the military went on vengeful killings, are literally
and figuratively ghost villages. Literally, because the villagers have
either been killed or displaced, and figuratively because even those
trickling back to pick the threads of their shattered lives are dogged by
anxiety, and do not have much enthusiasm for life.
In places like Vase, Gbeji and Anyim, the devastation is so much that even
the singing of birds cannot be heard, as the noise of battle emanating from
armoured tanks and the sound of gunfire have driven them far away.
Daily Trust investigations have revealed that for the Tiv villagers who are
mostly farmers, this October is a black month that heralds a bleak future.
For as one of them, Mr. Anigbien Ityo said, "This is a farming season, but
instead of digging yam-heaps, we are digging graves." Because he is speaking
for the large community of displaced and bereaved persons, it means the
"food basket" will have less food in the next harvest season.
Benue may be known as the food basket of Nigeria, but few know that
Zaki-Biam is the "food basket" of Benue State, as that is where the bulk of
the yams  in  Benue State are grown.
It is the tragic fate of these villagers and the piteous after-effects that
will haunt them, perhaps, for the rest of their lives that prompted
prominent Benue sons and daughters, who met at the weekend in Government
House, Makurdi, to issue a communiqué calling on the Federal Government to
immediately implement the 10-point communiqué issued in 1992 on the
Benue-Taraba crises.
Their reasoning is that if the federal government had summoned the moral
courage and political will to implement the 10-point communiqué, there would
probably be no illegal military presence on Tiv soil, no killing of 19
soldiers, and definitely no peace-time invasion of Tiv settlements, and the
genocidal massacre of their residents.
The meeting, which was attended by, political elite of Benue State, also
condemned the partisanship of the Nigerian Army, and called for the
withdrawal of soldiers from the state.
The Chief of Army Staff, Lt. Gen. Ogomudia, has repeatedly denied the charge
of genocide levelled against his men, saying soldiers were only asked to
fish out the murderers of their 19 colleagues earlier killed, while on
peace-keeping operations in the area.
But in what appears to be an indirect acceptance of responsibility,
President Olusegun Obasanjo last week ordered the army to cease revenge
operations in Tiv land.
As the army withdraws, it leaves in its wake the ugly imprint chacteristic
of all military operations of this nature-blood, blight, tears, sorrow,
shattered dreams, broken hopes and ruined lives.
Prominent Benue State Indigenes speak
Mr. Audu Ogbeh  (former Minister of Communications and PDP National
Chairmanship aspirant)
What has happened in Katsina-Ala, Logo and Ukum local governments of Benue
State is as tragic as it is unfortunate. Villages being wiped out and
defenceless citizens either killed or maimed or displaced-it is unfortunate
to say the least.
Sentiments cannot be introduced into this. One thing is clear. This tragedy
overwhelms the so-called Tiv-Idoma dichotomy. For instance, the other day,
some diplomats came to see me  to find out about the situation of things. It
would have been stupid of me to say I am not a Tiv man, and therefore don't
know what is happening.
What I am saying  is: This is not an ethnic or Tiv issue. The whole of Benue
is in mourning. We are sad that 19 soldiers were killed, but we are even
sadder that entire villages were razed.
I will caution against the use of military personnel in civil matters, as we
will be sending the wrong signals to certain quarters.
Chief (Senator) J.K.N. Waku (Wan-Begha u Tiv)
The Chief of Army Staff, Lt. General Ogomudia should resign. He has failed.
The entire genocidal campaign by the Nigerian Army against the Tiv nation
is a set up. We must get to the root of the matter. We won't accept anything
short of this.
How can the Nigerian Army invade Tivland, a part of Nigeria, and begin to
kill and maim and loot? Between Dan Anacha and Wukari is about 300 km. Why
did the army go to Dan Anacha, a predominantly Tiv settlement and clear Tiv
people if it is not organised genocide or orchestrated ethnic cleansing?
They got there and cleared the place, raping women and turning children into
orphans. This is terrorism. The United States and the United Nations should
ban Obasanjo from travelling abroad until he has sorted his domestic
problems. The cruelty against my people is unprecedented. This is an
irresponsible and indefensible act of terrorism. The entire Benue people
call on the federal government to implement without further delay, the
10-point communiqué issued in 1992 on the Benue-Taraba crisis.
Chief Godwin Daboh Adzuana
(The Akpenlamen of Ute and PDP stalwart)
I am surprised, first and foremost, that soldiers of the Nigerian Army could
invade the private and ancestral home of a general, a former Chief of Army
Staff, a general of national and international renown. Where is the much
touted esprit de corps?
It is unfortunate that President Obasanjo yielded to military might to
destroy the Tiv people. This thing has not happened anywhere else in the
world.
The invasion of Tiv land by the army was premature. The police should have
been called in to handle the matter. For goodness sake, this is a democracy
and the police should be encouraged to fulfil its constitutional function,
instead of relying on brute or arbitrary force. When you appreciate the fact
that there was no evidence of any exchange of fire, you will realise that
there is an agenda for grand ethnic-cleansing which is directed against the
Tiv people. And it is really unfortunate.
Brig. Gen. David Mark (rtd)
(Senator of the Federal Republic)
I am shocked beyond measure. This has all along been a Benue-Taraba problem,
a problem between the Tiv and Jukuns of Taraba State origin. So how and
where did the Nigerian Army get involved in this? It's terrible.
We are in a democratic dispensation and the military should be kept out of
the crisis as much as possible. I give my word that the senate will
investigate this. It is terrible.
The president ought to have been here by now, but he has not yet come. I
believe he is in constant touch with his Excellency, Mr. George Akume,
though. Not- withstanding that, we are still expecting him here. My advice
is that all displaced Tiv persons from Taraba State and from Nasarawa State,
for that matter should return to their states of origin.
Our provision of shelter for them here is simply humanitarian. They are not
Benue State indigenes. They are indigenes of Taraba State. The fact that
they are Tiv people does not mean they are indigenes of  Benue State.
There are Idoma in Plateau or Nasarawa State, but they are not Benue State
indigenes. People who have been in a place since time immemorial cannot be
justifiably labelled "settlers". Government should put a stop to this once
and for all.
Wantaregh Paul Unongo
(Former of Minister of Steel and President-General, Tiv Progressive Union)
We are sad, and the federal government must know how we feel. Government is
aware of the constitutional provision of deploying troops, so common sense
and decency should have guided troops movement especially given the fact
that we are in a democracy.
The killing of 19 soldiers was unfortunate, but it is common knowledge that
the Nigerian Army has been siding with the Jukuns in their hostilities
against Tiv people of Taraba State origin.
The Federal Government did nothing about this. Instead, it sent federal
troops, armoured tanks, and armoured personnel against Tiv people.
Tell me, if they say soldiers were killed in Zaki-Biam, which is in Benue
State, why did the Nigerian Army go to Dan Anacha settlement in Taraba
State, about 300km away, to massacre Tiv people there?
Granted that the Tiv killed 19 soldiers but do 19 soldiers approximate to
thousands of lives that the army snuffed out in their vengeance missions?
Soldiers even came to the home of Gen Malu, and demolished it. General Malu
is a general , a rare breed who brought Nigeria international honours. His
uncle and aunt were burnt to death in the process. And the army does not
even have the grace to own up to its criminal excesses.
We are fed up and we have suffered enough. We are committed to one Nigeria.
We have fought wars for Nigeria, and laid down our lives for the country.
During the civil war more than half of the sacrificed lives were Tiv lives,
but what do we have to show for it?
I have gone to jail 19 times and I am not a terrorist. I am only the voice
of my oppressed people. And I will continue to speak. We are computing the
extent of destruction in Naira and kobo in order to send it to the federal
government for compensation. Enough is enough.
Meanwhile, we have implored our people to remain calm as we await the
federal government response.
General Victor Samuel Malu (rtd)
(Immediate past Chief of Army Staff)
I do not know why they came to my house, which is 15km South-West of
Zaki-Biam. Zaki-Biam is in Ukum local government, while my village i.e.
Tse-Adoor is in Katsina-Ala local government area.
They destroyed my personal and family houses, killed my uncle and aunt among
others, and forced my mother to lie down in the sun and destroyed the houses
of my relations.
What were they looking for? I don't stay here, I stay in Lagos. In any case,
it would be foolish for me to train people in the open as a major road
divides my village into two, thereby giving every passer-by an unhindered
view of everything in the  village.
What has happened in these areas is unprecedented. Some may want to draw an
analogy between Zaki-Biam and Odi, but the situations are different.
In Odi, some eight policemen or so were killed by some youths. When the
police were having trouble tracing the killers, the army was invited to
cordon off the town, so the police could go in and search for the alleged
killers. In the process of cordoning off the town so the suspects would not
escape, four soldiers were killed. The military took the corpses to Port
Harcourt and went back to duty, only to come upon an ambush in Odi. As it
were, the soldier's lives were on the line. So what ensued was in
self-defence. Now what is the evidence that the soldiers met resistance in
the places they razed including my village, where my aged mother was
encountered having a meal, and where they murdered my uncle who was
sleeping?
In Liberia where the rebels were hostile and bent on killing us,  when we
caught them in battle, we did not kill them or their relations or demolish
their houses. We simply handed them over to the police or the appropriate
authorities. Today one of them is the president of that country.
What we are talking about is an unfortunate tragedy. I have made
representatiions to the President, the Defence Minister, the Chief of
Defence Staff, the National Security Adviser and the Inspector-General of
Police, and I am awaiting their response.
I am expecting that there would be some response. If the response does not
come, I am a Nigerian citizen, and as such I have the right to take whatever
measure to seek redress.
I am waiting to see the outcome of my report to the authorities. This is not
a case of unknown soldiers. The soldiers who invaded our communities were
commanded, and they came in armoured tanks. Nobody can clean an armoured
tank without authorisation from a superior officer. I know this as a soldier
who served this country for 34 years.
When I told the president, he said I should not take it personal. Till now,
I am trying to understand what he meant. If I don't take it personal, do I
wait until I am killed?
Mama Adue Malu
(Lt. Gen. Malu's aged mother)
I was in the house eating when the army invaded. They came in four vehicles.
Immediately they drove into the village, they started shooting and burning.
They burnt the "ate" (which is the traditional sitting room). They burnt our
barn, and they burnt my late husband's house and then my son's. While we
watched helplessly, we heard screams a young woman ran out from one corner,
crying that the soldiers had killed her father-in-law- i.e. the village
head-and his wife. (Mama doom).
And as we looked beyond her, we saw smoke envelop the village head's house.
He was burnt in the house. Later, we would know that he was shot on the
forehead and his wife at the back.
Then they asked me and those who were cowering beside me  to go out into the
sun, and lie prostrate. It was while lying face down in the sun, and my weak
heart almost exploding, that I heard them harassing my last born to open my
husband's grave for them. When he couldn't find the key, they descended on
him with their boots and the butts of their guns.
They were about to burn Akaa's (points to another of his children) house
when the leader, a captain, ordered them to stop, saying that he had
finished his assignment as given to him.
All through this, I was sweating. I almost died. You know,  I am
hyperhensive. It's a miracle I am still alive. I am told the Chief of Army
Staff is denying the involvement of the soldiers, but I want Nigerians to
know that the people, who burnt down our village, killed three people and
maimed one person, wore army uniforms and drove army vehicles.
Mr. Akaa Malu
(Gen Malu's younger brother and commercial driver)
I was in Zaki-Biam that fateful Tuesday. Some people and I were discussing
the invasion of Vase, Gbeji and the other villages by soldiers. Suddenly, we
saw five military vehicles-two armoured tanks, two trucks and one car in
that order, heading for Katsina-Ala.
Since we had already heard of how they trick people, I thought they wanted
to turn back on the town's outskirts and begin shelling. I decided to follow
them from a distance, so that once they made to stop or reverse, I will go
into a side street and come out behind them and go home.
They came on and on. I became confused. I said maybe they were going to
Katsina-ala. I could have overtaken them, but common sense warned me against
it. Somewhere along the road a detachment of soldiers form the 72 Airborne
Battalion Makurdi, who had mounted a road block at the Council Primary
School at Mbamena, hailed them for teaching the villagers good lessons.
I followed on from a safe distance still. When I reached Uli primary school,
I saw the vehicles turn and enter our village and immediately begin
shooting. This was about 12:10pm.
I took the vehicle into a bush path and hid with other villagers. From our
hiding place, we saw columns of smoke roll skywards, and we heard shelling.
it was like a nightmare. At about 12:50, they drove away. After some time,
we came out and I drove to what  was our once beautiful home.
I then discovered that Pa and Mama Pev were killed, another elder, Menan
Tyobo was also killed and another young man was virtually swimming in a pool
of blood.
Those around said their leader, one bulky captain, stopped his men from
burning more houses including mine, as according to him, he was acting
according to instructions and he had finished what he had been instructed to
do.
I moved my mother and some people to Makurdi, after we had moved our
remaining valuables into the bush. This was our salvation as the soldiers
returned by 8.a.m. the following day in five trucks to loot. Disappointed,
they mowed down the smouldering ruins of the general's bungalow, torched the
thatched-roof houses on both sides of our village, and mounted a road block
for about two hours before driving back.
In Zaki-Biam enroute Wukari,  they drove straight to the yam market, loaded
four trucks with yams and packed abandoned motor-bikes into the fifth, and
drove away. They returned on Thursday morning about 4.a.m. and razed the
town to the ground. This is what happened.

Original_Search_Title: Up to 300,000 Displaced in Central Region
Headline: Nigeria: Up to 300,000 Displaced in Central Region
Body_Text:
Lagos — Up to 300,000 people are currently displaced in Nigeria's central region as a result of communal clashes and recent attacks launched against several communities by the army, local officials said.
Shima Ayati, who heads a committee in Benue State that caters for displaced people, told journalists at the weekend that many of the victims were Tivs who fled fighting with Jukuns in neighbouring Taraba State. Others were survivors of the massacre of more than 200 people perpetrated by soldiers last week in several communities near the Benue/Taraba borders in apparent
reprisal for the killing of 19 soldiers by a Tiv militia earlier this month.
"In fact there are more than 300,000 people who are now homeless. I am really in a fix as we do not have the capacity to take care of them," Ayati told reporters.
Large numbers of displaced people are staying in several camps around the Benue State capital, Makurdi, where they joined others who had fled an earlier fighting in June between Tivs and Hausa-speaking Azeris in nearby Nasarawa State. Tivs are the majority ethnic group in Benue State but have significant populations in other central region states, including Taraba, Nasarawa, Adamawa and Plateau.
The conflicts involving the Tivs, who are mainly farmers, and their neighbours who are mainly from pastoral and fishing communities, are mostly over land ownership. There are widespread fears that prolonged conflict in the region, which is one of Nigeria's food producing areas, is likely to undermine the country's food security.
Zaki-Biam, a rural town of 50,000 people, which bore the main brunt of last week's reprisal attacks by soldiers, is reputed to be the biggest yam  market in Nigeria. Locals said many of the people who were killed by the rampaging soldiers were yam dealers who came from different parts of the country to buy the produce.
"Zaki-Biam remains largely deserted as most of its residents who survived are fearful of coming back while traders who came daily to buy yam from all over Nigeria are for now keeping away," Mike Oche, who comes from the town, told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: Tiv to Obasanjo: Sack Danjuma Now
Headline: Nigeria: Tiv to Obasanjo: Sack Danjuma Now
Body_Text:
Abuja and Enugu — Atiku cancels Enugu visit, goes to Benue
The Tiv people have asked President Olusegun Oba-sanjo to immediately rep-lace the Minister of Def-ence, Lt. Gen. Theophilus Dan-juma (rtd) whom they accused of fueling the Tiv/Junkun crisis which led to the killing of more than 100 people in Zaki Biam and three other communities in Benue State.
A group of soldiers last Tuesday took literally an order by President Olusegun Oba-sanjo directing security agents to get those behind the killings of 19 military personnel along Benue/Taraba border as they reportedly raided four villages in Benue with 100 persons feared dead.
But in a petition to the President, Mzough U Tiv, (the umbrella organization of the Tiv people), accused Danjuma of indiscriminate use of Army personnel to annihilate the Tiv people of Taraba State. The petition dated October 21 was signed by Brig-Gen. Atom Kpera and Prof. Iorwuese Hagher.
The 12 page petition which detailed the genesis of the crisis and the role played by Danjuma recalled how the soldiers were effectively used by the Jukun to attack the Tiv all over the conflict areas particularly in Wukari and Ibi local governments in 1992.
It also recalled how the soldiers embarked on disarming the Tiv while the Jukun militants were left with their arms with which they invaded the Tiv villages and killed people.
The petition which informed the president how the Nigerian Army personnel were drafted from 4 Motorised Battalion (the home town of Danjuma) to Kashimbilla area last year to lead attacks on Tiv villages by the Jukun stressed that "for more than three months, the soldiers attacked the Tiv villages with armoured tanks".
It also alleged that the "killings continued unabated and on 13th October 2001, the Jukun attacked Dan-Anacha, the largest yam Market in Nigeria, and the army looked on the other way as the Tiv escaping from burnt houses were hunted and slain by the Jukuns".
According to the petition "no effort was made to assist them escape. And today, Dan-Anacha has been renamed kwararafa town, confirming the spirit behind the ethnic cleansing".
The petition which provided details of how the Jukun have been using the soldiers to kill the Tiv people said, "We have attempted to show how the Nigerian Army has been misused consistently, to kill Tiv people, whenever there is conflict between them and the Jukun" adding that the "Kuteb, a major tribe in Takum local government area, was almost annihilated by the army-backed Jukun militia in 1995 and 1999".
While calling for the immediate removal of Danjuma as the Minister of Defence, they appealed to the government to set up a judicial commission of inquiry to look into the remote and immediate causes of the crises in Taraba and Nasarawa States and also take steps to ensure that the Tiv return to their ancestral lands.
The petition which called on Obasanjo to visit the affected areas to see things for himself appealed for adequate compensation of the displaced Tiv people who lost their properties in both Taraba and Nasarawa States.
Meanwhile, the widely publicised one-day working visit of Vice President Atiku Abubakar to Enugu State yesterday was cancelled one hour to the visit owing to the Tiv/Junkun crisis in Benue State.
Prior to the information of cancellation, thousands of enthusiastic indegenes of Enugu State who were anxious to catch a glimpse of the Vice President were disappointed as they were seen in groups at the Enugu Airport and Government House premises.
Those who had to go to the airport as early as 7.30 a.m to wait for the arrival of Atiku were the former Vice President, Dr. Alex Ekwueme, former Min-ister, Chief Silas Iloh, former Governor of Old Anambra State, Chief C. C. Onoh and the traditional rulers led by the chairman of Enugu State Council of Traditional Rulers, Igwe James Mamah.
The stage for the reception was set as different dancing groups from all nooks and crannies of the state were seen with their dancing attires, while youths and women were lined up in their uniforms, all in a bill to give the august visit a rousing welcome. School children and traders were not left out, as the abandoned their lessons and businesses to come and welcome the number two citizen of the country.
In a brief statement signed by the spokesman of the governor, Igbonekwu Ogazimorah, it said that "following some recent development in Nigeria, particularly Benue State, His Excellency, Atiku Abubakar will no longer be able to commission the Enugu State government projects that was slated for yesterday. He is now scheduled to visit Benue State."
The statement further said that "His Excellency's advance team already in Enugu since Sunday left yesterday morning for Benue State where the Vice President is now scheduled to join in the pe
The Vice President who returned from Italy last night was billed to inspect and commission some roads projects in Trans Ekala roads, foundation stone of Nya ba Bridge, law school link road, Nara-Ugbawka road, Nara Electricity and Ozalla Electricity projects in Enugu urban and East Senatorial zone.
In a related development the Federal Government yesterday assured foreign investors that the pocket of crises should not constitute threat to their operations in the country.
The government position was conveyed in Abuja through the Minister of State for Foreign Affairs, Chief Dubem Onyia when he declared open the meeting of Nigeria-Romania Joint Commission,
Onyia said that communal clashes were not peculiar to Ni-geria, stressing that other countries were equally going thro-ugh such minor internal uphe-avals which have not discouraged foreign investors..
In his remark General Dece-bal IIina, the Secretary of State of the Ministry of Industry and Resources of Romania and le-ader of the delegation to the me-eting, expressed optimism that the revived Nigeria-Romania joint commission would go a long way in boosting trade and attracting investors to the countries.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuff Still High
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuff Still High
Body_Text:
Prices of foodstuff in Abuja have remained high despite government's
assurance of  fall in the prices owing to the bumper harvests experienced in
this farming season.
Following bumper harvest this farming season in staple food commodities like
rice, garri, beans and their like, government had recently expressed
optimism over a remarkable drop in prices of food items.
Abuja Trust investigation in Karmo, Nyanya, Wuse and other markets across
the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja, revealed that prices of garri, yams,
beans and rice are still considerably high.
A respondent, John Edem who sells foodstuff in Karmo market told Abuja Trust
that in recent weeks, prices of food items only dropped slightly.
Said he: "I feel government is doing its best but the cost of living is
still high. However, prices of foodstuff are falling gradually, for
instance, a bag of rice which sold for N3,200 before has now fallen to
N3,000 while a bag of garri has fallen from N2,900 to N2,700.
Most traders believe, Abuja Trust gathered, that the spiraling prices of
commodities may have been compounded by increase in salary of civil
servants.
As another trader, Garba Aliyu, at the Wuse market rightly pointed out,
"consumers should not expect the prices of foodstuff to fall as expected
because farmers feel they too, with the salary increase, will increase the
cost of their products and we traders have to increase ours to strike a
balance."
For Mrs. Ajoke Oluwa, a civil servant and house wife who felt bitter about
the escalating prices of food items, "salary increment cannot provide
essential needs as most people have to improvise to make ends meet."
According to Mrs. Oluwa; "the salary can only do for feeding, it is not
enough for other expenses. We were buying a mudu of garri for N20, now it is
N70.
Instead of falling it is rising. Government should please do something about
this.
NFA board decides L'Sportos fate
The NFA board will today de-cide the fate of proleague would be sponsors -
L'Sporto, a Milan, Italy-based outfit.
The board will meet to deliberate on L'Sportos responses to its objection to
some preconditions adduced by the would be sponsors before it could assume
the role.
In the same vein, the NFA Marketing Committee a fortnight ago had rejected
the 350,000 dollars sponship fees proposed by the L'Sportos. Instead, the
NFA insisted on a minimum of 600,000 dollars.
L'Sportos agent Panodzi had promised to debrief the Italy - based firm on
the decision and proposals adduced to them by the NFA marketing committee
after a meeting in Abuja.
The Marketing Committee had been directed by the board during its convention
in Maiduguri to critically consider the proposals of L'Sportos with a view
to producing a clean contractual paper.
Among the terms that raised hiccups was the minimum and maximum of  teams
format which the board had put forward as reason for failing to abide by the
Asaba AGM's recommendation for the relegation of four teams from the elite
class and promotion of two from the lower division.
L'Sportos response will correspondingly decide the fate of teams which have
anchored their hopes for first division action on the alleged minimum of 16
teams format  proposed by the would be sponsors.

Original_Search_Title: Agwatashi Under Attack!
Headline: Nigeria: Agwatashi Under Attack!
Body_Text:
By 6.00 a.m. on Monday 22nd October, 2001 Agwatashi, a simple farming
community in Nasarawa State came under sustained and co-ordinated attack by
persons suspected to be Tiv. 10 persons were killed, and in the community
the physical scars, are no less traumatising than the psychological bruises.
Tashikalmah Hallah, our reporter sallies forth to Agwatashi, and wrote this
blood lurding account.
"My son you took a photograph of me carrying my gun; I want to know the
reason why you did that. I am not afraid of people seeing my picture, and I
am against your action, but you must follow me, and see the reason why I am
carrying a gun. I have a reason. I think after my explanation, you may know.
If you are in my position, you will not even act more violently by carrying
a dangerous gun, if you can get one." Thus spoke Saidu Ugwatashi, a 54
year-old man.
For Mallam Ugwatashi, Monday 22 October 2001 will forever remain a
nightmare. A day of sorrow and perhaps a day when the milk of human kindness
eluded him and was replaced by anger and a desire for vengeance.
Mallam Saidu Ugwatashi was not alone as the whole town of Agwatashi was on a
quest for vengeance and this depicted on their faces. From what they said it
was clear that they are bitter. The machetes, the done guns; the cudgels,
and the knives they were carrying perhaps provided dramatic illustration of
their vengeful mood.
They gathered in groups of ten or even more. They have refused to go to
their farms, for fear of  unexpected and only few vehicles plied the road.
Those that muster the courage came under tight scrutiny. Passengers were
always politely advised to alight from the vehicles. And they often
hesitated to do so.
When driving to the war (?) you have to muster courage. People were always
wiling to offer you all sorts of information. Usually, they will try to
dissuade you from the mission, by offering you information on how they
witnessed somebody being murdered. Equally, they will indicate how unruly
the Alago people were. And if you want to go to the Tiv side of the war
zone, they will simply ask you to send your obituary to your head office,
and make available contact telephones.
Upon arriving Lafiya on Wednesday, 24/10/01, it was already late, and I
couldn't reach the end of my mission.
After a night at Lafiya, and making some contacts, one way left in a
dilemma, because of the unpleasant stories that proliferated.
However, the Nigeria police force, Nasarawa State Police Command, proved
helpful. The Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO) Mr. Isaac Ochanahi (ASP)
dismissed my fears and request for police protection. Jokingly, he asked
whether all men would not die one day. Although it was a joke, it struck me.
This reminded me of a friend in Lafiya, who asked me to leave him, with my
obituary, as I was proceeding to Agwatashi.
But all the tales, about the Alagos proved to be a hoax although it was
scary. At Obi, the headquarters of Obi Local Government there had been
stampede early that morning. According to my informant that fateful morning,
a group of women went into the bush, apparently to ease themselves, but they
rushed back into the town, alleging that they had seen a group of Tiv
militias in hiding in the bush. This caused confusion in the town. Upon
contacting the Obi Divisional Police Head office, the DPO in charge of the
area, was not around. He had gone out on patrol.
But after about 30 minutes of waiting, a black jeep arrived. Six men
alighted from it, among whom was the DPO. Upon sighting me, he casually
asked after my mission. When he was told that I am a journalist he just
waved his arm and said, "you know I don't talk to journalists. You should go
to Lafiya and meet the PRO, who will tell you everything that you need."
But, I told him, that I was from Lafiya, and had met the PPRO, and my
mission is to visit Agwatashi, Keana and possibly Awe. Again he waved and
said, "go, you security is guaranteed, and there are many people that are
going there. The situation is calm, safe journey. I am just from there," he
added.
Agwatashi is just about 35 minutes drive from Obi town. It is the second
largest town in the local government. As the taxi approached the town, the
sense of shock and grief assails you. If not for the brigades of Alago
militia with arms, manning the checkpoint at the entrance to the town, the
whole town looked desolate. Apart from a lady we picked along the road,
there were no women, and children were seen in the vicinity.
At the check point our car was surrounded by about 15 people, all armed.
They were not hostile. I was the only person, unknown to them. They asked
after my identity and when I told them they waved us to go. The women
disembarked, and three other people joined us in the car, and off we drove
to the market square, where they disembarked and the driver took me to the
chief's palace.
The tragedy that had befallen the people of Agwatashi four days before my
visit was traumatic. Properties were destroyed. People were killed, some
were maimed, while others were rushed to hospital in Lafiya. The grief  of
the Alagos re-ignited their hatred for the Tiv and Kutebs, whom they accused
of being responsible for their present ordeal.
Thursday is the town's regular market day. But last Thursday, the market was
a shadow of its former self. Only few people were present, and few goods
were on display. Even the limited goods in the market were costly. Moreover,
there were only few buyers present. It was learnt that there were no
outsiders in the market.
At the Chief's palace, the security men drafted to the area were seen
resting, although they were armed. The youths had formed themselves into a
vigilante with their guns, and they had taken over the protection of the
police sent to checkmate the invaders, and protect lives and property there.
The chief of the area, Alhaji   Abubakar Atashi, was emotional. One could
see how terrified he was. When I was ushered into his palace, he was sitting
with about three other people, and some courtiers present.
He was willing to speak, only after, a thorough scrutiny of my
identification papers. Although, he was calm throughout the 25 minutes
interview, he spoke with anger. The hatred for the Tiv action was there. In
a reminiscence he said: "They were the people who invaded our land. They met
us here. I was young then," he said. "But I can still remember visibly how
the Tiv met us here." The Chief wondered at the action of the suspected Tiv
invaders and whether it tallied with the modern day. "Are we still
conquering town's today," he queried to which the courtiers chanted in
unison, Allahuakbar, may God save us from he hands of our enemies."
At the end of the interview, prayers were said. A Chief was attached, to
conduct me round the town, to view the level of destruction.
It was pathetic. Huts were burnt. The town clinic has been razed down and
major goal of the attackers seems to have been a desire to kill. In one
compound, there was a door with several holes on it. I was told that the
holes were as a result of gunshots fired by the Tiv invaders. A young man
was killed inside the room. In other room adjacent to the one with holes on
the door, was an empty room. With pools of blood. A child, and his mother
had been murdered there.
As they explained, it all started around six o clock on that fateful Monday,
when the entire town were woken up, with the sound of a trumpet, and the
shout, "make una enter, make una enter. Agwatashi people, this is your town,
but from today, you people are no longer going to live here." That was the
beginning of the massacre, they said.
As we were going round the town, the Chief beckoned upon a youth whom he
introduced as Nuhu Ibrahim Agie, the youth leader, to take over from him.
Mr. Agie, a middle age man was later joined by about 20 more youths. Most of
them were unarmed. Only about two or three people were holding knives, with
another boy from the group dressed in an old military uniform which looked
comical. As we went from house to house, the grief in the people was vividly
etched on their features. As we entered one compound, we saw a sober looking
woman.
As we consoled her and were about to leave, three girls rushed in, wailing.
In a moment, the whole compound was full. The daughter of the woman had been
shot on her thigh, and was reported to have died in the hospital. We were
advised to leave. As we left the compound we met Mallam Saidu Ugwashi
carrying a done gun. He was serious, but his appearance was rather comical.
Having taken a photograph of him, the atmosphere became frenzied, as there
was an increasing murmur among the people. Although, one cannot speak either
Alago, Eggon or Tiv, from the mood of the moment one perceived that danger
at the mercy.
The policemen were not nearby and I was of the youth. But they surprised me.
They only collected my bag, apparently to hold it for me, as I was battling
with my cassette recorder and camera. Also, a boy in the gathering collected
Mallam Saidu's gun and left the scene, only to resurface without the gun.
And Mallam Saidu stepped forward and demanded why his picture had been
taken. Some youths tried to caution him, while I thought he was going to be
violent. But instead he was not. He only asked us to follow him, and we
obliged. We were shown the remains of his compound. He bought out four
pellets which said were from the Tiv guns. He also showed us a gun, which he
boasted to have captured from the fleeing Tiv invaders. But, he could not
control himself, when he showed us the grave of his youngest wife.  He broke
down weeping and vowed never to have peace with the Tivs.
A stonethrow from the grave side, where we stood was  decomposed body of a
suspected invader. As we stood there, the odour emanating from the
decomposed body was intense. Twice we were forced to change from one spot to
another. I was told the body was that of a Kuteb man, whom the people of
Agwatashi alleged was the medicine man for the Tiv. They said that they
killed two  of the attackers, while the invaders killed 10 of the
inhabitants of Agwatashi.
The war between the Alagos and the Tiv in Agwatashi is complex and its
origin is obscure. Both the Alagos and Tiv have been living in peace. But
resentment has been brewing for some months. It exploded in June this year
when a prominent Chief of Azara, was alleged to have been waylaid and killed
by the Tiv subsequently violence and destruction on an unprecedented scale
erupted. It also started with the creation of chiefdoms which did not favour
the Tiv, said one Tiv man. The late Chief, he said, manipulated the exercise
favouring his kinsmen, and annihilating the Tiv living in the area.
But, the Alagos, the Eggon and the Azara people countered this claim
accusing Governor Abdullahi Adamu of favouring the Tiv, whom they considered
as foreigners, and who therefore deserve no place in the area.
Consequently, the Nasarawa South Senatorial district has not tasted any of
the dividends of democracy, they averred.
Refugees from both sides are increasing and many people in Keana, Awe and
Obi local government areas have not planted any crops. The food basket of
the nation is now in need of aid. Starvation is looming in the area and more
needs to be done to salvage the situation.
Back in Lafiya, Mr. David Gimba, the Nasarawa State Commissioner of Health
denied me access to the victims. In his summation my encounter with the
victims may help ignite the conflict. The situation is under control, he
said. People area asking for the cooling tempers and not for a revival of
the crisis. In his opinion, media houses should direct their energies in
writing editorials that proffer solutions, to the crisis, and not publishing
the victims' pictures or their comments on the pages of newspapers.
The chairman of Obi local government area Alhaji Sidi M. Bako and the
Nasarawa State Commissioner for Information, Mr. Labaran Maku, shared the
same view. According to Mr Maku, the state government was about to reconcile
the two communities when the crisis erupted again. However, he was
optimistic that before the end of the year, the government will come up with
a policy on the peace plan.
The Nasarawa State Police Command on her part has ordered the inspection of
any corpse being carried in the area, because of the rumour of arms
smuggling.
We heard the sound of gun fire - Chief of Agwatashi
Can you recall the events of last Monday?
Well, I am Alhaji Abubakar Afashi, the Chief of Agwatashi. It was 6.00a.m
and then we heard the sound of gunfire, outside the village. Before we
realised what was happening, they had already entered the town. That was the
beginning of the war. The mobile police force were in town. But we didn't
know that they had left. They went to ease themselves in the nearby bush.
So, it was when they left that the Tiv came, and started shooting.
The mobile police, have guns, and they started shooting at them. That's the
genesis of the crisis. So, that, all that I know. They (Tivs) are the people
that came to our homes. We did nothing to them. But they were here. They
launched an attack on us, and there was no reason for that. So, that's what
happened to us. The invaders were Tivs.
The Tivs came in the 1930s - Youth leader
How did the invasion started?
First of all, my name is Ibrahim Nuhu Agie. I am a native of this community.
My father was born and bought up here. So also my ancestors. We cannot
understand why this crisis happened. We gave them land freely and our
parents stayed with them peacefully. Suddenly, they started the trouble. Our
people cannot sleep comfortably, and they cannot go to their farms, and we
have lost 10 lives. I think there is no justification for this. They don't
have the right to come and do all these things. We have been here since 1234
AD. But the Tiv came to this place in the 1930s. So I wonder why the Tiv
should insist on killing people that have hosted them. They are farming the
most fertile soils and we are farming the land that is almost barren, yet we
still manage to stay with them like that. Yet we are asking the whole world
why is it that the Tiv are attacking us? It is the place that the Alagons,
Jukuns and the Idomas left, that the Tiv are occupying today. We occupied
Benue State before the Tivs came.
A dane gun is not a dangerous weapon -
Mr. Isaac Ochenechen (ASP) Nasarawa State Police spokesman.
Is it correct for people to go about with done guns, firearms and knives?
From the legal perspective, dane guns are not prohibited firearms.
Individuals have the right to possess dane guns. But after processing same,
under the firearms Act you must license them. The use of knives is the
normal way of dressing. Some traditions allow people to dress with knives
and what have you.
Actually, the aim of forming vigilante groups is to supplement police
efforts and we are still lacking manpower in the police force. There is no
way the police can cover every nook and cranny of the trouble zone in
Nasarawa South Senatorial district. That is why you saw these youths
carrying out some essential services by forming vigilante groups. But they
are not allowed to mount road blocks. The road block can only be mounted by
the police. They have not mounted any illegal road block.
Sir, you mentioned that people are legally allowed to own a locally  made
gun?
Yes, legal possession, not illegal possession. The law allows an individual
to possess a dane gun. A dane gun is not a prohibited firearm. It is a
muzzle-loading firearm. And under the firearm Act, a mature individual has
the right to possess this, and then afterwards you can obtain a license from
the authority. But it is a prohibited firearm, like a bomb, a grenade,
pistols no individuals are allowed to possess it.
Then you have another category, we call it personal firearm. The personal
firearm is like the double barrel, or the single barrel. All these things
are personal rifles that individuals can possess. But you must obtain a
license first before you purchase them. But the dane gun, which a local
hunter can possess, is not a dangerous weapon. An individual can have it,
but afterwards he must obtain a license.
Nigerians are free to live wherever they choose - Labarun Maku, Commissioner
for Information, Nasarawa State.
I have been to Agwatashi and seen the level of destruction there. What
effort is the government making in finding solution to the problem?
The eruption of the crisis in this part of the state, has become a great
source of worry to the government, because this area happens to be the
agricultural haven of the state. It is supposed to be the centre of food
production, and this particular part of the state that has been affected,
produces mush of the yams we are seeing in Lagos. Much of what is being
exported, either rice, or yam is from that area. So, it is very strategic to
the economic well being of Nasarawa State. In fact June 12, to be specific
when the late Chief of Azara was murdered by gunmen, the state government
immediately interviewed in the crisis.
Since that time, we have been working closely with the Benue State
Government to ensure that there is lasting peace and reconciliation in this
important part of the state. We have met several times at the instance of
the Nasarawa State government. This was done not because, Nasarawa State is
part of the problem, and not because Benue State is part of the problem. But
because Benue State is the heart hand of the Tivs whose population spills
over into Nasarawa State, into Taraba State, and into the surrounding
states. So, anything affecting the Tivs, affects all the surrounding states.
Nasarawa State felt it was very important that the Benue State government
should be involved in resolving this problem. So, when this episode took
place last Monday, it dislocated the entire discussion, because whenever
there is a fresh attack, it will open the old wound. Now, we have to go
through the process again, to be able to persuade the various groups and
this is frustrating for the government. The state government has made it
clear that the Tivs living in Nasarawa State are part of the people of
Nasarawa State. We made that very clear. This issue is a constitutional
issue to the effect that Nigerians are free to live wherever they choose.

Original_Search_Title: Between the Military And the Militia
Headline: Nigeria: Between the Military And the Militia
Body_Text:
All through last week there were reports of an operation allegedly carried
out by the Nigerian army in some towns of Benue state in an apparent
reprisal for the killings of 19 soldiers who were buried with honours
exactly a week today. Details of that operation are still emerging but what
is confirmed now gives us cause for serious concern. Newsmen who visited the
town of Zaki Biam were greeted with scenes reminiscent of a war zone. In
this township famous for its yam market, it is easier to count the number of
buildings still not burnt than to attempt to count the number of vehicles,
shops houses, and even government offices that have been razed.
Other journalists who went to Gbeji, another town say the damage there is
even worse, with makeshift graves all over the town and survivors saying up
to 150 people were killed there alone. Similar destruction but with fewer
casualties are reported in Vaase, the village where the 23 soldiers were
captured by alleged Tiv militia. While the Tivs continue to bury their dead
and tempers are at dangerous levels, sometimes leading to violence as in
Makurdi where ten people lost their lives last week, there is need for cool
heads to ponder on the matter.
A crisis meeting of Benue notables condemned the killings and called for the
immediate and unconditional withdrawal of all military personnel from the
area.  The events in the border areas of Benue state have led to wide spread
panic especially among the Tiv communities who have been fleeing in their
thousands from Katsina-Ala and other neighbouring areas. Indeed many Tivs
now feel persecuted because of this recent events and the wider problems
that Tiv speaking people have been having with their neighbours in both
Nassarawa and Plateau states. The killings in Benue should give a lot of
concern to every Nigerian because it was Odi two years ago and now it
happened in Benue with even more ferocity. Who knows where it may be next?
No one will support the killing of law enforcement agents, whether policemen
or soldiers anywhere in Nigeria. Such action touch on the basis of our
existence as a nation which is only possible where law and order is upheld.
As the funeral of those 19 soldiers in Abuja last week showed, all right
thinking Nigerians mourned with their families. Everyone was gladdened to
hear President Obasanjo saying that he had directed the security agencies to
bring those responsible to justice. Little did anyone expect that even
before that pledge was given, the soldiers have began an operation of their
own on that day in the area.
Although the military authorities continue to deny any knowledge of this
wanton killing of Nigerian citizens and destruction of their properties in
Benue state, it is surprising that everything went quiet after the order
reportedly given by President Obasanjo that all hostilities should cease
immediately on Wednesday. All this raises the need to fully investigate the
whole matter so as to find out what really happened in those seven towns and
villages last week and who gave the order to those troops who acted in such
brutal manner. Anything short of that could mean that the army or elements
within it can act with impunity. This will not augur well for our fledgling
democracy  which entails strict adherence to the rule of law. We believe
that the essential distinction between a militia and an army is that the
latter operates within the law and observes certain conventions.
There is also the need to go beyond the immediate matter at hand and try to
settle the dispute between the Tivs and the Jukuns and the Tivs and the
various groups in Nassarawa that they have been having skirmishes with. We
do not see any quick fix solution, but can recommend that the elites of all
these groups need to exert themselves and see that they do not encourage the
escalation of the crisis. With displaced people running into tens of
thousands the Federal government should in the immediate assist all those
displaced by these different crises, even as solutions are being sought. The
National assembly should also fulfil the promise it made after the recent
Jos crisis and begin to look into the issue of indigeneship in Nigeria. We
feel that the question of the rights and privileges of people in the places
they reside needs to be settled to forestall the frequent bloody conflicts
that are often related to land ownership and other privileges of
indegenesship.

Original_Search_Title: Rage of Idol Worshippers
Headline: Nigeria: Rage of Idol Worshippers
Body_Text:
Traditional worshippers disrupt Christian crusade, burn down churches, banish Christians from Ezekwe community
Cosmas Egana, 68, a member of Desciple Ministry, in Ezekwe community, Yala local government area of   Cross  River  State  , narrowly escaped death last month when members of his church clashed with pagans in the community during a five-day crusade in the area. Egana sustained injuries during the clash and was hospitalised for one week. He had been discharged from the hospital by the time Newswatch met him.
But his problem has not ended. He has been banished from Ezekwe land for participating in the crusade, alongside all members of his church from Ezekwe. He told Newswatch in his "exile" at a community in Ogoja local government area that he was not allowed to take any of his belongings from his house.
The trouble  between Christians and pagans in the area is said to have started September 6, when Christians in the community, under the aegis of Ezekwe Christian Association, organised an interdenominational revival fellowship to preach the gospel of repentance. The church activities commenced with its usual praise worship and music. Three days into the crusade, an emissary was reportedly sent by the clan head, on behalf of the community to the church leaders, requesting them to stop their worship. The reason, Newswatch gathered, was to enable the traditionalists observe their annual ritual of sacrifices, preparatory to the new yam festival. A week to the celebration is said to be set aside as "peace week," a period when no form of noise is condoned. It is believed to be a time when ancestral spirits commune with the gods of the land for prosperity.
So, the church activities were perceived as disturbing the intercession of the gods for the land. The church leaders were said to have refused to stop the crusade, arguing, that each faith had a right to worship. The traditionalists were said to have stormed the venue of the crusade with matchets and clubs, and seized their musical instruments. The church at the crusade venue was immediately set ablaze. The violence reportedly spread beyond the venue of the revival. Another church, the Assemblies of God, about two kilometres away was pulled down. The Christians were attacked.
Thomas Ogwogwo, a member of Assemblies of God Church in the area who managed to escape into the forest told Newswatch, September 28, in his hide-out, that he thanks God for being alive. He said he lost everything to the idol worshippers. He said as soon as he escaped, the people rushed to his house, humiliated his family, ransacked the place and made away with valuable property and domestic animals, while his family were later chased out of the community. He said even his farm was not spared. "They invaded my farm, destroyed the crops. And now I am banished from the community," Ogwogwo lamented.
The case of Egana and Ogwogwo represents the fate of other Christians in the community who have suffered one form of humiliation and torture in the hands of the traditionalists. Some were said to have been mercilessly beaten, banished or ostracised, depending on the level of their involvement in the church activities.
In the heat of the crisis, the police in the area could not contain the situation. The youths in the community were said to have launched an attack on the police team, breaking the windscreen of police vehicles, and later rescued their colleagues who were arrested by the police.
Paul Bisong, an inspector of police and acting station officer at the Okpoma police post told Newswatch, September 30, that though the situation had been brought under control, the case was being investigated by the area commander in Ogoja. He said that the culprits will be made to face due process of law.
Cosmas Adeke, the clan head, admitted that they ordered that the churches be destroyed because the Christians violated the ancestral laws, and that the consequences would be great on the land should they watch the Christians continue in their practices. He, however, denied that any harm was caused. He said his men only seized the musical instruments which the Christians used to disturb the peace of the gods. He told Newswatch, September 28, that the songs, clapping and drumming scared away the gods of the land, particularly within the peace week. He said they did not stop Christians from worshipping their God, but that they were only asked to do so in silence.He said after all, the traditional religion existed before the advent of the foreign one, and wondered why Christinity should erode their own belief and religion.
The entire Ezekwe community was deserted by Christians when Newswatch visited the area recently. Newswatch learnt they are all now living in exile. But Adeke claimed that most of the Christians were teachers, and that they had gone to their stations outside the community as schools reopened, and that other members might have gone to their farms.
But Newswatch investigation showed that most of the Christians were banished from the community and are taking refuge elsewhere. Some of them who spoke to Newswatch later in their hide-outs outside the community said they have been in exile since the incident started September 6. They said the traditionalists  ordered them to give live cow, goat, kolanuts and other items to appease the gods for  "sanity" to return to the land before they would be pardoned and accepted back into the community.
But one of the Christians said he would prefer to die in exile than to appease idols. " I can never offer any sacrifice to dead gods. God forbids that. Instead, I will die here in exile," he swore.
Christian Ekiri, vice-chairman of Yala local government council, who is also an indigene of the community was also banished  from his community for harbouring some of the Christians. Ekiri told Newswatch September 28, that he has not been to his community since then. He said he would give them time to either rescind their decision on him, or take the key players in the community to court for trampling on his fundamental human rights. He described the religious clash as "primitive," particularly, at this period when civilisation has reached every remote part of the world. Ekiri expressed disappointment with the clan head over his role in the matter, saying he was surprised that an elite of Adeke's standing could condescend to the level of fetish matters,  causing untold hardship for his community members.
Matthew Abru  Ojugbo, chairman of the local government council, decried the religious squabble, saying it was unfortunate. He said the clash was strange and unheard of anywhere in the country. He has enjoined the parties concerned to find a lasting solution to a peaceful resolution of the crisis.
Ojugbo also cautioned that village heads would be held responsible for any such crisis in the local government area again. He told Newswatch that he was working closely with the area command of the   Nigeria  police,  Ogoja, to step up efforts at bringing the perpetrators of the clash to book.

Original_Search_Title: Army Chief Absolves Soldiers of Blame...
Headline: Nigeria: Army Chief Absolves Soldiers of Blame...
Body_Text:
How Tiv Villages Were Sacked...Eyewitness Report
Zaki Biam, a border town in Ukum Local Government of Benue State, is the food basket of the state, as it has the largest yam market in the country. Traders usually come from far and near to buy yam which they carry to as far flung areas as the Southeast and Lagos.
Last Tuesday, the once lively town, which attracts people from across the country, lost its innocence. Soldiers, apparently on a revenge mission, over the abduction and killing of 19 of their colleagues, swooped on the inhabitants, leaving on their trail death, anguish and desolation.
Indeed, by the time the soldiers left, they left behind a city in ruin. There is no single building standing in Zaki-Biam. Those that could not be outrightly destroyed, were razed down. This was the spectacle that greeted us yesterday as we toured the affected areas. But the Chief of Army Staff (COAS), Lt-Gen. Alexander Ogomudia, said in Abuja that soldiers might have acted in self-defence on Tuesday.
As at yesterday afternoon, smoke of fire billowed from most of the buildings, an indication of the freshness of the "madness" and "mayhem" visited on the communities.
To those familiar with the city, Zaki-Biam lost its innocence in the early hours of Wednesday. Eyewitnesses and inhabitants say that about seven truck loads of soldiers from neighbouring Taraba State came into the city waving the olive branch. They reportedly told the people that there was no cause for alarm as they had come to keep the peace.
Unsuspectingly, the residents welcomed their strange visitors, unmindful of the fate that awaited them. In no time, as the story goes, the soldiers opened fire. The residents took to their heels. They fled into the bush. Those who were unlucky were felled by bullets. At the last count, eyewitnesses said that about 100 people had been killed before the invading solders retreated. When THISDAY visited Zaki-Biam alongside reporters from CNN, Reuters, BBC and newsmen from the local media, about five corpses were yet to be cleared and buried. The stench oozing from the bodies, did not prevent passers-by from casting glances at them as they laid motionless, breathless.
In terms of physical destruction, Zaki-Biam was unparalleled. No single building is standing in the area intact owing to the destruction. Many of them were burnt down. The city was deserted and desolate, except for individuals who occasionally came out of their hiding in the bush.
The destruction of properties and killings were not restricted to Zaki-Biam. Elsewhere in Gbeju and Anyin border villages in the area, the destruction was mind-boggling. Residents say that the invading soldiers killed between 200 to 300 persons.
As in Zaki-Biam the soldiers were said to have stormed the villages in five armoured personnel carriers.
On arrival at the villages, they allegedly told the residents to gather at the village square for a meeting. As they gathered, the spokesman of the soldiers asked the men to separate from the women. They opened fire on the men killing as many as their fire power could sustain. Those left behind, mainly women and children, have fled the villages and taken refuge in the bush and in other far-flung areas.
However, the most pathetic is the destruction of the country home of Lt.-General Victor Malu, former Chief of Army Chief and ECOMOG Field Commander.
The former Army chief's country home in Katsina Ala was utterly destroyed. The only standing building is bullet-ridden while the one-story building was burnt completely.
A Peugeot 504 saloon car with Registration number AA 479 KAL parked in the garage was torched. Ortega Bollogo who narrated the invasion said that the soldiers who carried out the action, came with hand grenade with which they burnt the house.
He added that the men said they had orders from the Federal Government to destroy the building. Malu's houses were levelled to ground zero.
Eighty-year old blind Pa. Pev Adoor, the family head of the Malu's and an uncle of the former Army Chief was burnt while in the comfort of his house. His wife, who was with him when the soldiers came, was burnt alongside him. The most visible thing in the large compound are the graves of the parents of the former Army Chief. The once imposing structure is now in ruins.
Chief of Army Staff, General Ogomudia, while fielding questions from reporters at a world press conference yesterday in Abuja said the soldiers must have acted in self-defence.
Asked if it was true that soldiers opened fire on some family members of the erstwhile Chief of Army Staff (COAS), General Victor Malu and the destruction of their home, Ogomudia said the matter was under investigation and he could not yet confirm the authenticity of the report.
However, the COAS said that if media reports on the killings were true, then the soldiers may have been acting in self-defence particularly in the circumstance that inmates of Malu's house opened fire on the soldiers.
The COAS denied that the soldiers were on revenge mission. His words: "I wish to categorically state that NA soldiers will for no reason engage in any vengeance mission during internal security operations as this is against out Rules of Engagement and Code of Conduct which are very clear.
'For instance, it is quite clear that in this type of operations, soldiers are expected to use minimum force to achieve their objectives, and that use of force are only permitted in the following situation: (a) in self defence, (b) when murder is being committed, (c) when arson is being committed and (d) when resisting arrest."
While justifying the movement of soldiers to the Benue communities, Ogomudia said the troops would remain in the areas until the militia group disarmed and turned a new leaf, insisting that they are there "to arrest the criminals who killed the soldiers, recover the remaining bodies, cordon and search any suspicious area for arms".
Furthermore, Ogomudia said the troops have been charged with the responsibility of removing "...road blocks belonging to the ethnic militias in order to encourage free flow of traffic and restore normalcy to the area".
"...I wish to state that the on going operations is in Ukum LGA only and that as long as the militias carry weapons and have refused the call to surrender these weapons and the criminals, the area is still volatile.
"The idea therefore that the Nigerian Army has invaded Tiv land is mischievous as one local government does constitute Tivland."
He said the troops were deployed to the area following the expiration of the Federal Government ultimatum on the Benue state government directing it to produce those who murdered the soldiers.
"It will be recalled too that following this cold blooded murder of these troops, the Federal Government had directed that the culprits be produced by the Government of Benue State within a specified period."
This, according to him, was not met.
President Olusegun Obasanjo has, however, ordered the cesssatin of military actions.
Obasanjo gave the order after a 45-minute telephone discussion yesterday with the Governor of Benue State, Mr. George Akume.
Akume made the disclosure yesterday while briefing representatives of world media who were in the state to have first hand account of the extent of the military raids.
He explained that Obasanjo denied ordering any action on any part of the state, adding that the destruction did not have his authorisation.
Akume said he explained the extent of damage to property and the number of death occasioned by the incident which might have informed the President's decision.
He expressed regrets about the wanton loss of lives, adding that in Gbeji alone, about 130 people were killed.
"We suffered casualties, and material damages. I took these up with the President. I told him that Zakibiam, Gbeji and seven settlements have been destroyed in the process", he said, adding "I cannot be definite on the number of casualties."
Reacting, London-based Amnesty International described the killings of villagers by soldiers earlier in the week as "an act of revenge" and "a killing spree."
Amnesty urged "a prompt, efective and impartial inquiry" and noted that the army had denied any killings had taken place.
A Nigerian human rights activist, Felix Ofou, called on the government to say who had deployed troops in the area where the Tiv and Jukun ethnic groups have skirmished for years, largely over land: "on no account should soldiers overrun civilians in the guise of peacekeeping," Ofou told Reuters.
The Constitutional Rights Project (CRP) called for judicial inquiry and described the action of the soldiers as akin to the Odi incident "which remains fresh in the minds of Nigerians."
"Constitutional Rights Project would like to emphasise that the action of the soldiers is not an act of justice but of vengeance. The soldiers took the laws into their own hands, thus subverting the cause of justice," the CRP noted.
Research and Publications Officer of the CRP, Modupe Omipintemi, in a statement further called on the military authorities to conduct what it called an immediate and full-fledged investigation into the matter.
"The Federal Government is also called upon to set up a judicial commission of inquiry and the findings of the commission published."
Meanwhile, normalcy is gradually returning to Makurdi after violent demonstration on Wednesday by
some irate youths which led to the killing of some persons and destruction of property valued at millions of naira.
Problem started when the students of the Benue State University, Makurdi trooped out of their campus to protest the alleged killing of many people and destruction of property by soldiers deployed to keep peace on the war-torn Benue-Taraba border.
The students also protested the destruction of the family house of the former Chief of Army Staff, retired Lt-Gen.
Victor Malu, in his village Adoor, in Katsina-Ala local government area during which three members of Malu family were said to have been killed.
The protesting students had blocked all the major roads in Makurdi and burnt tyres as they attempted to march to government house.
They were joined by the other youths, mainly hoodlums, while innocent citizens ran helter-skelter for their lives.
Many vehicles and motorcycles were either burnt or smashed, while shops were looted. Burnt bodies of unidentified persons were seen in parts of Wurukum, and along Gboko road. Schools were forced to close as parents withdrew their children and wards.
A newly-constructed police station at the Wurukum area of Makurdi was also burnt, while attempts to burn the mosque in the area were foiled by hundreds of anti-riot policemen who were confronted by the missile-throwing irate youths.
The protesters also condemned the recent donation of N5 million to the muslim community in Gboko by governor George Akume as compensation to enable them rebuild a mosque that was destroyed by youths in Gboko last week.
The Benue police commissioner, Mr. Cyprain Onephrojire, personally went round the state capital to ensure that peace was restored.
Benue police public relations officer, Mr. Emmanue Deebom, told journalists that 10 persons had been arrested in connection with the violence.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Tired of Eating Bush Yams, Border Residents Complain
Headline: Sierra Leone: We Are Tired of Eating Bush Yams, Border Residents Complain
Body_Text:
Freetown — Residents around the border villages of Falaba and Gberia Katombo in
the Koinadugu District have called on the Government and NGOs to
assist them with food and medicines.
"We are tired of eating bush yams," they told a team of journalists
Tuesday.
The Section Chief of Gberia Fatombo, Manga Issa said their village
was a common battle ground during the days of the RUF.
He said they have undergone all sorts of punishments and lamented
that they cannot even boast of fowls again.
Speaking on problems of getting access to health facilities, the
chief said sick people are placed on hammocks and walk about nine
miles to enter the first town in Guinea.
He said most children do not return to school after lunch as a result
of hunger as they most times go to learn on empty stomachs.
Sierra Leone Army officer, Captain J.S. Jalloh O/C 'B' company in
Falaba told journalists including this reporter "as you can see, the
people here are starving and the children are malnourished".
Capt. Jalloh noted that most residents live on Kola nut and water for
most part of the day.
"Some of the elderly who could not go out into the bush to look for
bush yam share our ration with us," he said.
A nurse, Jeneba Samura said most of the people around the villages
suffer from malaria.

Original_Search_Title: Upper West Faces Looming Food Crisis
Headline: Ghana: Upper West Faces Looming Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Illegal exportation of foodstuff from the Upper West Region to Burkina Faso is causing price hikes and pushing the region to the verge of scarcity.
The situation has generated public outcry and people from all walks of life are calling for government's intervention.
Investigations conducted by the Ghana News Agency showed that about eight cargo truck-fulls of maize and yams leave Wa every market day for markets at Fielmua, a Ghanaian border town and Leo in Burkina Faso.
As a result, prices of the two commodities, which are normally low at this time of the year, are rising astronomically.
Maize, which sold at 100,000 cedis two weeks ago, now sells at 140,000 per maxi bag while three large tubers of yam, which cost 5,000 cedis, can now be purchased at 10,000 cedis.
Mr Iddrisu Mahama, an educationist described the situation as unprecedented and called on the government to intervene to prevent famine during the lean season.
He suggested the formation of a task force to team up with the GPRTU to bring the situation under control.
Mallam Mahamudu Iddrisu, Chairman of the Wa Cargo Truck drivers branch of the GPRTU admitted that their members were involved in conveying food across the borders but said the union lacked the power to stop them.
"They are our members but we do not control their movement. We can only stop them if we are given power to do so."
Alhaji Seidu Fuseini, a member of the GPRTU said smuggling of foodstuff would compound the problem of scarcity following the abrupt end of the rainy season, which had caused many cereal farms to wither.

Original_Search_Title: Funfair At Agric Fair
Headline: Nigeria: Funfair At Agric Fair
Body_Text:
Yams can now cure malaria.  That was what our reporter, Ikenna Emeka Okpani
learnt at the agricultural exhibition which held at the Old Parade Ground
recently. He recounts the thrills and spills of the event.
The World Food Day was celebrated all over the world penultimate Tuesday.
In Abuja, the National Association of Farmers of Nigeria (NAFAN) marked the
day with an agricultural exhibition at the old parade ground.
Farmers from all over the federation who were just smarting from an earlier
