National Summit held a day before the celebration trooped to the ground in
their numbers with various agricultural products.
Agro-based government agencies were not left out.  The venue was
carnival-like with different dancers performing to entertain the farmers.
Abuja Trust went round the stands.  At the Ondo State stand, the Yam Growers
Association chairman, Mr. S. Illasanmi who was voted best farmer of the year
1995 told us how yams can be grown to cure malaria and other diseases.
"This yam you see here can cure malaria and other sickness.  For those who
don't like injections or taking tablet, they can eat pounded yam and get
their cure while enjoying themselves," he said.
Mr. Illasanmi explained that to grow yams that can cure malaria all you need
do is to use neem leaves as manure round the yam seed until it grows to
maturity.
According to him the yams absorb the content of the leaves, which is capable
of curing malaria.
Another farmer who is the chairman of Group of Farmers Multipurpose Union,
Mr. Emmanuel Olatunji said his group was the highest producer of potatoes in
the whole of Nigeria.
He displayed red potatoes that were as big as yams, which he said people
from other West African countries come to buy from them at Elegbeka Central
Market in Ondo State.
He sought for government assistance through funding and purchase of their
surplus produce.
At the stand of the Rubber Research Institute of Nigeria, the Assistant
Director Extension, Dr. OPC Irua explained the process of producing rubber
and took Abuja Trust round the products they were exhibiting.
He said to help the owner of a rubber plantation make ends meet, the
institute has been advising them to grow cooking bananas on the fringes of
their plantation and to grow snail in pens on the spaces between trees.
According to him the snails will be fed with unripe pawpaw and cocoyam
leaves.  The National Institute for Oil Palm Research came with cocked palm
wine, which the crowd devoured in a matter of seconds.  Buyers who had to
queue to get the commodity emptied their trailer load of palm oil packaged
in five litre gallons.
Other people that made brisk business were poultry organisations who sold
birds at the cost of N300 each.  At a point, the seller placed a ban on the
purchase of more than one by a single person to enable the birds go round.
A cross section of people who spoke to Abuja Trust said Nigeria was not
supposed to be looking for food.
"We are not supposed to be hungry in this town," one of them said.  "Our
problem is with government attitude to agriculture in the country.
People attended the exhibition from all walks of life including the
Ministers of FCT and Agriculture and the country representative of the Food
and Agricultural Organisation in Nigeria.
In his speech, the Minister of the Federal Capital Territory said the World
Food Day celebration was a forum for continuing search for a lasting
solution to the problems affecting agricultural development and by
implication food security.
He described Abuja as the "Food Basket of the Nation' and said there was a
deliberate effort to produce food to cater for the growing population of the
capital.
"The Ministry of Federal Capital Territory in its resolve to meet up with
the compelling need to evolve a National Food Reserve, intends to replicate
the Special Programme on Food Production (SPFP) in Kawu in Bwari and Ganbe
in Kuje Area Councils respectively,' he said.
In his speech, the Minister of Agriculture, Mallam Adamu Bello said, "when
hunger is removed from poverty, poverty is finished.
"I wish to re-affirm the determination and the resolve of this
administration to fight hunger amongst the entire citizenry to its logical
conclusion with a view to eliminating poverty eventually," he said.
Mallam Bello said the Federal Government was setting up a National
Agricultural Development Fund to support agricultural activities in all its
ramifications.
According to him, the administration has intensified its commitment towards
the development of food and industrial crops through the provision of seeds
and seedlings, extension service training, publicity and research.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Urged to Uphold Oputa Panel Recommendations
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Urged to Uphold Oputa Panel Recommendations
Body_Text:
The Chairman of Ohanaeze Ndi Igbo, an Igbo pan-cultural group, Justice Eze Ozobu advised President Olusegun Obasanjo, to act on the recommendations of the Human Rights Violations Investigation Commi-ssion (HRVIC) (Oputa panel).
Ozobu said the commission's recommendations were not the types to be swept under the official carpet.
He explained that everything was in the hand of Obasanjo, adding that Ohanaeze had made a recommendation, reiterating that it was left for the Federal Government to accept it.
Speaking at the weekend in Lagos at the celebration of new yam festival by the Eze Ndi Igbo of Lagos, Eze H. N Ohazulike at the Ohanaeze secretariat, Agege, Lagos.
On the inter-ethnic crisis in Kano, Ozobu explained that Ohanaeze advised the Igbo's in Kano to compile the names of the victims, stressing that he would not comment on the matter until the list received. He stressed that Igbo were the most liberal people adding that he had never had been mass killing of either Hausa or the Yoruba in Igboland.
Ozobu explained that the Igbo would always resist any form of violation of their rights by any group. He said, Ohanaeze had been calming down Igbo in the North otherwise the boys would have reacted to the unjust killing of their kinsmen in the Northern states.
On the electoral bill, he told THISDAY that he would not comment until he got the a copy of the bill.

Original_Search_Title: Royalty and Splendour At Odwira
Headline: Ghana: Royalty and Splendour At Odwira
Body_Text:
Accra — The heavy drumming ,the slow procession of the chiefs clad in the
best of customary costumes and the milling crowd that filled every
tiny spot of the main road gave every indication of what was
happening: the culmination of a traditional festival. There was
excitement in the air. Intangible but felt all the same.
The venue was the otherwise quiet town of Akropong-Akwapim. The
occasion: the grand durbar of chiefs and queen mothers to round off
the week-long celebration of the Odwira Festival, last Friday.
Dressed in Kente and rich cloths of all kinds, and bedecked in their
gold bracelets, necklaces and rings, the Akwapim traditional leaders
slowly paraded in splendour through the traditional capital of the
Akwapim state to Mpeniase, the durbar grounds, to be addressed by the
Okuapehene, Oseadeeyo Addo Dankwa III and the Minister of
Presidential Affairs and Information, Jake Obetsebi-Lamptey.
The Minister stood in for President J. A. Kufuor. Other important
traditional leaders also graced the occasion. They included the
resident f the Greater Accra Regional House of Chiefs, Nii Tetteh
Otu, and the queen mother of Ga, Naa Omaadru.
This year's Odwira also marked the 35th anniversary of the
enstoolment of the Okuapehemma, Nana Dokua 1.
Interestedly and indeed uniquely, the durbar was an interaction
between the traditional state and modern national politics, with each
side jostling for support from the other for its own agenda.
The current chieftancy dispute in the Akwapim State featured
prominently in the speech of the Okuapehene.
Lawlessness and disregard for traditional customs and practices have
caused the dispute, Okuapehene observed.
At the heart of the dispute is the demand by the Chiefs of Aburi,
Adukrom and Larteh to be elevated and recognised as paramount chiefs,
a situation which Nana Addo Dankwa said could be attained if the
procedure for elevation is followed.
The Okuapehene who is also the President of the Eastern Regional
House of Chiefs also outlined some programmes the traditional
authority has planned for the year.
The authority will embark on vigorous HIV/AIDS, environmental issues
and drug abuse campaigns. Girl-child education would also be
promoted, he said.
The Okuapehene recalled the days when Akwapims were famous for their
politeness, hardwork, selflessness and high degree of literacy, and
urged the present generation to re-dedicate themselves to those
principles and achievement.
Presidential Affairs Minister Obetsebi-Lamptey told the gathering
that though the government is working towards realising the positive
change promised by the NPP, it would take time.
"Ghana is a rich country that is temporarily poor. Together we can
make it rich again", the Jake said. He pointed to the relative
stability in the rate of the cedi and inflation as positive signs in
the government's drive to put the nation's economy back on track.
Obetsebi-Lamptey announced that government has voted ¢110 billion for
the pest control exercise of all cocoa farms in the country, a
gesture he hoped Akwapims would benefit from because of their long
association with cocoa farming.
He also disclosed that ¢5 billion has been set aside for the award of
the Cocoa Farmers Scholarship to deserving students who are entering
the secondary school this year.
Being the most important event in the traditional calendar, Odwira
attracts Akwapims and non-Akwapims alike to Akropong and this year's
Odwira was no exception. Every alley and conceivable space in the
centre of the town was taken up. Foreign tourists strained themselves
to catch glimpses and take shots of the cultural display that
enfolded. The bars and restaurants blared the latest songs from huge
sound speakers and sold out by the gallons, beer and other alcoholic
beverages to their excited customers.
The Odwira spectacle was one of contrast: the enactment of a
traditional past co-existing with modernity represented by "the youth
and their willful ways", as some old folks' described it.
In their clothing, the youth also offered some contrast. While the
males wore baggy jeans and oversize shirts, the females were bold in
their sartorial scantiness: skimpy skirts, tight jeans and
bikini-like tops. It appeared that they were less interested in the
cultural and historical stuff on display than in the opportunity the
festival offered them to strut about, get drunk, flirt and possibly
have unprotected sex. "Chilling out", they call it.
From the reaction of the older people it was obvious that the
celebration of Odwira was different in their hey days. And definitely
the scene was different when the festival was first celebrated 175
years ago during the latter part of the reign of the late Okuapehene,
Nana Addo Dankwa I. "Odwira" is an Akan word meaning "purification".
The festival period, it is believed, is used to cleanse the
traditional area and the people of the trouble and the woes of the
passing year. The festival embraces such related purposes like
thanksgiving, the introduction of the new yam, sacrifices to the
ancestors and remembrance of the dead. The people and chiefs of
Akwapim also use the occasion to pledge their allegiance to the
Okuapehene.
The Akwapim State is composed of two main linguistic groups: the Guan
and the Twi speakers. History has it that following the maltreatment
meted out to the Guan, the aborigines of the Akwapim Mountains, by
their Akwamu overlords; the Guans sought help from the Akyems.
The then Akyem king, Ofori Panin, dispatched his nephew, Ofori Kuma
or Safori, to the rescue of the Guan. After the combined forces of
the Akyem and Guan warriors had defeated the Akwamu, a pact was
signed at Abotakyi (the Abotakyi Accord) which allowed the Akyem
people to stay on at Akwapim.
A political structure was developed with Akropong as the traditional
capital where the Okuapehene, the paramount chief, resides. Larteh
became the head of the Benkum division; Adukrom headed the Nifa
division and Aburi led the Adonten division.
The Odwira festival, until the current dispute flared up, was what
attracted the other divisional chiefs to the capital to renew their
allegiance to the state and the Okuapehene.

Original_Search_Title: World Food Day and the Food Situation
Headline: Ghana: World Food Day and the Food Situation
Body_Text:
Accra — Another way we can increase food production is by assisting our
farmers to use improved varieties of crops and livestock.
It is a fact that the Crop Research Institute of the Council for
Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) and some of the Agric
faculties of our universities have developed improved, high-yielding
varieties of some staple crops like cassawa, maize, yams, plantain
and rice.
However, apart from maize, whose high-yielding varieties like
okomasa, abrotia and abaatanpa are well known to a sizeable
proportion of our farmers, the other crops are virtually unknown to
our farmers.
A vigorous exercise to introduce these high-yielding and usually
disease-and drought- resistant varieties of crops to our farmers
should, therefore, be embarked upon to boost food production.
A few days ago, the District Chief Executive (DCE) for Nkwanta in the
Volta Region was seen on television appealing to the Ministry of Food
and Agriculture to rehabilitate silos constructed by the CPP
government during the First Republic but which have now been
abandoned in the bush.
The construction of those silos was to ensure food security and
minimize post-harvest losses of cereals and legumes in the savanna
areas of the country.
Unfortunately, and for reasons difficult to fathom, there silos are
now choked by weeds, providing five-star habitats for lizards, rats
and mice.
Their rehabilitation for use by our cereal and legume farmers will go
a long way to reduce post-harvest losses and ensure the much-needed
food security.
Aside of storage in silos and other receptacles, processing of
agricultural produce could also assist in reducing post-harvest
losses.
Pineapple farmers at Nsawam and other areas and tomato farmers in
Wenchi, Offinso, Pwalugu and Tolon areas each year bear the agony of
watching their bumper harvests go bad.
Nothing can be more injurious to agriculture than such painful
experiences that can saddle the farmers with debts and discourage
them from going into large-scale farming.
It is, therefore, reassuring to hear the announcement from the
Ministry of Food and Agriculture that the country's meat and tomato
factories (which were also built in the First Republic but have
become disused) are going to be rehabilitated.
We have also over-relied on natural sources for our supply of meat
and fish products.
Attempts were made in the early 1980s to encourage fish farming and
pig rearing in the country.
Though enthusiasm was high, lack of technical knowhow led to massive
failures.
It is time we encouraged aquaculture and the breading of
grass-cutter, rabbits and snails to ensure the availability of animal
protein to Ghanaians.

Original_Search_Title: Accra to Receive 300,000 Pounds to Build Storage Facilities
Headline: Ghana: Accra to Receive 300,000 Pounds to Build Storage Facilities
Body_Text:
Ghana will receive 300,000 pounds sterling from the Commonwealth Local Government Good Practice Scheme (CLGGPS) to build food storage facilities in all 110 districts in the next five years.
The country will also benefit from private and public resources from Commonwealth countries to support the District Response Initiative on HIV/AIDS launched by Vice President Aliu Mahama in August this year.
Mr Kwadwo Baah-Wiredu, Minister of Local Government and Rural Development, told the GNA after the just ended conference of the Commonwealth Local Government Forum in Brisbane, Australia that the offer to Ghana was as a result of her allocation of one per cent of the District Assemblies Common Fund to support the fight against HIV/AIDS and malaria.
"I convinced the delegates that sustainable development cannot be achieved if many of our employable human resources die of AIDS and malaria," he said.
"This revelation fired the interest of the Canadian delegation at the forum and they pledged to support Ghana to combat AIDS and malaria in a sustainable manner."
Mr Baah-Wiredu said food security constitutes one big challenge to farmers and the government, adding that a high percentage of food, including yam, plantain, maize and tomato, goes waste after bountiful harvests.
He said the five-year CLGGPS fund would help build infrastructure and human capacities in the districts to ensure that enough food is preserved during bountiful periods.
Mr Baah-Wiredu said the Poverty Reduction Loan Support (PRLS) scheme poses a challenge to the administration of the District Assemblies' Common Fund, adding "currently about 160 billion cedis are in arrears to beneficiaries."
"My ministry has proposed that each of the 16,000 unit committees in the country should be tasked to mobilize and use at least 10 million cedis from the debtors," he said.
"This would make the collection and application of the fund more localized." He said the unit committees would target areas such as the Poverty Alleviation Fund (PAF), Emergency Social Relief Program (ESRP), Social Investment Fund (SIF) and the Village Infrastructure Project (VIP) for the mobilization of the 160 billion cedis.
This proposal is modelled on a system in India called the Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRI) set up by direct elections.  Under it representatives mobilize, apply and manage the finances of various villages.
The Indian model does not only allow communities to have a more free hand in deciding who benefits from the fund but also helps in the easy recovery of loans.
Mr Baah-Wiredu said the five municipalities in Ghana have also been earmarked to benefit from direct CLGF programmes.
These programmes include the lower current techniques, the best practice of clean city for safety, good sanitation and clean environment and a sustainable maintenance and repairs culture.
He said efforts are also underway for leading concrete waste management and plastic and paper recycling companies in the Commonwealth to assist the district assemblies to manage waste and to convert waste materials into useable building materials.
The minister said Ghana retained its positions on the CLGF board and on the finance and membership committee, adding that the memberships of Accra, Kumasi, Tema, Tamale and Senya-Awutu-Enyan assemblies have all been accepted on the CCLGF.

Original_Search_Title: The Thrilling Pulse of Federal Markets
Headline: Nigeria: The Thrilling Pulse of Federal Markets
Body_Text:
Around Abuja, the Federal Capital City, there are
numerous markets, which hold once a week, and where
all manner of  rare and attractive items, are
available for purchase. Here are young men and women
engaged in lively banter from dawn to husk. There is
the sound of bells, and the enchanting soprano of the
young lady, busy calling out her wares. Ahmed Tahir,
of our Features Desk went around the weekly markets
within the FCT, and returned with this ringing
account.
One of the most striking characteristics of Nigerian
cities is their domination by the informal sector
especially low-income businessmen and women alike.
These operators are encouraged to thrive as the local
government administration continues to build stalls in
strategic locations in our urban centres. Thus, in a
colourful line stretching from Lagos to Port Harcourt
and Kano markets exist in different locations, in the
state capitals, and even beyond.
However, in some of these cities, markets exist and
they operate on some days of the week or even once a
week. These markets are colourful settings, and one
usually finds rare items on sale. We have markets like
that of Garki, which holds on Mondays, Karmo holds on
Tuesdays, Wuse on Fridays, Gwagwalada/Kubwa on
Saturdays, Karshi/Karu every Friday etc, manifesting
an unlimited vibrancy amidst the scores of daily
markets.
These markets variously established by the original
founders of these areas who were mostly subsistence
farmers, have a long history stretching back several
centuries. For instance, Karmo market was said to have
been established by Gade people who first settled
there some centuries ago. Madalla, Gwagwalada/Kabusa,
Karu etc all have centuries of existence traceable to
their founders. The Gbagyi seem to have been
fundamental in the establishment of Madalla.
Perhaps one of the major characteristics of these
markets is their open nature. The locked up shop
arrangement that characterizes, for instance, the Wuse
old Market, or any of such markets is mostly absent.
Goods are displayed on every available space in the
open. So, customers need not search too long for
whatever he or she wishes to buy. The arrangement of
items in rows or sections gives a sense of order, and
this quality is usually found in any of the modern
markets.
Another feature of these markets is the large number
of buyers and sellers who patronise the market from
within and outside Abuja. Kabir Lawal, the assistant
chairman of the Yam seller's association, Garki
Market, told Daily Trust that he has friends who come
all the way from Kazaure in Kano State to patronise
the market. Those traders who cannot afford the high
cost of stores at Area 1, and Wuse old markets, found
solace in these markets. There is no doubt that some
of those who patronise these markets are top civil
servants. Vehicles with federal government
registration numbers with either madam, the maid, for
servants, or just the drivers are a regular sight at
the market.
Majority of those who spoke to Daily Trust agreed that
the vast influx of people into the market was due to
the prices of some items in these markets, which they
regarded as low, compared to that of the daily
markets. When asked why she has to drive all the way
from Maitama, Abuja to Karmo to do her shopping,
Comfort Ganiyu said the prices of goods at Karmo
market were affordable. "Prices of goods here are
cheaper. I usually visit this market to buy whatever
my family needs for the week. But until a friend
introduced me to this market, I occasionally
patronised the Wuse old market, and the prices of
things in these two markets are not in anyway
comparable," she said.
Kabir Lawal spoke thus: "There is no doubt that the
prices of items are cheaper in the Karmo Tuesday
market, Garki, Karu and all such markets, than the
daily markets of Wuse, and Area 1 etc.
While a basket of yams (made up of about 100 tubers)
sells between N2, 200 and N2, 300 depending on the
weight, such a basket would go for between N2, 600 and
N2, 800," he said.
The reason for the difference in the prices of items
in these markets according to him, is the high cost of
shops or stores in the daily markets especially within
the city centres, he explained. While a shop costs
several thousands of naira in Wuse old market for
instance, one doesn't need a shop in Karmo Tuesday
market or any of such market to do business. One only
needs to pay N20 per space, on the market day."
Various kinds of goods ranging from foodstuff and
fruits, to household utensils are sold here. Second
hand materials especially those dealing with clothing
is the section which stands out among other sections
in these markets. It is the most vibrant and
boisterous sequence, and draws a lot of customers and
other on lookers, which include boys and girls, and
the old and young. There is an endless stream to this
section of the market. The reasons are not
far-fetched. Apart from the fact that most of the
dealers are young, they are mostly young and
attractive girls.
At Karmo market, Angela provided the answer. She is 25
and a 1999 Lagos High School graduate, but she
couldn't proceed for further education because her
parents could not afford the fees, so a friend
introduced her to the trade, and she has sustained her
parents and her younger brother with the proceeds from
the trade. Unlike Angela, Patience is a drop out. She
claimed she attended a commercial school in Jos, but
dropped out in the second year, as her parents could
not continue with the payment of her school fees. So,
she learned the trade from a woman from her area, and
subsequently went on her own after the period of
apprenticeship.
When asked whether she is considering going back to
school she declined saying that her fate seemed sealed
in her present trade.
They may be drop-outs but professionals in their
chosen trade. With their looks they attract and woo
customers, and with their voices they solicit
patronage. They bring glamour, humour, and freshness
to these markets. The goings on in the markets revolve
around them. They are the life of these markets. They
are amazons as Peter, a shoe dealer would  refer to
them.
With this trade they are already attracting their
kind from the streets. Angela has two apprentices, one
of which is a primary school drop out, while Nkechi
her partner has never been to school, but she
communicates well in pidgin English. Patience has
three of such apprentices, and they are all secondary
school leavers who could not receive further education
due to one constraint or another. She sells second
hand socks, shirts and blouses and Jeans trousers.
Also adding vibrancy to these markets are the dealers
in second hand shoes, the group to which Peter
belongs. They chant and ring bells to attract
customers. These chants and the ringing of bells
sometimes result in songs and dancing which often
stops as abruptly as it began. These shows draw
attention from visitors. They provoke smiles; sorrow
and problems seem to be temporarily consumed by the
melody. They invoke purgation. They give life to these
markets, and they make them different from the daily
ones.
But the dramatization, glamour and vibrancy associated
with the open settings of these markets and other
advantages therein may soon be over as the Abuja
Municipal Area Council Chairperson, Mrs. Esther Audu
was recently  reported to be making moves to develop
all such markets within her domain.
Already, traders who were allocated space in Karu
market by AMAC have commenced development of such
plots. For those who couldn't obtain plots of land on
their own to develop, the romance hitherto enjoyed
seemed over. Mrs. Christiana Nwagbara, a cashier
attached to the market, told Daily Trust that there
may be no open space transactions again if the market
is formally commissioned. But before then, what stops
us from enjoying the romance - the glamour, thrill and
vibrancy associated with these markets.

Original_Search_Title: Government Releases Additional Grains - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Government Releases Additional Grains - Minister
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development has
released more than 443,966 bags of grains from the strategic grains
reserve for distribution to the public.
This, the minister, Adamu Bello, said was to cushion the effect of
rising prices of food.
Speaking at a news conference to mark the World Food Day in Abuja
yesterday, Bello stated that all the states of the federation had
paid for their allocated grains
He also said that all the 774 local governments in the country and
Federal Government agencies, benefited from the grains release.
The minister announced that the ministry would buy off from farmers,
all excess grains produced during this year's harvest, adding that
this was with a view to encouraging them to boost production and earn
more income.
Mallam Bello emphasised that to achieve self-sufficiency in food
production, the ministry had continued to uphold its mandate by
initiating and coordinating agricultural programmes including food
and industrial crops production.
He said that under the programme, the minitry had embarked on the
provision of improved seeds and seedlings to farmers, backed up by a
virile extension service, training, publicity and research.
"The industrial crops production programme will promote the
cultivation of cocoa, rubber, gum-arabic, oil palm, cashew, cotton,
groundnut and soyabeans," the minister said.
Other crops approved under the programme this year included beniseed,
wheat, sorghum, cowpea, ginger, maize, kenaf, sun-flower and millet
among others, Bello added.
The minister further said that to consolidate the gains of the nation
as a leading cassava producer in the world with an average annual
output of 3,410 million tonnes, the ministry had similarly initiated
the Roots and Tuber Expansion Programme (RTEP).
The RTEP programme seeks to enhance hunger and poverty reduction
through increased production of roots and tubers such as cassava,
yams, sweet pototoes, Irish potatoes and cocoyams, Bello explained.

Original_Search_Title: Flood Destroys Farms in Jikwoyi
Headline: Nigeria: Flood Destroys Farms in Jikwoyi
Body_Text:
Farm products worth over N2.5 million have been washed
away by flood in Jikwoyi near Karu, a satellite town
in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT).
Abuja Trust gathered yesterday that the flood, which
followed a heavy down pour on Monday, destroyed mostly
yam, guinea corn and maize farms.
The Sarkin Jikwoyi, Alhaji Musa Baba Aliyu, described
the incident as a big setback to the dreams of a
bumper harvest by farmers in the area, noting that,
"this is the first time the area is witnessing a flood
disaster of such magnitude in recent times."
He called on the Ministry of Federal Capital Territory
to assist the affected farmers.
One of the worst hit farmers, Mr. Peter Katung, who
spoke to Abuja Trust, said the flood had adverse
effect on him and his family.
Another farmer who lost about four hectares of
farmland remarked that it would take him a very long
time to recover from the shock of the disaster and
appealed to both Abuja Municipal Area Council (AMAC)
and MFCT to come to their aid.

Original_Search_Title: Markets Spring Up Around Government Offices
Headline: Nigeria: Markets Spring Up Around Government Offices
Body_Text:
The new Federal Secretariat and the Federal Capital
Development Authority offices are fast becoming big
markets capable of competing with any contemporary
market in Abuja.
At the Federal Secretariat, along Shehu Shagari way,
Abuja, Investigations carried out by Abuja Trust
revealed that there was no payment made by businessmen
and women who sell in the covered stands or the open
places.
A major thriving business at the secretariat is the
Buka (cafeteria business).  Thousands of workers troop
to the food market to have lunch.
The sellers scramble for customers to come and eat
food in their own stand. Those who were not able to
get portions in the covered stand at the back of the
Eagle Square have mounted giant canopies and umbrellas
where their customers sit to have meals served from
giant coolers.
Those who use canopies mostly sell roasted plantain
and yam and are greatly patronised.
When Abuja Trust called at the park, which it learnt
was originally meant to serve as a parking lot, some
workers were seen having lunch of roasted yam and
peppered palm oil.
One of the customers told Abuja Trust that food was
cheaper in the open place than the covered stand.
Apart from food sellers, vendors of other items like
suits, clothing materials, shoes, suya, oranges, cds
and cassettes and other household items were seen
displaying their goods.
At the Bullet end of the market, raw meat,  vegetables
and tomatoe sellers were seen attending to customers.
One of the sellers told Abuja Trust that workers do
not have time to go to market after work and therefore
prefer to patronise them at the secretariat before
going home.
"Again we can give them credit facilities knowing very
well that they cannot run away from their offices.  At
the end of the month, they pay us," she said.
At the FCDA, there is no difference between the Wuse
new Market and the one directly in front of the office
complex.
When Abuja Trust called at the market, workers were
seen haggling with 'sheda' material sellers and
others.
A major denomination was the use of umbrellas, which
were opened all over the place.
Investigations revealed that the capital territory
authorities refused them the construction of permanent
structures.
A civil servant who declined to have her name in print
said the sellers  were doing them a great service by
bringing the goods nearer to them.
"There is virtually nothing you cannot buy from here.
So what we do is do our purchases here and go home
direct instead of stopping over at any other place to
buy what we may need at home," she said.
An FCDA official said the authority had been trying to
chase the sellers away.  "But when you do, you see
them come back the next day," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Rising Food Price: Cooperative Societies to the Rescue
Headline: Nigeria: Rising Food Price: Cooperative Societies to the Rescue
Body_Text:
WITHIN the past five months or thereabout, prices of foodstuffs in
Nigerian markets have remained astronomically high despite the resort
to farming by many Nigerians this farming season which ensured
bountiful harvest. In this write-up, Charles Onyekamuo examines this
disturbing phenomenon.
AMONG man's biological needs, food remains perhaps the most
sought-after besides clothing and shelter. Whereas, many may cut cost
on clothling, and some others lay their heads under the bridge when
night falls, same cannot possibly be said of man's desire to fill his
stomach.
Hunger, as a matter of fact, has been identified as man's prime enemy
since the beginning of time. A nation that cannot therefore feed its
citizens is said to be damned to perdition. This is because, it would
discover that it cannot stand its own among the comity of nations.
Such a nation most often is a pawn on the chessboard of rich nations
who will use food aids to sear its conscience and dim its voice.
The story of Nigeria's fall from agricultural grace is as interesting
as it is paradoxical. At independence in 1960, she was already a
major producer of agricultural goods.
According to the Federal Government's economic direction, agriculture
accounted for 80 per cent of Nigeria's export earnings and
employment, 60 per cent of its Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and 50
per cent of government revenue. There were then farm settlements
scattered all over the place, and licensed buying agents for cash
crops produced in the regions with their beaches as collection
centres for export. These became were the propelling centres for
economic growth.
Besides the massive production of such cash crops like palm oil in
the eastern region, groundnuts and cotton in the North as well as
cocoa in the western region for exports, Nigeria was known the world
over for growing tuber and root crops, cereals and vegetables like
yam, cassava, cocoyam, potatoes, millet, rice, corn, pumpkin, melon,
onions, etc in very large quantities. Nigeria depended so much on
these agricultural products for her foreign earnings and national
growth. This was evident in the landmark physical development
recorded by the regional governments at the time. But all that
changed with the discovery of oil. Rather than depend on home grown
foods, Nigerians acquired a new taste for imported foods which
petrol-dollar would easily purchase.
It is expected that since more Nigerians eat imported food items
these days in the face of a stable and bountiful farm produce, the
prices of locally produced items would drop. But this is not the
case. A trip to any of the food stuff markets in the country reveals
that prices of food items have been on the increase since April this
year. While a cigarette cup of garri costs about N 15 as against five
cups of the commodity for the same amount in December, 2000, a cup of
beans which sold for N5 then now goes for N13 depending on the
specie. Same goes for other food items which have recorded some
marginal increases in prices in the past couple of months.
At face value, the impression one gets is that there is food scarcity
across the country which in turn is reflecting on the sky-rocketing
prices of food items in our markets. If this is so, what it means is
that the Nigerian food market is merely responding to the forces of
supply and demand which tended to favour price increase in the face
of inadequate supply and high demand.
At any rate, this sounds doubtful given that Nigerians have in the
past couple of years embraced farming with bountiful harvests to show
for it.
The green luxuriant crops that characterize our farms during planting
seasons attest to this. But it is observed that instead of farmers
flooding our markets with these food items after harvesting, the food
shelves remain scantily filled. This in essence, has made nonsense of
the laws of bumper supply which is expected to force down prices.
This entire business of increased prices is a reflection of
unpredictability of the Nigerian market which has not in the main
been homogenous. Prices of food items are now high after years of
fair stability in the face of the dwindling value of the naira. The
truth however, is that the escalating food prices cannot be said to
be responding to the falling value of the naira since these food
items, are grown here and not imported.
Rather, imported food items like macaroni, spaghetti, even toiletries
and cosmetics on which duties are paid appeared to be cheaper. And
here lies the paradox of Nigerian markets and our efforts to grow
more food.
Investigations reveal that the reasons behind the escalating prices
of food items in our markets are far more than meet the eyes. In an
interactive session organized by the National Orientation Agency
(NOA) for market women and other market operators in the Federal
Capital Territory (FCT) on Friday, August 24, 2001, in Abuja, the
Director-General of the Agency, Dr.
Ifeanyi Chukwuka harped on the efforts of the present administration
of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo to change the orientation of Nigerian
citizens for a better country and to bring down the prices of food
stuffs.
Dr. Chukwuka who addressed well over 10,000 market men and women who
thronged the Wuse New Market, Abuja, venue of the occasion compared
life in the '50s and '70s with what obtains now and concluded that
life in those days was sweeter than what it is today.
Although the national economy then depended so much on agricultural
products, Dr. Chukwuka recalled with nostalgia that prices of
agricultural products were low and Nigerians were able to feed
themselves ; regretting however that even with diversified food items
and bountiful harvest that now abound everywhere, prices have
remained high.
While urging these market women to bring down prices of food items
and rediscover Nigeria for the good of all, the NOA boss identified
unscrupulous middlemen amongst them who buy farm produce in bulk from
rural farmers only to hoard same with the sole aim of controlling
market prices to the detriment of a greater majority of our people.
He condemned the act, describing it as sabotage even as he sued for a
change of heart and proper orientation. "Honestly, we are the cause
of our problems. It is possible for us to change these high prices of
goods for the benefit of Nigerians; shun greed and love for money, as
well as the tendency to cheat", he said, adding that it is in
realization of this truism of change that the Agency recently
launched what he called "Operation say the truth and make just a
little gain." He decried a situation where market men and women
embark on adulteration of products like palm oil and other cooking
condiments for selfish ends and regretted that perpetrators never
bothered to ponder on the health implications of their actions on the
people.
Dr. Chukwuka who was accompanied to the parley by the Agency's
Executive Director, Mobilization and Orientation, Mrs. Bridget
Okujagu , as well as other directors and senior staff of the Agency
told the market operators during the carnival-like session with the
theme: "Dialoguing to bring down prices," that the market women
especially as mothers should take decisions to bring down prices,
enforce same on the recalcitrant ones amongst them whom he noted
would invariably change. He stressed that such a move would gladden
the heart of the President, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo.
Dr. Chukwuka conveyed government's worry over the escalating prices
of food stuffs in Nigerian markets and expressed the fear that it was
capable of obliterating people's appreciation of the giant democratic
dividends delivered by the present administration.
Said he: "Persistent power outage can be accommodated by the common
man because he has an alternative in lanterns, dry taps, streams and
wells, but alas, scarcity of food stuffs or high cost of garri,
beans, soya beans, yams, rice and all that we grow cannot be
accommodated in any form by the common man. Indeed, the efforts of
the Federal Government in providing living or minimum wage to the
teeming workers of this nation would have been wasted should the
present cost of food stuff in our markets be allowed to go on
unabated," he told the market women whom he reminded that nobody
would expect a hungry and angry man to fully appreciate all the
physical infrastructure laboriously provided by the government to
alleviate the sufferings of the common man. The NOA Director-General
observed that unbridled greed and quest for material acquisition have
obviously driven many traders in our markets to play the hostage
keeper against consumers as these traders form mush-room associations
under the pretext of providing for the welfare of the people.
With such high level conspiracies going on in these associations
whose members have formed themselves into middlemen food stuff
cartels, Dr.
Chukwuka noted that the agenda of their meetings normally centre on
how to increase prices of food items and prevent non-union members
from sel1ing their own commodities. Violators, he said were often
witch-hunted and charged heavy fines or prevented from selling in the
market. He described the method as discriminatory, intimidatory,
selfish and unpatriotic. The NOA boss said the conduct of these
market associations who prevent farmers from selling their goods
directly to consumers but force them to concede same at ridiculous
prices for them to fix the prices has succeeded in eliminating
competition from our markets.
This scenario he emphasized was regrettable as it diminishes the
inter-play of the forces of demand and supply in our markets. Dr.
Chukwuka recalled that with the formation of the Consumer Protection
Council by the Federal Government in 1995, he had suggested the
setting up of an inter-ministerial cooperative societies for the
workers to act as a counter competitor to the market associations by
sourcing food stuffs directly from the farmers and sell at highly
reduced prices in the same market with a view to checkmating
unscrupulous market associations, discourage hoarding and force down
prices.
He added that NOA with a good number of workers in the states and
local governments across the country could be empowered with seed
money by the Federal Government to purchase these food items, erect
stands in the market to be called "NOA/Federal Government Stands" and
sell directly to the people and expressed the view that this approach
will definitely inspire competition in the markets. He also struck a
deal with the market men and women who urged him, to plead with the
Federal Government to provide their different associations with
lorries to ferry their goods from the hinterlands and reduce their
transport fares if they resolved to bring down prices. Many of the
market men and women who spoke on the occasion identified hoarding
and high cost of transportation as solely responsible for the high
prices of food stuffs in our markets saying that the scarcity problem
was artificial.
Mrs. Felicia Sani, the chairperson of Market Women Association in
Abuja, however, said these associations may not wholly be responsible
for increase in the prices of food stuffs because they work hand in
hand with producers and know from where the roof leaks. According to
her, part of the problems leading to inability to produce more food
is the diversion of fertilizers meant for farmers by the farm input
distributors. She called for the empowerment of women via the
establishment of interest-free credit loans scheme to enable women in
the hinterlands produce more food, and pleaded that the poverty
alleviation scheme of the Federal Government should be directed at
the target population and wrestled from what she referred to as "419
coordinators" now handling the programme. The market women, she
stressed would work with the Federal Government to enthrone an
enduring democracy, even as she called for the construction of more
stalls for teeming traders who form 95percent of Abuja population as
well as the encouragement of women cooperative societies to stabilize
prices.
She also lamented the growing filth in Abuja which she said was
turning the seat of government into a dirty capital city.
The coordinator of market women in Abuja, Mrs. Patience Nnorom picked
holes with the high transport fares which her members have had to pay
to enable them bring food items to the cities and said it constituted
part of the reasons for price increase. While advocating the slashing
down of transport fares for farmers and food marketers, Mrs. Nnorom
also identified the high cost of market stalls and high taxation, the
incidence of which she said was passed on to the final consumer,
adding that the market women can do little or nothing to influence a
downward slide of prices without a corresponding assistance from
government.
Similarly, Mr. Chima, the Chairman, Palm Oil Dealers Association in
Wuse market, Abuja, and Mrs. Salamatu Abdullahi, chairman, Gari
Dealers Association in the same market agreed that transport fares
and cost of renting stalls in the market impact negatively on prices
of commodities, but said that, market women had decided tentatively
to slash prices by 20 percent before the parley.
Mrs. Abdullahi said farmers from whom they buy the garri in the
village complain of their inability to make ends meet as a result of
high cost of production, adding that since the farmers now seem to be
the problem, they should be helped with soft loans.
But the chairman of Yam Dealers Association in the market, Mr. Ahmed
while identifying excessive taxation as a major problem affecting
market women in Abuja and indeed elsewhere in the country, said that
food crops like yams and beans are seasonal, a factor that influences
upward or downward price fluctuation. He also called for reduction in
transport fares, empowerment of traders through loans and the
promotion of cooperative societies by both the federal, state and
local governments through which it can farm, import food items
directly from the manufacturers as the case may be and inspire
competition in the markets.

Original_Search_Title: Governor Kure Challenges Benue, Ebonyi Food Basket Claim
Headline: Nigeria: Governor Kure Challenges Benue, Ebonyi Food Basket Claim
Body_Text:
Minna — The claim by Benue and Ebonyi States as the food baskets of the nation has
been described as a ruse.
This claim was contested by the Governor of Niger State, Engineer Abdulkadir
Kure.
The governor debunked the claim before a crowded hall among whom were
journalists from the South, when the Minister of Information and National
Orientation, Professor Jerry Gana, paid a courtesy call on him, saying that
Niger State is the unrivalled food basket of the nation.
He dared the journalists to challenge his claim after the tour if they did
not see evidence that the state is the largest producer of staple foods in
the nation, especially yam, which the Gwari tribe is renowned for.
Professor Jerry Gana made a similar statement when he declared at the
commissioning of Kateregi-Petegi road at Bida, that Niger State is the
largest producer of rice in the nation.
Gana disclosed that Niger State, and particularly Nupe Land is fertile for
paddy cultivation, saying that if the entire land in Nupe speaking area was
properly utilised, the country would be capable to meet the rice demand of
the whole of West Africa.
The minister, therefore, urged the government to give attention to the area
in improving the facilities that could accelerate its development as he made
reference to the existence of the research institute on cereal food in Bida.

Original_Search_Title: Ndigbo and the Challenges of Nation Building
Headline: Nigeria: Ndigbo and the Challenges of Nation Building
Body_Text:
Chimaroke Nnamani, medical doctor, and Governor of Enugu State
prescribes an edifying role for his people now and in the future I
consider it an act of rare honour that the Enugu Sports Club invited
me to do the inaugural edition of its emerging lecture series at a
time every voice is needed to join in the drive to straighten our
track of new political environment. I have always believed in this
culture of dialogue, using every available podium, saying what is in
our minds, listening to other views as we strive to have a better
Nigeria.
The Enugu Sports Club, we know, has come of age. Way back, it was
just an elitist and evidently exclusivist gathering of those that
matter. It had rode the tradition of the ruling class' pastime which
hardly had a direct link with the people on the other side of the
great divide. The divide between the rich and the poor. Those who
have and those who do not have. Those who lead and those they lead.
The bourgeoisie and in our parlance, the talakawas. But even at that,
it had worked as a collective of the upper class' source of
inspiration for the others, even as it had earned the derision of
those who could not swing on the same platform with those
establishment figures.
Besides the individual roles of the members, it had offered much
insight to the lesser citizens of this region that in time, members
were, though informally, evaluated on the basis of the much they did
to lead their then colonized fellow countrymen to "civilization."
Well, good or bad, as they acted, the Sports Club, as a joint for the
cream of our society or the relaxed intellectual ferment of our
leaders, must change because our society is changing. Basically, the
class-conscious British society was freeing our shores for the hard
drive of the liberal, open, American society which was set to envelop
us.
I want to believe, and strongly too that it was the reigning attitude
in openness that is creating this forum for social and political
discussion as was never done before. This, certainly, is a fruit of
the type of leadership and current membership. The current executive
of the Enugu Sports Club and indeed the suave Think Tank will go down
in history as having given the members, Ndigbo and of course, the
larger Nigerians another forum, another voice and another option in
finding a direction for the emerging nation state.
The Enugu Sports Club and its leadership must have properly evaluated
our emerging society to establish that Ndigbo, vigorous as they are,
creative in enterprise and dogged in drive, deserve a leadership of a
forum which may not bother to get into distilling of varied opinions
but to create the enabling atmosphere for the growth of culture and
building of the mind.
In standing before you, I sound out without mincing words that I will
be a part of furthering the discussion, elaborating on the rise of
culture and learning and the battle of the mind. I do not stand
before you because I have the last view on our issues.
In fact, mine may just be to start it and retire while we crystallize
in ways unknown in our region.
But even as I stand here, I acknowledge the other fora which had
worked at establishing the Igbo bearing in the rise and growth of
Nigeria.
Somehow, I dare say that it is in getting to speak at a Forum of the
cream of my immediate society that I can really pick or magnify
matters more germane to our being and in relation to other factors of
our larger world. But I must admit that I never really bargained for
a forum of a sole speaker and in this case my humble self holding the
well sought after intention of this great audience reputed in
individual accomplishments and weighty societal commitments.
Besides, I am being drawn into a topic which very many meanings could
draw the fire from the great minds which had worked assiduously to
produce the all-acceptable Igbo frame of mind on issues of our
nation. Indeed, if we take into account the very boisterous and
cerebral works of great Ahiajoku Lecture laureates; Professors
Michael Echeruo, Adiele Afigbo, Ibeakwadalam Nwoga, Ben Nwabueze,
Michael Angulu Onwuejeogwu and Gabriel Umezurike, among others, we
can say that we already have those bits which had been expertly
established for the building of a focus for the Igbo and their quest
for a role in our emerging nation.
Those were the brains which situated Ndigbo and established the
essence of intellectual inquisition on a people whose encounter with
the forces of national alignment brought about the hope for a
meaningful contribution and the veracity of historical stages, good
or bad.
Because Ndigbo had a peculiar historical outing and because it is yet
to be determined what their creative roles should be in a nation in
search of true formation and onward mobility, it is a bit tasking to
have myself planted before you to build on the body of emerging
opinion on what should be the duty of our collective being.
Really, I consider it seriously that before us here are men who had
played one great role or the other in establishing a thread of
coherence for Ndigbo, either here or elsewhere. I am particularly
humbled that these great Igbo sons and daughters here, gathered, had
one way or the other perpetuated Ndigbo and so established their
presence in their emerging Nigeria nation.
To that effect, the challenge thrown to me by your topic: Ndigbo and
the Challenges of Nation Building - can only flow naturally, along
the track long followed by our people in leadership and followership.
In therefore considering the matters in your topic, I have elected to
pursue the angles of this daring topic along two operative words and
a phrase. The words are Ndigbo and Challenge while the phrase is
nation building.
Unless we wish to remain toddlers who are not true to ourselves, the
word Ndigbo evoke a lot of emotions, even if not equal meaning to
every Nigerian. Among us and particularly those intellectuals and
social analysts who did much of elevating us at the various Ahiajoku
lectures, Ndigbo are a people of distinct identity, consciousness and
destiny, even within Nigeria.
Though they did not go to make much ado about where Ndigbo could be
found, they had led us to appreciate whenever we encounter an Igboman
or when we enter his abode or place of traditional and modern
business.
By the same token, intellectuals and researchers appear to be
definite on what the people aspire to be and how they will ordinarily
relate with other Nigerians. Some have claimed to have found out what
the collective Igbo drive will be and so designed how the Igbo, in
his individual and group endeavours should be related to for the
perpetuation of the Nigeria project. That way, Ndigbo have fallen
into a pronounced stereotyping on the basis of what others said or
thought of them. We shall return to this.
The word challenge, may indeed be more arduous for me to explain as a
functional part of this topic. Taken relatively it could be the
enormous obstacle, real and imagined, on the path of any onye Igbo
and indeed other Nigerians to actualize the individual and the group.
It can mean the detected impediment to the Igbo man who had to emerge
from the war of man and gun only to join a society which dispossessed
him save N20 or N40 and which seized his landed property and at the
same time cancelled all the deposits he made in banks in the months
of the Biafra-Nigeria War, while recognizing the whole withdrawals
within the same period.
Objectively, the challenge of Ndigbo could be seen as the creative
drive to overcome the obstacles peculiar and general- to emerge as a
fortified Nigerian in this season of resurfacing ethnicism.
The expression "nation building" is certainly more intimidating than
any of the two. In the last one-and-a-half decades, nation building,
nationalism and national interest had been subjected to the
interpretation of a few privileged citizens. These privileged few
were made of the ruling elite in uniform whose usually subjective
views formed the values for nation building, nationalism and national
interest. To that effect, these had been reduced to the extent of
supportive gestures extended to the persons in government. To a large
extent, nation building was even reduced to donating large sums of
money to the individual projects of those in government, while
nationalism or the more personal angle of it; patriotism was reduced
to mere feasting in the homes of people in power. Such trivialisation
had actually threatened our nation that people were hardly able to
identify what roles they were required to play in their state.
In the case of Ndigbo as an indicator of a more global reference to
this phenomenon, the people whose leadership then typified these
phony expressions in challenge, nation building and patriotism, were
largely groping.
By simple calculations, Ndigbo are the people who occupy the vast
mangrove and forest terrains in the political as well as geographical
East of Nigeria.
Many may wonder what I mean by political alongside geographical
eastern Nigeria. This is simple. By political actions, particularly
emanating from colonialism, Eastern Nigeria starts from the eastern
tip of the Niger Bridge in Onitsha but this is a reductionist
partitioning against the more meaningful and natural habitation by
which Ndigbo are also known as occupying the vast plains from the
Western tip of the same Niger bridge at Asaba to points far beyond
the Western borders of Agbor in Delta State.
Of course, while I say that Ndigbo are the predominant people in the
old Eastern Region, I recognized the right of identity of those
ethnic groups who have healthily lived with Ndigbo in the region, but
who seek to be seen to be different, even in unexplained resentment
of their erstwhile kin and neighbours.
Ndigbo are not of any emergent ethnic group or striding ones with
hazy ethno-cultural identity.
Indeed, Ndigbo are one of the most distinctly defined and classified
nationalities in the world, even though they are yet baffled about
their lack of commitment to history and failure at streamlining their
energy for political gains. It is on record that as far back as 1591,
the Igbo areas of today's Nigeria were put on Portuguese world map as
inhabited by some vigorous people whose deep culture celebrated
energy, accomplishment and wisdom. The Spanish in 1593 were to expand
on this view in identifying the terrain as deepening in a stretch of
Bight called Biafra whose people live their lives in lifting to art
form the career in sojourn (njepu), thought (echiche), industry (olu)
and accomplishment (ntozu).
Unfortunately, those early chroniclers and cartographers were not
sure whether they were encountering Igbos or Heebos or Heebrews. But
they were sure that in the centuries before their arrival, a vast,
complex and glittering culture and civilisation had existed.
The truth of this glorious past and the joy of the reminiscences have
been celebrated by our modern historians and writers who, though,
regret that the same Igbo areas (Biafra) had exploded in one ball of
fire with the introduction of slave trade which depleted the
manpower, the resources as it upturned values.
With the strongest, the best and the brightest forcibly exported to
Europe and the Americas as slaves, the Igbo areas were set in an
unprecedented track in retrogression. So, about two hundred years
after formal abolition and about 100 years after effective extinction
of slave dealing businesses, the Igbo areas, the people and their
resources lie prostrate, yet to recover even in the face of
pernicious modern allocation of values.
Yet, even in the grim periods in history, Ndigbo have held on to the
dominant values and character traits which elevated those forebears
of the people who though (ehiche), sojourned (njepu), worked (olu)
and accomplished (ntozu). Their accomplishment showed in the
glamorous Biafra culture seen by the Portuguese and the Spanish. Till
date, the modern Igbo explore to the fullest those which are
identified as the trinity of Igbo character trait and process of
personality.
Every Igboman employs his. The same Igbo sojourns, home and abroad
and at the same time acts, works and creates wealth. We all know that
sojourning is a great industry of the Igbo, which is achieved with
the proper deployment of one of the greatest Igbo media of
actualization - Ukwu n'ije.
Ndigbo, rising in their cradle in the Holy City of Nri, had deployed
the feet and fanned out into the global arena. As they journeyed,
they bore the cot of reason we call akpa uche. This, we all know, we
use to direct the strokes of physical gesture which we know as our
aka Ikenga. These form the trinity of the Igbo character, at home and
abroad. The end product is the accomplishment (ntozu). When it is
told in the hills, valleys, cities and villages, then we are
celebrating that accomplishment. That is Odenigbo - The universal
applause for fame.
The success of these stages of the Igbo personality, though, has been
atomized and taken down to the individual, but that has not removed
the evidence of the capacity of the people to reinvent, one way or
the other, a yet more glamorous and stately civilisation akin to what
the Portuguese and Spanish saw in 1591 and 1593 respectively.
But while we celebrate our past as well as the ability to continue in
our creative traits, we cannot deny that Ndigbo in their present
state are confronted with a political culture quite different from
what obtained in the pristine region on the banks of the Bight of
Biafra. Ndigbo like other Nigerians are today like dancers on stage
whose drummers they hardly see. Such drummers change tunes and those
who must dance must quickly learn the new tunes and change the
dancing steps. This was the trend which compelled Ndigbo to adapt to
the values of others and begin to work at realizing a nation not
necessarily emerging from their peculiar culture and history.
No doubt, Ndigbo, in the pristine time had gainfully discharged their
responsibilities and filled the gap of a state in their gone-by
kingdoms, states, communities, but it is the challenge of the modern
state and the difficulty in blending that the test of ability in
nation-building hold.
In the past, Ndigbo occupied the "Biafra" areas whether by conquest
or ancestral claim. They had built their civilisation according to
their values. They had related with their neighbours on their own
terms and mutually agreed terms but largely, they had had no drummers
staying beyond their reach and command, compelling them to dance.
Well, today, starting with the day of disastrous colonial incursion,
the tune is called elsewhere and Ndigbo, as partakers, outsiders,
co-dancers and seekers, have to dance.
The questions now are: Would they dance as they did to the tune of
old Biafra culture or would they wait for a central tune to evolve so
as to join in the dance?
Would they elbow every other dancer out of the floor and seize the
stage to do the thing for others or would they shuffle feet and
pretend to dance as others may have done? Would they wait to grasp
the cacophony of the motley feet on the stage so as to blend theirs
with the so many other patterns? Or would they pick a particular
dance pattern and follow even to the chagrin of the others whose
style may be left out?
Hm, "questions" you would say. But let me boldly tell you that this
is not about the first time these questions have been raised from
within as from outside. From within, Ndigbo had taken a look at
themselves and concluded that they were confused about this
enterprise called nation building. They were quite dazed by the
reality of playing to suit the fancies of others who may never have
cared about the plight of Ndigbo- either as the individual or as the
group- in this business.
Those great Igbo minds who made the searches and concluded that
Ndigbo were confused about this business had drawn their conclusion
from some actions and inactions of the people which portrayed them as
lacking in the basic ingredients of making a nation other than one
peculiar to the people. However, the same minds and writers could not
tell why Ndigbo failed to make a nation of theirs in the strive to
have the Biafra of 1966-1970. They could not answer why the then
Biafra which was totally in their hands was not realized if they had
indeed perfected the making of a nation of their own while they knew
next to nothing on the making of nations encompassing other persons
and interests.
The views from the inside are not actually remarkably different from
the views from the outside. What appears to be distinctive is the
capacity of the outsiders to name what they consider to be the
impediment of Ndigbo in harmonizing with other factors in the
exercise of nation building. One of these is statecraft. Another is
diplomacy and yet another is headstrong disposition. Lately, new
views have emerged to classify Ndigbo as hurting themselves in their
inclusion in the drive to nation building by over applying their
higher attributes and those character traits which had aided their
forebears to think, move, work, and achieve. These they call
excessive vibrancy of presence, excessive industry and thoughtless
deployment of wealth.
Ladies and gentlemen, these are weighty charges. They are even made
weightier by the courage of the Enugu Sports Club to take it up in
its first ever lecture. I accept the hint that holding the above as
the topic of the inaugural lecture and with my humble self as the
lecturer, we have taken up this decisive enterprise capable of making
or marring the club. For this reason, my great audience, I plead to
thread carefully even as I am determined to call a spade a spade.
Actually, taking on the Igbo in the scheme of statecraft (echiche)
can be quite tough. Yes, we have had such feasible and rewarding
character traits as in effective thought ako n'uche pattern. We have
perfected sojourn njepu in our land as in other lands.
We have never bowed to forces of retardation and so have remained
industrious ike olu from generation to generation and we have
attained wisdom amamihe and accomplishment ntozu. But these were
actions and results mainly of the individual and not of the group.
Perhaps, in the pristine Biafra met by the Portuguese and Spanish,
there was a state with the attendant organization and expression
which subordinated the individual while elevating the group. It is
even tempting to believe that such culture level survived in the
seasoned Igbo culture of communality which empowered the people to
quickly adapt to lift persons and groups to such levels dictated in
the then arriving colonialism. It was what they called the
communalist tradition in which the handicap of the individual was
easily overcome with the communal pooling of resources.
Everything was a festival. The festival of farming, the festival of
marriage, the festival of child-bearing, the festival of harvest and
even the festival of death and burial.
Ndigbo in the past, were communal to a point of perfection. They
harvest palm fruits together. They farmed together. They harvested
their farms together.
They tackled problems together. And when colonialism arrived with the
new culture of formal education as a way of building the mind and
creating the work-force for the emerging political and economic
environment, Ndigbo in their various communities pooled resources to
train their handicapped but brilliant and resourceful sons and
daughters in overseas universities. Actually, Ndigbo elevated the
values of republicanism in their practice of Umunna - kindred group.
They practised a perfect aggregation of communal resources to attain
such societies which lived in contentment while it strengthened the
individual. It was a society which provided for the expression of the
individual vigour within the values rigidly pursued by the larger
society. What each civilized being aspired to attain was made
possible by the force of support and encouragement from the kindred.
These were for the good old days which grew from the civilisation
which impressed the Portuguese. But surprisingly, Ndigbo followed
this with an explosion of the individual clout which swamped the
age-old umunna. That was the arrival of the celebration of the
person, his wealth and his interests without regard for the community
and the attendant values.
This individual success, we regret to say had been hardly applied as
a force for intervention, let alone have it work in the
interrelationship of the more global Igbo with others in the wider
society.
So, while the old but glamorous Sokoto empire approached the Nigeria
national arena in its stately disposition and while the Yoruba
retreated to dig up the indices of their previously bartered Oduduwa
nation so as to approach with a collective force of pedigree, Ndigbo,
in their enormous population had advanced as school children let
loose on a rolling football in a disorderly pitch.
Naturally, a group as scattered and lacking in focus as school
children would not have a pitch captain. It would not have focus and
it would not have force.
Rather it will have the vibration of presence which can be handled
without effort. In that form, Ndigbo could not organise their
strength, their culture, their ideas and their needs. They just
stormed the arena to create a cacophony which had worked against
them.
Yet, we know that as their seeming failure in nation building looked,
Ndigbo had proven to be highly regenerative and resilient in their
survival of their handicap. That way, they had produced leaders who
had gathered the scattering children and provided a direction. But
even as the great Zik, the indubitable Michael Okpara and the others
had knocked their kind together to take up a more cohesive role in
the exercise in nation building, we have had our kind fly off the
handle and cause damage to our collective goal. To that effect, what
may be more gainfully argued was the lack of capacity to exhibit
those fine points in intergroup relations. They call it diplomacy and
such has been the point made from the outside, that had Ndigbo been
deft diplomats, Nigeria would have been on their fingertips.
Well, one thing we know about the craft of running a state and
balancing the factors therein draws from long practice- learned and
inherent. In fact, it is mostly learned as we know that in the modern
world, the evolving diplomacy has been learned and hardly inherited.
But learned or inherent, the failure of Ndigbo to exercise caution or
restraint in utterances have seriously hurt their inclusion as
factors in Nigeria.
Perhaps, we have ingrained a tactless culture and so have to fall a
victim of the view of Robert Greene in the art of power. He said that
"speaking your mind, giving them the piece of your tongue and letting
everybody know how you feel about it,' constitute the greatest
impediment on the road to power".
Ndigbo have immense courage, they have unbeatable talents, they are
risk-takers and this partly explains their successes. The individuals
have been propelled in ways unprecedented but at the same time
emerging confidence on the basis of material accomplishment has
eroded the erstwhile culture of discretion. That heavy material
accomplishment which has hardly come with the refinement of character
gives vent to the impudent refraint: aku n'esi obi ike. Wealth is the
confidence of the man. That is the root of our growing penchant for
negating the values on which our forebears built character, organized
themselves and perpetuated Ndigbo.
Today, as the individual and in ever-readiness to assert ourselves or
to hold on to our position we growl and thunder that Nku di na mba
n'eghere mba nri.
With every man at his own helm, insisting that he is the best that
has emerged, we erode our capacity for organization and so get easily
described as the motley without leadership and without direction.
While it is easy to detect, concede to and prop the best hunter of
every area of Nigeria, Ndigbo seek to be seen to have too many best
hunters. Such hunters are like pockets of warriors or warlords who
would not concede to any other. Basically, on this, springs the claim
that "ka odi be onye k'odi be ibeya"- as it is in a man's home so it
is in another man's. We have even furthered this in our claim that
Ubochi a muru dike na mba k'amuru ibeya- so to say, as a warrior
rises in your place, so one also rises in mine. Perhaps, this had
informed the tactless refrain "e si be gi e je be onye"? As we say in
diffidence and total disregard for the view of others.
Well, ladies and gentlemen, the Igbo thought pattern cannot be said
to be dominated by such disdain for control or permanent drive at
asserting superiority.
It is neither in a permanent drive to wriggle out of dominance as
these acts in verbal diffidence. But I see in them a challenge in
working our ways through the maze of a multi-ethnic configuration as
Nigeria.
The reality is that the man of the modern world must learn to
appreciate the other, his sensibilities, his fears and his
motivation. These must have informed the strong admonition of the
African-American writer, William E.B. du Bois in the work "The Souls
of the Black Folk." He said that "there is ever the feeling over this
two-ness- an American, a Negro; two souls, two thoughts, two
unreconciled strivings; two warring ideals in one dark body, whose
dogged strength alone keeps it from being torn asunder.
So, invariably there is an Igbo, a Nigerian, two souls mmuo n'abo
n'ofu onye, two thoughts uzo echiche abuo, two irreconcilable
strivings nkukuorita achomacho, two warring ideals in one dark body
nkukorita elulo n'ime ofu onye, whose dogged strength alone keeps it
from being torn asunder eji obi na ike dimkpa buru.
Even our own Prof. Alexander Animalu of the University of Nigeria,
Nsukka, brought it home in the 1990 edition of the Ahiajoku lecture.
"A Nigerian, an Igbo, these are the two dimensions of the problem of
Igbo identity." Gentlemen, my taking this path does not mean that I
have advocated a timorous Igbo or a culture of the dumb. I have only
said that we now have a tradition of misinterpretation. Misunderstood
in all we have said, misunderstood in how we have acted, but barely
appreciated in what we have avoided to say. Shall we now heighten our
uncertainty in utterances which defy cohesion. Certainly, a modern
state is not run barely on the mastery of restraint in expressions,
important though it is.
Indeed, the ultimate challenge of building a nation had confronted
the founding fathers of Nigeria. One way or the other, they had made
a nation but they had at the same time left a neck-breaking challenge
to us in the area of groupings, social categories and tending
interests. In the pursuit of what is possible we have had our
upsetting moments. We surely have had some sparks of glory; but we
have had immense frustrations and the temptation to throw away the
baby with the bathtub, yet, we would not run from the possibilities
of a nation in upward political, social and economic swing.
Now that we know, as Nigerians, as Ndigbo, as Yorubas, as Hausas, as
Fulanis, as Niger Deltans; shall we taunt and stultify the
intermingling, cross cultural and mercantilist dexterity of Ndigbo?
Shall we deride and seek to obliterate the aristocracy, deft
political and stately disposition of the Fulani? Shall we quake and
thunder in revulsion of our inability to play the vociferous and
literary Yoruba? And shall we swoon and growl over the political
reality of the effusive minority interests which have yet to settle
the appropriate status of the emerging nation?
For Ndigbo, it is not so much a matter for easy answers to put aside
these fundamental questions. It is not a matter of deciding that
their capacity to cross geographical boundaries, cultural barriers
and lift goods and services for individual economic advancement,
would aid the building of our nation.
Indeed, in the inter-ethnic sphere, Ndigbo appear to have discharged
themselves creditably. They have left their homes in their millions
to work and perpetuate industry in their new places of domicile. They
have exploded rocks, torn down forests, filled mangrove swamps and
erected structures at the riverside. In parts of Nigeria where modern
goods would not have ordinarily reached, Ndigbo had hauled containers
to the spots, even against the most pernicious of odds.
Beyond this, they have perfected the celebration of their host's
culture sometimes to the detriment of their own culture. Consciously
and unconsciously, they have given fillip to cultures, which were
hitherto on the verge of extinction and thus extended the beauty of
Nigeria's variety.
In the economic sphere, Ndigbo have exhibited the resilience of the
archetypal elasticity of a race.
From coast to desert, they have extended the wealth of nations to the
reach of all. They have made others to enjoy the gains of oneness of
Nigeria at their doorstep. The dexterity of Igbo profile in
entrepreneurship has its lifting in the composite nature of it. As
they are good in buying and selling, they are leaders in
transport/haulaging. As they are good in technology, they are masters
in the rising small and medium-scale enterprises.
In fact, at the individual level, Ndigbo have proven the workability
of Nigeria.
Yet, our happiness with the Igbo scale and achievements in the area
of the economy has not beefed up my analysis in any way to reach a
conclusion for a state in real upward swing.
Before I proceed to lead you into my doubts and apprehension, let us
stop, once, to assume that Nigeria is a state made of only Ndigbo. If
that were to be true, let us now pose the question of social, cum
political organization. Are Ndigbo truly organized even in their
efficiency and industry of the individual?
I have no answer to the question. I will only proceed to raise
another question. Would Nigeria run as a social and political state
if every facet of life is in the hands of Ndigbo as they carry
themselves in Nigeria today?
Again I have no immediate answer. But if we take back this issue to
the question of the character of group and the drive of the
individual, we begin to see that Ndigbo may not have acted in proper
deployment of their greatest weapon. To our knowledge, shops have
risen in various parts of Nigeria; dilapidated structures have been
bought and refitted to compete in architectural magnificence with any
of their kind in the world. More young and active population have
poured out of the Igbo villages. Supposed leaders have entered
individual deals, even against erstwhile group aspiration; but the
question of collective enterprise has not been raised. The whole
thing suited Eader Mangrof's symphony of brutal individualism. Oke
n'ohia, ngwere n'uzo- every man to himself- as we say in our
parlance.
Of course, we still have our decidedly diffident expression that no
true Igbo waits to be led by the other- o nweghi onye na ezi nwanneya
uzo, but I have the suspicion that we may have fallen to what
philosopher Michael Hoover called, an unpardonable level of
entrepreneurial-leadership complacence. Sure, entrepreneurship is
productivity. It is energy and it is perseverance. But at the same
time it is leadership. The entrepreneur creates wealth, jobs,
economic direction and in this modern society, political-way-forward.
It is also built on the capacity to collate, aggregate and harmonize.
I am so afraid that the highly industrious Igbo have exhibited all
save the challenge of leadership. This has easily shown in their
political adventure which is as complacent as their leadership in
entrepreneurship.
