Indeed our political leadership is akin to a complacent society's
approach to daily life. Ihe nkwu gbatara si n'uba. Whatever the
tapper gets, let us drink.
The impression of the Portuguese in 1591 was not a Hobbesian state
where each man was to himself and the deity to all. They had found an
elaborately organized society with the attendant flowering of the
civilisation of the time. As it was not a political oke n'ohia,
ngwere n'uzo, then it could not have been complacent as the present
day ihe nkwu gbabara si n'uba. Indeed, the society they met which
gave birth to generations of our time could not have imagined the
complacence which would have endangered them among their warlike
neighbours. Granted that there are hardly such wolfish neighbours as
they possibly were then, and taking in the fact that the Igbo society
of today is more interdependent than independent, the rudderless
complacence of our time is hard to explain.
Many have argued that the root cause of the growing political
individualism was the repeated creation of states and local
government areas. They hold the view that as these new centres of
political expression and administration take their toll on the
individual, then the semblance of collective character-trait will
snap and each man goes for himself. It is not for me now to say that
we have a good point in this. It is not also for me to say, it is all
missing the point. I can only say that as the Igbo areas have been
split, so other areas of Nigeria have been split, even more than in
Igbo land. I can also say that it appears that there is hardly any
direct or indirect link between political complacence and
multi-political units.
For us to duly appreciate the poverty of Igbo political vigilance
against the hair-tinging consciousness of others we can begin to take
in the import of the welter of Igbo enterprises across the nation.
As hinted above, the people have been described as the most vibrant
and commercially (and profit-driven) focused of black Africans. Their
vibrancy has resounded in the individual and group and the presence
of the people, whether as individual or group has been appreciated.
Yet, the tragedy of lack of due entrepreneurial, politically
conscious leadership had left the vibrancy in industry unorganized.
As it was not collated, it was not channelled. Much same the
politically-lacking entrepreneurial leadership.
It is possible that as argued by some analysts, Ndigbo suffered
political regression due to the long military rule which we had over
the time. Indeed, Ndigbo had insignificant presence in the military
and so had acted as mere onlookers as the soldiers of other ethnic
extraction ruled the country.
However, this is hardly enough excuse. In fact, such political
disadvantage ought to have been the ginger for the people to sit up.
Mind you, we are not here talking about sitting up on what you do not
have. We are talking about sitting up on the entrepreneurial
dexterity already developed. Such would have been pursued to the
extent of building reasonable and requisite political muscle which is
capable of reasonable intervention at the appropriate time.
Take for instance the preponderance of Igbo investments outside
Igboland. It is sometimes intimidating to appreciate the quantum and
value but when we compare this with the investment in Igboland- by
the same entrepreneurs- they pale to insignificance: nothing
actually.
Please, do understand that I am not in any way canvassing the
transfer of Igbo fortune to Igbo land.
I want to draw you into the spectre of heavy Igbo presence and
promotion of wealth, good neighbourliness and industrial development
in preponderant number in other parts of Nigeria without as much
attention to their areas. This has further intensified the departure
of the more productive force from Igboland and depletion of the
economy of the Igbo areas.
Lately, the democratic government of this part of Nigeria has
advanced the argument in Ndigbo Think Home. By this we have tried to
establish the necessary infrastructure for the take off of industrial
and commercial enterprises in the Igbo areas. This is primarily to
say that as our people invest elsewhere, they should invest in the
Igbo areas so that the development which they spread elsewhere is not
denied their kith and kin.
When I spoke at the Eastern Chambers of Commerce Forum in November
1999, I indicated that regional economic integration at the level of
the nine Eastern States was, and still remains, the valid way out of
our current low rate and poor level of development. Please permit me
to quote myself in the very words in which I articulated my vision on
that occasion: "...I take the view that this integration is
imperative and urgent.
It is in the context of this integration that state governments in
Eastern Nigeria would be able to consider the joint planning and
prosecution of key infrastructure projects such as roads, electric
power generation and education. I talk about education in a
discriminatory way, especially in the metaphorical.
The aggregation and entrepreneurial leadership, which we seek, would
also enable us to plan jointly for development projects, which may
nevertheless be prosecuted individually. It is in this synergy that
we would be able to provide the necessary enabling environment for
robust private-sector presence and growth in the Igbo areas of
Nigeria within the shortest possible time. It is also this synergy
that would offer us the critical mass, which would sustain projects
that, if embarked upon on an individual basis, may not be
sustainable." What I did not do on that occasion was to attempt to
suggest how this idea of regional integration could be actualized as
well as how the cooperative states and the private sector should work
to achieve the essence of the arrangement.
In the area of economy in this topic, this is what I attempt to do
today.
I have a vision of an Eastern Economic Region, which would operate on
the basis of a development plan agreed jointly by the cooperating
states and key representatives of the private sector, with a
trans-regional body to be called the Eastern Nigeria Development
Coordinating Commission as the planning, coordinating and or
implementing organ. If we use South East Nigeria as our focus, then
the organ would be the South East Development Coordinating
Commission.
The structure of the regional organ, which would be fashioned out by
a body of experts, with inputs from the cooperating governments and
key representatives of the private sector, would be reduced into an
agreement between the cooperating states and equally established by
law passed by the legislature of every such state.
The critical success factor in this endeavour would lie in the
quality of direction and coordination offered by the commission. The
structure of the commission would nevertheless be such as would
guarantee adequate consultation with all stakeholders aimed at
healthy conscious building. It is only a competent and independent
commission that can give us a well-articulated, simple, focused,
targeted and realistic plan. It is also such a commission that would
guarantee the professional and strict implementation of the plan as
agreed, with minimum interference from the subjectivism of our
current level and brand of politics which is disastrously hinged on
the individual. The cooperating states should be willing to give up
some portions of their competence to the commission, especially with
respect to matters that fall within the framework of the regional
development plan.
I have a vision of a region in which development and economic
expansion activities would be focused and targeted in the medium to
long term. Given the limitations of available resources and the
imperatives of globalization, we must pick and choose critical areas,
on which we must focus and target our investments; and this I believe
should apply to both public-sector spending and private-sector
investments.
I also have a vision of a region in which major centres in each
cooperating state would be designated and promoted as an economic
centre of excellence for specific products and services, as far as
practicable, along the perimeter of a big economic circle, clearly
marked out and connected by a focused developed belt way.
Infrastructurally, there are existing road network starting from
Agbor to Onitsha, Enugu to Abakaliki (cutting through Abba-Omege and
Ugep) to Calabar, to Uyo, to Eket, to Port Harcourt, to Yenagoa; and
from the middle, is the dual carriageway from Enugu to Port Harcourt.
Then, for us to begin to establish centres for specific areas of
entrepreneurial activities we have to take in the facilities
available and those which can be improved upon.
For example, in manufacturing and distributive trading activities, we
can designate Port Harcourt as the centre for oil and gas business;
we can designate Nnewi as the centre for automobile business; we can
designate Aba as the centre for textile and cloth making business; we
can designate Enugu as the centre for information technology business
and so forth and so on. In the area of agriculture, we can allocate
the production of specific cash and food crops to particular centres,
such as Cross River, rubber and pineapple; Rivers State, palm
produce; Ebonyi State, rice and yam, and so on and so forth.
A major support service or industry in this entire endeavour is
training and provision of quality manpower. It is also my view that
while the cooperating states may retain continued responsibility over
primary and secondary education, tertiary education should be planned
and provided at the regional level in line with the core requirements
of the regional development plan. What I advocate is the
transformation of existing state universities into respective centre
of excellence for specific areas of study such as medicine, basic
sciences, engineering, information technology, business management
and law.
Ultimately, the goal should be to develop a collegiate university
system, where existing state universities would be colleges of one
university, with standardized entry and graduation requirements. It
is clear that as the private sector continues to take the upper hand
in the economy of our country, only candidates with competitive
skills would survive in the market place.
I emphasize training and education because following a terrifying
drop in education enrolment in the Igbo areas, we may soon face a
situation where we lack requisite professionals to manage our
enterprises.
The designation and specialization, which I propose would achieve two
main objectives. At the economic level it would make for
specialization and enable the targeted provision of necessary support
infrastructure for business which would reinforce the commitment of
each section of the Eastern areas to the process and sustainability
of the integration effort.
I make haste to note that we can only succeed in this development
business if, and only if, both the public sector and the private
sector understand their respective responsibilities and each group
makes a serious commitment to discharge its responsibilities.
Put simply, the role of the public sector is to ensure the existence
of good governance necessary to provide an enabling environment for
the private sector to thrive. Implicit in the notion of good
governance is the efficient provision of those public goods, which
are better produced and provided by the state. This is both by way of
the provision of physical infrastructure and social amenities and
services. The public sector should also work to ensure that the
wealth so created in the collaborative effort is distributed
equitably among the stakeholders.
Please permit me to itemize what the Igbo areas as a group should
bring to this partnership for development, namely: -The provision of
a good transport infrastructure (land, air and sea); -The provision
of regular energy supply; -Provision of good telecommunication
network; -The provision of reliable water supply systems; -The
provision of basic qualitative education as well as encouragement of
relevant specialized education and research at the tertiary level;
-The protection of life and personal property, supported with a sound
system of administration of justice; -The provision of access to
qualitative health-care, especially at the primary and secondary
levels; -The creation and promotion of such policy environment as
would enable the private sector to participate in the provision of
such public services and amenities, which are now capable of being
provided profitably and efficiently by the private sector; and -The
provision of relevant support systems by way of extension services
and technical and other support systems for small and medium scale
farmers and businesses.
The question might then be asked: how does this fit into a proper
politically conscious entrepreneurship capable of contributing to
nation building? It is possible that, as a matter of common regional
policy, Ndigbo in this cooperating arrangement would have
responsibility for funding and developing the portion of the economic
highway, which falls within their planned economic belt way. Where
existing highways are identified and incorporated into the regional
economic highway design, the joint effort may then be targeted at
either bringing the condition of the highway up to the desired
standard and or in establishing the key public facilities along the
highway such as industrial estates and markets.
The new initiative to bring about the accelerated development would
have the desired impact only if the private sector is commited to it.
First, members of the private sector and the political leadership
should begin to take sincere interest in reshaping the fortunes of
the region. Our brothers and sisters in business must also begin to
take active steps in developing our region. As the saying goes, you
cannot expect to see the rising sun by looking away from the East.
You must look to the East. The demonstration of confidence in the
economy of the region by our businessmen and women would elicit more
confidence in the region by other investors. And this interest should
start at the level of policy formulation so that the policies and
projects to be embarked upon by the public sector would reflect and
accommodate the concerns of the private sector. I also attempted to
accommodate this dimension of my proposal when I suggested that the
establishment of a commission must be the joint responsibility of
both the public and private sectors. This is what I call the
partnership for development. Without this partnership, the proposed
development enterprise would surely fail.
Consider for a moment what would happen if the public sector develops
the regional economic highway and industrial estates, and the private
sector refuses or fails to set up businesses therein.
The exact extent of the efficiency gains to be made from this synergy
and economy of scale is best left to our economists to work out. But
I am confident that our working together through the commission,
would achieve an impact more than three times the effect of our
current budgets, without having to spend additional funds.
In specific terms therefore, the private sector should: -Take active
part in the design, implementation and periodic review of the
region's development plan; -Mobilize private capital for the
establishment of businesses in the region; -Consider participating in
the provision of certain key infrastructure (such as electricity and
water supply) in partnership with the public sector; -Set up venture
capital funds for supporting young entrepreneurs or for the expansion
of small businesses in the region; -Work with the region's centres of
excellence to increase the pace and scope of targeted and
market-driven research and development activities in the region;
-Investment in manpower development at both the industry and firm
levels, especially in collaboration with the region's centres of
excellence; and -Contribute to funding of the public treasury by
paying appropriate taxes at minimum cost to the government.
I do not have any doubt that, given the entrepreneurial spirit and
drive in our people, what our businessmen and women require to unlock
and unleash their potential is the minimum- and the minimum- support
from the public sector in the provision of common and basic
infrastructure and a stable macroeconomic environment, as far as is
consistent with our level of development and our political
antecedents. Thus, if the support should exceed the minimum levels,
our region would represent a case study as an unimaginable model for
national development and growth.
But if I say this of the public sector and the individual private
operators, I will have to return to the political leadership which
must coordinate our transition from multifarious enterprises to the
one powered by organized private sector.
Actually, the challenge of the Igbo in building the Nigerian nation
cannot be tackled without having the commensurate deciding political
character capable of due intervention in the national communal arena.
Whereas the record performance of Ndigbo in other regions have so
gainfully propelled the nation Nigeria, it has created an unusual
economic and political imbalance which indeed erode the confidence of
the region's political leadership. To that effect, a conscious effort
must be advanced to have forthwith such lopsided private sector
presence in the region as the absence of such has done more harm than
good. I may sound immodest when I claim that Ndigbo form a greater
part of the engine room of this nation. I said boldly that if we
should embark on truly restructuring to achieve true federalism,
Ndigbo would have had the singular opportunity of making this nation.
The due regional economies that will be established will be driven to
perfection by Ndigbo. Our people will work the hi-tech. They will
provide the plant and auto parts. They will move people and goods to
the right directions. Perhaps, then, they would have woken up to
proper and meaningful intervention process in the nation. That way,
they will bargain and get paid in material and kind for their leading
contribution to the building of the nation. That way, we would have
confronted our challenge and built a nation for all. I join those who
say that with introduction of democracy, it shall come to pass.
To God be the Glory.
- Excerpts of an address by the Governor of Enugu State, Dr.
Chimaroke Nnamani, at the inaugural lecture of the Enugu Sports Club
recently.

Original_Search_Title: Gemade's Re-Election Battles
Headline: Nigeria: Gemade's Re-Election Battles: the Odds, the Strategies As the Leadership of the Ruling Peoples Democratic Party (pdp) Gets Set for Its National Convention in Early November, Wale Olaleye Unveils the Plot to Unseat the Incumbent National Chairman, Chief Barnabas Gemade And His Strategies for Survival
Body_Text:
The next national convention of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)
promises to be an interesting one not just for members of the party
alone but also for the generality of Nigerians who would be watching
the event closely as the outcome could decide where the pendulum
swings in the next general election between the party on one hand and
the other parties. It could also signify which direction Nigeria
would move after 2003. As the convention moves closer the shape of
what to expect is getting clearer and an epic battle for the national
Chairmanship is looking increasingly likely between the incumbent
Chief Barnabas Gemade and second Republic Minister of Communication,
Audu Ogbe
The convention which some members have dubbed the real convention
being the first to be conducted by an elected leadership of the party
could also be a test for Nigeria's democracy as it could showcase
Nigeria'scivilian government ability or lack of it to conduct a
general election successfully. This 2001 convention would be a
decider of many fates and a finisher of many aspirations. But beyond
the surface of the normal voting, characteristic of a convention,
stares many a surprise. From the seat of the National Chairman to the
least elected position in the party, awaits the biggest stun.The stun
is simply entrenched in some meticulously thought out plans to cut
short the dreams of many elected party office holders by not
returning them to office. This would be in apparent contrast with
what obtained at the November 1999 convention that brought the Gemade
executive to power. Even though tensions were high as candidates
scouted for offices at all cost, little was known about what were at
stake then. After all, new executives were just elected to chase
aside the appointed protem officers. Suffice to say that normal
political manouvering of wanting some candidates at the expense
others equally played out
The Plot Against Gemade
The arrow head of the plot to get Gemade out of the exalted
chairmanship post is believed to be no other man than Mr. Fix It
,himself. The man who perhaps, aside from President Olusegun Obasanjo
is the only one who can get things done both within the government
and the PDP. He is the Minister of Works and Housing, Chief Tony
Anenih. THISDAY investigation revealed that the entire game plan had
begun at the meeting allegedly called at the instance of Anenih. At
the meeting, it was further learnt, were the Vice President, some
members of the National Executive Committee (NEC) including some
governors of PDP controlled states. Anenih, sources said had called
the meeting to unveil to them, the purported decision from the top
that the Chairman, Chief Barnabas Gemade had not done enough since he
was elected national chairman two years ago to merit a second term in
office. In justifying this position, Anenih was also said to have
told them that the caucus that decided the fate of Gemade had also
resolved to field as the party's new boss, Audu Ogbe, second republic
Minister of Communication. Surprisingly Anenih did not have it as
envisaged as he encountered stiff opposition to the plot from most
members of the NEC and some of the state governors who were present.
And behold, the meeting ended in deadlock.
Allegations Against Gemade
Ironically, Anenih's apparent failure at getting these people behind
this projct was said to be largely due to the unconvincing reasons he
adduced for the plot. His allegations against Gemade was said to have
been dismissed as lacking substance and of no political weight. He
was quoted to have told the gathering that Gemade was becoming unduly
powerful and independent minded. He was further quoted to have
alleged that the PDP chairman had courted too much, the state
governors and was therefore becoming an institution. Beside these,
however he was said to have said nothing damning against the Chairman
that could possibly have been tenable to the meeting,hence those
present were not swayed by his arguments.
Gemade's Adamance
Apparently unpertubed, Gemade was said to have staked his neck that
come rain, come shine he would seek re-election. Even while Ogbe was
tipped as the annointed candidate, he was said to have insisted at
the meeting that he would test his popularity and electability before
the rank and file of the party at the convenion. At this stage a
source at the meeting said Gemade got more of the sympathy of some
governors and members of the NEC present They thereafter, THISDAY
gathered, kicked against the resolve to push aside Gemade basically
because of what it described as too much interference from the
government, specifically the Presidency. They further argued that
some of the problems plaguing the party, even at some state levels,
was because of the vested interest and interference the government
was having in the affairs of the party. They picked quarrel with the
Samuel Ogbemudia led-convention committee for releasing the
convention time table without having gone through the ratification of
the NEC. Such development, they described as insurbodination and
would not let go. And this perhaps, may have been responsible for the
slight alteration in the party's convention time-tableby the NEC at
its meeting in Abuja last Thursday .That meeting was said to have
been polarized by the chairmanship election as some of the state
governors who have been routing for Gemade, had under pressure,
supposedly from the Presidencysuccumbed o backing Ogbe .At the
meeting no fewer than 12 out of the 21 PDP governors may have changed
over to Ogbe. Their reason being that with Ogbe increasingly becoming
the favoured candidate of the Presidency, it would be politically
suicidal for them to oppose him knowing fully well that the
Presidency holds the knife and the yam so to speak in Nigeria.
Moreover majority if not all of them are interested in a second term
in office and the support of the Presidency would be very crucial to
their aspiration. Another development that could have swayed their
support for Ogbe was that PDP National Secretary, Okweisilezie Nwodo
may have finally accepted not to recontest but take up the offer of
Nigeria's High Commisioner to Zimbabwe believed to have been
offeredby Obasanjo. InNwodo's place Chief Vincent Ogbulafor is being
tipped and highly favoured. But these notwithstanding Gemade, THISDAY
gathered,was still insisting on giving Ogbe a good fight,even in
spite of the offer of a ministerial appointment made to him, which he
may eventually be persuaded to accept.
The Strength and Support of Gemade
In as much as it could not be ruled out that Gemade may have risen to
power on the strength of the Presidency support, there is also the
tendency that the incumbency factor which he may have wielded while
in office remain a useful tool to him as his faithfulls moves round
the party members to garner support for him. These loyalists have
begun serious campaign to thwart all the plans against his
re-election. This was sequel to the decisions reached at the end of
their caucus meeting. A competent party source who also attended the
meeting gave myriad reasons why the party boss would be re-elected.
He cited amongst several other reasons, the fact that Gemade had
brought the party to a lime light and that through him, a lot of
personalities have begun to join the party. He was instrumental in
luring two prominent Senators and a member of the House of
Representatives from the rival Alliance for Democracy party (AD),
into the PDP. Similarly he influenced prominent politician, Pa
Anthony Enahoro into teaming up with the party as well as getting one
time Foreign Affair Minister Chief Tom Ikimi from the rival All
Peoples Party (APP) to come on board.Also his not belonging to any of
the groupswithin the pdp has enabled him to cut across members from
all the sides building bridges . The source maintained that any
attempt to frustrate Gemade's re-election could in turn spell doom
for the party. The source which cited continuity as another major
factor for the man to be re-elected, reasoned that the task of
repositioning the party which Gemade had started would only
materialise if a stretch of sufficient time is allowed for such
planning and execution of innovations that his era had brought into
the party politics.
The Presidency As License
Even as the inter-play of interest continues to take its toll on the
major players, the only joker and guarantor of position that is being
used is the consent of the Presidency. And this has been used
severally used in the past even without its knowledge and consent for
selfish and personal reasons. Now, the name had become prominent in
this chairmanship struggle. And the way the anti-Gemade group had
been using the name may have confirmed the belief that the Presidency
was practically sponsoring some of the issues that are now
contentious in the party. But this was ironically being done as if
the Chairman did not know or have a one-on-one relationship with the
President. Gemade who seemed not perturbed by the trends had
therefore resolved to jettison whatever threat and put to test, his
credibility to win election, the source further hinted.
...And the Probable Consequences
Certainly, all the interplay of interest and muscle flexing could not
come and take its leave without leaving behind marks, either
positively or negatively.That the PDP is about the only party with
some seeming credibility is more of a mere fallacy now. The three
political parties are no better than one another. The difference
could be that the PDP at all times had tried to manage its crisis
such that it did not degenerate into a lousy affair, though this has
failed often times. But with the convention approaching faster and
the events still trailing, the out-come could be a surprising
misfortune that could spin around any likely success. If the party
does not put its house together and design further plans towards next
election, the November convention could surprisingly vitiate whatever
dreams it may have been nurturing of winning the next round of
elections. All other parties that are today seen as sleeping may
after all be working on their own success story to push the PDP out
of power.

Original_Search_Title: Igbo Day As Cultural Outing
Headline: Nigeria: Igbo Day As Cultural Outing
Body_Text:
Lagos — On September 29, 2001, Ndigbo Marked Their Day With Funfair. There Were
Gatherings of the Igbos in Parts of the Country Including Lagos
State. Fidelis Njoku Who Witnessed One of Such Gatherings Sponsored By the
Pan Ndigbo Foundation, Kosofe Chapter in Mende, Maryland, Lagos Relives the
Artistic And Cultural Relevance of the Igbo Day
Igbo Day celebrated recently worldwide means different things to
different individuals and groups. For politicians, Igbo Day is more of a day
of political rallies for the Igbo than anything else. For social elements,
it is a day that provides an opportunity for the highest level of
interaction and socialisation among the Igbo and their admirers. For the
economists, it is a great opportunity for fund raising to prosecute the Igbo
cause. To this reporter, the cultural value of Igbo Day is of primary focus
and concern.
This year, the Igbo Day was marked worldwide in different locations in
the country and in the diaspora. Among the places the day was observed in
parts of the country was Mende, Maryland, Lagos State.
The Mende celebration was organised and funded by an Igbo socio-cultural
cum political organisation called Pan Ndigbo Foundation (NPF), Kosofe Local
Government Council Chapter. The venue was Mende Hall playground and the date
was September 29, 2001.
The acceptable attire for the celebration was the Igbo traditional
native dress comprising a long robe of Isi-agu dress upon a neatly tailored
pair of trousers that fits the colour of the robe with red cap to match. The
title holders who feature in such occasions are expected to appear in their
regalia made up of expensive traditional dress with a red cap decorated with
white feathers of eagles, as well as beads. In those days, the number of
feathers put on a man's cap depicted the number of titles he held, but it
doesn't necessarily follow these days.
Presently, it is a question of choice. Some title holders, especially
the highly educated ones don't put any feather at all on their caps even
though such titled elites have in their kitty several titles from different
traditional rulers across the country which they got while in public
service. But the basic thing is that every titled Igbo man or woman making a
public appearance on occasions as Igbo Day is expected to wear this cultural
and traditional Igbo attire otherwise the defaulter may not be given full
regards and honours at such occasions. This aspect of the Igbo culture was
fairly observed at the Mende Igbo Day. It is on record that the Igbo have
high regard for kolanuts. There are unavoidable rituals ranging from the
order of presentation of the kolanuts among communities or states of Igbo
extraction present at a given ceremony or meeting, the blessing of the
kolanuts, the sharing and distributions of them. These are rites that must
be strictly observed. Anyone who breaches or makes a mistake about them is
usually treated with contempt and regarded as a one who is not a true Igbo
son. Such an error is a very big offence punishable by monetary fine of the
person, whether titled or not. Of course, it is an open secret that women,
no matter their social standings in the land do not partake in the
traditional kola presentation and sharing process in any gathering involving
men in Igbo land. This vital and die-hard aspect of the Igbo culture was
equally, neatly observed at the Igbo Day held in Mende, Maryland.
Then, the main thrust of the occasion was the New Yam Festival, the most
common and respected culture in Igboland right from the days of yore till
date. Pan Ndigbo Foundation, Kosofe Chapter, the organisers of the
celebration made it clear that they wished to use the occasion to mark the
New Yam Festival. In other words, the Igbo community in Kosofe Local
Government Area used the Day to observe the all important New Yam Festival
culture.
As Igbo tradition and customs demand, the organisers roasted some tubers
of yam at the venue of the occasion. The roasted yams were distributed among
the guests along with fresh palm oil mixed with fresh 'ground' pepper. Such
yams cannot be cooked but only roasted and eaten with only palm oil. This
was appropriately done at the Mende Igbo Day as a major item in the
programme of the celebration.
Therefore, pounded yam with Egusi soup only as required by Igbo custom
was also served to the guests, especially, the high table guests. Other
popular Igbo dishes like Ugba (African salad) were handy and were
served. Not left out was palmwine, the most popular drink in Igboland.
Although the Igbo Day was celebrated at Mende and other parts of the
state which are thousands of kilometres away from Igboland, these
traditional dishes and delicacies the Igbo are identified with were prepared
and served at the occasion irrespective of their high cost implications here
in Lagos State, just to ensure that the cultural and traditional relevance
of the celebration was not sacrificed on the altar of observing the Igbo Day
outside Igboland.
In a recent exclusive chat with Eze-Igbo, Alimoso, Eze (Dr.) Emmanuel
Eze Okolie (Abiama) this writer gathered that in the years past the New Yam
Festival was associated with a traditional belief that barren women and
newly married women who tasted the pieces of the yam roasted in public arena
to mark the festival would bear children of their choice. On account of this
belief system associated with the new yam festival, women who desired the
fruits of the womb used to look up to the Yam Festival Day as an opportunity
to receive the blessing of children from the god of yam which was believed
to be the god of procreation. As the Eze-Igbo, Alimoso, Eze Okolie rightly
stated, this aspect of the festival tends to be affected by western and
religious influences. Even where this belief is still upheld, perhaps among
the die-hard traditionalists in Igboland it can hardly be as effective and
result-yielding as in the time of Igbo ancestors.  Definitely modernism with
its attendant civilization has touched all traditional belief systems across
races and nations including the Igbo race.
There is also this unique aspect of the Igbo Day, the display of Igbo
cultural arts such as traditional Igbo music and acrobatic dances.  For
instance, non-Igbo music whether modern or traditional are usually not
tolerated during Igbo Day celebrations. This aspect of the Igbo custom and
tradition was maintained to the letter at the recent Igbo Day celebrated at
Mende, Maryland.
Where such an event takes place in Igboland, all manner of traditional
dances as well as masquerades, women dances, "abigho' dance, "ekpe" dance,
"abigolo" dance and the popular Ohafia war dance and a host of others do
feature. But due to distance, not all these traditional dances associated
with the New Yam Festival and Igbo Day could feature at the Igbo Day marked
at Mende, Maryland. However, there was a peculiar Igbo traditional dance
that gave warmth and traditional touch to the Mende, Igbo Day.
Everything about the dance was traditional. The attire of the dancers,
the dance instruments and the music itself were tailored to showcase a
typical Igbo traditional dance. Moreso, the acrobatic nature of the dance
coupled with the artistry displayed by the dancers and the drummers held the
guests spellbound. This traditional dance called "igba-ndi-eze" usually has
girls as dancers and young men as drummers. They usually appear in dazzling
uniform and dance attires of choice. Besides, the dancers at times go extra
miles to decorate their bodies with whitish, reddish and other suitable
colours to make their appearance captivating and exciting. The dancers that
featured at the Mende Igbo Day held recently brought to the fore all these
vital cultural angles to the Igbo Day to the admiration of the guests which
cut across ethnic divides, among whom was the Baale of Mende.

Original_Search_Title: Ghanaian Lizards for Europe
Headline: Ghanaian Lizards for Europe
Body_Text:
Non-traditional exports to many Ghanaians connotes food crops like yams, handicrafts and a host of others. The list however goes beyond these as it includes reptiles like snakes, poisonous and non-poisonous alike, scorpions, wall geckos, the agama lizard and many others.
For twenty five years, this is what Mr. Jacobs Oguns' company, Oguns & Company Ltd. has been engaged in, with the support of his wife and other staff.
From his Kotobabi office he executes orders from countries like Malaysia, Japan, Belgium and South Africa. The reptiles are supplied to his outfit by trappers who are registered with the Department of Game and Wildlife. He also relies on people who rear some species of the python.
Although the business has been sailing smoothly, Jacob like other exporters wants government to help the sub sector with funds so that they can increase their output. Servicing of orders is sometimes rather difficult because of this shortage, he says.
He belongs to the Game and Wildlife Exporters Association which he said is beneficial because through it the voices of members are heard and attempts are made to solve their problems.
On other problems his company experiences, apart from the dearth of funds, he mentioned the perfidiousness of some importers abroad who overstate the number of dead animals on arrival. The animals must arrive alive for payment. He added that because exporters do not have representatives in the importing countries, reptile exporters are at the mercy of the importers.
The poisonous snakes are used in the manufacture of anti-snake serums. A few years ago some pharmaceutical manufacturing companies in Europe complained about the shortage of some species of poisonous snakes from Ghana for which they wanted suppliers.
Reptiles are also required for research in laboratories and for other educational purposes. Zoos too are outlets for some of them. Reptiles like snakes, scorpions and the agama lizard are certainly not on the endangered list and so the activities of the company are not infracting on any international wildlife conservation law.
His company has mastered the art of packaging the reptiles for export such that the death rate is reduced to the barest minimum.
He has nice words for personnel of CEPS who are part of the chain of procedures his export consignments go through at the airport. He describes them as cooperative.

Original_Search_Title: Mastering Agric Technologies
Headline: Nigeria: Mastering Agric Technologies
Body_Text:
With the help of International Institute of Tropical Agriculture in   Ibadan  , Nigerians are improving the yield of their crops and dealing more effectively with pests and bugs
International Institute of Tropical Agriculture, IITA, Ibadan and International Livestock Research Institute, ILRI, also in Ibadan is helping Nigeria to improve its agricultural produce and livestock by developing and improving maize, cassava, plantain, cowpea and soyabean production, thereby increasing yield stability in Nigeria. A Food and Agricultural Organisation, FAO, statistics shows that maize production has improved drastically in the country as average yields increased by 50 percent from 1,846 kilogrammes per hectare in 1996/97 to 2,763 kilogrammes per hectare in 1998/99.
Lucas Brader, the director-general of IITA told Newswatch in   Ibadan  , that the institute's achievement on the crop was based on series of studies with a special focus on developing high-yielding open pollinated varieties with resistance to crop-ravaging diseases. "IITA developed integrated pest management programme incorporating the use of resistant varieties combined with practices such as early planting, chemical seed treatment in laboratories, and good farm sanitation by removing infected plants, to boost maize production in the country," he explained.
A contributing factor to maize improvement was the institute's ability to work with local farmers to saturate some areas with mildew-resistant variety as well as make fertilisers available to them. In   Oyo  State  , where downy mildew was causing serious losses each year, Newswatch learnt that nine villages were initially selected. Three farmers from each village were given the resistant seed, organic fertilisers, and guidance on how to produce the seeds. By the third year, 625 farmers in 159 villages were already producing resistant seed varieties. Last year, between 23 and 29 varieties of maize had so far been released to both the private and public sectors respectively.
The institute has similarly helped the nation to improve cassava production. Annual production increased from between seven and eight million tonnes in the early 1960s to more than 30 million tonnes in the late 1990s in the country.   Nigeria  is now the largest cassava producing country in the world. The gain made through cassava research alone is valued at $500 million per year.
Taye Babaleye, head, public affairs  department of the institute, told Newswatch in   Ibadan  that scientists in IITA researched means of increasing cassava yields through the development of disease-resistant higher yielding varieties in the 1970. The scientists discovered that the most widespread and economically damaging cassava disease were cassava mosaic disease, CMD, and cassava bacterial blight, CBB. CMD is caused in  Africa by two geminiviruses transmitted by the ubiquitous whitefly, bemisia tabaci, causing a yield reduction estimated at $2 billion per year. CBB which was first reported in   Nigeria  in 1972, is caused by a bacteria and results in complete loss of leaves and death of plants. It was estimated that CBB caused losses worth $77 million in eastern   Nigeria  in 1973.
"Breeding for resistance to these diseases began at IITA . Scientists started work with a resistant genotype obtained from interspecific hybridisation in  East Africa which was found to also have resistance to CBB. Selected germplasm was also collected from  Latin America ,  Asia , and  East Africa along with local varieties from within   Nigeria  . This scientific work resulted in several elite genotype that had resistance to CMD and CBB as well as high stable yields and good consumer acceptability," Babaleye said. IITA is exploring its success to develop more cassava varieties with improved characteristics for  Africa 's agro-ecologies. Between 1970 and 1998, 15 varieties of cassava had been released to farmers in   Nigeria  .
Apart from the development of improved varieties, the most spectacular result achieved on this crop by agricultural research was the biological control of the cassava mealbug. IITA discovered a parasite in  Latin America for the control of the mealbug. Tests released were carried out in southern   Nigeria  from 1981 onwards and within three years the mealbug was reduced to insignificance.
Newswatch gathered that if there is absence of the improved widely grown disease resistant varieties or failure in mealbug control, there would be a reduction of the current cassava production in   Nigeria  by 50 percent. This means a reduction in production of 15 million tonnes of fresh cassava, or about five million tonnes of dry cassava. This quantity is said to be enough to meet the calorie requirements of 25 million people, one quarter of the Nigerian population.
Statistics shows that cowpea production has also seen significant improvement in   Nigeria  since 1980s. The area cultivated has varied over the years with a peak of 3.7 million hectares from 1997-1999. The yield which used to be from 500,000 to 800,000 tonnes in 1980s per year has increased to more than two million tonnes in recent years.
Brader attributed the improvement to IITA research systems. He said that "IITA started to evaluate improved cowpea varieties in the dry season for this purpose at Wudil and Kadawa from 1991 in collaboration with the Hadejia Jama'are River Basin Authority and the Kano Agricultural and Rural Development Authority." Some of the cowpea varieties yielded up to 1.9 tonnes per hectare grain over three tonnes per hectare fodder.
But losses caused by pest species such as flower thrips, maruca pod borer and pod sucking bugs have continued to be high. Efforts to develop resistant varieties to these particular pests through traditional breeding have failed. The director-general said that "biological control options are now studied for flower thrips and the development of transgenic cowpeas that might offer opportunities to the control of maruca pod borer and pod sucking bugs."
Soyabean has been improved upon as a major food and industrial crop in   Nigeria  . The institute adopted newly developed varieties with high yield, good storage, inexpensive and imported rhizobium which could be grown profitably by small scale farmers. Between 1991 and 1998 the statistics of area harvested in   Nigeria  increased by 16 percent reaching 543,000 hectares. Total production also increased from 145,000 to 361,000 tonnes or 149 percent increases in yield per hectare.
IITA has also trained a total of 47,000 people in the production and potential utilisation of soyabean in their family diet. The nutritional status of children had improved in soyabean producing and using households.
The institute admitted that it has not been able to achieve much in areas of yam and plantain production in   Nigeria  . Joseph Jimoh Olobashola, headquarters' farm manager, attributed this to the vulnerable nature of the crops to diseases and pests like tiny-worm like nematodes. The yam tuber is also affected by previous harvest and, cut as planting material, could propagate the disease from one year to another while the suckers that emerge at the base of the mother plant of plantain help in the spread of nematodes leading to loss in the field.
Scientists are now investigating the ecology of the nematodes, including the traditional preventive measures, such as soaking yams in a solution of wood ash. They believe it would help to evaluate yams and plantains and identify those with higher resistance to nematodes.
On livestock, Timothy Olalekan Williams, project manager, market-oriented Livestock Systems in  West Africa , told Newswatch in   Ibadan  that ILRI has been conducting research and training activities in   Nigeria  to alleviate the severe problems of animal agriculture in a holistic way - tackling constraints of both production and health.
Newswatch learnt ILRI developed what it called Strategy 2010 to address new opportunities arising from increasing demand for livestock products in order to improve the welfare of poor livestock keepers while protecting the environment. The plan is aimed at linking livestock production systems in the semi-arid range lands with increased feed production opportunities in the sub-humid zone and market outlets in the urban consumption centres in humid zone.
The goal is to encourage resource poor farmers and livestock keepers to engage in sustainable, market-oriented livestock production as a means of increasing incomes thereby alleviate poverty and improve food security while protecting the environment.
Williams explained that "the institute in a collaborative effort with National Livestock Projects Division, NLPD, developed "fodder banks" to help alleviate the problem of dry season feed shortage for livestock keepers. A recent assessment study showed that total net benefits to society that had accrued up to 1997 from the adoption of fodder banks in Nigeria amounted to $16.5 million in the second livestock development project to support project related research activities."
ILRI also has four other major products, like alley farming, PPR vaccination, nationwide livestock census involving aerial survey and ground truthing and improvements in peri-urban dairy production systems. The institute has contributed to removing the myths surrounding the Fulani cattle rearers by bringing them into the mainstream of livestock research and development in the country.
Newswatch also learnt that ILRI in conjunction with Kano Agricultural Development Programme, ADP, is involved in the area of nutrient management involving participatory testing of best-bet-technologies that include improved varieties of sorghum and dual-purpose cowpea, feeding of small ruminants with crop by-products from the agronomic trials and return manure from these animals to fields for soil fertility.

Original_Search_Title: Nnamani Advocates Establishment of Eastern Economic Bloc
Headline: Nigeria: Nnamani Advocates Establishment of Eastern Economic Bloc
Body_Text:
Governor Chimaroke Nnamani of Enugu State has advocated the establishment of
an Eastern Economic Bloc aimed at developing the region jointly by the nine
eastern states and the private sector.
Speaking at the inaugural edition of the annual lecture series organised by
the Enugu Sport Club on Monday in Enugu, Nnamani said that the idea was his
vision of a regional economic integration at the level of the Eastern states
comprising Abia, Anambra, Akwa Ibom, Bayelsa, Ebonyi, Enugu, Cross River,
Rivers and Delta.
The governor said that the trans-regional body, if well articulated, should
be called the "eastern Nigeria Development Co-ordinating Commission (ENDCC)
"as the planning, coordinating, and implementing organ."
He explained that the structure of the regional organ, which would be
fashioned out by a body of experts, with input from co-operating governments
and key representatives of the private sector, would be reduced to an
agreement between the co-operating states and equally established by law
passed by the legislatures of such states.
Nnamani said that necessary facilities have already been put in place,
adding that there is existing road networks from
Agbor-Onitsha-Enugu-Abakaliki to Calabar-Uyo-Eket-Port Harcourt-Yenegoa.
He also proposed the establishment of specific area of entrepreneurial
activities, designating Port Harcourt as the centre for oil and gas, and
Nnewi for automobile, while Aba would be the centre for textile and
cloth-making.
"We can designate Enugu as the centre for information technology, while in
the area of agriculture, we can allocate the production of specific cash and
food crops to particular centres, such as Cross River, Rubber and Pineapple;
Rivers, Palm produce; and Ebonyi, Rice and Yam," he added.
Other areas the governor advocated include the transformation of existing
state universities into centrer of excellence for specific areas of study,
with a view to developing a collegiate university system.
"We can only succeed in this development business, if both the public and
the private sectors understand their respective responsibilities and each
group makes a serious commitment to discharge those responsibilities,"
Nnamani noted.

Original_Search_Title: FJC Accuses Opposition of Sinister Plans
Headline: Gambia: FJC Accuses Opposition of Sinister Plans
Body_Text:
The director of press and public relations at State House, Mrs.
Fatoumata Jahumpa Ceesay has divulged plans,reportedly
concocted by the opposition in the run up to the October Presidential
election.
Speaking recently at a rally organised by the "Gikko Nyima" group on
Tobaco Road Banjul, Mrs. Jahumpha said the opposition had already
"succumbed," accusing them of using unscrupulous means to "woo APRC
stalwarts". "Even me, they (the opposition) have assigned the
Independent newspaper to ask me if President Jammeh had sacked me, so
that I would be angry and call it a quit with the APRC and join
them," she revealed.
She accused the opposition of instigating or
influencing some youths to hang around the British Embassy in
the quest for visas. She charged that this was in most cases motivated
by the opposition's desire to paint a negative image of the country by
creating the impression that a state of insecurity or panic loomed
over the country ahead of the election. "We the greater percentage of
the Gambian population (the women), are calling on the British
Embassy at Fajara not to accept such a situation.
However, such moves by the opposition would be contrary to the youth
empowerment policy of the APRC Government. More so, President Jammeh
is also currently sponsoring over 800 students, regardless of
political affiliation".
The SoS for Justice, Hon. Joseph Joof, urged APRC militants to give a
deaf ear to what he called "detractors" who were bent on instigating
violence.
Hon. Joof also challenged Omar Amadou Jallow (OJ), whom he referred to
as "East Timor," to be mute and respect constitutional provisions.
"The repeal of Decree 89 is not the problem. 'East Timor' has to
satisfy sections 52, 9 and 90 in order to be eligible," observed SoS
Joof. He also added, "the constitution states that for eligibility,
you must be in the country for at least 5 years," which OJ does not
satisfy. "Who asked him to go to East Timor?," he questioned.
"Among those that barred OJ from contesting are the findings of the
commission of inquiry, ranging from abuse of office, failure to
declare his total earnings and property and several others," said
Joof.
He also observed that the projects and aspirations of President
Jammeh, were magnanimous enough to speak for themselves as within a
span of seven years, Jammeh had accomplished projects neglected for
the past thirty years. "Vote Jammeh and the APRC and you will see
progress and development. The whole country will be electrified,
roads will be constructed and many other projects embarked upon," SoS
Joof promised. On behalf of the president, SoS Joof expressed
gratitude for the support and loyalty accorded to him by Gambians,
especially the women.
Other speakers at the rally included Aji Fatou Sallah, Mayor Samba
Faal, NAM, Saihou Omar and NAM Suku Singhateh, the adopted 'father'
of "Gikko Nyima", who all hailed Jammeh's government. "If you want to
experience another half decade of absolute progress and development,
Jammeh is the answer," they concurred.
It was also attended by Nyimasata Sanneh-Bojang, deputy mayor Biri
Njie, NAM, David Jones, Aji Yam Jobe-Jeng, the adopted 'mother' of
"Gikko Nyima' and other dignitaries.

Original_Search_Title: Periodic Cleansing of the Body
Headline: Nigeria: Periodic Cleansing of the Body
Body_Text:
The human body is a marvelous machine, and if well controlled and
maintained carries its owner through life cycle like an obedient
servant.
From day-to-day, month after month, year in and year out, man
continues to nourish and feed himself, thereby fulfilling one of the
laws of the Almighty in creation. All what he needs in form of foods
e.g. fruits, vegetables and other crops have been provided by nature
from the beginning of existence. Indeed, the solar energy has already
cooked these foods. As such, man is supposed to eat most of these
foods raw with the exception of beans and tubers like yam and fresh
meats, which must be cooked before eating.
When man is hale and hearty, he does not think of cleansing his whole
body of the toxins accumulating in his body from day-to-day. Some of
these wastes are overdue for elimination must have been acquired
through eating of foods generally, and in the normal course of living
supposed to be eliminated immediately. These foods turned into
poisons in his body, lodging in various organs and causing
disharmonies to his entire system (body) most of the foods eaten are
those deprived of their nutritive values such as fried foods, over
cooked foods, canned foods in aluminum tins dangerous to human
health. In African set-up, we consume a lot of starchy foods without
the balance of vegetables and fruits in our diets. The fibre or
roughages in most of these foods are insufficient. The metabolic and
digestive system have long been derailed through wrongful living
habits and eating of wrong foods.
Although, nature has blessed us in Africa abundantly with lots of
fruits, vegetables etc., we do not tap from all these resources in
the right manner. We eat in excesses denatured foods, etc.
All the glands of the body play important roles/functions in the
daily maintenance of the body. The lymphatic glands helps the body
with the production of antibodies etc.
These functions are :-
To transport lymphocytes from the lymphatic glands to circulation.
To return fluid and protein from the tissues to circulation. (The
protein of lymph is not as much as that of the plasma (blood).
To carry emulsified fat from the intestine to circulation. The
lymphatic performing this function are known as the lacteals.
A lymph is similar to plasma but it contains less protein. One of the
functions of the lymphatic gland is to add lymphocytes to the lymph
(as earlier mentioned), so as to increase their numbers in the large
lymphatic vessels.
Following an infection, the lymphatic glands produce antibodies to
protect the body against subsequent infection. Also, when the toxic
accumulations in the body has reached a peak, then some of the lymph
nodes around the deep cervical etc., may become visible. At such
times, the body is signaling that a thorough cleansing of the whole
body must be done.
We must remember that when we continue to eat and drink without
stimulating dead cells and toxins from our bodies, we are gradually
digging our own graves and this is shortening our lives before old
age.
Daily we attend to nature's call in form of one elimination or the
other. Do we really take our time to eliminate fully all the wastes
in our system as nature has willed it? No, we hurry through the
toilet and also through the bath- rooms. Even we hurry to eat our
foods and gulp our drinks instead of taking our time to do all these.
Our metabolic system has long been disorganized thus giving room for
poor elimination of wastes and low absorption of nutrients from the
digestive organ. This state of health over many years has brought
several disease manifestations, especially, degenerative diseases as
cancer, diabetes, constipation, bowel problems. Our breathing has
become too shallow with the result that respiratory diseases are
common especially, asthma.
We do not cherish our bodies as gifts given us by the Almighty to
enable us go through life without much hindrances in terms of good
health. The body that carries man about has suffered total neglect.
Pollution in all manners has also worsened our health situation. The
quality of food we put into our months is very poor. We must daily
ask ourselves what benefit, at the end of the day, will any food
eaten or put into our mouth be.
Our hearts are congested with cholesterol etc. There are many
incidences for heart failures around. One heart specialist in USA, a
Dr. Paul White, said that the key to heart disease is in the kitchen,
that much heart disease begins in childhood and stars with
overeating. He blames mothers for raising a generation of prospective
heart cases, and shortening the lives of their husbands by feeding
them too much of wrong foods. He particularly condemned the starchy
foods of refined carbohydrates, such as white sugar and white flour
and too much meat, milk and milk products. These, he said, are the
chief fat producers are direct causes of heart diseases.
In order to fully appreciate or understand how faulty nutrition can
cause heart disease or any other degenerative diseases, we need to
know the following:
Health is a harmonious functioning of all the organs of the body,
including the arteries and heart and is a result of living in a
healthy natural environment and eating natural foods (organically
grown).
A faulty nutrition with too much of refined carbohydrates, white
sugar, tobacco and white flour, animal fat and coffee etc., plus lack
of exercise leads to obesity, high blood pressure, hardening of the
arteries, digestive disturbances, constipation and many other
conditions. Often, these may be in sub-chemical stages.
How then can we maintain good health throughout life cycles? By
adopting the attitude of regular periodic cleansing of our internal
organs through few days of juice or fruit fasting monthly. Lots of
fluid must be taken daily to flush impurities out of our bodies. All
our eliminative channels must be made to function at their peak.
During the fasting or cleansing regimes, the bowel must be evacuated
either by enema douching or herbal laxative.
We must not just eat and eat and drink alone. We must allow the body
to take some respite in form of abstention from heavy foods. If we do
not do all the above voluntarily, nature will force us to do it and
then we will have to spend a longer time before recovery and the body
may not be the same again. Prevention is always better than cure.
Those with diseases such as high blood pressure, diabetes, asthma and
weak heart have had to adjust their diet to lighter ones instead of
going on a fasting regimes. But we all need to flush our systems out
of toxic wastes accumulation from time-to-time this is the secret of
improved good health.

Original_Search_Title: Succession: So What's the Big Deal?
Headline: Kenya: Succession: So What's the Big Deal?
Body_Text:
Nothing makes me more livid than all this hullabaloo about the succession to President Moi. So what is the big deal? This country will have a President when Moi is somewhere growing yams, but we shall have wasted a great deal of time, energy, goodwill and money.
Just think about it. For the past two years, Kenyans have been talking about nothing else. More important things like the course the constitutional review is taking have all but been forgotten.
But besides the politicians, we in the Fourth Estate are also to blame for contributing to this ridiculous hoopla. The most outrageous utterances by politicians is given such play that the ordinary Kenyan may have come to believe that politics is all that matters in their lives.
Of course no one is saying that what our politicians are doing is absolutely wrong. Politics is an occupation like any other and the pursuit of power is quite legitimate. It is the almost total obsession with it that annoys many. Could it be that politics has become just another way to escape bleak reality? Shouldn't we be discussing our dormant economy, dilapidated infrastructure, stone-age communication network, moribund agricultural sector and dwindling hopes as a nation with equal fervour?
Just consider what is happening right now on the political front. President Moi ups and says that he wishes to bequeath his office to a younger person. The chorus that greets his simple assertion, which cannot even stand the test of reason, is astounding.
Had those chaps who became so exercised over the remark paused to think, they would have realised that it was not only personal, Moi must have been talking about his own party, Kanu, which is not synonymous with the presidency, and that even on that count, he has no constitutional mandate whatsoever to do what he said he would.
But as though on cue, 34 politicians of indeterminate pedigree meet in the President's own constituency and loudly proclaim themselves the heirs to the presidency, as though they believe that talking about it would make power fall on their laps like manna from heaven? Who ever told them that the President has the power to appoint Kenya's next leader? Don't the rest of Kenyans have a say? In any case, since when has statesmanship been synonymous with youth?
Don't get me wrong. I am not suggesting that a youthful person cannot make a successful leader. In the words of Aaliyah's immortal song, "age ain't nothing but a number". It is not the grey hair on your head or lack thereof that will make a leader.
It is a fact that some youthful people have proved to be shrewd managers of men. A case in point is Britain's Tony Blair. The man became Premier in his early forties and by the end of his second term, he will be only hitting fifty, an age in which life, for some, begins. Thomas Joseph Mboya, a brilliant politician, became minister when he was only 28, and was assassinated in 1969 when he was only 35. But in those seven years, Mboya had a done a lot more for this country than a lot of politicians will ever do. And then there are those who gained power at a tender age - mostly hereditary, and therefore out of the scope of this argument - and ruled for the rest of their lives, even in dotage. Emperor Haile Selassie of Ethiopia who rose to greatness and then steadily deteriorated, comes to mind. A third example are those leaders who got into the game late in life and proved to be either brilliant, or at least, quite capable. Here in Africa we have a few good examples, among them our own Jomo Kenyatta who became President when he was at least 75 and was not disabled by age until well into his final years, and the masterful Nelson Mandela who has become the darling of the world since he relinquished power after his first term.
And why do Johnny-come-latelys believe Kenyans will ever entertain the mere thought of them taking over the reins of power? What achievements can they boast of? Kenyans ought to be spared such self-serving delusions. The next President of Kenya will be elected by Kenyans themselves - or something close to that - and not by a clique of neophytes with far too much money to do an honest day's work.
It is a fact that the old codgers in politics today seem to have been there forever. So the frustration of younger politicians is understandable. But the whole thing ought to be looked into carefully, and not as a short-term fix to a major problem. Were I to be asked, I would send the whole lot of politicians packing to their home villages and start on a new slate with a fresh crop. But this is wishful thinking.

Original_Search_Title: A Taste of Congo Food
Headline: South Africa: A Taste of Congo Food
Body_Text:
At first I was convinced it was a hangover that triggered the feeling I  was back in Paris. But when I returned to the newly established Congolese
Restaurant in Yeoville two weeks later, French tourists dining with me  felt the same way.
With kwasa kwasa music constantly vibrating in the background, the  European map on the bar counter, the antique Congolese curios and paintings, any
Parisian visiting the restaurant for the first time may, for a  nanosecond, think he's back home, in one of the many African restaurants there.
However, despite the languages spoken by the guests - including  French,
Portuguese, Swahili and English - there was one language all of them understood: that of the deliciously strange and nutritive Congolese  cuisine.
Says Corrine Langlois, the Congolese lady running the restaurant:  "Yeoville is a cosmopolitan environment and the restaurant was opened to cater  for fans of Congolese food, as well as those who may never have the chance  of travelling abroad to taste it."
She says public response has been enthusiastic despite Yeoville's high  crime rate and the restaurant has done all it can to improve security.
The food is packed with protein and carbohydrates, and it's filling.
Langlois says many Congolese follow ancestral tradition and eat only  once a day, and solid food will keep them until the next meal.
Particularly sought after is saka saka, a soup made from fresh, pounded cassava leaves. The resultant soft pulp is simmered for two hours  before fresh fish, eggplant, peanut butter, palm oil, onions, bay leaves and  red pepper are added. The mixture is cooked for another hour, left to cool  and served with fried plantains, rice, cassava or cooked mealie meal or  bread.
Another favourite is mabote, a dish featuring a highly flavoured and  fiery gravy. Fish, meat or chicken is wrapped with garlic, green and black  pepper, onion and other spices of choice, with a drop of water and placed  inside a pot. It's traditionally wrapped in plantain or banana leaves, but with  the scarcity of these leaves in the cosmopole, aluminium foil is used as a substitute. It's usually served hot with cassava, yams or plantains.
Viande de brousse (bouillon a la pâte d'arachide), or game meat  cooked in groundnut soup, is a delicacy that might offend conservationists. But  most of the game meat, including monkeys, porcupines and crocodiles, is  imported from Congo, Côte d'Ivoire or other Central African countries. The  meat, which is usually smoked or sun-dried, is cooked and served in the saka  saka style.
Poulette à la mouambe is a protein-rich sauce, oily with a nutty  taste and definitely worth trying. Its condiments are a type of Congolese palm  nuts that are cracked, pounded to dust and used in making sauce. Chicken and  salt are added to the mixture with other spices and left to cook for 30  minutes, with the occasional addition of palm oil. The resultant neither-sweet-nor-sour sauce is served also with cassava, plantain or  rice.
For those not used to Congolese cookery, a good alternative at the  Congolese
Restaurant is brochette boeuf or beef kebab. The kebab is marinated in lemon, white pepper, onion and garlic for about six hours before being grilled or roasted.
Traditionally made Congolese manioc may taste a bit dreary, but it is a mainstay. Cassava is soaked in water and left to soften for several  days.
It's then grated into fine grains and a thin portion of the paste  spread on a banana leaf, which is tied and cooked in shallow water for four or  five hours. Quite solid, it is eaten with peanut butter, sauce or soup.
For starters, there are crabs cooked in white wine, spring onion and  garlic; a salad of shrimp, onion, mayonnaise and avocado; or peri peri chicken livers. Also available as side orders or extras are roasted fish or  chicken, fried plantain, banana or potato chips.
Pap-and-vleis, stew and rice are available all the time, but for a  Congolese meal, booking is essential. The restaurant can accommodate more than 40 people, but parking can be tricky.
The details
The Congolese Restaurant, Piccadilly Centre, Rockey Street, Yeoville
(opposite the Shell garage), is open Monday to Thursday, 1pm to  midnight, later on weekends. Parking in Hunter Street. Contact Corrine Langlois  on
Tel: 082413-8965.

Original_Search_Title: End Ethnic Clashes, Museveni Tells Nigerians
Headline: Nigeria: End Ethnic Clashes, Museveni Tells Nigerians
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni urged an end to religious and ethnic strife
in Nigeria newspapers reported on Tuesday.
Speaking on Monday at the Nigerian War College, Museveni called for
peace between Nigeria's religious and ethnic communities and a
market-led approach to promote African development.
The remarks come a week after clashes left more than 500 dead.
"Our need is unity, not to quarrel as to whether somebody prefers
pork or yam, whether one prays five times a day or once a day or one
does not pray at all," The Guardian reported.
"In Uganda, we never allow religion to interfere with state matters
and religions are free to evangelise," he said.
Africa's need was economic development and that meant promoting peace
and a market-led approach to the economy, he told an audience
including the Nigerian vice president.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Laments Africa's Marginalisation in World Affairs
Headline: Africa: Museveni Laments Africa's Marginalisation in World Affairs
Body_Text:
Abuja — Ugandan President, Yoweri Kaguta Museveni, yesterday lamented the
marginalization of Africa in global trade, saying that while world
trade in agriculture was worth US$1.2 trillion, Africa's share stood
at a paltry $13 billion, representing one per cent of the world
total.
Painting a gloomy picture of Africa vis- a-vis the industrialized
nations, Museveni stated that in order for the continent to make an
impact in world trade and compete effectively, there was the need for
an African Union as key for the continent's development.
Speaking yesterday at the inaugural lecture of Course 10 of the
National War College, Abuja, Museveni, who was accompanied by the
Vice President, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar, who stood in for President
Olusegun Obasanjo, also expressed the optimism that Nigeria would
achieve greatness if she invests in the development of her human
resources.
He lamented that Africa lagged behind in world trade, a development
he blamed on lack of investment in human resources and
industrialization, adding that while world tourism was valued at US
$444 billion, Africa's share was only $541 million, representing 12
per cent of world total.
"Automobiles world wide are worth US$1.3 trillion, Africa's share is
only $60 million; much much less than one per cent of world total.
Total world trade in services is about $1.35 trillion", he said
adding "the combined share of Africa, Latin America, Central Europe,
the Baltic and the Middle East is about US$171 billion, about 13 per
cent of world total".
He stated that Africa needs to unite in order to trade adding, "The
great need is to unite in order to give viable markets to our
potential industrial capacity. The great need is to unite in order to
defend our sovereignty. Our need is unity, not to quarrel as to
whether somebody who so prefers to eat pork or yams; or whether one
prays five times a day or once a day or does not pray at all".
Alluding to the vexed issue of resource control in the country,
Museveni, whose speech was well applauded, carpeted advocates of
resource control emphasizing that while there was need to protect the
environment of oil-producing areas, the long term wealth of Nigeria
lay not in oil but in the human resources.
"The only natural resources that are next to the human resources
(people) of a country in importance are fresh water and arable land.
Otherwise the people are the greatest national resource and the more
you have, provided they are empowered the better".
Citing the example of Japan and South Korea as countries without any
natural resource, but which had invested heavily in the development
of their human resources, the Ugandan President said "Japan and South
Korea are countries without petroleum or minerals of any type. I
think that even their agriculture is quite modest. They however have
empowered (educated) populations and fresh water, adding "tiny South
Korea(half of land area of Uganda) earns about $200 billion in
exports per annum more than what Nigeria earns."
In order to redress the imbalance, Museveni maintained that "What we
actually need is to amalgamate the present 53 states of Africa into
either one African Union or at least, seven or more viable states.
"A union of African states would obviously be a super power with 750
million people, given our vast natural resources provided we address
the problems of chauvinism exhibited by some groups", he submitted.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Urges Curbing of Clashes, Lauds African Union
Headline: Africa: Museveni Urges Curbing of Clashes, Lauds African Union
Body_Text:
Abuja — For Nigeria to tread the path of greatness and maintain its
leadership role in Africa, its citizens and leaders must rise above
ethnic and religious cleavages now threatening its cohesion, Ugandan
President Yoweri Museveni said yesterday.
Museveni who spoke in Abuja said the ethnic and religious crises
ravaging the continent would retard its development in the new
millennium.
Besides the resort to such atavistic tendencies instead of
development of trade and human resources would only keep Africa away
from the path of prosperity, he noted.
Making a case for the African Union (AU) which replaced the
Organisation of African Unity (OAU), the Ugandan leader stated that
the present structure of 53 countries in the continent made them
individually weak to compete and survive in a world that is
increasingly becoming competitive.
Nigeria, Museveni said, being the most populatous and endowed black
nation in the world, should not squander its manifest destiny of
greatness and leadership of the race because of "extraneous and
irrelevant considerations" of tribe, religion and clan by some of its
citizens.
He contended that the development of the continent's human resources
and not just the abundance of petroleum and other natural resources,
was the only guarantee of a prosperous Africa.
Museveni, who enunciated his standpoint as guest lecturer at the
inaugural lecture of National War College, Abuja Course 10 with the
title, "Regional Integration," stressed that development of
intra-African trade rather than aid from Western countries holds the
key to its growth.
His words "Our great need is to unite in order to trade, to give
viable markets to our potential industrial capacity. The great need
is to unite in order to defend our sovereignty. Our need is unity,
not to quarrel as to whether somebody prefers pork or yam, whether
one prays five times a day or once a day or does not pray at all. As
far as religion is concerned, we shall know who is right and who is
wrong on the judgment day. We can, in the meantime, prepare for our
future in heaven aware that since we do not have a uniform vision
about the message of God, we should not interfere with others at all
in this connection.
"While I talk of African unity, I need to remind you of what Jesus
said, 'man shall not live by bread alone.' Man has got culture and
spirituality. We should, therefore, manage our unity in such a way
that we do not practise ethnocide. We should not endanger the
identities of the constituent parts of the respective African units.
The diversities within unity are a tremendous source of strength and
potential. In Uganda, we never allow religion to interfere with state
matters while the religions are free to evangelise for adherents. We
also practise a three-language policy. We cannot forcefully kill the
identities of our various peoples because some of them are uniquely
rich", he emphasised.
The president cautioned against Africa's over-dependence on natural
resources instead of developing human resources.
He went on: "In a country like Nigeria, I sometimes hear about
arguments relating to the areas that are currently producing
petroleum. A thinking exists that oil-producing areas are being
exploited by the non-producing areas. There could be need for
facilitating the oil producing areas more, including protecting the
environment.
"However, the long-term wealth of Nigeria or any other country is
more in its people than in most natural resources including oil. It
is the more than 120 million Nigerians. The only natural resources
that are next to human resources (people) of a country, in
importance, are fresh water and arable land. Otherwise, the people
are the greatest natural resource. The more you have, provided they
are empowered, the better. The failure to grasp this creates
unnecessary problems.
"Japan and South Korea are countries without petroleum or minerals of
any type. Even their agriculture is quite modest. They, however, have
