providing technical support and Cote d'Ivoire's Ministry of Family, Women
and Children which is monitoring the social impact of the project. The West
Africa Rice Development Association (WARDA) is providing rice-related
technical support. WFP says it hopes to obtain the participation of other
institutions in the provision of education and training, and the compilation
of socioeconomic data.
The US $8-million project is expected to ensure a minimum of food security
for people in the impoverished target areas, promote rural development, and
provide financial stability for the beneficiaries through the sale of
surplus food.
WFP has decided to link this lowland project with a school-feeding programme
(*) which it has been implementing since 1989: a portion of the PBF output
is to be used to provide lunches for the children. The ultimate goal is for
communities to provide all the food needed for the school canteens in about
four years.
The National Directorate for School Canteens and WFP "agreed to build a
strong synergy between the PBF project and the school-feeding programme,
recognising that the objectives of both projects were complementary", WFP
said in its 2000 project report. "Accordingly, the existence of a school
canteen in the village is now considered to be one of the main criteria for
the selection of a new site."
Soil type, topography, hydrology, poverty, motivation, interest for the
project and capacity to work in groups are some of the other main selection
criteria. Once a site is identified, rural extension officers explain to
farmers the project's framework, strategies, objectives and the type of
technical assistance that will be provided.
The main obstacle the project faces is locals' unwillingness to buy into it,
ANADER Director-General Yao Kouassi, said. The key to success is to ensure
that they fully participate in the project so that they can experience its
benefits, he said.
Farmers are encouraged to participate in the project through the
"Food-for-Work" strategy under which they receive rations of rice donated by
Japan in exchange for work performed in the fields, including land clearing,
digging, and helping to survey the land.
The three kilogrammes of rice participants receive for each day's work is
given to the women to ensure that the food goes to the entire family.
Food-for-Work aims to help households cope during the lean agricultural
season and reduce their food bills so that savings are used to meet other
needs.
It is also a way of involving women, who constitute the project's main
target group. It aims to give them more decision-making power within the
community, enables them to have greater control of resources such as food,
land and money, and boosts their self-confidence, project sponsors said.
Women account for 51 percent of participants and WFP hopes this figure will
soon reach 60 percent, in line with its goal of committing 60 percent of
project resources and interventions to helping women in countries where they
lag behind.
The project's national coordinator, Germain Akoubia, said he was pleased to
see women engaged in traditionally male activities but he noted that strong
female participation could be a sign of food scarcity since men's labour in
the fields was not enough to feed their families.
One of the major effects the project has had on women is to reduce their
desire to head south to look for work in major towns, from where they
usually came back with no earnings and "dirty diseases" (STDs), the
president of the Bodokro women's cooperative, Kouakou Eugenie, said. Women
now have an activity in which they can participate fully and they are
motivated to do so, she added.
WFP and its partners hope that, by the end of the year, the number of rice
and vegetable plots will have increased. They also hope to provide, by then,
another 10 boreholes, 25 warehouses and other facilities as needed.
WFP said its immediate concern was to obtain positive results that would
convince the Ivorian and Japanese authorities, and other possible partners,
to finance and expand the project to other impoverished areas.
The idea, Kouakou said, is "to get us out of poverty".
[(*) For more on the schoolfeeding programme see
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN/wa/countrystories/cotedivoire/20010615.phtml ]

Original_Search_Title: Industrialising Edo State
Headline: Nigeria: Industrialising Edo State
Body_Text:
As Edo State government takes giant stride in industrialisation,
residents should be patient for the gains to materialise writes Tom
Anuge.
It is on record and there is no gain saying that agriculture was the
main stay of Nigerian's economy before the discovery of oil in 1959
in commercial qualities. We had cocoa, cotton, groundnut, rubber and
staple foods like yam, cassava and millet in large quantities. The
country earned a lot of foreign exchange from the exportation of
these products and our per capita income was high.
But with the discovery of the money-spinning product - oil, the need
to continue to cultivate agricultural products was thrown into the
sea. So much money was realised from the sales of oil that it became
a problem knowing what to do with the available cash. We then decided
to engage on a spending spree importing all that took our fancy. The
result was that agriculture was completely neglected; and therefore,
the base for a sound industrial take-off was lost.
States were contended to sit akimbo and wait for monthly allocation
from the Federation Account in order to meet their needs. It is now
that the cry for resource control is on that we have realised that
there is need to diversify. Even though, oil brought some degree of
prosperity to this country, it has also helped to keep us lazy: with
the result that other sectors of the economy have been grossly and
sadly neglected.
It is a known fact that oil is not a renewable resource. As a result,
our oil reserves would one day be exhausted. Even before such a
period, it is also possible that advancement in technology would make
the use of oil less attractive as alternative and cheaper source of
power would have been discovered. When this happens, the country
would be in trouble as it would be difficult to meet the needs of the
inhabitants. The only way generations yet unborn would be saved from
such planlessness is to begin to industrialise now.
It is on the basis of this that the executive governor of Edo State,
Chief Lucky Nosakhare Igbinedion, has taken a giant stride to
industrialise Edo State in the true sense of the word. The commitment
of the governor in this regard can be seen in the establishment of
small and medium scale industries in the state.
There are the fertilizer blending plant at Auchi, the granite
polishing plant at Ikpeshi, Cassavita processing factory at Uromi,
fruit juice at Ehor and a host of others. This is a welcome
development, as activities in this sector of the Nigerian economy
have been sadly below expectations.
In the last decade or so, aggregate output measured by the Gross
Domestic Product (GDP), has been growing at the stunted rate of
between 2.2 per cent and 3.8per cent respectively.
The Edo State government is set to reverse this trend. It has been
observed that the achievement of the real sector has been hampered
largely by low level investments, weak infrastructural facilities,
and dismal performance of equipment and inappropriate technology in
the agricultural sector.
These and other problems of economic growth, this government has set
out to tackle by establishing these industries. A visit to some of
these industries sites reveal that the factories have been built
applying the latest technology available. There is no doubt
therefore, that this laudable initiative of the government would
eventually take Edo State to a permanent plateau of industrialisation
and prosperity.
It is the hope of the state government that these efforts begin to
yield fruits. Edo State would no longer be described as a "civil
service state", but rather, an industrial giant in the comity of
states. We have the human resources to harness the mineral and
agricultural products which abound in the state. And the government
is not hesitating to provide the enabling environment.
While Dr. Sam Ogbemudia is today being remembered and acknowledged
for the development of sports and social infrastructure in Edo State,
the late Professor Ambrose Alli is remembered for the development of
education in the state. The only area left is the industrialisation
of the state which has attracted the attention and goodwill of the
present government. Chief Lucky Igbinedon, like his predecessor,
would also be remembered for it.
Inspite of all these, skeptics wonder why new industries are being
set up when the ones on ground are moribund. They need to be told
that even though Nigeria and other developing countries have shown
interest in industrialisation through small and medium scale
industries (SMEs), Edo State (formerly Midwest and Bendel States),
had lagged behind in no small measure in embracing the concept and
putting the necessary infrastructure in place. The time to reverse
the trend is now.
Government wants to development the state and SMEs are relevant in
the achievement of growth and full employment; development of local
technology and stimulation of indigenous entrepreneurship. Other
factors which are stimulating the establishment of these industries
are the potential to foster linkages within and between the industry
vis-a-vis other sectors of the economy. All of which will accelerate
rural development and promote the utilisation of domestic resources.
An evaluation of the performance of small and medium scale industries
in areas where they have been established and with respect to
employment generation, contribution to the growth of industrial
output, degree of local sourcing of raw materials, shows that they
have made a positive impact on the life of the people. And, this is
what government has envisaged for the people of Edo State.
However, these contributions notwithstanding, small scale industries
in Nigeria face many problems such as lack of functional
infrastructural facilities. This has compelled most of them to resort
to provision of water, electricity, roads etc., by themselves. All
these lead to extra cost and often hinder production.
We are happy in Edo State that these problems will not inhibit our
desire to industrialise the state. Already, government has negotiated
and signed an agreement with a gas turbine firm to supply
uninterrupted power to all nooks and crannies of the state. The issue
of water is also being vigorously tackled. We are aware of the
discussion with a South African firm for this purpose, and also, of
the numerous boreholes being sunk for the purpose and interest of all
and sundry. Rural communities are also being linked by massive road
construction projects in all corners of the state.
In recognition of the potential role of SME's, various measures and
programmes have been designed and are being executed by the state
government to encourage their development. One of such measure is the
state's Micro Credit Scheme, which is open to any eligible Edo person
who wants to set up a small or medium sized industry.
Even though new companies have been set up and others in the
pipeline, it is not the intention of government to actively take part
in the management of these companies. Privatisation is the concept in
vogue, and the state government has already embraced it. This is the
reason that the old and ailing companies, as well as the new ones
would all be privatised. Government is only willing to maintain a low
equity participation in them. This is the trend the world over. Since
individuals do not have the required resources to set up these
companies, government has to set them up and provide the enabling
environment for their smooth operations. Through privatisation,
government gets back its initial investment which can now be
channeled to other areas. This practice allows individuals who would
not have been able to muster the resources to establish companies, to
now become part-owners (shareholders), in blue chip companies.
There is no doubt that the resources available to the state
government would not be able to cope with the rate at which the
governor would want to industrialise the state. It is, therefore,
necessary that government take advantage of the new funding window
about to be opened for small-scale industries in Nigeria. The
arrangement which is between the Nigeria Industrial Development Bank
(NIDB), and the Industrial Development Corporation (IDC), of South
Africa would finance companies in the mining, agro-processing,
energy, textiles, information technology, communication, and tourism.
We know Edo State would take the lead to explore this beautiful
opportunity. Some of these projects definitely have long gestation
periods and as a result, the gain would not be immediate. We,
therefore, implore Edo residents to be patient as these laudable
programmes come to fruition.
Mr. Anuge writes from Benin city.

Original_Search_Title: Statistics Reports Cautioned Inflationary Pressure
Headline: Nigeria: Statistics Reports Cautioned Inflationary Pressure
Body_Text:
Reports of the federal office of statistics (FOS) has indicated a
continued inflationary pressure in the economy as the composite
consumer price Index (CCPI) which level of retail measures changes in
the average prices paid by consumers in both urban and rural areas,
rose by 2.9 per cent in the month of May, 2001, against April, 2001
record.
The index which stood at 4330.2 compared with 4207.1, according to
FOS, rose as a result of increases in the prices of food items and
drinks, though prices of kerosine and transport fares fell in many
centres of the economy.
FOS reported that the average consumer price level moved up by 23.0
percent between May 2000 and May 2001 a slight decline in the 2.3 per
cent observed in the preceding month of the same period. The price
indices in the urban and rural areas surged by 22.5 and 23.0 percent
respectively.
The changes notices in the percentage of an average CCPI for the
twelve month period under review which terminates in May 2001 as
against the previous twelve month was 15.9 per cent..
This infact means that a market price of consumer goods and services
was bought with N100.00 during the twelve-month period ending May
2000 and sold for N115.7 for the period ending May 2-001.
The composite food index took an upward movement by 5.2 percent in
May 2001 this was necessitated by increase in price of staples e.g
Gari, yam, tuber, plantain, beans and rice. The prices in processed
foods and edible oil rose in many centres.
Consequently, between May 2000 and May 2001 advanced by 30.2 per
cent, the average annual rate of rise of the composite food index in
the twelve month period ending May 2001 recorded an 11.3 percent
increase.
The composite consumer indexes less farm produce in exclusion of the
prices of agricultural produce dropped by 0.3 per cent in May 2001,
the index rose by 11.3 percent, the average annual rate rise of the
index was 20.9 per cent for the twelve month period ending May 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis May Lead to Revolution
Headline: Nigeria: Food Crisis May Lead to Revolution
Body_Text:
Calabar — The rising cost of staple food in Nigeria may lead to a revolution if
the Federal Government does not take urgent steps to check the trend,
Second Republic Senate President, Dr. Joseph Wayas has warned.
Wayas was of the view that, governments in some parts of the world
have been brought down by rising cost of staple food prices,
insisting that Nigerians possess the ability and resources to feed
themselves.
Speaking at a party organised in his honour by the Cross River State
Government for his 60th birthday, Senator Wayas said the government
should not allow the peoples' anger to degenerate to violence because
of hunger occasioned by the unaffordable prices of foodstuff.
At the banquet in Calabar, weekend, attended by Vice-President Atiku
Abubakar, Wayas said: "the situation becomes worrisome when people
can no longer afford gari, rice and yam which are locally cultivated
bearing in mind that in majority of the 778 local governments in
Nigeria about 600 of them are farmers."
"We may wake up one morning and we find people stoning cars on the
streets. They will not be selective in their violence, they simply
will stone any car in sight believing that these are the people who
have made food unavailable" Wayas said.
On the cause of the present socio-political problem in the country,
he said, every blame must be placed at the door of military people
who stray into politics, "creating so much problems both
economically, politically and otherwise."
Wayas likened Nigeria's poor showing in democracy to a child which is
just preparing to walk, pointing out that if such a child is always
lifted from the ground by the parents rather than allowed to learn
the process of walking "it will take quite some time for such a child
to know how to walk."
Consequently, "Nigeria must be allowed to walk (learn the art of
democracy) and if mistakes are made, we should be given the
opportunity to learn from these mistakes," adding that, "the constant
interference in politics by the Nigerian military is killing
democracy and Nigerians will never learn."
He described himself as an ardent federalist who will "fight and die
for the unity of Nigeria," stating that, if anyone insists on
dismembering Nigeria, "such a person has another thing coming because
Nigeria will never be divided by anyone or group of people for their
selfish intentions."
Also at the forum attended by former Inspector-General of Police,
Alhaji M. D. Yusuf, Major General Olu Bajowa, former senate president
Chief Evan Enwerem as well as Senator Ita-Giwa, the special guest,
Vice President Abubakar praised Wayas for piloting the senate at the
time in a dignified manner.

Original_Search_Title: Cooperatives Urged to Be Open
Headline: Ghana: Cooperatives Urged to Be Open
Body_Text:
Mr. Okyere Darko, Nkoranza District Co-operative officer on Monday called on co-operative societies and groups to exhibit transparency, accountability and probity in their activities.
Addressing the fifth annual general meeting of the Techiman District branch of the Co-operative Yam Producers and Marketing Society Limited, he advised the members to unite to promote their welfare.
Mr. James Okyere, Deputy District Co-operative Officer said the society made a net surplus of more than 24 million cedis in the 1999/2000 financial year as against 22.7 million cedis in the previous year.
Mr. Okyere said the society's total income of 54,561,036 cedis was derived from the sale of yams, bank interests and entrance fees.The society spent 30,277,748 cedis on committees' allowances, wages and salaries, transport, stationery, donations and funerals.
Mr Okyere said since the society was established no member had contributed to the share capital, adding that 2,512,934 cedis being dividend on shares accruing from the net surplus has been turned into shares for the members.

Original_Search_Title: Author's Latest Novel, Purple Yams, Released
Headline: South Africa: A South African Abroad
Body_Text:
Shortlisted for this year's Sanlam Literary Award, Sheila Roberts's
new novel, Purple Yams, is just out from Penguin. Stephen Gray interviewed
her on her recent return to Johannesburg.
Since 1975 when she made her debut with the prize-winning collection,
Outside Life's Feast, Sheila Roberts has sustained an impressive career in
publishing.
Two later collections have followed, and she has three previous novels
to her credit -- the first, He's My Brother (1977), was banned in a
notorious case for the way it portrayed foul-spoken South African men.
That year she went into voluntary exile in the United States, where she
teaches creative writing at the University of Wisconsin at Milwaukee.  One
of her recent stories was included in The Picador Book of African
Stories.
Q: Quite unexpectedly your new novel pulls your bipolar world
into a triangle.  It's set, not in the US, but in Japan.
A: I got the chance of a visiting professorship there and being single
by then I was in an ideal situation to tackle it. So I found myself a lone
professional woman, for more than two years. Halfway through the loneliness
came down on me, and I started to need to write about it. The first versions
of Purple Yams were much more autobiographical; later I saw the
possibilities in fictionalising -- and we're talking about a work that's
seen eight drafts! When I had the distance, it began to come out right.
Q: From South Africa we've only ever had William Plomer and
Laurens van der Post taking on Japan before, but their work seems a bit
touristic now.
A: Yes, but once I had my protagonist being really critical I could get,
I hope, more deeply into things. And also deeply into memories of her
earlier life here. While in Japan she is their "other", so she is forced to
remember situations at home in which there were various other "others" --
not only black people, but those troepies in Potchefstroom, for example. I
hoped in that way to force her towards a point of change.
Q: It takes a natural disaster, in those terrifying earthquake
scenes.
A: Well, when I was there the Kobe earthquake did take place, in which 5
000 people were killed. In Japan you are always aware of these frightening
tremors in any event. I needed a dramatic moment like that to alter the
situation, and although I wasn't at Kobe itself, I researched it all -- via
the documentation on the great San Francisco quakes. That disaster happens
and by the end her relationship with the Japanese has become utterly
altered.
Q: Has your being in the US for more than 20 years had a bearing
on how you see your readership?
A: Yes, but I didn't want in Purple Yams to have an American heroine,
because that would have put me into language problems I couldn't handle.
Anyway, I've never been able to rinse my mind of South Africa. My own
memories keep coming back and I often return, as you know. So logically I
kept to having a South African woman and her speciality being rock art just
followed.
Q: But Japan was obviously good for you, since you haven't
produced such a major work for years.
Thanks to the release of time I had there. I felt there I could at last
write and write forever ... Back in Wisconsin I find the workshops and so on
very, very taxing.
Q: Now Purple Yams is going to be a tough one for South African
readers, perhaps. Too mature.
A: I suggest readers put up at first with any dislike they have for my
character. Just be aware that the situation she finds herself in is not
static -- she is on the move, towards frightening events. But also towards a
broadening of her own mind and her understanding. After all, in the end she
decides to stay on in Japan, however alien.

Original_Search_Title: Food Glut At Techiman
Headline: Ghana: Food Glut At Techiman
Body_Text:
Farmers in the Techiman District are complaining about poor returns on their investment as a result of low prices of
their produce.
Mr Kweku Minka-Fordjour, District Director of the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MOFA), told the Ghana News Agency in an interview that the prices of items like tomato and groundnuts have fallen so low that "farmers cannot even recover their production cost, let alone to make profit".
He said if nothing was done to reverse the perennial problem it would discourage farmers from increasing production.
Mr Minka-Fordjour said as an interim measure, tomato growers could make use of the tomato factory at Tuobodom and hoped that more of such factories would be sited in the region to process the excess.  Groundnut farmers could also dry and store to attract a higher price.
He advised the farmers to diversify into the production of other crops so that they do not over-produce to create a glut, which causes the fall in prices.
He said the price of fresh yams is relatively high as a result of their export to the European markets and this would continue to be so till mid-August when production reaches its peak.GNA

Original_Search_Title: Institute Develops Local Food Varieties
Headline: Nigeria: Fiiro Defies Poor Funding, Develops Local Food Varieties
Body_Text:
"I see FIIRO as a place that can turn Nigeria around" was the respon
se of Mrs. Pauline Tallen, the Minister of State for Science and
Technology after she was conducted round the various laboratories,
the engineering and pilot plant workshops recently in one of her
visits to the Federal Institute of Industrial Research, Oshodi,
FIIRO. The Minister of State who did not shy away from the fact that
research is poorly funded in the country however stated the Federal
Government's plans to step up the level of funding of the science and
technology sector.
The research institute which has evolved through several
developmental stages since it was founded in 1956 has several
research products to its credit. Kunnu which is the generic name for
all types of non-alcoholic, cereal-based beverages produced in the
Hausa communities in Nigeria, exists in different varieties denoted
by the suffix following it e.g. kunnu - zaki which is the variety
prepared from sorghum, millet, guinea corn extracts, spiced with
ginger cloves, pepper and usually sweetened. But this very popular
drink is characterised by the problem of an extremely short shelf
life. Traditionally produced kunnu spoils within 24-36 hours which is
about a day and a half shelf life. So the Hausa drink which has
become popular throughout the country can hardly be stored reserved
or transported over long distances to distant consumers.
This shortcoming elaborated above has however been removed so as to
raise the status of Kunnu and enhance its commercial importance not
only for the gains of the local consumers but for enhancement of the
national economy and increase of the gross domestic product (GDP). To
achieve this end, FIIRO produced the combat kit through pasturising,
adding preservatives and bottling. This process helps to eliminates
the micro organisms responsible for spoilage of the product and
strengthen the inherent chemical composition of the drink. Using the
"HACCP" System therefore, the shelf life has thus been increased
beyond 9 months without altering the appearance and taste of the
drink. The equipment needed to apply the FIIRO treatment are
unsophisticated and are locally available. Their design and
specifications have been completed for local fabricators to produce
at the request of interested entrepreneurs for commercial production
of kunnu. With all these, FIIRO intends to encourage the small/medium
as well as large scale investors to go into commercial production of
kunnu.
Sobo is a traditional refreshing drink that has gained house hold
prominence in Nigeria recently. It is made from a plant called
roselle (hibiscus sabadariffa, L.) The extract of this plant which is
used for the manufacture of sobo drink has a brilliantly red colour
which is also useful as a food colourant. Sobo drink which is
believed to be medicinal and corrective against illnesses like,
hypertension, and high blood pressure, till date, is prepared
haphazardly without standard formulation and proper processing
technique. One of FIIRO's contributions has been to raise the quality
of this drink by developing a hygienic processing and preservation
method suitable for adoption by processors, and for commercialisation
by potential entrepreneurs.
It is not news any more that most grains indigenous to the Nigerian
climate are only available seasonally. A large quantity of these
grains which would have been useful to consumers after the season of
the crops are usually wasted during their season due to the poor
level of storage and processing equipments in the country. To check
this problem FIIRO has designed and fabricated a 300kg per hour
all-purpose of grain milling plant. This plant, apart from carrying
out its function of milling, sifting and recyling very efficiently it
is designed to accommodate the unsophisticatedness and low capital
problems of the small scale indigenous millers.
In the areas of fermented food production and organic agriculture,
FIIRO's tremendous effort includes the implementation of a Hazard
Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP) quality system for the
production of Soy-ogi at the institutes pilot plant. Soy-ogi is
produced from maize or millet and Soya beans. FIIRO has established
the various stages and processes to be followed to yield Soy-ogi of
sound micro biological and organoleptic quality and the procedure
which meets the standard of Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) is
available for adoption by both traditional and modern producers of
Soy-ogi. Mushroom, a delicate and organically produced plant with
very high economic importance based on its diverse uses for food and
its potential danger when not properly identified and cultivated.
FIIRO in this direction has developed a simple technology for the
cultivation of tropical edible mushrooms on small and medium scales,
using simple and affordable waste products such as cotton waste, rice
straw tobacco stalk etc. as compost. FIIRO has also developed a cheap
preservation method by simple dehydration to increase the shelf life
and market penetration of mushroom.
Other areas where FIIRO has made frantic productive efforts include
developing detoxification measures against aflatoxins, particularly
in maize and sorghum food products and fruit extraction and
preservation in form of bottled and canned fruit drinks etc.
Speaking recently with FIIRO image maker Mr. Segun Fuwa, he disclosed
that the institute has concluded arrangements to organise
entrepreneurial workshops in Lagos, in the months of July and August
according to him the workshops are designed to furnish potential
entrepreneurs with productive ideas and information in the following
areas:
Edible mushroom cultivation
Instant pounded yam flour production
Laundry and toilet soap production
Fruit juices production from Nigerian fruits.
Bread and confectionery Baking Technology. (including the use of non
composite flours)
Essential oil extraction from Lemon grass for perfumes and essences.
Sobo drink production and preservation.
Vegetable oil extraction from groundnut and beniseed.
The FIIRO PRO said similar workshops will also take place in FIIRO -
Kano during the same period. In Kano the workshops will be on the
following:
Bread and confectionery baking technology
Body and hair pomade production
Laundry and toilet soap production
According to Segun Fuwa "the workshops will be suitable for all those
contemplating the establishment of micro, small to medium scale
industries, those already in production with quality control problems
as well as for agro-industrial consultants and machines/equipment
fabricators".

Original_Search_Title: Mobilisation of Grassroots Towards Economic Sustenance
Headline: Nigeria: Mobilisation of Grassroots Towards Economic Sustenance
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is no longer news that the world economy is in shambles.
Every nation is crying under the yoke of economic woes. But then,
some nations are better than the others, under a good leadership
systems.
Yet, the world is economically sick and is going through the worst
form of economic depression that has ever been experienced, as people
in the majority, are living from hands to mouths. However, there is a
better tomorrow for the nation that focuses its attention on the
grassroots emancipation.
This write-up is therefore aimed at challenging the countries of the
world and Nigeria in particular to an active participation in
co-operative systems more than before - being an agent of social
mobilisation for economic sustenance; and a means of relieving
poverty, creating employment and generation income, using its
fourfold characteristics of : a. Voluntary membership b.
Autonomous decision making c. Democratic control d.
Equitable distribution of benefits and risks. Nigeria as a country,
should take the advantage of the benefits of the co-operative
systems, to change the lifestyle of her citizenry, particularly at
the grassroots level.
The poverty alleviation programme can better be organised towards
achieving this goal. The States and the Local Governments can be
empowered to implement this programme at the grassroots level.
The fact is that, the nations that have changed the destiny of their
citizenry, have empowered the operators of their economic sustainable
programmes, and that is why they are better placed in the committee
of other nations that have failed to grant their citizenry economic
freedom. It is believed that if the co-operative system is
efficiently organised, it is an effective economic means of providing
people in both the rural and urban settings, the opportunity for
improving their economic and social structure.
The benefits of co-operative systems cannot be over emphasised, where
the government allows its effective operation, through granting it
full backing. Co- operative systems as a means towards economic self
help, has been growing to the status of big time investment
opportunities in production, consumption and self employment.
This write-up therefore aims at three important areas of mobilisation
strategy:
. Government mobilisation of the grassroots for effective membership
participation;
. The challenge of the existing co-operative groupings towards
profitable investments;
. Education of co-operative members on the areas of economic
activities that they can explore for economic sustenance.
The government, through the State and the Local Government, should
encourage and strengthen co-operative systems, since co-operative
systems aims at economic emancipation towards self reliance through
self help; at least, to alleviate the current suffering of the
citizenry that are not economically engaged, particularly, the under
privileged who are in the majority; the peasants, the women, the
school leavers, the unskilled youth, the unemployed graduates and the
low income earners, so that through full back-up and powerful
enlightenment programme, these categories of people can be exposed to
the benefits of active participation and membership. It is evident
that the two ways that membership drive can be easily carried out is
through the government source, that is, through the various
co-operative departments in the ministries of commerce, industries,
economic planning, and development, and also through the existing
co-operative societies.
The government source will help in campaign and enlightenment
programmes, as well as giving financial support, mainly directed at
the rural dwellers, educating them on the support offered it by the
government, for them to voluntarily and enthusiastically participate.
The co-operative societies on the other hand can be exposed to better
management systems by way of proper education, demonstration of good
leadership system and the promise of effective delivery, through the
use of all methods of awareness creation at the grassroots level.
The unfortunate thing is that both the government source and that of
the co- operative societies have failed woefully to achieve the
co-operative objectives of educating and enlightening the potential
people towards effective participation. If at all, the attitudes of
some government representatives in some States can best be described
as below expectation.
Amidst the existing co-operative societies, the unpleasant news of
cases of embezzlement and fraudulent practices have destroyed and
killed people's interest in co-operate systems. Today, the attitudes
of the co-operative societies' representatives at the State and Local
Government levels can best be described as demoralising.
It is high time that they arose to demonstrating good leadership
skills and good morals. There is need for good quality of characters
from both the government representatives and the existing
co-operative societies executive members before more members can be
interested and be genuinely recruited.
The government at all levels should encourage good investment at the
grass root level. Also, the existing co-operative societies should
aim at profitable investments.
Co-operative societies can engage in profitable investments. The
primary goal of the co-operative common pool is for profitable
returns on investments.
Therefore, the capital formation through members' savings should be
well engaged in active business activities for the benefits of the
members in terms of access to loans, buying of products at reduced
prices and profit sharing in terms of dividend at the end of the
accounting year. The leaders at the executive level must be
responsible and committed people: people with vision and zeal to turn
the society around for growth, success and efficient operation.
There are co-operative societies, that through their efficient
leadership, they are engaged in large scale production of goods- some
in marketing and supply, savings and credit, banking, consumer supply
and agriculture. There are investment areas that co-operative
societies should be interested in.
This cannot happen unless there is government back up and the strong
leadership commitment of those at the helm of affairs. Therefore,
before engaging in any such investment pot-folio, such leaders must
be visioners, believe in their vision, and set goals for
accomplishing greater results.
The following are some economically viable investments that
co-operative societies can engage in: a. Production / Processing
1. Food and fast food
2. Fruit processing
3. Ice Cream Production
4. Plastics Production
5. Candle Production
6. Food Packaging
7. Bread and Confectioneries making
8. Detergents and Soap Production
9. Wood Processing for export
10. Paper Conversion and Processing
b. Agriculture
1. Full Scale Farming (all types of cultivation).
2. Animal Husbandry (cow, goat, sheep, rabbit, snail etc).
3. Crops cultivation (cassava, yam, cocoyam, etc).
4. Grain planting (maize, millet, rice etc).
5. Fisheries.
6. Poultry(fowls, ducks, egg production).
7. Peggery.
8. Fruits Planting.
c. Small Scale Farming
i. Planting of vegetables.
ii. Seed Production for sale (Okra, Ewedu, Tomatoes, Vegetables,
Pepper).
iii. Planting of exotic vegetable (cucumber, water melon etc.
iv. Husbandry (pig, goat, rabbit etc).
v. Rearing of guard dogs.
vi. Soya beans planting and processing etc d. Trading and Domestic
Services
i. Food vendors.
ii. Selling of ware and general merchandise.
iii. Dry cleaning.
iv. Barbing salon.
v. Canteen operation.
vi. Selling of food items.
vii. Selling of used shoes and clothing items viii. Washing and
pressing
ix. House cleaning etc.
e. Professional Services
i. Typing-Typing Institute.
ii. Estate Agency etc.
iii. Business centres (computer, telephone, internet operations,
photocopying etc.) There is no need for further procrastination.
All that have been said are possible. Government can give her full
backing; members can be generated; profitable investments can be
carried out, and members interest to engage in economic activities
can be geared up.
The collapsed domestic economies can be revived and nursed through
engagement in co-operative activities. Effective planning is what is
required.
Good leadership is what is needed. The very way out is at the door
step of the Federal Government, the State Government and the Local
Government, as well as the good leadership to promote it.
Where withal is the poverty alleviation programme? Is it moribund or
is it continuing? Who is doing what to alleviate the suffering of the
masses at the grassroots level? Something can begin to happen for the
better.

Original_Search_Title: RUF Claims CDF Attacked its Men
Headline: Sierra Leone: RUF Claims CDF Attacked its Men
Body_Text:
The rebel Revolutionary United Front (RUF) said this week it would abide by
a ceasefire it signed in mid-May with the pro-government Civil Defence
Forces (CDF) militia despite CDF attacks on the villages of Lelewah and
Woodu in northern Sierra Leone on 17 and 19 June.
UN Mission in Sierra Leone (UNAMSIL) spokesman Major Mohamed Yerima told
IRIN on Thursday that the attacks were carried out by militiamen who had
been in Guinea and were largely unaware of the RUF-CDF ceasefire. He said
UNAMSIL patrols were investigating the incidents in which, according to the
RUF, at least 35 civilians and four RUF fighters were killed.
Meanwhile, the RUF has asked the UN for more information on a proposed truth
and reconciliation commission, UNAMSIL reported. The rebels also wanted
information on a proposed UN tribunal that would try crimes committed during
Sierra Leone's war.
The war, which began in 1991, caused great destruction, including in the
diamond-rich eastern district of Kono where, according to World Vision (WV),
90 percent of the buildings were damaged or destroyed, particularly in the
district's main town, Koidu.
WV reported on 16 June that health care and schools were "practically
non-existent" in Kono and that people were surviving on bananas and wild
yams. Up to 80 percent of Kono's pre-war population, estimated at 510,000,
had fled fighting in the district, which used to be the country's
breadbasket. WV said tens of thousands had recently gone back to the
district and more were returning daily from refugee camps in Guinea.

Original_Search_Title: Ninety Percent of Buildings Destroyed in Kono
Headline: Sierra Leone: Ninety Percent of Buildings Destroyed in Kono
Body_Text:
A decade of fighting in Sierra Leone has left 90 percent of the buildings
damaged or destroyed in the eastern district of Kono, particularly in its
main town, Koidu, World Vision (WV) reported on Saturday.
Homes, schools and clinics are among the buildings affected. WV reported
that health care and schools were "practically non-existent" in Kono and
that people were surviving on bananas and wild yams. The NGO said tens of
thousands of people had recently gone back to the district and more were
returning each day from refugee camps in Guinea.
Up to 80 percent of Kono's pre-war population, estimated at 510,000, fled
fighting for control of the district, which used to be the country's
breadbasket. World Vision had been the only international NGO operating in
Kono since 1996, until it, too, was forced to leave the area after it fell
under rebel control in 1998 and became inaccessible.
A World Vision team returned to Kono last week after an initial assessment
of critical needs in the diamond-rich area.

Original_Search_Title: Endangered Art
Headline: Africa: Endangered Art
Body_Text:
Africa's cultural treasures are now prized by collectors and
connoisseurs the world over, but this popularity is fuelling looting and
illicit sales, write SIMON ROBINSON, Kawu, (Abuja) and AISHA LABI, London
THE digging began early in 1995.
A farmer who had uncovered a sculpted terra-cotta head sold it to a
travelling antiquities dealer for $30, nearly twice what he could make a
month by growing and selling yams. Word of the windfall quickly spread and
locals began tilling the ground around kawu, a village of some 3,000 people
50 km Northeast of the Nigerian capital, Abuja.
Other prospectors soon arrived, and within months ,more than 2,000
diggers were burrowing through Kawu's stony earth. Dealers bid against each
other, pushing up prices. Bars and brothels opened. Newly rich locals bought
motorcycles and reroofed their houses. Palm wine producers brewed around the
clock. "Everybody was looking for money," say Abubakar Sala, the local
primary school teacher, who headed to the fields after classes to try his
luck. "Farmers let their crops rot because they were busy digging for
terra-cota.
Africa, already plundered of its people by slavers, its animal by
big-game hunters and poachers and its mineral wealth by miners, is now
yielding up its cultural heritage. Across the continent, art and artifacts
are being looted from Museums, Universities and-as at-from the ground. Most
of the objects-ancient terra-cotta and stone sculptures, brass heads, wooden
grave makers, masks and doors-end up in Europe or the United States. The
figurines unearthed in Kawu were remnants of the Nok culture that lived in
present day central Nigeria between 500 B.C. and 200 A.D., and they are
among the most sought-after items.
Interpol, the International Police Organisation, estimates that the
illicit trade in cultural property is worth $4.5 billion a year worldwide,
up from the $1 billion annual figure of a decade ago. Art experts estimate
that Africa accounts for 10 per cent of this trade, and the continent's
share is growing. "It's a fantastically big problem," says Omotosho Eluyemi,
an archaeologist and director general at Nigeria's National Commission for
Museums and Monuments (NCMM). "We ended the slave trade more than a century
ago. Now it's the objects of antiquity that are being carried off into the
West."
Long dismissed by the Western art establishment as primitive and of
interest only to ethnographers and anthropologists, African art has slowly
come to be valued for its intrinsic beauty and artistic merit.  A flurry of
activity at major Museums across Europe is evidence of its enhanced
stature. After nearly 30 years in "temporary" accommodations, the British
Museum's African Collection returned triumphantly this spring to a new
setting at the museum's London headquarters. The Sainsbury African Galleries
can accommodate only 600 of the Museum's inventory of more than 200,000
African objects, but some of these have been in the collection since the
Museum's founding in 1753, and the new showcase marks the first time they
have enjoyed such prominence.
In Paris, a new wing devoted to art from Africa, Asia, Oceanic and the
Americas opened last year at the Louvre. The sculptures on display in the
Pavillon des Sessions, which will be transferred to the Quai Branly Museum
for non-European art when it opens in 2004, include two 14th century wooden
figures from Mali and a 19th century Congolese Luba headrest.
These items were donated by the French collector Hubert Goldet shortly
before his death last year. The remainder of his 640 piece collection, which
he began amassing in 1971, long before the current vogue for African art,
will be auctioned in paris at the end of June in what organisers are calling
the "world's most important primitive art sale since 1966." It has been
estimated that it could fetch as much as $7 million.
Even Belgium's Royal Museum for Central Africa, which houses one of the
world's largest collections of African art and has long been a monument to
that country's brutal colonial legacy, has mounted an exhibition, on display
until the end of June, that attempts to address some of the wrongs of the
past. Entitled "Exitcongomuseum," the show illustrates how indigenous
valuables were looted with impunity as the colonial masters imposed their
rule. The 125 objects on display were included in a travelling exhibit that
returned to Belgium last year after the successful four-year tour of museums
in the U.S., Canada and Europe.
"An African should feel proud that his art, his heritage is being
displayed in the Louvre,'s says Nureldin Satti, a senior official with the
United Nations' cultural organisation, UNESCO, in Nairobi and enthusiast for
the ancient arts of his own country, Sudan. "It helps raise awareness of the
inventiveness and creativity of African man-that he is not the savage that
people think."
Paradoxically, though, that greater appreciation has placed many African
art objects in jeopardy from looters few cultures have been
spared. Three-quarters of Mali's archaeological sites, including most of the
graves built into the cliffs along the world Heritage-listed Bandiagara
escarpment, have looted. During the past decade thieves in Niger have
damaged more than 90 per cent of all known Bura sites, where 1,500-year-old
terra-cotta figures have been found. Ethiopia is struggling to protect its
oldest silver Coptic Christian crosses and medieval manuscripts. Over the
past 30 years, illegal traders in coastal Kenya and Tanzania have carted off
hundreds of vigango, or Swahili wooden grave markers. When fighting broke
out in the Somali capital of Mogadishu in 1991, one of the first casualties
was the National Museum. Within weeks many of its prized exhibits, including
ancient Egyptian pottery, were on sale to tourists in neighbouring
Kenya.
Perhaps the hardest-hit country is Nigeria, where over the past two
decades Museums have been robbed of hundreds of their most valuable
items. In one infamous break-in at the National Museum in Ile-Ife in 1994,
thieves working with a Museum guard drugged the other guards and then
smashed the glass of 11 unalarmed display cases. Their haul, which included
some of the best known examples of 12th and 13th century Ife terra-cotta and
brass heads-all uninsured-was worth an estimated $200 million. It was the
third burglary at the museum within a year. Nigerian traders also target
villages like Kawu, buying artifacts from locals or encouraging rudimentary
digging in areas likely to yield treasures. "It's not exactly excavation,"
says Abiye Ichaba, an archaeologist and head of research and documentation
at the Abuja Council for Arts and Culture. "There's nothing systematic about
it, no pattern to it. We call it plundering."
Some of the plunders end up in Tourist markets like the one at Lekki, in
Nigeria's business capital, Lagos. The rarest pieces aren't prominently
displayed, but ask the right questions and you can get 150-year-old Tiv
wooden village posts used to protect against spirits ($3,000); a pair of
1,200-year-old husband-and-wife Nok figurines ($10,000); or a 95-year-old
Ijaw water-spirit mask (a bargain at $100). Across the continent, in the
Masai market in Kenya's capital, Nairobi, collectors can place orders for
Swahili grave pillars ($400-$1,300, depending on the size) or much larger
Sudanese grave posts ($20,000). Delivery can be arranged in less than two
weeks.
The tourist market, though, is limited and the farther from their source
such objects are purchased, the higher the prices they can command. In
Manhattan, for example, a Swahili grave post may cost as much as $10,000
while an Ijaw mask can go for $1,000. In Africa, the best artifacts are
bought by more sophisticated dealers who specialise in the filling orders
from Europe, the U.S. and South Africa. The European dealers "usually want
to see photos so I'll e-mail them some," says Chinedu and cameras, that's
what's bringing (the costs of) my promotion down." Once Idezuna get the nod
from his European contact-he uses several, including, he claims, Amsterdam
gallery owner Paul Rutten-he wraps the works in a thin foam cushion and
carefully packs them into a handmade wooden box. Using a letter from the
N.C.M.M. permitting him to export contemporary arts and crafts-but not
antiquities -Idezuna books the crate onto KLM flight to Amsterdam. "Customs
check the shipment for narcotics, for this and that, but because as I've got
the letter I'm fine," he says. "Our government doesn't permit it, of
course. They discourage it. But we gallery owners get (objects) out by
telling them that we are having a show of African culture. Then you make
your money by selling it once you're in Europe."
Rutten, for his part, states that he has no recollection of doing
business with Idezuna and says he deals mostly with seven runners with whom
he has an established relationship and whose integrity he trusts. "Maybe the
runner gets the goods from the person who is really the supplier, that's
possible," he concedes, but says that is beyond his control. Rutten deals
mostly in Asian art-African objects account for roughly a third of his
inventory-and that he points to the complete ban on the trade in Cambodian
and Thai art introduced in 1971 as an example of how unyielding art
importation laws have become. "In Holland we have very strict customs
regulations," he says. "If they believe something is illegal, they will
confiscate it."

Original_Search_Title: Increase in Food Prices Impact On FOS Report
Headline: Nigeria: Increase in Food Prices Impact On FOS Report
Body_Text:
Recent increase in the composite consumer price index has been
attributed to "Sharp increases in the prices of some food items,
drinks, clothing and household appliances".
The findings reflected in the consumer price index report edition of
April, 2001 issued by the Federal Office of Statistics.
According to the report, the composite consumer price index stood at
4207.1 in April, 2001 representing a substantial increase of 6.3
percent over 3956.4 recorded in March, 2001.
Equally a high percentage change in the average composite Consumer
price index for the twelve month period ending April, 2001 was
recorded against the preceeding tweleve month period with the
increase of 13.9 percent. This, according to the report implies that
a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with
N100.00 during the twelve month period ending April, 2000 sold for
N113.9 on the Average for the corresponding period ending April,
2001.
In the twelve months to April, 2001, the report revealed, the average
consumer price level rose by 23.2 percent, recording 5 percent rise
than 18.2 percent recorded in the previous month. It further states
that during the same period, the Urban and rural indices advanced by
25.7 and 22.7 percent respectively. However, the prices of Kerosine
and transport fares according to the paper, fell in many centres.
Contrary to the report, transport fares in Lagos area has been
recording tremedous increase, especially during morning and evening
hours daily. Also in Enugu, some notable increase has been observed,
as markable distance formally costing N40.00 in twelve months ending
April, 2000 now costs N70.00 from the same point recently. For
example, a journey from Enugu, the state capital to Nsukka Urban area
whose fares formally costs N40.00 by commercial buses now stood at
N70.00. Also a journey from the same capital city to Ihe, a nearby
town in the state, which formally costs N30.00, now rose to N70.00.
The high increase in transportation in Lagos area according a finding
from the commercial operator is due to recent increase of touts and
police estorting money from the operators. While the fare increase in
Enugu was attributed to scarcity and persistent high cost of
petroleum products in the area.
Composite Food index also rose by 11.0 percent between March and
April, 2001 which resulted in notable increase in the prices of
staples such as Gari, Yam tuber, Plantain, Beans, Rice etc. A market
research revealed that a measuring cup of gari, which was sold at
N5.00 per measure in certain areas, is now sold for N15.00.
While a bag of rice previously sold for N2,800.00 rose to N3, 500.00
and N3,700.00 in different areas.
The prices of Vegetable and fruits also moved up in many centres.
Between April, 2000, and April 2001 the index advanced by 26.7
percent as against 16.3 percent observed in the preceding month, CPI
report observed.
The average annual rate of rise of the composite food index for the
twelve month period ending April, 2001 was 8.7 percent, it pointed.
The "All Items Less Farm Produce" (AILFP) index which excludes the
prices of agricultural produce dropped by 0.8 percent in April 2001.

Original_Search_Title: FAO/WFP Crop And Food Supply Assessment Mission to Swaziland
Headline: Eswatini: FAO/WFP Crop And Food Supply Assessment Mission to Swaziland
Body_Text:
Rome — Mission Highlights: * Cereal production in 2000/01 is estimated at 73
000 tonnes, close to last year's poor harvest, but some 33 percent
below the previous five year average, due mainly to a mid-season dry
spell and a heat wave.
* Cereal import requirement for 2001/2002 (April/March) is estimated
at 123 000 tonnes, and is expected to be covered entirely by
commercial imports.
* A needs assessment is currently being conducted jointly by the
Disaster Management Task Force and NGOs in drought affected areas.
The results will be important in identifying precisely the vulnerable
groups that should be assisted.
1. OVERVIEW
Following reports of a prolonged dry spell that affected the 2000/01
crops, an FAO/WFP Crop and Supply Assessment Mission visited
Swaziland from 16 to 28 May 2001, to review harvest prospects and the
overall food supply situation, and to estimate cereal import
requirements, including food aid, if any, for the 2001/02 marketing
year. The Mission received full cooperation from the National Early
Warning Unit (NEWU), the Ministry of Agriculture and Co-operatives
and the United Nations Development Programme. Discussions were held
with the Deputy Prime Minister, the National Disaster Management
Service, the National Maize Corporation (NMC), relevant UN agencies
and NGOs. SADC Regional Early Warning Unit (REWU) participated in the
Mission as an observer. Extensive field visits were undertaken in all
agro-ecological regions of the country and interviews were conducted
with farmers, extension service, millers and traders. The Mission
split into two groups to ensure adequate coverage of key areas within
the time allocated.
The Mission forecasts the 2000/01 maize production at 73 000 tonnes,
which is about 66 percent of the last five years average. Swaziland
does not produce other cereal crops, except for a few hundred tonnes
of sorghum. Other crops like sweet potato, beans and cow peas are
produced but in small and declining quantities. Field observations by
the Mission indicated that production of these crops has also been
lower than average. The main factors responsible for reduced crop
yields were the mid-season dry spell and the accompanying heat wave.
The reduction in maize production was particularly serious in the
Middle and Lowerveld where late planted crops were affected at the
critical flowering /tasseling stage. The early-planted crops escaped
the drought and generally produced good yields. For the 2001/02
marketing year (April-March), domestic cereal supply, estimated at 85
000 tonnes, falls far short of national consumption requirements.
With a mid-marketing year population estimate of 1 034 000, cereal
import requirement is estimated at 123 000 tonnes. The Mission
estimates that the requirement will be fully covered by commercial
imports. Even in normal years, Swaziland's cereal production covers
only 60 percent of its domestic consumption requirements. The one-
to-one convertibility of the local currency with the South African
Rand means that availability of foreign exchange is not a constraint
to commercial imports from South Africa.
FOOD AND AGRICULTURE ORGANIZATION OF The UNITED NATIONS, ROME
WORLD FOOD PROGRAMME, ROME
Nonetheless, because of reduced production, food shortages may be
experienced by vulnerable population groups which have limited access
to food. A needs assessment is currently being conducted jointly by
the Disaster Management Task Force and NGOs in drought affected
areas. This exercise will be important in identifying more precisely
the vulnerable groups that should be assisted.
2. ECONOMY AND AGRICULTURE1
Swaziland is a small land-locked country with limited domestic
markets. Exports of agricultural commodities and industrial products
play a significant role in the country's economic growth, development
and food security. Major contributors to real Gross Domestic Product
(GDP) in 1998/99 were manufacturing (37 percent), government services
(15 percent), service sector (10 percent) and agriculture (9.8
percent, down from 19 percent in 1985/86).
Recently, expansion of output within the manufacturing sector has
been the result of strong export performance, especially of
sugar-based products, refrigerators, textile products, citrus and
canned food and to some extent wood pulp. Estimates of the balance of
payments in 2000 point to another surplus and, at year's end,
Swaziland's net official reserves were equivalent to 4.5 months of
total imports, a relatively comfortable external reserve position. As
for the country's external debt and debt-service obligations, they
have remained low, at the equivalent of 22 percent of GDP and 2
percent of exports respectively.
Real GDP growth rate has ranged from 2.4 percent to 2.7 percent per
annum between 1998 and 2000. This rate indicates negligible growth in
per capita income, since the Government estimates the population
annual growth rate at 2.7 percent. Employment in the private sector
decreased from 65 000 in 1993 to 61 000 in 1999. The inflation rate
hovers around 6 percent per annum and is estimated at 6.4 percent for
2000. This inflation stems from the sharp depreciation of the South
African rand vis-a-vis major currencies over the recent years. The
Swazi currency -- the lilangeni --- which is pegged at par with the
rand, fell by around 23 percent against the US dollar between 1998-
2000. In 2001 and 2002, further depreciation of the currency is
expected. Lending interest rates have averaged 14 percent in 2000, 7
to 8 percentage points over the rate of inflation.
The Government budget in financial year 1999/00 (April-March)
recorded a deficit estimated at 4.5 percent of GDP, owing largely to
an increase in the wage bill (stemming from the full-year impact of a
15 percent pay raise) and larger subsidies and transfers to public
entities. The IMF observed that the Government budget had moved into
deficit at a time when receipts from the Southern African Customs
Union (SACU), which constitute 50 percent of Swaziland's total
government revenue, are expected to decline. The conclusion of the
EU-RSA preferential trade agreement in late 1999 and the fact that
the SACU revenue formula is currently being renegotiated will result
in lower revenues, adding further pressure on the macroeconomic
stability of the country.
Whilst Swaziland is classified as a middle-income country because of
its relatively high GDP per capita (over US$ 1 340), per capita
income for the poorest 40 percent of its population is equivalent to
about US$230. Distribution of income is highly skewed, with about 43
percent of total income received by an estimated 10 percent. It is
estimated that 55 percent of the rural population live below the
poverty line.
Of the agricultural sector's contribution to GDP, production on Title
Deed Land (TDL), which consists mainly of sugar cane, pineapple and
citrus, accounts for 60 percent. This sub-sector is market-oriented,
uses better quality technology and has access to irrigation.
Production on Swaziland National Land (SNL), accounts for the balance
of 40 percent and comprises mostly subsistence farming. On SNL, maize
is grown for subsistence and for supplementary income, along with
cotton.
Without an increase in land and water resources, the capacity for
growth in TDL is limited. Growth in this modern sub-sector has
remained stagnant in recent years while the SNL sector experienced
low or negative growth. Maize production has declined over the past
years and is highly dependant upon weather conditions. Production of
other cereals is insignificant.
3. FOOD PRODUCTION IN 2000/01
Rainfall
The month of January registered lower than normal rainfall in all
parts of the country. Rainfall in the Highveld region for example,
was only 34 percent of the normal for January. The Lubombo Plateau
was much less affected. Rainfall conditions improved in February in
every region except in the Highveld, which continued to experience a
rain deficit. During the month of March, below normal rainfall was
again recorded throughout the country. The Highveld region received
67 percent of its long-term average for that month. The situation was
no better in other regions (see Figure 1).
Supply of agricultural inputs
Fertiliser use for food crop production (excluding commercial
farming) ranged between 14 000 tonnes and 17 000 tonnes during the
period 1995/96, to 1998/99. Fertilisers, which are no longer
subsidised, are traded by the private sector and cooperatives through
national networks.
As for hybrid maize seeds, use went down from 4 000 tonnes in 1995/96
to 1 316 tonnes in 1998/99. This massive decline followed a
government decision to stop providing free seeds to farmers. Seeds
are now supplied by the private sector and cooperatives at market
prices. Hybrid maize seed use recommended is estimated at 20 kg per
hectare.
Fig. 1. Swaziland: Actual vs. Normal Rainfall, by Agro ecological
Zone During the 2000/01 Cropping Season
Planted Areas
The Mission's estimates of planted area are based on data provided by
the National Early Warning Unit. The data had been collected by the
Extension Service by agro-ecological region and were the most
reliable at the time of the Mission. The Central Statistical Office
(CSO) will provide final estimates during the course of the year. The
total national maize area is estimated at 58 259 hectares, which is
the same as last year. The plantings could have been larger were it
not for the excessive wet conditions at sowing in the Highveld and
limited access to tractors and purchased inputs in the Middleveld,
the Lubombo Plateau and the Lowveld. The estimated area planted to
maize in each agro-ecological region is shown in Table 1.The area
planted to sorghum has over the years declined to insignificant
levels because of labour problems associated with bird scaring, lack
of appropriate varieties and a poorly developed market for the crop.
Even though the Mission observed the serious efforts by the Extension
Service in promoting cassava and sweet potato production, it noted
the limited crop diversity in the fields of farmers particularly in
the Lowveld. The Mission did however come across farmers who had
small plantings of beans, cowpeas, jugo beans, pumpkins and yams.
Cotton remains one of the major crops of the Middleveld and the
Lowveld covering about 17000 hectares, but the area continues to
decline because of poor prices and competition from irrigated sugar
cane which is fetching better export prices. With further development
of irrigation being planned by the government, the area under maize
is also likely to be affected.
Yields
The yield forecast for each agro-ecological zone is presented in
Table 1. Once the maize production estimate for the year 2000/01
season is released by the CSO, the yield and production figures may
need to be revised. The Mission did assess crop yields on 70 randomly
selected farms in all agro-ecological regions and considered that the
estimates made by NEWU were satisfactory. Yields vary with
management, rainfall and agro- ecological conditions even within the
same agro-ecological zone. The Mission came across farmers who had
absolutely no harvest and others who had up to seven tonnes per
hectare, within the same vicinity, indicating that husbandry
practices (early planting, use of appropriate hybrid seeds, proper
use of fertiliser and animal manure) can make a great difference to
the impact of drought. However, in general maize yields declined
substantially compared to the of the last five years average
(1995/1996 to1999/2000). This yield decline was particularly serious
in the Middle and Lowerveld where the January/February dry spell
affected the late planted crops at the critical flowering /tasseling
stage. The impact of the drought was further worsened by a heat wave
that hit the country at the same time. The early-planted crops
escaped the drought and generally produced good yields. Some farmers
indicated to the Mission that they have had limited access to
tractors and that they could not get inputs in good time.
On the other hand, many farmers reported that they had achieved the
same or better yields compared to the last year. It was reported that
last year had excessive rains which resulted in floods that damaged
crops, leached nutrients and caused cob to rot.
Based on the mission's investigation and NEWU forecast, the national
average yield for maize is estimated at 1.13 t/ha and total
production at 73 000 tonnes which represents 66 percent of the
average for the last five years (Table 1). Figure 2 shows that maize
production is trending downwards, despite price support. This is
mainly due to adverse weather and probably falling land productivity.

Other crops
Cotton, one of the major cash crops, occupies 17000 ha and plays an
important role in the food security of many households. However,
production is on the decline: the hectarage was 35 000 ha during the
1998/99 production year. Sugar cane with 43 000 ha under cultivation
is now the main agricultural export and an important source of
foreign currency. Grapefruit, orange, soft citrus and lime also form
an important part of nutrition and source of foreign currency.
Sorghum, pumpkins, sweet potatoes, beans, peanuts, cowpeas, cassava,
bananas, peaches and avocados are also produced, but in limited
quantities.
Livestock situation
Livestock and pasture conditions are good throughout the country,
especially now that the Foot and Mouth Disease seems to have been
brought under control. Livestock production is a major activity and
about 77 percent of the total cattle population is owned by small
farmers.
Although Swaziland has a quota to export 3 360 tonnes of beef to the
EU, it only manages to export a fraction of this. Farmers seem
reluctant to sell good quality cattle unless forced by economic or
climatic conditions.
For the period 1996 to 1999, the cattle population has declined
slightly from 656 484 to 613 832 (Table 3). Goat population has also
declined from 403 664 in 1996 to 332 939 in 1999.

4. FOOD SUPPLY SITUATION
Access to food and prices
In normal years, Swaziland's cereal production covers about 60
percent of domestic consumption requirements. As for the national
staple food, maize, the level of self-sufficiency is in a range
between 48 percent (1991/92) and 94 percent (1995/96), depending upon
weather conditions. Commercial imports of maize from South Africa
fluctuate accordingly. For the last ten years, they averaged 24 900
tons a year, with a peak of 63 000 tons in 1991-92. Commercial
imports of wheat have reached 50 000 tons in 2000/01 and rice imports
hover around 6 000 tons a year.
No more than 10 percent of the maize production is marketed, most of
it through the National Maize Corporation (NMC). Domestic maize
marketing is liberalised, but NMC is legally the sole importer of
maize. The Government has a policy of floor prices to farmers to
induce them to produce maize. The minimum price to farmers has been
set at E52.5 per 70kg bag (E750 per ton) for the marketing year
2001/02, which is higher than the CIF import prices from South
Africa. The selling price by the NMC to the maize mills is a weighed
average between actual cost of imported maize and local maize
procurement costs. This price policy, aimed at self-sufficiency, and
the fact that milling businesses cannot import cheaper maize grain
from South Africa, puts processed maize prices up by 20 percent over
what they could be if imports were liberalised. This higher price is
passed on to the consumer. In a country where a significant
percentage of households are net buyers of maize (confirmed by field
visits of the Mission team) and where the poor spend 32 percent of
their income on maize, high prices have a significant negative impact
on households' purchasing power. Maize imports and floor price
policies need to be reassessed as part of a long- term food security
strategy.
Other food imports are liberalised, but importers need a permit from
the National Agricultural Marketing Board (NAMBOARD). As the country
is relatively small and the road network is maintained in fair
condition, physical access to food supply is not major constraint. A
majority of farmers interviewed during the field visits were engaged
in off-farm income earning activities, such as work related to the
wood-pulp industry, sugar cane or pineapple processing and other
manufacturing activities. Others earned some income from petty trade
or were receiving remittances from relatives working in South Africa.
But a minority who had no off-farm income worried about their access
to food once their harvest had been consumed.
There are no systematic food price monitoring mechanisms in
Swaziland. However, from the National Early Warning Unit (NEWU) field
surveys, it was possible to gather some information on maize selling
prices in informal markets scattered in the various ecological zones.
Maize prices have been relatively stable since last September, except
in the Lubombo Plateau zone where prices jumped in November and
stayed high (see Fig.4 below). This relatively isolated zone/region
has the smallest planted area in maize (see Table 1). Located between
the Lowveld region, which was hard hit by the dry spell, and
Mozambique, it is also further away from South Africa maize markets.
Source: National Early Warning Unit
Maize prices will require close monitoring until the next harvest in
May 2002, as South Africa too has suffered from the consequences of
the dry spell and maize production is forecast to be substantially
lower this year. Even though Swaziland's requirements are relatively
small, competition from other countries of the region for access to
South African maize may put pressure on prices and impact negatively
on households' access to imported maize.
Cereal supply/demand balance, 2001/2002
The forecast of the cereal supply/demand situation for the 2001/2002
marketing year (April/March), summarized in Table 4 below, is based
on the following assumptions and Mission observations:
* Farmers interviewed by the mission stated that they have no stocks
left. Last year was also characterized by a poor harvest. Extension
services and other Ministry of Agriculture officials confirmed that
situation. Therefore, it has been assumed that opening farm stocks
are zero. NMC and millers' stocks were estimated at 2 000 tonnes for
wheat and 10 000 tonnes for maize. * On the demand side, national
cereal consumption requirement has been calculated in close
collaboration with the National Early Warning Unit (NEWU). The
mid-marketing year 2001/02 population is estimated at 1 034 000, and
the per capita consumption at 125 kg for maize, 46 kg for wheat and 6
kg for rice. * "Other uses" cover essentially post harvest losses,
estimated at 15 percent by the Ministry of Agriculture. Most farmers
buy hybrid seeds and a negligible fraction is used for animal feed.
