From a macroeconomic point of view, the direct convertibility of the
lilangeni into South African rands means that availability of foreign
exchange is not a constraint to commercial imports from South Africa.
In the past, commercial food imports appear to have been quite
responsive fluctuations in the national production. At a household
level, most of farmers should be able to cope, mainly because most
engage in off-farm income earning activities and/or receive
remittances from relatives working in South Africa. However, a number
of households may need support to reach the next harvest. An on-going
survey, conducted jointly by the Disaster Management Task Force and
NGOs will assess emergency food needs which are expected to be
relatively small.
This scenario seems realistic, provided that RSA's maize prices do
not increase significantly in the second half of the marketing year
because of lower domestic production and higher regional demand for
maize. Otherwise, the number of households at risk will increase.
5. FOOD SECURITY AND VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT
5.1 General hazards and vulnerability factors
The Mission is concerned that a serious maize harvest reduction could
accelerate food insecurity among the most vulnerable households in
rural areas. However, the economic situation in Swaziland does not
show serious signals of deterioration. Nevertheless, it is concluded
that joint government and external assistance targeted to the most
vulnerable population groups will prevent acute food shortage over
the coming twelve months and strengthen self-reliance and long-term
food security.
5.2 Vulnerability analysis
An increasing number of households fall into socio-economic strata
more vulnerable to further natural and economic shocks and the number
of social welfare-like cases is anticipated to grow:
* In rural areas affected by climatic vagaries, near food secure
households will increasingly depend on food purchase and solidarity
networks to compensate for gaps in their own production; * in urban
and peri-urban areas, unemployed and underemployed workers' extended
families will face growing food and income deficits. Problems with
socio-economic entitlements will exacerbate the effects of the
current situation for some population groups.
On the other hand, the labour market is active and wages -in
commercial agriculture, industry and public sector - do not show
signs of depression. Livestock was not affected by the dry spell and
is in good condition. Also, remittances are reliable sources of food
and income.
Currently, there is no indication of irreversible food security
deterioration -as long as the anticipated cereal shortage can be
managed. To date, no life threatening situations have been reported,
although some individuals are already experiencing well below normal
food intake levels in a context of increasing poverty and HIV/AIDS
incidence. Nonetheless, Swaziland's endowment in natural resources,
human resources and social capital, coupled with civil and productive
infrastructure and public services represent major assets and suggest
an overall coping capacity.
A joint assessment is being conducted by the Disaster Management Task
Force (DMTF) and NGOs in drought affected areas, through a survey at
household level. The results will be available soon and it is
strongly recommended to review the present FAO/WFP assessment in the
light of the information generated by the DMTF/NGOs exercise.
Complementarities should emerge that can provide a solid basis for:
* Conclusive judgement on the severity of the current situation in
the different areas. * Needs assessment - i.e., who and where are the
needy; when, which and how much assistance is required. * Response
requirements - against the standards for humanitarian intervention
that the government and international community are willing to adopt
in this specific situation.
In fact, only a small proportion of the population (i.e., social
welfare-like cases) is expected to be unable to maintain acceptable
standards of food intake -it has to be recalled that the current
assistance level is generally considered inadequate vis-a-vis the
actual needs. Nonetheless, large population strata may be forced into
asset (distress) sale in order to avoid severe food shortage,
particularly in rural areas.
5.3 Strategies for Possible Food Aid Assistance
Coordination mechanisms exist, as well as platforms for peer review
and accountability -particularly, CANGO's Swaziland Food Security and
Rural Development Programme. A similar platform exists to help
coordinating initiatives in the social area -particularly, HIV/AIDS.
At government level, the National Disaster Task Force is being
converted into a permanent institution and the National Disaster
Management Policy enacted.
There is no need for planning large-scale food aid distribution, as
long as:
* The coping mechanisms are viable and ensure a minimum income above
the food poverty line. * Markets and distribution mechanisms can
ensure an adequate grain supply at affordable prices in the deficit
areas - both rural and urban.
The on-going joint assessment by the Disaster Management Task Force
and the NGOs is expected to provide relevant information on
households to be targeted for possible assistance in food aid, seeds
and fertilizers.
A number of NGOs are ready to submit to potential donors their
proposal for a second four-year phase of the "Swaziland Food Security
and Rural Development Programme". Ongoing activities, including
community based home care for orphans and chronically ill,
supplementary feeding of malnourished children and assistance to
their families, are implemented by NGOs and churches. These
programmes are insufficiently funded at present.
6. AGRICULTURE REHABILITATION NEEDS: A MEDIUM TO LONG-TERM STRATEGY
Beyond short-term measures necessary to restore productivity and
production, the Mission also considers it essential that the country
take long-term measures aimed at tackling some of the main underlying
constraints which seriously affect agricultural development. A
comprehensive participatory approach is needed that raises farm
productivity and produces economic and resource conservation
benefits. This approach would emphasise building up of soil organic
matter levels through proper use of inorganic fertilizers, manure and
ash, to improve soil structure and fertility, coupled with
intercropping of improved cereals and legumes, conservation farming
and agro forestry practices. Improved soil structure and fertility
result in efficiency of plant nutrient use, thus enhancing much
needed profitability of crop production. The overall benefits would
be improved food security, cash incomes, dietary diversity and
protection of the environment.
This report is prepared on the responsibility of the FAO and WFP
Secretariats with information from official and unofficial sources.
Since conditions may change rapidly, please contact the undersigned
for further information if required.

Original_Search_Title: Ogoni Oil Spill; A Closer Look
Headline: Nigeria: Ogoni Oil Spill; A Closer Look
Body_Text:
At Yaata, a tiny village in the Ogoni area of southeastern Nigeria dying
vegetation in various shades of ochre stretch as far as the eye can see,
poisoned by soil turned soggy and a dark, greasy hue since crude oil began
seeping through over a month ago.
It all started with a huge bang on 29 April at the Royal/Dutch Shell
Yorla oil field. The quake-like tremor sent shockwaves onto Yaata and
surrounding villages. Within minutes, before people could guess the cause,
jets of crude oil were already shooting up 100 metres - visible from a
three-kilometre radius - and raining on the surroundings.
"When this was quickly followed by strong fumes of natural gas, people
knew it was time to run for dear life," John Nwikine, a student from Yaata,
told IRIN. "They knew from experience that any accidental fire was going to
light up the area and spread as fast and as far as the fumes were going."
For nine days the shower of crude oil and gas poured on Yaata
unabated. The rapidly resulting streams of crude oil swamped neighbouring
farmlands, forests, streams and rivers in Kpean, Kwawa, Kono, Baen, Teera-ue
and Buan; all in the Khana local government council of Rivers State. The
Movement for the Survival of Ogoni People (MOSOP), which defends the
interests of the 500,000 Ogoni ethnic minority, estimates that up to 10
communities in all were affected to varying degrees by the spill.
Though Shell was quickly alerted to the disaster, the oil giant appeared
helpless. Its surveillance teams circled the scene in helicopters daily
without landing. Bands of local youths quickly organised themselves into a
vigilante, shielding people away from the scene and alerting inhabitants to
the danger of lighting fires. The success of their effort limited what could
have been a monumental disaster.
Nevertheless, people in the areas pervaded by the fumes complained of
breathing difficulties, in a number of cases combined with cough and runny
noses.
Shell, meanwhile, called on a US company for help. Experts from Boots
and Coots International Well Control in Houston, Texas, capped the broken
well-head on the ninth day.
When Yaata's residents returned they found their village was
uninhabitable.  Their maize, cassava and yam crops were stained with crude
oil, wilted and dying. Much of their livestock had either died or were dying
from eating polluted vegetation and drinking contaminated water. Dead fish
rose to the surface of creeks and ponds.
"No one needed to be told that we could not drink water from our streams
and wells anymore or that we had lost all the expected harvest from the
crops we laboured to plant earlier in the year," Nwikine said. "So there was
no point in staying."
The former inhabitants of Yaata have now resettled in neighbouring
villages, hoping to return when the spill is cleaned up. More than five
weeks later, this task has not started. All that has been undertaken, so
far, are containment measures: basically, local labour has been used to dig
trenches and ditches into which the streams of crude oil from the broken
well-head have been diverted.
"Essentially, what has set back the clean up is the position of MOSOP,"
a Shell (Nigeria) spokesman told IRIN on condition of anonymity. He said
MOSOP opposed Shell's intent to send surveillance teams to the spill
site.
MOSOP said it opposed the visit because Shell had failed to consult the
local people. "Since Shell did not consult with all the stakeholders in the
Yorla blow-out, the security of the personnel of the oil company could not
be guaranteed at the site of the incident," the group said.
The Yorla field is one of several in the Ogoni area, producing a total
of 28,000 barrels of crude oil per day. It was abandoned by Shell in 1993 in
the face of militant local opposition led by MOSOP. The group alleged there
had been decades of neglect, environmental degradation and denial of access
to the wealth produced on their land by the company and its joint venture
partner, the Nigerian government.
Subsequently, Shell warned that in its hurry to leave the area it was
unable to apply all necessary procedures to ensure the safety of the shut
oil facilities. MOSOP maintains that Shell ignored guarantees from local
people that its employees would not be harmed if they came to make the wells
safe, but this claim remains controversial. Relations between Shell and the
Ogonis deteriorated further after military ruler General Sani Abacha in 1995
executed author Ken Saro-Wiwa, the MOSOP leader, and eight other activists
after a murder trial widely viewed as flawed.
Previous spills at the Yorla field, in 1993 and in 1999, came with major
fires but locals and Shell officials admit these were relatively small in
scale compared to the latest disaster.
One highly contentious issue informing MOSOP's position on the latest
incident has been Shell's claims that the blow-out was caused by sabotage.
Oil multinationals in Nigeria routinely refuse to pay compensation for
spills caused by sabotage. This has given rise to local fears that Shell is
bracing to adopt a similar stance over the Yorla spill.
Shell said a clean-up and other remedial steps would have to await the
outcome of a joint investigation visit (JIV in industry parlance),
comprising company and government officials as well as representatives of
the affected communities, to the spill site. "We have been meeting with the
people in the affected communities to choose their representatives so that
we can undertake the joint investigation visit," the Shell spokesman
said.
JIV will not only seek to determine the cause of the blow-out and the
extent of damage it caused to the environment but also what remedial steps
should be taken, the official said. Shell is also seeking safety guarantees
to enable it secure its other facilities abandoned in Ogoniland.
However, a Port Harcourt-based geologist, Soetan Igeniwari, said JIV was
only useful for determining the short-term effects of an oil spill and
tended to ignore the longer term effects.
"The longer term effects of the series of spills we have had in the
Niger Delta over these years is what I find most worrisome," he told
IRIN.
"For instance, we are now in the rainy season and the Yorla spill has
been there a month already," he said. "Each time rain falls, the oil is
washed deeper into the earth, polluting the underground water. And you can
find the traces much farther from where the incident occurred. Who will take
responsibility for that?"

Original_Search_Title: Point Of Order Of Hunger And Famine
Headline: Nigeria: Point Of Order Of Hunger And Famine
Body_Text:
Let me share with you what I wrote in 1988 when people seemed to be wanting to import food into this country as a stopgap for shortages. I said: "The only choice we have on the food question is to grow our own food, utilizing all the resources we have ... No country owes us a living. We on our part do not owe any country a living. This stance is consistent with our belief that we should have permanent interests not permanent friends or permanent enemies.
"The well-being of the Nigerian, wherever he may be, should be the national objective and the destination of governance. Any other considerations must be brushed aside in this bid to make Nigeria grow and create the soil for the Nigerian as a seed to grow in, progress in, mature in, and contribute consciously to the further development of his country and its people. We do not believe that Nigeria can grow if it depends on importing food from outside.
"You may not know it but the very policy of importing food was the greatest threat to our Operation Feed the Nation Programme of General Obasanjo and the Green Revolution food programme of Alhaji Shehu Shagari. Looking at the food bill since 1981, we will discover that we spent almost nine billion American dollars sustaining farmers abroad to grow food for our undisciplined national palate ...
"How can a nation wanting to take off go on a wild eating spree, spending N40 billion (then $60 billion) of the country's hard -earned money to feed less than 15,000 of the 97,000 communities? We should find out what each ecological zone offers, maximize its use and hope that in the next few years we would look at past indiscretions in the food area as one more lesson in our learning process ...
"Nigeria can no longer afford to have leaders who take advantage of the gullibility of our people to spend all their time in a factory where only lies are manufactured, where the raw materials for this most soothing opium of the emotions are glamour, songs of praise for work not done, and attributions of greatness where only pitiable mediocrity and short-sightedness reign.
In this year 1988, (that was the year this was written) Nigerians have no choice, repeat no choice, but to programme for change and growth so that at the time of harvest, we would have crops reflective of change and growth. Is it no longer true that only what you sow you shall reap?"
As I have said, all the above was written in 1988 (see The Food Question 1 & 2, Letters to my Countrymen) when Nigerians were wanting food imported into a country where more than 75 percent of the land was fertile for farming. I detailed what we would need by 1992 if we would not be yelling for food. And I said we would need to achieve defined levels of production of maize, millet, sorghum, rice, wheat, groundnut, cowpea, soya bean, cassava, sweet potato, Irish potato, yam, plantain, melon, other oilseeds, leafy vegetables, tomato, onion, okra, pepper, carrots, citrus, pineapple, banana, mango, pawpaw, other fruits, sugar cane and others. These many crops constitute staples in different parts of the country and could be produced in enough quantity for local use, in industry and for export.
I pleaded that using 1987 as the base year, we needed to grow certain irreducible amount of crops if we would not starve by 1992. Since garri has been synonymous with food in Nigeria, I indicated how much cassava we had to grow if we were not going to be in trouble. In 1987, we produced 11.6 million tonnes of cassava. To avert danger in that sector, we needed to have grown 12.4 million tonnes in 1988, 13.2 million tonnes in 1989; 14.1 million tonnes in 1990; 15.1 million tonnes in 1991; and 16.1 million tonnes in 1992. This year, we ought to have produced at least 26 million tonnes of cassava, but have we?
I even gave details of what we needed in livestock. We would need 25 million goats in 1992, nine million of them for slaughter; nine million sheep, four million for slaughter; 105 million chicken for poultry meat; 10 billion eggs and 10 million grass cutters and other equivalent varieties of bush meat. It meant that we did not have to depend on traditional hunting to have grass cutters. We had to have grass cutter farms just as we have had to have fish farms.
I do not know how many of us know that in the 80's more than 200 million people depended on cassava as a staple food, but that cassava was being endangered. I said so in 1988.
The fact is that since 1972 a disease devastating cassava had crept into Africa and was doing its worst to destroy this life-sustaining food of many. I said that the disease had spread far and wide and that it was no longer a threat to only one country. It had become a continental menace.
I do not know what has been happening but if we woke up one morning and discovered that our staple was nowhere near the table of the hungry lowly worker, then we did not do what we had to do more than 12 years ago when we were warned. Not only did we not meet the levels of production of food we would have worked for if we had grown more crops, we did not even exceed that level at which the danger signals were given.
I will continue to say that no country owes us a living. The National Supply Company which was set up in the early 70's to meet shortfalls in food production while we refocused for agricultural revival successfully undermined and survived the food programmes of four administrations - Gowon, Murtala Muhammad/Obasanjo, Shagari and Buhari. It was in Babangida's time, when it made more money than it had ever done, that it was wound up, even when many were crying and protesting. We just had to start something going in growing our own food.
Instead of pursuing a policy of eating what we grow and growing what we eat, as Ghana was doing, we have opted for enriching paddy rice farmers in Thailand and other places. When are we going to be worthy to be seen as serious people? The greatest disservice the President can do to this country is to encourage any form of importation of food, be it garri or beans. There is enough land to grow whatever food we need in this country, and if we are not willing to grow such food, then we have opted for hunger and should face the consequences.
Government has its role to play to ensure that the environment for growing our food is assured. The role of the Ministries of Agriculture countrywide has never been anything to write home about. They are drain pipes that channel our scarce resources into private pockets. If they are to lead in the recovery programme of feeding our people, then we should see ourselves as having been sentenced to another lap of hunger and famine.

Original_Search_Title: Industrialist Recommends Guarantee Agencies as Security for Smes
Headline: Nigeria: Industrialist Recommends Guarantee Agencies as Security for Smes
Body_Text:
THE provision of guarantee agencies as security for the 10 per cent
profit before Tax (PBT) Loan to small and medium scale enterprises
(SMEs) has been suggested as a solution to the fears being expressed
by banks that the beneficiaries may not redeem their loans in the
absence of collateral security.
According to the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Shower Limited,
Mrs. Ekema Akpan, "since guarantee agencies work like insurance
companies they could be used as security in event of default".
She added that the credit should be disbursed in growths with a
guaranty agency so that there could be some measures of checks and
balances among members.
On the Africa Growth opportunity Act (AGOA), Akpan was of the opinion
that the Nigerian government will have to enlighten the people
especially the small scale entrepreneurs on how they could benefit
from the programme, while condemning the mentality of some Nigerians
who would not take pride in having "made in Nigeria" label on their
product.
According to her, "the government must create time and make a policy
that will re-orientate the people on how to be proud of their work.
We should also encourage a lot of Nigerians abroad to come and invest
in the country.
Most Nigerians abroad export Moi-moi, Pounded yam and Egusi soup in
cans to Nigeria. But they were able to do that because the
environment there was conducive for such".
She regretted that in fabrics and textile production, that Ghana is
still ahead of Nigeria, saying "we need to catch up with them
otherwise we will lose our opportunity in AGOA because it is not
charity".
Lamenting that a lot of industries in Rivers State have closed shop
as a result of multiple levies and high cost of production, she
called on the government to intervene so as to shore up the
productive base of the nation.
Asked how the manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) has assisted
the SMEs in the past, Akpan who is a member of SME committee of MAN,
said MAN encourages bigger companies to assist the smaller ones
provided the products are good and competitive.

Original_Search_Title: Boy 13, in Court for Stealing Mobile Phone
Headline: Ghana: Boy 13, in Court for Stealing Mobile Phone
Body_Text:
A 13 year-old primary four pupil, who stole a mobile phone belonging to a student of the University of  Cape Coast while running an errand for her, was on Monday arraigned at a Community Tribunal at Cape Coast.
In the dock with the boy was Ebenezer Acquah, alias Jojo, unemployed, who was charged with dishonestly receiving the phone.
The boy pleaded guilty and the tribunal presided over by Mr Peter Asare-Duah bonded  him to be of good behaviour for a year and put him on a three million-cedi bail, pending a later sentence at a juvenile court and the disposal of the charge against Acquah.
Acquah, who pleaded not guilty to the charge of dishonestly receiving, was remanded in custody until June 18.
Prosecuting Police Chief Inspector Henry Tanor told the tribunal that on May 11, the student Miss Elaine Foxon-Owoo, asked the boy, who normally runs errands for the students to send a tuber of yam to her roommate.
He said Miss Foxon-Owoo later went to her room and detected that her mobile phone valued at two million cedis was missing and suspected the boy of taking it.
She, therefore, made a report to the Police, who arrested the boy and  questioned him. The boy admitted taking it and giving it to Acquah.
Chief Inspector Tanor said Acquah, however, denied taking any phone from the boy. He said he had collected a toy mobile phone a month earlier from another boy.   GNA

Original_Search_Title: The Rural Bent of Adamu's Administration
Headline: Nigeria: The Rural Bent of Adamu's Administration
Body_Text:
The injunction is as germane in governance as it is in business. The
wide margin existing between human wants and available resources
makes it imperative that every manager who wishes to succeed must
imbibe basic lessons in priority setting, and must be prudent.
For the government of Nasarawa State in these past two years of
democratic governance, these two factors, more than any other, have
worked to help the administration, led by Governor Abdullahi Adamu,
to deliver democratic dividends to the people of the state. But it is
not yet Eldorado for the young state as many of its communities have
a long way to go in the search for all round development.
Nonetheless, investment in projects with multiplier effects, and more
importantly, inroad into the rural areas are distinctively the
highpoints of the administration's efforts since assumption of office
in May 1999. In four strategic areas - rural water, roads and
electricity supply; education; health and industry - the
administration has made the acclaimed testimonial impact. The result
of this are praises from many who freely comment on the
administration's efforts so far; and prayers to Almighty God by the
silent downtrodden majority in the rural areas for the architects of
the new dawn.
Yet, this extension of democracy dividends to the hinterland has
become the taunted albatross of the administration. Though most
people acknowledge that the feat is unprecedented, and indeed
commendable, some others voice a preference for the development of
the urban areas, particularly Lafia, the state capital, even if
sacrificially at the expense of other projects in other areas. The
champions of this agenda posit that every state must have a rallying
point for its people, a city they would be proud of. Lafia, they
contend, is yet to attain such a status, nearly five years after it
became a capital.
But the geography and spread of the population of the state appear to
give impetus to the style adopted by the administration. Nasarawa is
agrarian with majority of its citizens living in the rural areas
where they engage in farming. Every council in the state is an
agricultural haven, hence the state is described by people as the
food basket of the country, especially in yam and cattle. It is also
the home of solid minerals which yearn to be tapped. Given this
setting, what is known of the state in terms of economic and social
life takes place in the rural areas.
Besides, the area that makes up the state, which was carved out from
the former Plateau State on October 1, 1996, was essentially the
lower Plateau that suffered neglect, deprivation and
underdevelopment, the relative academic advantage of its people
notwithstanding.
Adamu, 56, lawyer and businessman, did analyse the situation before
he took a stand on the policy thrust of his administration. He said
so in an interview with The Guardian. He is in a position to
critically review events in the state, having played politics in the
area for over 22 years during which he held the positions of chairman
of the defunct National Party of Nigeria (NPN) in the former Plateau
State, governorship candidate of the United Nigeria Congress Party
(UNCP), Nasarawa State and Minister of State for Works and Housing,
among others, before becoming the governor of the state.
He said of the situation: "From the word go, when I set out to seek
political office as the chief executive of the state, I never had any
dreams as to the tenure going to be a retreat. I never expected to
have time for pleasure and things like that, or to come here for
whatever goodies or pleasure one would get. Therefore, I was not
surprised to see the enormity of the challenges that confronted me as
soon as I was sworn in as governor".
The challenge? As a young state, Nasarawa is bound to experience some
teething developmental problems. Its rural nature and years of
neglect have been accentuated by the poor revenue base occasioned,
quite significantly, by its placement among the states that receive
the least statutory allocation. Denied of take-off grant on creation
as was the pattern earlier, the state could not make the initial
expected quantum leap in infrastructure development, particularly in
the capital.
Absence of self-help efforts in community development is also
pronounced. On another front, Governor Adamu characterised the
situation he met on ground: "Going through Nasarawa State, you don't
have a single private enterprise that is employing as many as 25
people. There is no company that is known to us that is actually into
production that is rolling anything worth N5 million as turnover in a
year".
The result of these are dearth of basic infrastructure, unemployment,
poverty and general underdevelopment. But the democratic dispensation
of the past two years has brought in a flurry of activities in the
state - political, economic, social and cultural activities - which
have improved the lives of the people and presented a ray of hope to
them.
Foremost in this direction is the administration's investment in
education. The sector has recorded a lot of improvement, including
the establishment of more institutions of learning - primary, post
primary and tertiary, improving the learning environment and welfare
of teachers.
The greatest investment in the sector appears to be in post primary
education. The last two years have marked the building of six new
science schools - two in each of the two senatorial zones; a boarding
school in each of the 13 councils; and general rehabilitation of all
schools in the state: a project that involved the building of new
classrooms and domitaries in schools that were hard pressed for
accommodation.
Some of the schools benefited from the provision of water and
electricity programme of the state government. Supply of free text
and exercise books as well as almost free boarding schools are also
part of the investment. The same applied at the primary school level.
The state was among the first to launch the Universal Basic Education
(UBE) scheme in the country.
A major aspect of the efforts put into tertiary education is the
establishment of a state polytechnic in Lafia, which admitted the
first batch of students in 2000/2001 session. A proposed university
will tentatively to take off this year.
A country-wide teachers recruitment exercise saw the engagement of
over 1000 new teachers into the secondary school system. Some
moral-boosting allowances were introduced for the teachers.
The administration's determination to go this far in the education
sector is described by Governor Adamu as a conscious effort he is
making to ensure that youths in the state are sufficiently equipped
for the future. And the result of the huge investment in education is
the degree of seriousness noticeable among pupils and teachers in the
school system.
The State Commissioner for Education, Dr. Emmanuel Okpede, also gave
an insight into the matter: "When we came into office in May 1999, we
were not left in doubt about the degree of neglect which education
had suffered. Nobody was left in doubt about the state of decay in
which the infrastructure had undergone. Nobody was also left in
oblivion as to the poor performance of our students in examinations",
he said, adding that the summary of the situation was that "in all
sectors of the economy, Nasarawa State had problem of manpower. In
all areas that required specialisation, we had problem there". He was
glad to note that "the situation has changed completely". Yes, it has
changed. For instance, candidates from the school system performed
brilliantly in the last Senior School Certificate and NECO
examinations.
The thrust of the rural electricity and water supply programme of the
administration is to, within the shortest possible time, reach as
many communities as possible, which have long expected a touch of
government. To this end, the electricity programme involved a
three-pronged approach, namely: the connection of some communities to
the national grid, use of generators and solar-powered equipment to
supply electricity to some others, particularly in the rural areas.
For a start, the administration was said to have earmarked such
projects for each of the 147 electoral wards in the state. Already
commissioned are solar-powered electricity projects at Kwara in
Wamba, Ungwan-Zaria in Akwanga, Mada Station in Nasarawa Egon, and
some others in Mararaba Gongon, Rifi Wamba, etc.
The water supply programme involved repair works on the Lafia Water
Scheme and rehabilitation of the gigantic Akwanga Water Scheme, which
serve many communities in the three senatorial zones of the state.
Solar-powered schemes planned for all the electoral wards have been
realised in over 20 areas so far. These include Wowen, Wulko, Mada
Station, Endehu, Masaka and Gora. Others are Marasaba, Agwada, GSS
Agwada, Wamba, Assakio, Agyragu, Marmara, Marasaba Udege and Giza.
Some others are awaiting commissioning.
However, some people have criticised the administration for embarking
on small-scale water and electricity schemes that serve few
communities, and have the higher rate of system breakdown. To these
people, major projects that cut across several communities, even
councils, would have served the state better.
But as Adamu put it, a government desirous to reach as many
communities - which are not ordinarily close-knit - as possible, and
which has lean resource, cannot afford to embark on white elephant
projects. In his words, "it is a deliberate policy to spread out and
maximise the reach".
In the health sector, the setting up of a referal centre by the
Federal Government and the policy of establishing a general hospital
in each of the councils and upgrading of clinics, have helped to
improve healthcare delivery in the state. The administration also
built two training institutions for health workers - a school of
nursing in Lafia and school of health technology in Keffi.
In Nasarawa, government is still the sole employer of labour as there
are no industries and businesses that attract a sizeable labour
force. The state government's efforts in the area of setting up of
industries are yet to mature as all the five industries the
administration has planned are at the foundation laying stage. These
include the packaging factory at Akwanga, whose foundation-laying
ceremony was conducted by the Minister of Industries, Chief Kola
Jamodu, and the ultra-modern beef processing factory at Masaka near
Abuja. Others are the fertilizer factory in Lafia, precious minerals
lapidary plant, Keffi, ropes production factory, Akwanga and
information technology centre, Lafia. The Karu International Market,
financed jointly by the government and Fortune Bank, is now letting.
Ignatius Aboki, senator in the Second Republic and former chairman of
the Bank of the North, commended the administration's efforts. His
words: "My impression is that the governor is quite energetic, quite
ambitious. He has laid down structures that will lift up the state".
The administration has also invested in other areas such as
agriculture, housing, roads, tourism, solid minerals, information and
sports. Of note is the introduction of a people's forum where, in
each senatorial zone, government officials and the people assemble to
exchange ideas on the administration of the state. Questions are
asked and answers given on government's policies and programmes.
Comments are also free.
According to the Information Commissioner, Labaran Maku, the people's
forum concept is akin to the community hall system whereby the people
and their leaders exchange ideas on the administration of their area.
The last of the forum was held in Keffi on April 10.
But Lafia, the state capital, is still to record significant
face-lift. Several new buildings, government buildings, housing
estates, secretariat, government house, etc., are springing up; but
the road network and environmental sanitation still leave much to be
desired. The administration has commenced the building of some
township roads, but the magnitude of the problem is such that
requires much more investment and a quality-driven construction
company.
The strides recorded in the past two years have been attributed to
the harmonious relationship existing among all the tiers of
government in the state. The administration's acceptance to pay the
minimum wage soon after it was proposed in May last year also worked
to ensure industrial peace in the state. This has also helped to
realise these projects some of which were executed by direct labour.
The crisis in Toto, between the Egburas and Bassas, has remained a
sore-point in the state till date. Elections have not been held in
the area. Few weeks ago, the administration set up a committee to
administer the council and ensure return of peace. It is hoped that
peace will eventually return there.
Expectations are high on what the government can do for the people.
Every community comes with thanks for what it has so far received or
promised, but with a bag full of requests for other needs that should
be addressed, the challenge of governing Nasarawa State is indeed
enormous.

Original_Search_Title: Nothing To Celebrate
Headline: Nigeria: Nothing To Celebrate
Body_Text:
If President Olusegun Obasanjo does not change his itinerary, he will
most probably miss activities marking the second anniversary of his
administration. He is expected to arrive the Asian country of
Indonesia on Monday, 28 May for a three day official visit to attend
the meeting of the G-15 group of developing countries. The president
may well consider the option of missing celebrating May 29 in
Nigeria. As it stands, Indonesia or any other country may prove a
more conducive place for him on that day. For many Nigerians, the
verdict on Obasanjo and his co-travellers in power is that of
resounding failure. Two years down the line, the much touted
dividends of democracy have not only failed to materialise for
millions of Nigerians, the living condition of the people have become
worse than it was under the military. Save for those in the corridors
of power, the two years of the current civilian rule has been full of
sorrow, tears and blood.
President Obasanjo knew what Nigerians wanted to hear on the day he
was sworn in. That day, after he took the mantle of leadership from
General Abdulsalami Abubakar, Obasanjo told Nigerians that "I am
determined with your full cooperation to make significant changes
within a year of my administration." One area where the people
expected the president to make a significant change is in the area of
power generation. Obasanjo himself knew this. He took a step towards
it by sacking the former helmsmen at the National Electric Power
Authority (NEPA). He made Chief Bola Ige (SAN), his minister of power
and steel and the former promised to turn stone into bread. An
initial promise of December 2000 was given by the administration for
Nigeria to achieve an uninterrupted supply of electricity. That
target has been shifted several times to accommodate NEPA's
inefficiency and the massive corruption in the organisation.
The 24-month-old administration of President Obasanjo has produced
five major NEPA system failure, an average of one in every four
months. With three hydro power stations and five thermal stations,
NEPA currently has an installed generating capacity of 6,000 mega
watts (MW) of electricity, what it gives the country now at best
hovers between 2,100 and 2,300MW, a far cry from the nation's minimum
capacity of 3,600. Again, the president set a target of 4000 MW by
the end of this year for NEPA but barely scratches the surface.
National President of the Institute of Directors (IOD), Mr. Emmanuel
Ijewere explained recently that 'even the 4000 megawatts would not
meet the demand." This is because Lagos State alone requires 4000MW.
All the state gets now is a miserable 800 megawatts. Speaking on why
NEPA has continually failed the nation, the incumbent minister for
power and steel, Dr. Segun Agagu puts the blame on capacity loss
suffered by the system due to poor maintenance and overhaul
schedules, prolonged neglect of equipment, seasonal fluctuations in
water levels at the major power stations and general indiscipline and
corruption by some NEPA officials. The last point is definitely the
most apt of them all. Even Obasanjo knows that himself and he has
been facing the challenge of putting the right man in charge of the
diseased NEPA.
Last year, he sacked Alhaji Bello Suliaman as the Managing Director
of NEPA, and placed the authority directly under the presidency. He
also appointed Engr. Joseph Makoju as the new M.D but all that has
failed to turn the power house fortunes around. Naturally, the
government lays most of the problems with NEPA at the door steps of
vandals, saying little about what happens to the millions that is
sunk into the organisation every year.
On 31 March 2001, the Federal Government read the riot act to
independent petroleum marketers and smugglers involved in the alleged
diversion of petroleum products to stop the act or face the full
weight of the law. The warning went on to say that marketers caught
in the process of fuel diversion shall be charged for economic
sabotage. Those who fail to deliver fuel lifted within three days
shall have their licences withdrawn and prosecuted accordingly. That
has, however, failed to ease the pains of fuel scarcity. With the
Obasanjo administration bent on deregulating the downstream petroleum
sector, the country has been thrown into the excruciating grip of
fuel scarcity. Last June, President Obasanjo frittered away so much
good will from the citizens when his government jacked the price of
petrol from N20 to N30. It took a well coordinated civil disobedience
spearheaded by the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), to get the
government to rescind its decision. Even then, a compromise was
reached and to save the Federal Government from total humiliation,
the price was pegged at the current official rate of N22. But the
government was not done yet.
It soon emerged with a campaign of appropriate pricing championed by
information minister, Prof. Jerry Gana and Philip Asiodu, the
president adviser on economic matters. To curb smuggling and increase
efficiency, the government is toying with the idea of further
increasing the price of petroleum products which will invariably
further emasculate the citizens. President Obasanjo gave an insight
into what his government has in stock for Nigeria when he said the
price of petrol will not exceed N40 when the oil sector is
deregulated. Deregulation, the government argues, will produce a
permanent solution to the scarcity of fuel and make it unattractive
to smugglers. Herein lies the cause of the present fuel scarcity.
Expectedly, the unavailability of fuel has affected all sectors of
life. The most noticeable is the price of foodstuff and the cost of
transportation.
The most glaring evidence of the failure of the Obasanjo government
is in the food sector. Almost all the staple food stuff common to the
common Nigerian has been priced out of their reach. Gari which used
to be the last resort of the impoverished masses has become gold
dust. With more people falling out of the elite class into the lower
rungs of existence, the demand on gari and other items like beans and
yam has increased geometrically. The result is the out pricing of the
products from the table of most Nigerians. A bag of gari which sold
for N1,000 on 29 May 1999, now attracts over N3,000 as Obasanjo gets
ready to celebrate his second year in office. Beans which was N4,600
per bay at his inauguration now cost about N7,100. No wonder a lowly
country like Burkina Faso, whose land mass is not big enough to
produce food crop for Nigeria could offer to export beans here. Apart
from the neglect of the agricultural sector by Obasanjo, who combines
his tenancy in Aso Rock with farming in Otta, the collapse of the
energy sector in Nigeria has made food production difficult. The
country now depend on importation of such staples from other
countries.
While Nigerians groan under ever rising high cost of living, official
corruption continues unabated despite the government's
anti-corruption law. An opinion poll conducted by a national daily
fingered corruption, unemployment and bad leadership as the worst
problems bedevilling the country's development. Little wonder that
Transparency International, ranked Nigeria as the most corrupt
country in the world. The position is an unenviable leap from the
27th slot that the country occupied in 1999 when this democratic
experience started. On his part, Dr. Pat Utomi, a business expert
blamed weak institutions, poor management and reward system as the
reason for the high incidence of corruption in Nigeria.
One other area where the president promised to work on during his
inauguration is that of unemployment. According to statistics
released by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), over 60
per cent of Nigerians are jobless while factories run at less than 30
per cent capacity. While Jerry Gana says that the government has so
far spent N125 million on job creation, his counterpart in the
ministry of women affairs and youths development, Hajia Aisha Ismail,
revealed that over 60 million Nigerians are unemployed. That
represents more than half of the 1999 government census of the total
population of Nigerian. According to the National Manpower Board,
about 5.66 million graduates were unemployed in 2000. The figure is
expected to hit 10 million by 2002. A smoke screen scheme, Poverty
Alleviation Programme (PAP), was launched last year by the government
to provide self employment N10 billion was budgeted for the scheme in
2000 and at the end, it failed woefully for all the administration
did with the money was to use it to pacify its party followers.
Hand in hand with the problems of unemployment is the increase in
violent crimes. No class of Nigerian is insulated from the growing
menace of armed robbers. From the National Chairman of the People's
Democratic Party (PDP), Chief Barnabas Gemade to popular actress, Liz
Benson, Nigerians daily live in fear of armed robbers. One month
after the celebration of one year of democracy, dare devil robbers
stormed the Otta farm of President Obasanjo, shot and killed a driver
before finally driving away with a Toyota Jeep that belongs to the
President's African Leadership Forum. Increasingly, men in police
uniforms are arrested for robbery. Recently, three policemen were
sentenced to death for robbing and killing Katsina State bound
traders.
Not content with destroying its present, the country is also steadily
destroying its future. Although, the Federal Government has launched
the Universal Basic Education (UBE), the quality of students produced
in the country is appalling. Presently, the Universities have been
closed for the umpteenth time because lecturers are on strike. When
the schools manage to open, the infrastructure in them are not only
grossly inadequate, they are not properly used by the teachers who
themselves were not properly taught. As the count down to 29 May 2001
draws near, most Nigerians have no plans to celebrate. Like it
happened last year, only those in power see something to celebrate.
For the masses, they have a little praise for the government.
Stripped of their security details, what the ruling class will get
from the citizens come May 29 are deafening jeers.
.Additional reports by: Adesina Abiodun, Jide Olatundun, Okwu
Timothy, Dickson Offre and Olusegun Ajiboye.

Original_Search_Title: Why Agriculture Deserves More Attention
Headline: Nigeria: Why Agriculture Deserves More Attention
Body_Text:
There is not much to write home about economic policy of the Obasanjo
administration because not much has happened. I have been very
critical that the economy is still sluggish. I should have thought
that we would have paid greater attention to the development of
agriculture and agro-allied industry. There are a few examples that
one can easily call to mind. You know in this country, we can grow
all the sugar we need and save ourselves from importation of sugar.
Some years back, we started the paper industry and newsprint plant in
Akwa Ibom was established. We can revive them very quickly. In
Iwopin, another paper plant was developed to produce culture paper
which did not see the light of the day up till now. In Jebba, another
one was developing.
It was also done in craft and industrial paper. Then, we have the
sugar factory in Baccita and all these can be revamped. The
plantations-palm plantation, rubber plantation, cocoa plantation and
varieties of tree plantations can be encouraged. There is, of course,
the normal production of staples like yam, cassava, maize, rice,
beans, guinea-corn etc. If we can put things in motion and produce
these things in large quantities we will get more people employed.
And the contribution of agriculture to the Gross Domestic Product
(GDP) is very high. I think it is still higher than that of petroleum
products. And no country can in the real sense develop without
developing agriculture. We have to be able to feed ourselves and we
are meant to do this. Until and unless we pay attention to thisñ
there are, of course, other areas such as the petro-chemical industry
and the oil and gasñ we are not likely to make much progress. The
refineries should be able to work too.
There are a lot of by-products we can get from the petro-chemicals;
instead of importing the raw materials that we use in our plastic
industry, we can produce the raw materials here. The fertilizer
companies can be expanded. All these are issues that the government
ought to pay attention to. If we come to the cement industry, why
should a bag of cement cost more than N1,000 when deposits of
limestone are in Nigeria and evenly spread everywhere. We have
millions of tons of depositsñ we have them in Sokoto, in Kogi, around
Lokoja area, Nkalagu, Ewekoro, Shagamu, Ukpela and there are lot of
other areas. So, we can produce the cement we require. The government
can improve infrastructures and expose the industry for high
production. The commodity can be exported and if we cannot export, at
least, we are self-sufficient and we would keep the price low.These
are a few examples.
I supported Obasanjo and I supported his election, but the issue is
that his hands are tied. There are lot of things that he cannot on
his own do, under the present circumstances. Let me give a few
examples. Here, in our country, we never see any policy that endures
for long. We keep on changing, you lay foundation, and the
expectation is that people who come after you should build upon that
foundation. But, in the Nigerian situation, we keep on changing and
keep on uprooting the foundation.
Somebody was telling me recently that the Indonesian minister for
finance had been in that position for 15 years or so. We need this
kind of continuation, where is the continuation in Nigeria? We keep
on changing everything overnight. And then we want it to go roundñ
that everybody must have a chance whether they are capable of doing
it or not. President Obasanjo, to that extent, is limited. Take the
issue of external borrowing. He gave state governments such a free
hand to borrow money offshore and many of them borrowed such money to
build hotels and that kind of thing. Which is quite unnecessary. Look
at the time of Abacha; they started improving the railways but what
has happened to it today? In the time of Abacha again, they invited
the Petroleum Trust Fund (PTF) and we got roads improved. I know for
certainty that there is no part of Nigeria the PTF did not have some
impact. I can recall Mile 2 in Lagos, Apapa, Ikorodu Road and various
areas. In water supply, there was also improvement, like the impact
in 1004, Ikoyi and Ijora. The PTF got involved in those places, but
as soon as this government took over power, nothing did they do but
to criticise PTF, for what? Why not examine what that organ had done
and build on it? You see, these are some of the problems that we are
facing. We have to be patient, but then we always want things done
overnight. And that is why we talk of dividend of democracy what
dividend do you want? If we are peaceful and development is going on
smoothly, quietly is that not enough? So, this talk of dividend of
democracy is not existing. I don't believe in the concept of dividend
of democracy. I think we should rather have a peaceful and orderly
development. Can you imagine how we are going to conduct the next
elections. Is it going to be easy from the way things are moving?
This is the problem people ought to address their minds to rather
than expect something from nothing. If you don't work for democracy,
why do you expect dividend from it? Are we working for democracy in
this country, the way people are doing things as they like? Look at a
country like China; look at how it has developed, even Southeast Asia
in recent years. Do you know that at the time of independence, we
were far ahead of Malaysia and Indonesia, but they are now
approaching First World status. Why are we still blaming ourselves,
struggling over the development of basic infrastructures? We build
roads, we don't maintain them well. Which town in Nigeria has
adequate water supply scheme and why should it be so? And if people
don't get good water, how can they be healthy and productive? But, we
keep on floating higher ideas and doing things that we shouldn't be
doing.
I have not travelled to the council areas. But, from the general
information everybody seems to have, we could have done better than
we have either at the council level, state or federal levels. Maybe
our attention has been diverted in dealing with political problems,
which are valid. I mean our leaders have that problem to deal with.
And they cannot just set aside their political claims from different
communities on political events. So, from that point of view, one
must be very understanding of the problem the authority faces. But,
having said so, with these large amounts of money that accrue to
states from the federation accountsñ I believe there are states down
south that get billions of naira monthly and I don't know how much of
that money has gone into the development of infrastructures,
expansion of agricultural projects and industry. But from all
accounts, there does not seem to be much praise for wisdom and
prudence in utilizing the resources at their disposal.
I have seen the amount of good work governor Makarfi (of Kaduna
State) has achieved. The simple one is the roads. Everybody talks
about it; you know there are areas here in Kaduna about which a lot
of people had given up any hope concerning road repairs, but they
have now got those roads constructed. So, the general improvement on
roads is acknowledged by everybody. I think he is also doing well in
education and water supply. These are of great importance to the
people. I don't know what other governors are doing, certainly Alhaji
Makarfi should be given credit here. He also brought peace, because
the creation of administrative organs at all levels certainly tended
to bring everybody on board. And generally speaking the people in
Kaduna are happier today.
If we can put things in motion and produce these things in large
quantities we will get more people employed. And the contribution of
agriculture to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is very high. I think
it is still higher than that of petroleum products. And no country
can in the real sense develop without developing agriculture. We have
to be able to feed ourselves and we are meant to do this. Until and
unless we pay attention to thisñ there are, of course, other areas
such as the petro-chemical industry and the oil and gasñ we are not
likely to make much progress. The refineries should be able to work
too.
There are a lot of by-products we can get from the petro-chemicals;
instead of importing the raw materials that we use in our plastic
industry, we can produce the raw materials here. The fertilizer
companies can be expanded. All these are issues that the government
ought to pay attention to. If we come to the cement industry, why
should a bag of cement cost more than N1,000 when deposits of
limestone are in Nigeria and evenly spread everywhere. We have
millions of tons of depositsñ we have them in Sokoto, in Kogi, around
Lokoja area, Nkalagu, Ewekoro, Shagamu, Ukpela and there are lot of
other areas. So, we can produce the cement we require. The government
can improve infrastructures and expose the industry for high
production.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Score Card: Hunger, Poverty Grips Country
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Score Card: Hunger, Poverty Grips Country
Body_Text:
Lagos — Midway into the first term of the democratic experiment and the
tenure of the PDP-led Federal Government of President Olusegun
Obasanjo, Nigerians sink deeper into hunger and hardship. PHILIPSON
ABAH writes
Exactly two years ago, Abuja and all the thirty six state capitals
were aglow with ceremonies of handing over by military to a
democratically elected government. Parades were mounted by members of
the armed forces and cultural performances were staged on the
historic day.
To mark the day, long speeches sugar-coated in the most alluring
prose were read to Nigerians who applauded thunderously at all venues
of the ceremony or from the comfort of their homes where they watched
the live proceedings of the function at Abuja and their various
states on television.
That day, Nigerians were not clapping with anxiety because of the
melodious voices of the people reading the speeches. The rounds of
applause were induced by the hope of a better, hunger-free and
comfortable Nigeria painted in the inaugural speeches.
Now, the same Nigerians who were so excited as a resullt of those
promises have discovered that it was just another antic of
politicians. During his inaugural speech, President Olusegun Obasanjo
promised to change the face of the country in one year. Specifically,
he emphasised without any display of cynicism, that his government
would, among many other things, provide for the suffering masses of
this country; food security through sound agricultural policies,
create and enabling environment for investments create jobs and also
introduce policies that would stimulate macro economic growth. This
same promises were replicated in the states by the various state
governors.
The hope of Nigerians were therefore brightened by these promises.
For a start, it appeared as if all was going to be well. With some
pronouncements on the operations of government parastatals like NEPA,
and NITEL, the introduction of the Poverty Alleviation Programme
(PAP), and some moves that looked as if targetted at provoking a
horde of investors into the country, Nigerians started to count their
eggs before they were hatched.
The hope for better living was further heightened when, in May last
year, President Obasanjo announced a minimum wage of N7500 to
government workers. Civil servants who had for years been pauperised
by insulting wages lauded the move.
That the government of President Obasanjo has lost focus, began to
dawn on Nigerians when firstly, the Poverty Alleviation Programme was
hijacked by some PDP stalwarts who milked the nation dry through it,
thereby diminishing its essence. Thousands of jobless youths who were
kept waiting for the PAP manna became more frustrated, having waited
endlessly.
Instead of taking adequate care of other variables before considering
the issue of fuel pricing, Obasanjo's government surprised Nigerians
with a directive to the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation
(NNPC) to commence the sale of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) at N30 from
its former price of N20. Though the Nigerian Labour Congress
ferociously battled with the government which finally slashed the
price. The move precipitated a chain of reaction in the market place.
Apart from the occasional fuel scarcity which was initially thought
to have gone with the military regime, transport fares all over the
country went up. The spiralling effect of this became conspicuous on
the prices of foodstuff which began an upward rise in price.
Yet again, the government began moves to endorse the deregulation of
the down stream sub-sector of the oil industry, not minding what
effect it may have on the domestic economy.
Garri, a food item considered the most affordable for the Nigerian
masses began to soar in price. Today, a bag of garri which used to
cost about N1500 now goes for as high as N4,600. A bag of imported
rice which was selling for N2,400 is now N3,500, signalling an
impending food crisis.
In the face of all these, the value of the naira continues to dip
with the government watching in complete helplessness.
Nigerians, who before the emergence of the civilian government were
groaning under poverty are today worse off as the grip of misery
continues to tighten around their necks. More than ever before,
joblessness is on the increase as the governments at various levels
have sacked more persons than they have employed since they came to
power.
NEPA's endemic problem and the crisis in the oil industry, including
other problems associated with the dearth of infrastructure, have
made it increasingly difficult for companies to survive, thus forcing
them to embark on retrenchment in the guise of restructuring.
The man at the helm of affairs does not feel ruffled by this
developments. Instead, all he does to keep himself busy is to shuttle
the entire globe. If P.M.News checks are correct, President Obasanjo
has made 45 trips since his assumption of office. Obasanjo's claim
that his trips are to solicit for foreign investments, amongst other
reasons, have already lost meaning as investigations have shown that
apart from few construction and service rendering firms, the
country's economic climate remains far from being attractive to
foreign investors who are said to be scared of the rising wave of
crime in the country and the high cost of investment following NEPA
and telecommunication inefficiency coupled also with the bad roads
that litter every part of the country. Another problem which
discourages foreign investors, it was gathered, is the absence of a
good transportation network.
Sadly, the president appears not to be admitting that these are part
of the problems hindering economic prosperity of the country. During
this year's May Day celebration, President Obasanjo told a
hungry-looking gathering of Nigerians that the option out of the
economic doldrums is for people to de-emphasise foreign products and
patronise locally made ones. This statement portrays him as a leader
who does not know that in his country, a common barbing salon can not
operate effectively without a generator; the least of which cost over
N50,000.
Recently, government officials, lauding Obasanjo's achievements in
the last two years pointed to the increased foreign reserve of the
country which they claimed has moved from $4b to about $7b. This, to
the majority of Nigerians who are wallowing in penury, does not mean
anything as it has not helped to put any meal on their table at a
cheaper cost.
With the depressing economic situation, not a few people affirm that
the Obasanjo government has not met the aspirations of Nigerians.
This opinion is shared by some objective privileged Nigerians as well
as the most indigent obscure ones who are now living as endangered
species.
According to Air Comdr. Dan Suleiman, a retired top military officer,
who spoke on the two years anniversary of President Obasanjo's
government, "talking about the fruits of democracy, which in essence
means the economic and general well being of the people, I think
there is a serious shortfall here. A short fall in the sense that the
infrastructure that ought to be in place are yet to be firmly in
place."
A house wife, Mrs. Gloria Onyemachi, painted the picture of the agony
of the woman at home in this austere period. "With the current
situation, one can not buy anything from the market. The last time I
bought garri, it was N50, when I got to the market yesterday, it was
N250. One can not buy other things like yam or plantain. We are
talking of food which is a basic requirement of life. I pity the
woman who is under pressure from her husband and the children. For
me, I have not seen or felt what they call democratic dividend. I
also do not think that Obasanjo has any plans for us," she said.
However, despite the outcry against what is now considered as
Obasanjo's failure, his supporters are pleading for time. Two years
have discretely slipped by and the hope that was visible in the
horizon is gradually getting lost in the cloud.
Will Obasanjo, after taking stock of his mid-term report, hasten to
extend to Nigerians the well deserved democratic dividends.
Obasanjo himself does not seem to be honest in appraising himself as
he has immodestly awarded a pass mark to his government, giving very
frivolous explanations to the rising cost of foodstuffs and indeed
every item in the market.
However, the truth is that: "he who knows it, feels it." And so, the
verdict of the people is that Obasanjo has failed at least in his
first two years.

Original_Search_Title: A Town of Four Kings
Headline: Nigeria: A Town of Four Kings
Body_Text:
Oke-agbe in Ondo State gives a lie to the Yoruba proverb that two
heads cannot rule a town at the same time, writes Olusegun Ajiboye.
Oke-agbe does not hide its rural ways. In the headquarters of Akoko
North-West Local Government Area, men, women and children file back
from the stream with water laden clay pots. From the goats that walk
on the tarmac to the people there is an ordered and unhurried pace to
life.
Oke-agbe, literally, "the hills shall be our boundaries" is a
conubation with a difference. Unlike in towns growing into
shoulder-lock with each other or swallowing up adjoining villages,
Oke-agbe came about its own way-everything planned and executed.
Its origin is not shrouded in the dim past but mercifully in the
early 1900s. As Pa Claudius Olanipekun Akorede, an 86-year-old native
told TEMPOLife, the story of Oke-agbe began in 1920. Nine different
communities had seen the wisdom in size and had begun talking on how
to abandon their old settlements and form a mega-settlement that
would mitigate the calamities small communities suffer in times of
war.
But only four communities followed through-Afa, Aje, Ido and Oge. The
hills they thought would be their boundaries soon saw homes on them.
Today, Oke-agbe is one seamless community with no demarcation-
natural or man made.
Yet, demarcations exist. Each of the communities is styled a kingdom.
So, Oke-agbe is made up of four kingdoms, each independent of the
other and none any less glamorous. Oke-agbe is a peculiar place. None
of the four kings reside in the town. The king nearest home lives in
Lagos. As Chief Aremo of Afa told TEMPOLife, "the kings are young and
educated men who are in their prime, hence the decisions to appoint
regents for them to help in the day-to-day activities of the town in
their absence." He added that these stay-away monarchs visit every
fortnight and are required by custom to be present at every festival.
There have been attempts at uniting these kingdoms by at least
getting one paramount ruler. But all such attempts have foundered
because the communities guard their independence jealously. "We don't
see ourselves throwing away our independence in the future," Aremo
asserted.
Of the four kingdoms that make up Oke-agbe, Oge is the only one with
a crown from Ile-Ife. On the origin of Oge, Pa Akorede has this to
say, "my fathers came from Ile-Ife. They left Ife at the same time
with other Yoruba towns such as Ajero, Oyo and so many other notable
Yoruba towns."
On the origin of Aje town, the Ewi of Aje, Chief Michael Oloinyo says
it can be traced to Ado-Ekiti. A pointer to this is the title of the
king which is the same as that of Ado-Ekiti. They both use the Ewi
title. This suggests that there is a link between the people of Aje
and Ado-Ekiti. Equally, their festivals are quite similar to those of
Ado-Ekiti.
The story of Afa as told by Chief Aremo, a retired teacher of repute
who has travelled far and near is a very rich one. The Afa comprises
several quarters with three principal ones, namely; Ogosi, Ogunna and
Ogotun with each claiming to have come from different places.
Chief Aremo stated categorically that the Ogotun quarters can be
linked to a town in Akoko North Local Government Area. The story of
the Ogunna people is said to be complex. Tradition has it that the
people had problems with their siblings over who should possess the
crown, a situation which led "our fathers to leave Owo." Chief Aremo
went further to say that the cognomen of the people is similar to
that of the Owo people. To give credence to this story he said, "you
see, the second in rank to the Olowo bears the same title with our
Oba, that is Ajana."
The Ido people of Oke-agbe have their own rich history too. According
to Prince E.O. Ojomu of the present ruling house in Ido, Oke-agbe,
"prior to 1924, the four towns lived in different parts, and because
of the inter-tribal wars which plagued the area in that period, the
people decided to come together to form a common front." He believes
his people came from the Igashi axis of Akoko. In the present day
Ondo State, according to him, the peaceful co-existence which has
prevailed since the inception of the town is based on trust among the
four settlements which make up Oke-agbe.
Cultural festivals are celebrated separately at different period of
the year by the four settlements. For example, the egungun festival
is celebrated by each settlement at different period of the year. The
only festival which enjoy universal celebration in the town are those
of the Christian and Muslim faiths.
The oldest church in Oke-agbe is the Saint George's Anglican Church,
built in 1929. The church is, however, enmeshed in controversy. The
Ido people on whose part of the settlement the church is located
claim ownership of the church. They also base their argument on the
claim that their son, the late Reverend Jacob Jegede together with
Reverend L.A. Lenon, a Jamaican brought the church to Oke-agbe.
The other settlement do not agree with Ido on the ownership of the
church. The regent of Aje, Chief Oloniyo told TEMPOLife that the
church does not belong to any particular quarter in Oke-agbe.
According to her, "if anybody should lay claim to Saint George's
Anglican Church, then that person should be Reverend Lenon."
Majority of the people of Oke-agbe are subsistence farmers. The men
do most of the farming, ably assisted by their wives and children.
They produce mostly food crops like yam, beans and cocoyam. Richer
farmers produce cash crops like cocoa and coffee.
The Aje women are professional clay pot makers. Regent of Aje, Chief
Michael Oloniyo avers that "our women are gifted in pot making, as
long as she is born of Aje parent, she does not have to learn it or
undergo any training."
Apart from some individual efforts to develop the town, there are
numerous clubs and societies that come together from time to time to
sponsor developmental projects. One of such projects is the
beautification of the town's roundabout. Tell tale signs of the
defunct Petroleum Trust Fund (PTF), is noticeable in Oke-agbe, as it
is in all of the Akoko region. The one man whose name always rings
bell in Oke-agbe is the town's most illustrious son, Chief Rufus
Giwa, the former chairman of Lever Brothers Nigeria Plc. As the head
of PTF in the zone, he used his influence to make sure that the only
road that leads to Akoko from Akure via Owo was tarred, and the tiny
colonial bridge, which had actually become a death trap was abandoned
and a new one constructed.
It is in realisation of this and other developmental projects carried
out by Chief Rufus Giwa that the four kingdoms came together to make
him the Aro. He hails from the Afa quarters of the town.
In this respect, Chief Rufus Giwa, is the only indigene of Oke-agbe
whose title transcends quarters. He is not just an Afa chief, but his
title cuts across the entire town.

Original_Search_Title: Our Wives the Magicians
Headline: Nigeria: Our Wives the Magicians
Body_Text:
I never really knew the depths into which we have sunk until two weeks back, when on my way back from the village, we made the usual stop over at Mosogar and Oghara to buy home made garri, a must eat component of my daily diet. Well, let me rephrase that. I knew prices of goods, especially, foodstuff were hitting the roof-tops, but I never suspected they had already burst through the roofs and are already aiming at the heavenly firmaments.
Since in bargaining, I am what the Yoruba traders would gladly call an alaroro, an astute bargainer, Christie, the best wife in the whole world, tactically stayed behind for a moment. And it never crossed my mind that that was her own way of letting me into the burden, she and I believe most Nigerian wives now bear on a daily basis - how to juggle the food budget to chase away kwashiorkor from their homes.
I pointed at an average sized bag, about the size we used to buy for N400. The sharp, confident reply from the lady incharge was N4000.
"Four thousand what?" I screamed. I drove off, but the next stop was not better. The lady simply said:
Oga, na N4000 last, with a large dose of mishief and sarcasm.
"You be thief, how you go say this small garri na N4000 last?" I replied, raising my voice involuntarily.
Unperturbed, she turned her back and headed back to the wooden stool, from which she had, a moment before, come to negotiate with me. But not before she had thrown in a deft uppercut: "Oga, you no dey dis Nigeria? E don tay wey garri price don go up. In fact, dis size na four-five (N4,500) we dey sellam; na bicos you speak our language naim even make I say make you pay N4000"
"Darling" my wife suddenly materialised beside me, pouring iced water on the anger threatening to burst over, "that is about what a bag of garri of this size now sells for; garri is very expensive now."
Haggle as we would, compromise was still 1000 per cent above what we paid six months back. As we say in Warri, no do, no do, we ended up paying N3200 for the tiny quantity which would probably last for one month.
I emerged from that encounter looking like the whole world had crumbled on my back; it made me look so foolish. Indeed, I felt like Alhaji Bashir Tofa who did not know how much a litre of petrol sold for during his presidential debate with Chief M.K.O. Abiola in 1993; or maybe like President Olusegun Obasanjo who did not know that the Naira now exchanges for about N140 to an American dollar; nor at that, what the combined refining capacity of our refineries is.
As I was to be later educated both by my wife and traders at the various places we had stopped to pick one item or the other, yam, plantain, fish, meat and even kolanuts have all joined the luxury flight destined for the homes of only Senators, House of Assembly members, Ministers, Special Advisers and all those whose lives are cushioned by one government office or the other.
It is perhaps only this set of people who do not feel the crunch, who do not know that there is a serious food crisis in the country. It is this level of what Professor Sylvanus Ekwelie of the University of Nigera would describe as voluminous ignorance, it is possibly this loss of touch with the realities of the common man in Nigeria that prompted one of President Olusegun Obasanjo's men to insult the intelligence of Nigerians when he said that the prices of garri, a common staple in the South, was soaring because it was then the dry season, that farmers were finding it hard to harvest cassava tubers because of the hard soil.
It is perhaps based on an advise from an ignoramus like this that President Obasanjo recently decided to recast himself as Emperor Nero's wife, who when she heard that the people were about dying of hunger because there was no bread to eat, promptly replied: "if there is no bread, let them eat cake!"
As it turned out, President Obasanjo, until we shouted him down, wanted us to eat Burkina Faso beans if we could no longer afford our regular staple foods.
The positive side of my encounter with the garri sellers of Delta State is that I have developed more respect for my wife and all Nigerian women. It also enabled me to know the true meaning of ghost workers. It dawned on me, suddenly, that we have a ghost ministry in the current government. You know, something like the non-existent committee which the Senate President, Anyim Pius Anyim, authorised some couple of hundred millions for the other time.
I am talking of what should properly be known as the Ghost Federal Ministry of Agriculture, which has a ghost minister, ghost parastatals, ghost permanent secretaries and officers down the line. It is only in a ghost ministry like this that billions of Naira could have disappeared so effectively in the last two years, impacting on the country the worst food crisis since the civil war.
It would not be a bad idea, assuming the so called anti-corruption tribunal set up by President Obasanjo is not a ghost tribunal, to start with successive ministers who have manned this ghost ministry over the years. At least, let them come and tell us what ghost projects they spent the more than N100 billion they received in the last five years on.
As we continued our journey, I looked often at my wife, who has had to take the brunt of my anger in recent times when she, in hindsight now, was forced to request for more funds to run the home. I had usually criticised her management of funds, not minding her protest that prices of foodstuffs have gone through the roof. The first inkling I had was when I was told that a can of 450gms Bournvita now sells for N420. And that a loaf of bread now goes for N100.
At a stage, I looked at her again and blurted out, my voice full of apology: "how have you been managing?" All she could do was give me a sad, yet understanding smile and a shrug of her shoulders.
Further discussions was to reveal clearly to me that, as a nation, we do not need to look too far now to see the best financial managers in Nigeria. Our wives are. They are simply magicians.

Original_Search_Title: Why Is Gari Expensive?
Headline: Nigeria: Why Is Gari Expensive?
Body_Text:
Why gari is scarce and expensive-Adams Idufueko, Chairman, Agricultural and Non-oil Trade Group, LCCI.
We presently seem to be faced with a food crisis arising from the high cost of gari, the supposedly common man's staple and other food items. What do you think is responsible for the problem?
To start with, we can look at the problem as it exists at present. There are two things that account for the high price of basic food stuffs like gari, yam, plantain, etc. In 1999, there was bumper harvest of cereals - maize and guinea corn and so on in the North. And so the price collapsed and food stuff became very cheap in the market.
So, last year, most of the Northern growers of maize, guinea corn, the basic grains refused to plant or planted very little. Consequently, supply became erratic and at the same time, because the planting wasn't high enough, it was obvious that there will be crisis; crisis because the prices were rising even during the harvest so that by January, February this year, the prices have gone high.
Now the only food stuff that is readily available happens to be gari.
