Expectedly, organised labour is in the forefront, the masses of Nigeria queuing up behind it in opposition to the vexatious matter. What this contribution is about is to state that the argument for and against deregulation is a combination of facts, fallacies and emotions from both sides of the divide. My contribution therefore, is to state the issues involved in oil pricing so that the debate may have some focus.
Nigeria operated free market price system in oil between 1960 up to about 1973. Also, in 1997, after General Sani Abacha, then Head of State joined the World Trade Organisation, he deregulated. As in the previous free price era of 1960-1973, the 1997 deregulation did not achieve the expected result. Parity prices of imported petroleum products made importation unprofitable and the marketers stopped importation. A current and still existing experience is the deregulation of (LPG) Liquefied Petroleum Gas, that is, the domestic cooking gas. The price of gas has reached the roof at about N1,800 per 12.5kg cylinder. Yet, it is not available in the market for the few that could afford it.
Have we asked why the two experiences in deregulation and the free play of market forces failed? We should even ask why the deregulated (LPG) domestic cooking gas supply now is failing to flood Nigerian market with domestic cooking gas. Perhaps, part of the answer lies in the fact that the handling facilities at the Nigerian ports are not designed to handle massive importation of such petroleum products. The importers need the off-loading, transportation and storage facilities that are not available and which cannot be provided by the simple deregulation cry, otherwise, they would have been provided in 1960s and 1970s and would have been provided in 1997-1998 and, for the domestic cooking gas, such equipment should have been provided since deregulation in 1997. There are therefore, bottlenecks that deregulation and market prices have failed to remove. There is no certainty that they would be solved by the announced programmed of deregulation.
In Nigeria, we are endowed by God with oil resources. The economic principle of the law of comparative advantage dictates that Nigeria should produce, consume and export the excess of her need in exchange for goods and services that she is less endowed by God to produce. The argument that Nigeria should sell petroleum products in Nigeria at the price in Europe or even other non-oil endowed Africa countries, ignores the economic principle of the law of comparative advantage.
The case of the deregulations is that:
Petroleum products are being smuggled out through our porous borders, and if the price is raised to the price at the border countries, then incentive to smuggle will cease;
Petroleum products that are in short supply will become sufficient and perhaps more than needed in Nigeria. This will lead to competitive prices and possibly lower prices after the initial high prices; and,
That the profit margin shall be enhanced and that will invite investors to the sector to the overall benefit of the economy.
The case is persuasive, but to my mind, fallacious. Nigeria employs all sorts of security personnel to guard her borders. These include the customs officers, the immigration officers, the navy, the police, the air force and other services. They are paid from taxes paid by Nigerians. The government seems to be admitting that these employees are not doing their work and still should keep such jobs and continue to be paid from the taxes being paid by Nigerians. Government is also saying Nigerians should pay border prices so as to prevent smuggling. Nigerians are therefore, being invited to undertake double expenditure - one to keep ineffective security officers who cannot protect our borders - and the other, pay for that failure in high prices of petroleum products that are being smuggled. There must be a way to eliminate one of these two expenditures. Nigerians should not be invited to pay the two bills. The factor that is responsible for smuggling of oil products is not price differential between Nigeria and her neighbours. The craze for U.S. dollars at any price is the reason that encourages smuggling of oil and any other exportable products. Experience from the cocoa trade is a classical evidence that price increase has no role in smuggling. After the abolition of the marketing boards, the commodity trade in Nigeria was totally deregulated in 1986. Cocoa was bought from the farmers at the Naira price that was higher than world price in cocoa at the then exchange rate of the Naira to the U.S. dollar or British Pound Sterling. The foreigners who were determined to repatriate their investment in Nigerian bought cocoa at the price they could not sell it in export market. They were desperate to repatriates their investment. Capital flight then and at this uncertain period of the Naira exchange rates was responsible and will continue to facilitate the smuggling of petroleum products. Nigerians of substance that want to keep their wealth in foreign currency will not stop smuggling oil no matter the deregulation and high prices that will follow. We will only succeed in raising the poverty level and hardship of Nigerians. The second argument that petroleum products will be made abundantly available by deregulation is not supported by the experiences of 1960-1973 and 1997 when petroleum products were deregulated nor is it supported by our current experience in domestic cooking gas which should not sell for more than N600 per 12.5kg cylinder but which, if you are lucky to get, you will pay N1,800 or more.
The third plank of the deregulations is higher profit margin. That was not the experience in the two periods when free market price operated.
Before we examine the case of subsidy, we need to define what subsidy is. In economic principle, a situation of subsidy exists when consumers are assisted by the government to pay less than the market price for the product they are consuming. Example is payment for medicine by the government for poor citizens, payment for food items that the populace cannot afford but are required for their subsistence. Other form of subsidy is the payment to producers of some commodities by the government not to produce at all or to augment their income when the price of their product is less than break-even point. In economic principle, a producer of a commodity will go on until his cost of production equals his marginal price. There is, therefore, no subsidy where the barrel of crude petroleum delivered to our Nigerian refineries covers the cost of production of that drum. At the moment, the cost of producing a barrel of crude oil is less than $9. The argument for subsidy will not arise until the cost of production is less than the delivery price to our refinery.
The point of the deregulations is that opportunity cost. The opportunity cost of exporting a barrel of crude oil is $30. On the basis of opportunity cost, therefore, the deregulations insist that $20.5 is the subsidy per barrel on the crude oil refined for consumption in Nigeria. In my view, this definition of subsidy ignores the economic principle earlier stated. A producer exports the excess of his need - a farmer will feed first from his farm maize or yam, before selling the balance. This is not a theoretical economic principle. It accords with human behaviour and experience. A farmer will not first export his yam to Europe and re-import it for his own consumption at the price that will now include freight, import duty, port surcharge and other government's payment including handling and transportation cost. This is not in my view, deregulation and it should not be.
The general comment on the topic is that petroleum products are so crucial and central to almost all other domestic economic and social activities. If deregulation is to raise the price in the uncertain hope that prices will adjust to its own competitive level at a point, that point may be the point at which Keynes says, "We will all be dead".
The Nigeria government must look at the earing capacity of Nigerians before plunging into this deregulation menace. The earing of a Nigerian best paid worker at N2 million a year amounts to less than U.S. $17,000 at today's Niara exchange rate.
An average worker in Europe earns some U.S. $25 - 30, 000. An unemployed European earns more than the best paid Nigerian from unemployment benefits. An European has easy means of transportation.
He does not need to own a car there are buses and city trains and other means that both the rich and the poor patronise.
By comparison, these facilities are not available to Nigerians. Many of the workers leave their house at 5a.m. in the morning to get to work at 8a.m. Many will not get back home having closed at work at 5p.m. until 9 or 10p.m.
Hee spends about 40% of his home pay n transportation. My driver told me of spending 200 naira on a bowl of gari that he used to by for 50-70 Naira year ago. We must contemplate and consider the transportation and food items that must be bought by the average Nigerian worker if deregulation means paying 40 Naira per litre for oil. We do not need to repeat some of the valid argument about the impact of prices increase on all economic activities. The costs of inputs of most of them will jump by more than hike in the price of oil. This is a reality we cannot ignore.
In conclusion, the rating for and against deregulation of the oil sector which is expected to raise prices of the pump price is valid if our consideration is the question of opportunity cost. The argument is not valid if it is view in term of production cost and the law of comparative advantage - considering the price of $9.5 at which a barrel is delivered to Nigerian refineries be use the cost o production and refining is less than $9.5 in Nigeria. The Trade Union and indeed all other Nigerians will adversely affected as an increase will raise their poverty level which is already bad. Industries and employment will be adversely affected. We must do nothing that will make the situation of Nigeria and our industries worse than what they are as both are at an unbearable level of hardship.
Chief Kuye, (OFR) wrote in from Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo, Are We Better Off Today?
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo, Are We Better Off Today?
Body_Text:
If we were to do a national opinion poll today, and the question is asked ordinary Nigerians, "are you better off today than you were two years ago?" What would the answer and indeed the rating of Nigerians of the Obasanjo regime be? Would most Nigerians for an example declare that they are better off today? Would their answer be that there has been no significant improvement in their lives; or would they say they are worse off today than they were before the "saint of Aso Rock", Chief Olusegun Obasanjo became the infallible custodian of all wisdom?
As it is, we are nearly at the mid way mark in the tenure of the current administration, both at the federal level and the states. And it is getting to that time of the year when the media would soon be swamped by self congratulatory supplements and advertisements by government officials, hangers-on and contractors eulogising the president and governors. Soon imaginations will run lose and fairy tales will be coated in glamorous language aimed at deluding the public, prodding them to award undeserved applause to their principals, the governors and the president.
While some like James Ibori of Delta, Orji Kalu of Abia, and their counterparts in Bauchi, Kaduna and Lagos might indeed earn genuine applause, others will be like President Obasanjo who has been long on promises and very short in delivering what has come to be known as dividends of democracy.
I, however, on behalf of my family have done the scoring of the Obasanjo regime, I have taken stock, just as a number of people who I know have, by their daily experiences. The result has not been flattering at all. I will vote with those whose lives have been affected negatively by the Obasanjo regime.
Indeed, I have become a laughing stock in the office, where the general taunt, from Obasanjo's kinsmen, has been "you see what that man you people elected into power is doing to this country and to Nigerians? "
Even I, I am now weary of my standard answer: "given another chance, I would rather vote for Obasanjo than for Falae." Indeed, I have had cause to ask myself over and over again, if Falae's government would have been as insensitive, if it would have been as non-productive as this one.
How for example would Falae have handled the question of NEPA? Two years on, would we still be in the dark, nebulous throes of promises, even as we gradually get less and less power supply from NEPA?
Baba, first told us, by December 2000, NEPA's darkness would be behind us; this was later edited to December 2001. Eight months to eldorado, most homes are worse off with power supply today. It is a miracle for example to have a 30 minutes power supply in any given day in my Amuwo Odofin GRA area. The Naira has never had it so rough. From N85 to $1 when Obasanjo took over, it is now about N135 to a $1.
Food too has become a major problem. I will not be shocked if one of President Obasanjo's ministers, preferably that one who has never lied in his life, comes up one day to announce to the nation that garri is to be deregulated; that afterall, Nigerians abroad pay far more than we currently pay for this common man's food, than Nigerians at home do; that indeed, it makes sense to continually increase the price of that common diet of the poor so that our neighbouring Benin Republic, or Cameroun, or even Niger indigenes will not develop long throat for it. It will then not matter that the cost of garri has risen more than 400% in the last two years, just like all staple food such as yam, plantain, millet, etc. It will be in government's character of inflicting the greatest havoc on the least resilient part of the populace, the majority poor.
I really wonder, given that nothing concrete has been achieved by this government, what in his quiet moments, the president would sincerely say he has achieved in his close to two years reign at Aso Rock. Is it the factories that are closing down in droves; the massive retrenchment which currently sees more than 35% of adult Nigerians out of work and graduates unemployed?
What would he point at? The dilapidated infrastructure nationwide or the hunger sweeping the land? Are these what he is beating his chest about and Chief Tony Anenih and co say there is no vacancy at Aso Rock?
Well, we shall meet this president at the polls in 2003, then he will know that his newly acquired sartorial elegance and saintly pretensions will not jell with Nigerians. At least, not with my wife and I.

Original_Search_Title: GSM: Is the Competition Won And Lost Already?
Headline: Nigeria: GSM: Is the Competition Won And Lost Already?
Body_Text:
The only good news coming from NITEL since the GSM auction is the news last week that it could continue serious discussions with MSI as a core partner that could take up to about 49 per cent of its GSM stakes.
Mind you this piece of news has nothing good to say about the ignominious treatment meted to Mr. Emanuel Ojeba, the former NITEL boss who was retired just three months away from going into full retirement. After serving this country for about 35 years, the man deserved a better treatment from some politicians who hold the yam and the knife.
On this page we had said that Ojeba didn't have the steam to stand the competition that was coming but nobody suggested such a shameful exit. There is always a reason for a sack but we dare say here that the gentleman is not responsible for the eclipse enshrouding NITEL's GSM license which it got on a platter of gold.
Those people who suggested that NITEL had no business with GSM but should instead concentrate on offering is primary but backbone services are already being justified. And quite interestingly, the two compertitors already rearing up to go, don't even believe that they have a fight coming from NITEL. MTN in a massive radio campaign says it is the better service provider while Econet on the hand is providing its own information bulletin on the advent of the GSM technology, where the company comes in, and what it plans to do. Merge this with the print campaign already awash in the newspapers, it becomes an over-kill.
Somebody asked me, where is NITEL? Moaning and being disillusioned. The answer comes straight from within the organization. On being told that both ECONET and MTN were due to rollout by next month, a highly placed NITEL source screamed: That is three months before we start. But the point here is that while others are busy recruiting personnel, importing equipment, and building a campaign pending a massive rollout, with a boast by one of them that it will hit a million mark in the first one year, dear friends, NITEL is quiet, extremely quiet and there is every indication that it will now come in as an under-dog.
The good news is that there will be phones soon with or without NITEL if you trust MTN , with time to operate outside NITEL network as company officials have promised. The very sad thing is that NITEL would have narrowed down the competition to just a straight fight between ECONET and MTN. The implications are grave, two of them being loss of revenue and playing a second fiddle in a land where it has been a superman.
The easier thing would be to leave NITEL to the mysterious strength of market forces but the most patriotic and expedient thing to do would be for concerned Nigerians to save NITEL from itself and from the choking hands of politicians.

Original_Search_Title: Consumer Price Index Still On the Rise
Headline: Kenya: Consumer Price Index Still On the Rise
Body_Text:
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) maintained its upward trend in the second month of this year, a trend that started in the first quarter of last year.
The upward movement was largely attributed to the rise in the prices of kerosene, cost of transportation and food items.
According to a report released by the Federal Office of Statistics (FOS), the Composite Consumer Price Index (CCPI) rose to 3927.3 in February up from 3848.9 recorded in January which show a 2.0 per cent increase.
In the preceding 12 months to February 2001, the average consumer price level increased by 18.6 per cent which was higher than the 17.6 per cent recorded in the preceding month. During the same period also, the urban and rural indices showed increases of 20.9 and 18.1 per cent respectively.
The report released recently also indicated that the percentage change in the average composite (CPI) for the 12 months period ending February 2001 as against the average of the CPI for the previous 12 months period of 2000 was 10.3. This means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00 during the 12 month period ending February 2000 sold for N110.3 on the average for the corresponding period ending February 2001.
Just as the composite consumer price index keeps to the upward trend so also the composite food index which also rose by 3.1 per cent between January 2001 and February 2001. In contrast however, the increase in food index, according to the same report was caused largely by the upward movement in the prices of staples such as Gari, guinea corn and yam tubers.
The report also observed increases in the prices of fish, processing foods and edible, which rose in many centers. Within the said period of February 2000 and February 2001 also, the composite Food Index rose by 13.8 per cent and the average annual rate of rise for the 12 months ending February 2001 was 4.8 per cent.
In the same vein the All Item less farm produce index which excludes the agricultural produce moved up by 0.8 per cent in February 2001 while in the 12 months before February 2001, the index rose by 24.3 per cent. The average annual rate of the rise of the index was 17.7 per cent for the 12-month period ending February 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Only True Federalism Can Make Nigeria Great-Omeruah
Headline: Nigeria: Only True Federalism Can Make Nigeria Great-Omeruah
Body_Text:
Yenagoa, Bayelsa — Former Minister of Sports, Air Commodore Samson Emeka Omeruah (rtd) has declared that only the practice of true federalism where all the federating units control their resources can set Nigeria on the track of actualising its full potentials.
The former airforce chief who was reacting to President Olusegun Obasanjo's declaration that the clamour for resource control will lead to another civil war said the gruesome 30 months civil war which rocked the 60s was not the struggle for resource control but because of the genocide committed against the Igbos.
Commodore Omeruah who spoke to Vanguard at the Port Harcourt International Airport described the Nigerian system as imperialistic and autocratic in nature for the federating states to attain their full potentials.
His words: "What is going on now in this country is not true federalism, if we must energise the kinetic energy existing in the individual state we must practice true federalism.
"The system that is presently operating in the country is imperialistic and autocratic. So we have to put in place true federalism. That is the only time when you can now release the abundant energy that exist in all the facets of our polity. If you really want to live in a place where your land grows yam and corn, you will want to be part and parcel of the eating of that corn so I don't see anything wrong with it. In the American system which we are trying to copy, resource control is in the states. What the federal government does at the national level is to tax those states with resources."
Continuing, he said: "If government can realise that there is need for those who are in the environment where the resources are to benefit from it, it will do our country well because it is God endowed and if God has given you a talent you should exploit it. I don't see anything wrong with it.
"But the fact is that the federal government, in order to meet up with other exigencies and expenditure should tax the people appropriately, that is the way it is done. The people within the vicinity should have control over their resources.
On the civil war, he said "most struggle this way has been that of resource control but the Nigerian civil war was not really resource control fight. The war started because the Igbos were purged, they were being massacred and they didn't know where to go but only to run to their homeland. "There were some argument which were not met and because of disagreement in meeting those agreement there was a civil war. "So it was basically not resource control war. If we know there was so much oil in Igboland as it is now the war would have been highly competitive."
He called on the people to be steadfast in their legitimate aspiration to have control of their resources saying the president's statement was meant to frighten them into abandoning the struggle.
On the alleged marginalisation of Ndigbo he said "marginalisation, I think, is a thing of the mind. Luckily for the Igbo race they have the capacity and sagacity do away with marginalisation."
According to him "if the Igbo can do those things they know best which is their belief is communalism and their drive for commercial ventures they should be able to do away slightly with the talk of marginalisation."
Said he: "you discover that even those we think are better off in terms of infrastructures are also talking about marginalisation. So you don't know who is being marginalised."

Original_Search_Title: OPD Settles Mile 12 Market Rift
Headline: Nigeria: OPD Settles Mile 12 Market Rift
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Office of Public Defender (OPD) has settled the age-long rift
between warring factions at the popular Mile 12 market.
The rift, which resulted in Yoruba/Hausa ethnic clashes last year,
continued unabated until the OPD waded in recently.
Speaking at the peace meeting brokered by the OPD, the Vice President
of the Shukura Yam Sellers Association, Alhaji Ismaila Umaru Lado,
expressed gratitude to government representatives at the meeting.
He appealed to both sides to forgive and forget the past in order to
give room for peaceful cohabitation among Yorubas and Hausas.
Alhaji Lado explained that the cross-cultural trading existing in the
market has led to different problems and misunderstandings. He,
however, stated that what mattered most is the peaceful resolution of
the cases without rancour.
He also called on the traders to report their grievances to their
respective leaders in the market so that such issues can be resolved
amicably.
While appealing for calm and patience from the different ethnic
groups in the market, he advised that all conflicts in the market
should be channelled through the OPD.
Responding, the head of OPD, Mrs. Akinlade called on all aggrieved
parties in the market to embrace peace.

Original_Search_Title: Government is Insensitive
Headline: Nigeria: Government is Insensitive
Body_Text:
Comrade John Ejoha Odah is the Acting General Secretary, Nigeria
Labour Congress, (NLC) which is organising the anti-fuel price
increase throughout Nigeria. He speaks on this conflict with the
Federal Government, salary arrears for civil servant and the
anti-people disposition of many federal legislators in this interview
with ADEMOLA ADEGBAMIGBE
Q: Many Nigerians do not quite understand the issues involved in this
NLC's showdown with the government...
A: The first one is to protest against the harrowing scarcity which
government has subjected Nigerians in the last couple of months. We
see it as part of the blackmail strategy to deceive and cow Nigerians
to submission in terms of accepting the increment. This blackmail is
further reinforced by the statement made by President Olusegun
Obasanjo at the Nigerian Defence Academy to the effect that Nigerians
are already paying above the official prices, so, they can pay much
higher for fuel!
Q: The Federal Government said that deregulation does not necessarily
mean increase in price...
A: No. During his February media chat, the president was categorical.
He said deregulation means they want to sell according to
international price so that they can get N200 billion to spend on
other things. On Monday, 19 March 2001, when he was addressing
students of the Nigerian Defence Academy, the president said the fact
that Nigerians had been paying above the official prices, is an
indication that they could pay more for fuel. Anybody talking to the
contrary (Jerry Gana and others) are just trying to confuse
Nigerians. That was on the basis of concrete recommendations to the
government by the I.M.F and the World Bank to increase the prices of
petroleum products so that they can get enough money to pay foreign
debts.
Q: The NLC has also argued that there is no subsidy. Do you have
statistical proofs to the contrary?
A: Yes. Even from government official documents, there is no subsidy.
According to the NNPC figures, government sells crude to NNPC at the
rate of $9.5. The NNPC spends $4 to refine it. At that level,
government is making a profit of $5.5. When it is refined, the NNPC
spends N15 on refining, transportation and the margin for marketers.
Here, government makes another N7 to N9 on a litre of fuel in Nigeria
through taxation and others. The argument about subsidy, therefore,
is not logical. What government is saying is that because crude is
sold at the international market at $26, it (government) should be
able to sell it to the NNPC at that margin. It is like if a yam
farmer sets aside some tubers for his family consumption, he cannot
price it the same way as the ones he takes to the market. The farmer
has no additional costs (on transportation, margin for middle men,
etc.) on the ones his family would consume. It is, therefore, wrong
to compare Nigerian price with the international price, because we
produce 2.1 million barrels of oil a day. We set aside only 300,000
barrels for local consumption . The balance is for the international
market. Nigerians do not receive international wages. Therefore, it
cannot pay international price for crude. In the United States, the
minimum wage ranges from $5.5 to $8 per hour. It translates to about
N700 per hour. Whereas, the minimum wage for one month in Nigeria is
N5,500.
Q: Many federal ministry workers are not being paid regularly. What
is the NLC doing about it?
A: If you can flash back to our new year message, we made a point
that this year, we would take on government and other employers who
are in the habit of not paying workers as at when due. We will
enforce the logic of "no pay no work."
Q: Nigeria Prisons and Immigration officials have not been paid for
four months running. Since they cannot go on strike, is there
anything the NLC can do about this?
A: By the time this comes to our attention, we will make a point
there . Government has no point keeping people in employment and
refusing to pay them.

Original_Search_Title: Experts Canvass Agricultural Revolution To Boost Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Experts Canvass Agricultural Revolution To Boost Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a bid to hit the projected target of six per cent growth in
agricultural output in 2001 against four per cent achieved last year,
the federal government has been advised to embark on an agricultural
revolution whereby states could identify certain areas and improve on
them.
Chief executive officer, Inter-Trust Limited, Dr. Chilo Offiah who
gave this advise in an interview with Vanguard recently frowned at
the non-challant attitude of government to agriculture, saying that
the sector should be given more attention particularly when viewed
against the fact that President Obasanjo is an established farmer.
Offiah however, suggested that Nigeria should embark on agricultural
revolution programmes where states should be mandated to handle
certain agricultural areas and improve on them.
According to him, "eastern states should go back to palm oil
production with a production target of one million to ten million
bags a year. The western states should be asked to go back to cocoa
producing or plantation and they should have a target".
He added that "the north also should be given target in animal
husbandry, groundnut-pyramid, the tuber yam seedlings and so many
other things they can produce.
In the 2001 fiscal year, the economy is projected to grow by about 5
per cent compared with 3 per cent in year 2000, the projected growth
of 5 per cent in anticipation of improvement in agriculture,
manufacturing, and other productive ventures. Secondly, the policy
thrust of the year includes stimulating of agricultural and
industrial production.
However, Dr. Offiah who was also the former managing director of FBN
(Merchant Bankers) Limited said that for the nation to meet the
estimated target on the contribution of agriculture to economic
growth, every state should go into agricultural production and
specialise in whatever area it has comparative advantage.
He stated that this would enable the country to be agriculturally
sufficient in the next few years as well as being able to export food
to West African and Africa as a whole.
Offiah in his words, 'we cannot keep on depending on oil which ha
been causing us a lot of problem. We have not done much in
agriculture and it is a shame. At times I wonder what the Ministry of
Agriculture has been doing in the past 18 months."
"I want to see the ministry take initiative on his own because the
ministry is quite our focus."
On a similar note, the president-general of Senior Staff Association
of University Teaching Hospitals, Research Institutes and Associated
Institutions of Nigeria (SSAUTHRIAI), Comrade Ralph A. Adeniyi
recently advised the government to emulate the advanced countries on
how they are committed to developing agriculture in their countries.
Adeniyi cited example with the International Institute for Tropical
Agriculture (IITA) based in Ibadan, but sponsored by American
government.
According to him, the Institute gets more funds than all the eighteen
National Agricultural Research Institutes in Nigeria. He stated that
the United States of America and Japan spend over 2.4 per cent of
their GDP on research and development annually while Ghana spends
about 0.3 per cent on agricultural research alone.
He complained "most of our relevant research projects in Nigeria have
been abandoned midway, the mass exist of experts is on ht increase
and many prototype technologies cannot be fully developed and
commercialised because the links with industry are yet to be finally
established,' Adeniji said.

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi: Rekindling Hope Through Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi: Rekindling Hope Through Agriculture
Body_Text:
Lagos — With the country's fluctuating oil revenue and increasing demand for
infrastructure, Ebonyi state state has turned to agriculture for a
life-line, writes Joseph Ushigiale
Several decades ago, Nigeria was reputed to have derived her mainstay
and sustainability through her agricultural endowments which were
adequately exploited for maximum proceeds. During these years, as the
history of economics shows, Nigeria currency was quite strong and
stable. The gross domestic product, income per capital and standard
of living indices including growth maintained an enviable status.
It is also on record that the country's immeasurable agricultural
potentials attracted British economic interest and eventual
colonisation. Nigeria's huge agricultural potentials made up largely
of unprocessed raw materials were used by British factories
especially during the Industrial1 revolution and period of the great
recession.
But the glorious period of groundnut pyramids in the Northern part of
Nigeria, cocoa and timber in the Southwest was punctuated with the
discovery of oil in commercial quantity in 1956 at Oloibiri.
Gradually, emphasis and attention of both individuals and government
shifted from the sector to oil where very little effort was employed
to reap what has come to be regarded as black gold.
The energy crises of the early seventies which was triggered by the
Arab- Israeli war which eventually shot up oil prices to an
unprecedented high was the last straw that obliterated whatever hopes
of returning agriculture to its past glory. This is because the
government of the day instead of formulating and implementing
appropriate agricultura1 policies for that purpose relapsed into
widespread and unrestrained importation of finished products, which
at the long run had catastrophic consequences on our economy.
Although two unsuccessful attempts have been made, not only to
position the country as an agricultura1 products exporting country
but also to ensure that there is self-sufficiency in food production.
The first was the Obasanjo Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) and the
Shagari's green revolution programmes. Both programmes laudable as
they appeared on paper failed to reach the implementation stages.
Today, however, with dwindling oil revenue, the federal government is
now grappling with the problem of sourcing complementary revenue to
oil through agriculture. The federal government is not alone,
following in this stead are state governments which have found
themselves bogged down by increasing demands for infrastructure from
the electorate and an equally disappointing share from the statutory
allocation. These increasing worries have culminated in the present
quest for resource control by some 17 governors in the south.
Again, some of these states are now confronted with the alternative
of turning to agriculture for a life-line. If there is any state
government in Nigeria that is greatly desirous of charting its growth
through agriculture, irrespective of its financia1 1imitations, it is
Ebonyi state State. The state government's direction in agriculture
within the run of governor Sam Egwu administration clearly indicates
its readiness to re-enact and rekindle hope through this sector.
"Before the advent of petroleum, the agricultural sector of the
Nigerian economy provided the greatest proportion of the most needed
foreign exchange earnings and the base for the social and economic
development of Nigeria," Egwu told Obasanjo during his visit 1ast
December.
He said past governments in the geopolitical zone covering Ebonyi
state State today, had always paid lip service and least attention to
agriculture with consequent disappearance of the notable Abakaliki
rice, Onueke cattle, Abakalilki chicken, yam and others adding that
the present lack of attention and the non provision of the necessary
incentives and auxiliary services to farmers in the state by past
administrations have resulted in the decreasing productivity of
farmers and increased marginalisation of this segment of the state
population that constitute the majority.
According to him, on assumption of office, his administration had
re-assessed the state developmental priorities and concluded that
improvement in the productivity of agriculture that engage over 90%
of the total population could result in the improvement of the total
standard of living of the generality of the people and ensure
sustainable poverty alleviation.
The state commissioner for agriculture Dr Donatus Onu agreeing with
the assertion explained that the major thrust of the administration
has been to improve agricultural production in the state on the
understanding that the majority of farmers in the state are basical1y
small-scale farmers.
According to him, "we believe that if we extend to the people,
technology that can improve the individua1 farmer productivity, it
will lead to commensurate improvement in their standard of living".
He clarified that "since the rural population constitute the majority
of the population if the standard of living of this percentage is
improved, we can comfortably conclude that the state government has
achieved its goals in this direction. This explains why most of our
agricultural packages are highly subsidised by the state, it is not
aimed at maximising revenue for itself. The facilities being put in
place are to facilitate our production system".
At the moment, he explained that it is socio-economically impossible
for farmers here to acquire fairly large agro production technology
like installation of irrigation facilities.
As soon as the administration came on stream Egwu's first step
towards realising his agricultural objectives was to lead trade
delegation to Israel for the purpose of attracting Israel technica1
assistance in agricultural development and food production; another
visit was equally undertaken to South Africa for the same purpose,
the fruits of these visits are now manifest in the joint venture
agreement between the state government and an Israeli company
Dizengoff W.A. which is currently engaged in the bui1ding of an
integrated modern Poultry farm at Nkaliki valued at about Sl3m.
Ebonyi state State possesses huge agricultura1 potential, which are
especially manifest in its endowment with good climatic conditions.
Onu affirmed that all the agro-ecological conditions of the state
favour the production of all types of crops and livestock confirming
that the soil type and other relevant climatic condition stand the
state out as a potential investment haven for agro based
entrepreneur.
Although the state government thrust has not begun to tap these
potentials in a manner that will chart an alternative revenue base
for the state in the light of its least position on the statutory
revenue allocation table, it has implanted specific technology which
will in the long run ensure that revenue accrues to the state in
future.
One of these steps is the establishment of an ultra modern oil palm
nursery in Ezzamgbo, About 40,200 palm seedlings are sold monthly to
farmers at subsidised prices. For this initiative the state
government spent N80m for the construction of standard net house oil
palm nursery. The nursery which is located at the veterinary
investigation centre with a facility of producing 2m palm seedlings
per annum and is equipped with modern irrigation facilities.
The state government's direction, according to Egwu, is to assist and
raise small holders in the sub-sector with inputs such as hybrid
seedling species developed in the National Institute for oil Palm
Research (NIFOPR) Benin which are currently being made available to
include farmers and agricultural agencies for pilot schemes.
As an agrarian state, where farmers have consistently relied on old
and archaic traditional methods of farming, the state government in
its drive to create awareness and improve yields for its farmers, has
introduced modern technique of farming especially in the use of
fertilisers and tractors. Millions of naira have been committed by
the Egwu administration towards the procurement and distribution of
different grades of fertiliser at 50 percent subsidy.
To underscore the level of importance it attached to the project, a
company, Solidarity International, an affiliate of Yagus
Internationa1 of Illinois, renowned manufacturers of blending plants
across the world, Onu said, was contracted and mobilised to the tune
of N191m to supply and insta1l the plant.
Another area that has gained significant attention of the state
government in the sector is in poultry production. Soon after Egwu
assumed office, he led a trade delegation to Israel the outcome of
that visit today is a contract with an Israeli firm, Dizengaff W.A
for the modernisation of the Nkaliki hatchery When completed, it is
projected that the poultry project considered about the largest in
the west coast will at its full capacity produce 16,800 day old
chicks and 8000 broilers weekly, 4.16m broilers per year and 8.74m
eggs yearly.
The poultry will not only be a source of revenue and foreign exchange
earning but also a major source of employment as well as meeting the
challenge of protein production for the entire South East zone and
Nigeria.
On the philosophy behind the poultry investment Onu stressed that
"the philosophy lies in the history that before now, the only
livestock that attracted attention and practice of indigenous farmers
in Ebonyi state is poultry production.
In the process, these farmers encountered problems of procuring
day-old chicks, the production of this strategic poultry in put
require high capital and machinery that are unavailable to them. He
further said that their search for day-old chicks sometimes took the
farmers to Otta Farms in Abeokuta in Ogun state to procure them, a
course that often exposed them to risks that affect their
productivity.
He said that at the inception of the Egwu administration, the state
government evaluated the situation and evolved a plan to ensure that
day old chicks were produced to provide a lasting solution to
problems of the small- scale poultry farmers.
"We have achieved all these for now, we are selling eggs at N5 per
egg broilers at N350 per one. The poultry project is going to be
about the highest this side of Africa and there are p1ans to
privatise it for efficient equipment utilisation, materia1 and human
development". he enthused.
As an indication of its seriousness the state government recently
awarded contract for the construction of a 2.5km road, to provide
access to the hatchery.
Onu said government is ready to build houses for interested investors
and to assist them to mechanise their activities at highly reduced
rate. We are still looking at the possibilities of direct involvement
in production that will be shared by equity between the state
government and the private investors. But at the same time, we are
1ooking for potential investors who are ready to enter into agro
-based ventures with the state. We want an element of private
initiative in collaboration with the state" For the grassroot
farmers, the state government has been able to assist them through
the state Agricultural Development Programme strengthen an extend
improved agricultura1 to services, which are capable of not only
revolutionising their production capacity alone but also to
remarkably ensure a clear cut departure from old traditiona1 ways to
modern day service.
Onu noted that funding for the agricultural programme was done
through tripartite arrangement with the World Bank, federal
government and the benefiting state after which the state takes over
after the programme has run its course; the state is still operating
its programme under the arrangement.
To ensure that the state's rural farmers are not incapacitated by
lack of funds, the government recently procured a N50m loan from the
Nigerian Agricultural and Co-operative Bank (NACB) to fund its
micro-credit facilities for which each farmer is to get about N75,000
loan to strengthen the financial base of their farms. In all, Ebonyi
state expects these efforts to result in food sufficiency for her, as
well the country.
What's more? Observers note that Ebonyi's novel 'green' efforts
should be a worthy model to be cloned by other state's who share her
socio-economic constraints.

Original_Search_Title: Battling the Weeds of Destruction
Headline: Nigeria: Battling the Weeds of Destruction
Body_Text:
Lagos — When water hyacinth made its surprise appearance on Nigeria's water
around 1979 on a massive scale, it caught the authorities at the
different tiers of government unawares. As a result, they were at a
loss as to how to control the destructive weed.
Various suggestions were made ranging from the sensible to the
bizarre. Some experts even suggested the importation of a particular
kind of weevil that would eat up the seed that germinate the water
hyacinth. Efforts made then at controlling the menace were believed
to be half-hearted, hence the weeds returned even stronger some years
later.
Since then, it has been off and on and economic life in the riverine
areas that centre mainly on fishing has been the worse for it. But it
is not only the fishermen that have been suffering. Speedboat
operators who use the waterways on a daily basis also have bitter
tales to tell. Perhaps, the story is better told by a first time
visitor to Aiyetoro, the popular riverine community near Igbokoda in
Ondo State.
Sometime in 1985, this reporter had cause to go to Aiyetoro on an
assignment to see how the people of this Island run their lives.
Socialism, one had been told, was their way of life and in a backward
capitalist economy like Nigeria's socialism beckons to the average
Nigerian who could barely afford to make ends meet.
On arrival at Igbokoda, the sprawling water of the lagoon became an
attraction and a source of fear having never travelled on water
before. But the boisterous manner the speedboat operators were
conducting their business and the movement back and forth of the
passengers, particularly smoked fish sellers, exchanging their wares
for other agricultural produce, like yam, not grown in their
waterlogged islands reassured me all was well and safe to travel by
the boats afterall.
After some haggling over the fare, the speed boat with about 20
passengers on board, sitting on five rows of seat made of planks
departed, heading for Aiyetoro and other nearby islands. The
estimated one and a half hours journey began well for me but as I was
beginning to enjoy the voyage, tragedy nearly struck. The boat ran
into a large expanse of water hyacinth, the kind I had never seen
before. The entire water was covered by the weeds. As the captain, or
skipper, of the boat was trying to navigate through, the engine
suddenly stopped, the speedboat lost its speed, stalled in the weeds
and began listing. I became afraid, my confidence deserted me when I
remembered I cannot swim. I began to pray to God to deliver us safely
at our destination. My co passengers were calm but annoyed and began
to narrate their ordeal in the hands of water hyacinth. It was there
and then it dawned on me that those of us who live in cities do not
appreciate the sufferings of the rural communities even when they
tell us. Since then I became aware of the menace of this weed of
destruction to riverine communities.
Many speed boat operators have lost their outboard engines several
times to the weed and in the process their means of livelihood.
Fishermen and women are the worst hit.
In Epe, a serene and unspoiled Lagos community, fishing is about the
only industry there, but even that is being over-run by water
hyacinth. With the biggest fishing market in Lagos, this dangerous
weed which is ravaging over 100 fishing posts on the banks of Lekki
lagoon, is destroying the main source of livelihood of the people.
The nearby villages of Olugbokere, Mansa, Ikin and other riverine
settlements are equally affected and expectedly the people are
worried and have made representation to the local government
authority for help.
But water hyacinth is not restricted to Western Nigeria alone. It is
a national phenomenon. From the waters of Lake Chad in the northeast
to the lagoon of Warri in the Delta to Calabar in the South-South and
also in Yauri, the weed has not proved less destructive. But what can
Nigeria do to save her waterways and restore aquatic life to the good
old days.
After years of time wasting, the federal government finally found its
bearing when, by Decree No. 13 of 1997, it set up the Nigerian Inland
Waterways Authority (NIWA), whose duties, among others, include the
clearing of water hyacinth and other harmful weeds (from our
waterways); undertaking capital and maintenance dredging, removing
and receiving wrecks and derelicts and conducting hydrological and
hydrographic surveys, as part of its vital marine and environmental
functions. It also runs ferry services, constructs and repair boats.
The authority has acquired a water hyacinth harvester from the
Netherlands at a cost of N55 million. Bought with the support o the
defunct Petroleum Trust Fund (PTF), the harvester has been cleared
from the port (after a long delay) and would soon be deployed to the
most critical areas after being assembled by the foreign experts who
would also train Nigerians on operations and maintenance.
In addition to using hyacinth stoppers, NIWA has also launched a
massive manual clearing programme. It is practical and involves the
fishing and transport communities who are faced with the harsh
realities of water hyacinth. Expectedly, Epe is the spearhead of
these projects, although plans are on to site the headquarters at
Igbokoda.
Following a N49 million aid from the Federal Government a manual
clearing exercise is underway in Epe. And although the money provided
for just 600 workers, the local government has chipped in more funds
in order to hire up to 2000 labourers. Sixteen supervisors run
regular checks on the workers. So with the combined help of the
federal and local government, working through NIWA, Epe indigenes
have not only taken matters into their hands fighting the water
hyacinth scourge but have also gained temporary employment.
And the effort is already paying off. NIWA chairman, Chief Sule
Onabiyi, recently inspected dozens of infested fishing ports in the
area. When he saw what was on the ground, he said "I am disturbed
that so much damage has been done. At the same time, I am happy that
so much progress is being made in only a few days of work." The Epe
council chairman, Chief S. O. Areh agrees: "Water hyacinth is a real
menace to my people and fishing. We've started the fight well. We
won't rest until we succeed. With the continued support of NIWA we
will bring it under control."
NIWA itself is aware of its enormous responsibilities, and is
determined to make the best of available resources to succeed in the
difficult but surmountable task. But it needs the support and
cooperation of everyone including the affected local communities to
succeed. According to Engineer Rabiu Abubakar, the authority's
Managing Director, "Water hyacinth is an eyesore capable of rendering
our waterways treacherous. It must be controlled with all available
resources. NIWA is willing and determined to do this, especially with
the support of the affected communities".
The support of the affected communities, without gainsaying, is of
utmost importance and since the menace of water hyacinth is a
national phenomenon, those other communities being ravaged by this
scourge can learn from the Epe experience where a bad situation is
being turned into a job employment scheme.
But beyond the immediate communities, controlling water hyacinth is
also of national importance. According to experts, it can not be
eradicated. So if properly controlled it would not only enhance
fishing in the affected communities, but also conserve the foreign
exchange Nigeria use in fish import.
Statistics show that Nigeria imports about 160 million US dollars
worth of fish annually, exporting just about 72 million US dollars
worth of fish yearly. With the country's abundant fishing resources,
if the waterways are cleared of water hyacinth, fish export could
improve and import reduced considerably. This is the challenge before
NIWA and indeed the authorities at both federal, state and local
government levels.

Original_Search_Title: Deregulation Fever
Headline: Nigeria: Deregulation Fever
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — The debate on the deregulation of the downstream sector of the oil
industry meets stiff resistance in Rivers State he senate committee
on petroleum Resources currently investigating the crisis in the
Petroleum industry in Nigeria brought its deregulation orchestra to
Port Harcourt last Tuesday, 13 March.
They wanted to know how the people of Rivers State would dance to the
oil industry deregulation tune being played by the Federal Government
as the only cure for the petroleum products scarcity ravaging the
country. Majority of the people rejected the music outright and
called for a change of melody.
For Chief Chukwudifu Dickson Orike, a former Attorney General and
Commissioner for Justice in Rivers State, the word deregulation as it
applies to the Nigerian situation is a euphemism for increase in
petroleum pump prices. "The argument in favour of deregulation is
mainly based on attracting investors to establish refineries in
Nigeria and that investors would be discouraged if the sector is not
deregulated."
Orike described this argument as ridiculous. According to him, any
investor who chooses to establish a refinery in Nigeria was under no
obligation to sell his finished products in Nigeria. He said he (the
private investor) would want to make profit as he would buy the crude
oil from the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) at the
prevailing international market price. This would eventually
translate to high prices of the product for the ordinary Nigerian.
Dr. Amaechi Dibia, a member of the South-South Peoples Conference
(SSOPEC), regretted that in spite of efforts by many states of the
federation to solve the scarcity of petroleum products, the problem
has persisted. Using Rivers State as an example, Dibia stated that
SSOPEC conducted some investigations and discovered that Port
Harcourt and environs happened to be the only zone in the East where
petrol was sold, whenever available, at the normal price of N22 per
litre. In all other urban centres of Eastern Nigeria, he stressed
prices range from N30 to N35. He blamed the situation on a mafia
which he said, owned about 400 out of 415 petrol stations in Port
Harcourt and environs.
Dibia suggested leaving the control of the executive council of
Tanker Drivers wing of NUPENG in each state to indigenes since, as he
said, "in some states where indigenes are in control, cases of
diversion of fuel tankers to outlets other than those designated on
their way bills are very minimal. The Rivers State House of Assembly
(RSHA) put the blame on the doorsteps of the Federal Government.
Amachukwu Ogbowu urged the Federal Government to check diversion and
make the refineries functional. He expressed the opposition of the
State Assembly to deregulation because of the negative impact it
would have on the society, even as he called on the Federal
Government to establish another depot in Rivers State.
To Mr. Ledum Mitee, the President of the Movement for the survival of
the Ogoni People (MOSOP), NNPC should be probed and possibly
scrapped. He opposed deregulation, arguing that "It's the ordinary
people who are subsidizing the government because the money enters
into the pockets of those who are in power, or at least their
accolades,"
On its part, the Department of Petroleum Resources (DPR), saddled
with the responsibility of overseeing and monitoring the operations
and activities of the entire oil industry, came up with data within
the time frame of January 2000 to February 2001.The intention,
according to the operations controller, DPR, Eastern Zone, Mr. Joseph
O. Enweluzo, was to highlight a production and distribution picture
especially of the Premium Motor Spirit (PMS). For instance, between
January and December, 2000, the processing units of the two Port
Harcourt refineries were shut down due to either power failure or
equipment problems. The combined installed capacity for the two
refineries, Mr. Enweluzo said, is 8.12million litres per day, but the
average daily production for 2000 was 4.15million litres per day
which is about 50 per cent of installed capacity.
As for distribution, Enweluzo noted that the Aba Depot, which covers
Abia, Ebonyi, Imo, parts of Akwa Ibom and Anambra States, for
instance, had only about 34.4 per cent of anticipated PMS supply
lifted from it in 1999 as against the optimum expected daily PMS
lifting of 1.6million litres per day. In year 2000, the situation
worsened. The shortfall in PMS in Aba Depot, according to the DPR
controller, was due to the shortfall in supply from Port Harcourt
Depot/Pump Station due to pipeline breaks.He suggested increasing and
sustaining surveillance of product pipeline network as a way out of
the problem. .
Stating that Venezuela, a country with fewer population than Nigeria,
has 15 functional refineries while Nigeria has four that perform
below expectation, Enweluzo advocated for additional refineries and
preventive maintenance culture for all the refineries and depots in
the zone.
But , it could take about three years to have a refinery on ground.
Which is why the former Managing Director of the Kaduna Refinery,
M.N.K. Tamuno, recommended in the interim that "we rehabilitate the
refineries we have and get the 450,000 barrels refining capacity on
the ground and improve on our evacuation facilities, maintained the
pipelines and go back to the use of railway transportation."
On deregulation, Tamuno told TheNEWS Business that Nigeria was not
ripe for this now. "If we now have to deregulate, definitely the
Federal Government is not going to release 450,000 barrels or 600,000
barrels as the case maybe... There is no way it will not go higher
than the N22". Tamuno affirmed.
However, Chief Tari J. Sekibo, the chairman of the Rivers State
Housing and Property Development Authority, believes that it was
necessary to deregulate but that this must be done in phases
According to him"deregulation is not just a complete answer. It is
part of the answer... Since PMS cost more in Cameroun, Chad, Niger,
Dahomey than in Nigeria, the issue of smuggling will not be easily
contained. So, there is need to deregulate the sector so that if
Camerounians who do not produce oil pay say, N70 per litre, we who
produce it should pay a bit less than that." Summing up the argument
of the apostles of deregulation, Mr. Fred Alasia, the Rivers State
Director of the National Orientation Agency (NOA), said nobody has
determined the prices of fish, yam, or even "kpomo". So? "Let the
market forces determine the prices of petroleum products," he said

Original_Search_Title: Startling Find By Arms Recovery Team
Headline: Ghana: Startling Find By Arms Recovery Team
Body_Text:
Acrra — A startling find by security personnel involved in the small arms
recovery exercise has further gone to show the extent to which the
NDC government mismanaged the issue of weapon control in the country.
No wonder there were many cases of maiming of innocent citizens and
in some cases the termination of lives by weapon wielding
politicians. A number of the gun-touting citizens derived their power
from the Osu Castle, the main citadel of power.
A mere boast of "I am from the Office of the President" was enough to
send conventional law enforcing agents scuttling for cover.
The wanton possession of weapons in the Ghanaian society which
emanated from the proclamation of "democratisation of violence" by
the PNDC/NDC government no doubt was responsible for this
development. In the 'kume preko' killings of 1995, in which Ghanaians
on a peaceful demonstration were fired upon, the perpetrators were
identified as government armed members of a political organisation.
Though the shootings ended in deaths, the NDC government shielded the
culprits from prosecution. To this day, they are walking scot-free.
The reckless manner in which weapons of various
sophistication changed hands in the society gave impetus to the
proliferation, the repercussions of which are giving Ghanaians and
the new NPP government nightmares. To stem activities of miscreants,
who are using these arms to plunder, kill and destabilise security,
the government ordered the police and military to recover arms in
unauthorised hands in the country. The Joint Operational Team (JOT)
has since receiving its orders chalked some success, in the process
coming up with some highly bizarre discoveries. A handicapped
individual, who by all considerations would be considered a helpless
cripple, was instead discovered to be a Baheavily armed individual.
The handicapped man who has to be assisted to board a car and when
alighting needs a wheel chair to move around even in his house had
amongst his prized property an AK47, a pistol and a revolver. The
amazing revelation is that they were all supported with a permit
issued from the "Office of the President of the Republic of Ghana."
In other words, the seat of government had licensed him to carry such
lethal weapons. Just what important function this handicapped
individual was performing for the state to make it necessary for
Rawlings to authorise him to bear arms beats the imagination, heavily
armed individual. The handicapped man who has to be assisted to board
a car and when alighting needs a wheel chair to move around even in
his house had amongst his prized property an AK47, a pistol and a
revolver. The amazing revelation is that they were all supported with
a permit issued from the "Office of the President of the Republic of
Ghana."
In other words, the seat of government had licensed him to carry such
lethal weapons. Just what important function this handicapped
individual was performing for the state to make it necessary for
Rawlings to authorise him to bear arms beats the imagination.
This disturbing revelation was made by, Mr. Yaw Adu Gyimah, Deputy
Commissioner of Police at the Police Headquarters a member of the
small arms recovery team of the JOT, at a one-day workshop organised
by the Foundation for Security in West Africa (FOSDA).
The workshop was part of a continuing complimentary support to
government by the NGO considering the recent upsurge of small arms
related violence, especially armed robberies, in Ghana and the
government's endeavour at managing the societal problems posed by the
proliferation.
Mr. Adu Gyimah's presentation at the workshop, which took place at
the Novotel Hotel, was as interesting as it was disturbing. Various
types of weapons, he added have been retrieved, ranging from the
famous AK47s to locally manufactured pistols. Though it is the
responsibility of the police to issue licences to people who want to
bear arms, the Office of the President in the NDC government, gave
permits to others who the office said performed special duties for
which the possession of weapons was vital. The Police could not tell
what these functions were and were left guessing like anyone else.
That's how come that a severely handicapped man without any
independence of use of limbs could be handed an AK 47 to keep at
home.
The former Volta Regional Minister, Lt. Col. (Rtd.) Agbenaza's
intervention was interesting considering the allegations against him
during the heady days of the last elections. He was reported at one
instance to have brandished a weapon at a time when such an act went
further to aggravate the tension which had gripped the nation. He was
reacting to the fear expressed by one of the participants about the
excessive possession of illegal weapons in Bawku, which to him could
be tackled through a cordon and search procedure. The irony of the
reaction lay in the fact that the government of which he was part did
not do anything to obviate the mayhem, which turned the border town
into a bedlam in which dozens of lives were lost. An age-long ethnic
rivalry in the border town has made the possession of small arms a
fashionable thing among young men.
The Chief Director of the Ministry of the Interior, who read his
minister's address at the function announced that an institutional
structure on the moratorium on small arms, the Ghana National
Commission on Small Arms was under way. The Minister expressed
satisfaction that the arms recovery exercise had produced some
results.
The importance the current government attaches to the recovery of
arms found expression in the presence of the Attorney General and
Minister of Justice Nana Akuffo Addo at the workshop who announced
that the legal aspects of the establishment of the commission on the
moratorium on small arms were in the works in his ministry.
Nana Akuffo Addo who launched the NGO's publication, "Focus on Small
Arms In West Africa", paid tribute to General Erskine who chaired the
function and General Amadou Toure who unlike other West African
soldiers he said have invested their careers in the promotion of
peace in the sub region.
General Erskine on his part praised the media in Ghana but for who he
said the country would not have been where it is today; he was
however critical of the fourth estate for doing little about
sensitising citizens about the issue of the dangers of the
proliferation of small arms in the country.
He said good governance could curb the demand for small arms and
therefore proliferation.
In his contribution, Mr. Alfred Salia Fawundu, UNDP Resident
Representative in Ghana, said the UNDP's initiatives to constrain
small arms supplies encompass efforts to counter illicit trafficking
through legal measures and helping governments strengthen judicial,
administrative and enforcement measures.
The UNDP small arms curbing measures include conflict prevention,
demobilisation and reintegration of former combatants and helping
communities to improve education and livelihoods to provide
constructive alternatives to violence and conflict and promoting
democratisation and the rule of law.
Small arms are weapons designed for personal use and include various
types of assault weapons like the AK47, SLR, SMG, G3 and shotguns.
Light weapons on the other hand include the MG3 fired from a tripod.
Some versions of rocket propelled guns are designed for use by
several persons or a crew who among them are responsible for loading,
firing and carriage.
The problems posed by these small arms are varied. They truncate
social activities and pose serious problems for international
humanitarian agencies and threaten democracies.
During the northern conflict, which claimed several lives, various
assault weapons were in use by the civilian combatants. Security
personnel during the conflict arrested traders who bought these
weapons which they attempted moving up north after selling their farm
produce like yams at the Accra Yam Market.
Another danger about the proliferation of small arms is that once it
happens it is difficult and expensive reversing it. To a great
extent, intelligence and co-operation from the members of the public
are required for its success.
In the current recovery exercise however, Mr. Yaw Adu Gyimah said the
co-operation from members of the public has been excellent even
though some of them turned out to be red herrings!
The question still remains: why was a severely handicapped individual
given a permit to keep an AK 47 at home?

Original_Search_Title: Supreme Court Decision Not Final
Headline: Nigeria: Supreme Court Decision Not Final
Body_Text:
Chief Bola Ige, Attorney-General and Minister of Justice, is a man in
the eye of the storm. Since he went to the Supreme Court to seek
judicial interpretation of sections of the 1999 Constitution central
to resource control, he has been the butt of attacks by those who
believe that the action runs contrary to his long-held notion of true
federalism. Even among his colleagues in the Afenifere, Ige has been
under serious attacks for differing with some of them on their call
for a Sovereign National Conference. Not one to be humbled by
critics, Ige has been firing back. For more than one hour on Monday
night, he entertained questions from TEMPO's team of WILSON UWUJAREN
and Goodluck Ebelo
We would like to begin with the Sharia issue. You have been quoted
as saying that the Sharia is constitutional. What informed this view?
I guess newspapers have their way of construing whatever you say.
I gave an interview to ThisDay and they decided to do a caption in
the way they liked which was not quite the same as what I said in the
body of the interview which they reported verbatim. What I said is
this, from 1979, both in the 1979 Constitution and in the 1999
Constitution, Sharia is provided for. You in fact, had not only
Sharia courts, you had Sharia courts of appeal whose custodians, the
Grand Khadis, our constitution recognises. I said what some of the
states in the North have been doing is political Sharia. The Sharia
allowed in the constitution has to do with how Sharia affects the
civil life of Moslems. Political Sharia pretends that it is invoking
everything that a Moslem accepts as his way of life. The penal code
of the northern states, which is different from the criminal code of
the southern states has always taken into consideration some of the
elements that you might call criminal Sharia and they are part and
parcel of the penal code. What our latter day evangelists of Sharia
are doing is to say we are going to establish Sharia courts which
will deal with the total life of Moslems.
Now, if you meet any Moslem, whether northern or southern or eastern
or western, any Moslem will tell you that anybody who accepts the
Islamic faith accepts that his life will be governed by the Sharia.
And so, what the political Sharia people are doing is to use that
emotion to take Moslems to Sharia courts where the Moslems allow
themselves to be governed by the criminal aspects of Sharia which is
not in the penal code.
And so, what they're doing is not flying in the face of the
constitution, but it is not at the same time what you will say is
what is provided for in the constitution. Up till today, in spite of
the fact that Bariya Magazu, that is the girl who was given a hundred
strokes of the cane... I have received about 10,000 protests from
within and outside Nigeria but the girl herself has not complained
that her rights have been violated.
It is true that we do not like some of the things that are taking
place in the Sharia courts, the so-called Sharia courts. But the
truth of the matter is if somebody willingly submits himself to the
jurisdiction and says at the end of it Allahu Akbar, God is Great,
how does the Attorney-General of the Federation go to court and say
'you have violated the man's rights.'
And the Christian Association of Nigeria and Olisa Agbakoba have
taken cases to court and the problems that they have had to face is
the issue of locus standi. That is, wetin be your own. The people
about whom you are complaining have not complained and I said I am
waiting for someone whose rights have been violated to come and
complain and then see whether I will not do anything. That's what I
said and I have said, and I want to repeat it, not one Moslem has
gone to court to say his rights have been violated and no Christian
has complained that he has been taken to the Sharia court, which the
political Sharia people have set up, and tried in accordance with
Sharia whether the civil or the political Sharia after he has told
them that he is a Christian.
And, I still ask any of you journalists, whether anyone has come to
your office to say 'look I am a Moslem or I am a Christian, I have
been taken to a Sharia court and I have told them that I shall not
submit myself to their jurisdiction' and they have been tried in even
this Sharia court about which we are complaining. That's what I said.
You have made a distinction between civil and political Sharia.
Of course.
Is the political Sharia constitutional?
If it was constitutional, I would not make a difference between
the Sharia that is in the constitution and political Sharia.
What are the goals of political Sharia?
Plainly political. You see, people don't do their homework. The
people who started political Sharia are the All Peoples Party
governors in the old Kebbi, Zamfara and Sokoto states. And it is and
was an attempt of the APP governors and mark you, all the nine
governors of the APP are in the North. Not one is in the South. The
intention of the APP governors in introducing political Sharia is to
put the back of the PDP state government in the North up and to show
them to the Moslems that they're not true. And, of course, the PDP
state governors in reaction now want to show that they are more
fundamentalist and more Sharia people of the old Sokoto State. You do
not know that there has always been what you would call theological
tension between the Quaddriya Moslems of the old Sokoto State and the
Tijjaniyya of the old Kano State. And those of you who are Moslems
will know that the Kano people are more liberal politically but when
it comes to a point where they want to show that they are true
Moslems, they can be more fundamentalist or appear to be so than the
Quadriyya people.
Now, people who don't even know what is happening, they dabble in
some of these things and then they would notice however, that whereas
the governors of the three states of the old Sokoto are what you
would call unabashed in their advocacy of Sharia because they live
within the Sokoto caliphate, the others are only pandering to the
politics of Sharia. Now, part of the problems we face in Nigeria is
that people who don't even know the nuances of these things and who
don't find out exactly what the situation is, they just make comments
and what I continue to say is, for example, the governors who say
they're going by Sharia will tell you that they have not imposed a
state religion. Or have you heard anyone of them say that they have
imposed a state religion? Because they know that the day that they
say they have imposed a state religion, that is contrary to the
constitution. But they say what they're doing is to affirm what every
Moslem affirms. And that what they want is to give them a playing
ground where Moslems can be truly Moslems. And can you fault that?
People have talked about double standards. You mention the fact
that nobody has come out to complain that his or her rights have been
violated by the implementation of Sharia laws. Ironically, we are not
aware that anybody complained about resource control, yet you went to
court why?
I have not gone to court for resource control.
Who went to court?
Have you read my writ of summons?
They said you've gone to the Supreme Court for an
interpretation...
Of section 162(2). I have not gone to court for resource control.
I continue to ask you my friend, go and read my writ of summons and
go and read my statement of claims which are only 10 paragraphs. Have
you read it?
I have
What did I ask for?
You were asking for interpretation of that section you quoted.
Good, where did you get the idea that I went to court for resource
control where did you get that from?
But the issue of littoral status and off-shore/onshore dichotomy
which is central to your suit is a crucial element of the resource
control advocacy...
Now what is the meaning of resource control?
I was going to ask you what the meaning is.
Do I know? Was I the one who introduced it?
If I understand those who use the word they say they've been
dispossessed of their natural resources and they want resources based
in their different states or communities back.
They are perfectly right to want anything. And I have never said
that I am opposed to what they want. I have never said so, have I?
But that is what your action presupposes
It is not, young man!
Then what informed your decision to seek interpretation of that
part of the Constitution that deals with control of resource between
units of the federation and the centre?
Go and read section 162, subsection 2
You can educate us, you are the attorney-general
Go and read my statement of claims. Look, this matter is in court.
It is what in law is called subjudice. I cannot discuss it. Go and
read my statement of claims. You know, people just talk glibly that I
have gone to court for resource control. I have not gone to court for
resource control. I have gone to court for an interpretation of
section 162(2) and that is the territorial limit of a littoral state.
You say you have read it, is it different from what I've just told
you?
No sir. But this...
So where is resource control in that? Not only that, I have asked
for a determination. I said the federal government said this, the
littoral states said this and by the way, it's not just the people of
the Niger Delta that are littoral states. For the third time, I am
letting Nigerians know that Lagos, Ogun and Ondo are also littoral
states. In the past, nobody considered them as littoral states, were
they? Were they?
They were not and that is probably because there is no oil in
commercial quantities in some of those states.
Don't you think I have done Nigerians some good? And so where do
you people find that I took anybody to court for resource control?
I'm interested, where did you people find it?
That is the key element in the argument of people clamouring for
resource control.
Isn't that what I've asked the court to do for us? That Federal
Government thinks this way, states which are littoral states think
this way. Court, please, what is the correct thing to do. Is there
anything wrong with that? In your heart of hearts. And if you read my
statement of claims. I said, FG thinks this way, littoral states
think this way, what is the correct position.
Have I done any wrong?
But there are many Nigerians who have followed your political
career over the years and they look up to you as someone who has
always fought for true Federalism in Nigeria. Many of them feel
disappointed by this whole action of offshore/onshore dichotomy,
littoral states and all that. What have you got to say to them?
I just feel sorry that what we need in this country is to be able
to understand what we mouth glibly. At the risk of being called
immodest, I don 't think there is any federalist icon bigger or
greater than myself in this country. And, therefore, it ought to be
granted that my track record is not such that I would throw away my
belief. I will never do that.
And why is it that people never even look at the positive side of
things. Why have our people become so cynical? And I ask a question:
In 1982, Ambrose Alli went to court against the Federal Government,
did he not win? Why does everybody think negatively and cynically.
