The action that I've taken to court, can someone enlighten me how
that infringes on my credentials as a federalist?
I suppose that when you include this onshore and offshore
dichotomy, people think that Nigeria is supposed to be a federation
and that you are fighting against the same causes you have espoused
over the years.
What have I said? Again, I go back, go and read my statement of
claims. Have you read my statement of claims? What is it in that
statement of claims that makes you think that my statement of claims
does not uphold the federalist cause? You tell me one paragraph.
Like the issue of dichotomy between on-shore and offshore. Nigeria
is supposed to be a Federation of 36 states. Doesn't that settle the
littoral argument?
If the constitution is not clear about that, should I not ask the
court to make it clear?
Is the constitution ambiguous the way it is now?
It is so. If the constitution is not ambiguous, I would not go to
court. Or do you think that section is clear?
But we have been managing it over the years and people fear that
you are merely working to the answer by going to court.
Which years? Since 1999, is that years? Have you really studied
the constitution? Have you studied section 162? Why didn't you my
young brothers, when you were coming to me, take the constitution and
come and challenge me. Why must you just come merely because of
people throwing words about. About which very few have thought out.
Sir, these are not just idle talk. A lot of people who really love
you, are genuinely worried?
I agree. I want to express my appreciation especially for the
people who love me but I also have a duty to the people who love me
that we must always pursue truth and that I, at my age let those who
love me know the truth of what I am doing. And they have a duty also
not to allow themselves to be prejudiced by people who have other
motives.
As Attorney-General, given that there are ambiguities in the
constitution wouldn't a review of the constitution be a better and
faster all inclusive solution than going to the Supreme Court.
Yes and no
How?
Yes, in the sense that if we were sure what the result of the
review of the constitution would be, I would agree. Do you know what
the review of the constitution can bring forth? Do you know?
We can't tell.
If you can't tell, then the answer is no. In other words, we must
take a situation as it is because you yourself say you do not know
what the answer will be. So let us take the matter as it is. The
Federal Government feels one way, the littorial states feel another
way. Let court be the arbiter. And I continue to say one thing, the
judgement of the court, whatever it is, will be the beginning of
further political engineering in Nigeria. I hope you understand what
I mean.
Would the review of laws going on lead to the removal of the Land
Use Act and the Petroleum Act of 1969?
The Nigeria Law Commission has a statutory duty to look at the
laws that need to be removed. I have all the decrees which have been
passed in the last 10 to 15 yeas. We are looking at that and those
that need to be thrown out will be thrown out if they are in conflict
with the constitution. With regard to the Land Use Decree, I can say
specifically that you know it has been entrenched into the
constitution and if you want anything done, it would have to be
through a constitutional amendment.
Why is it that you did not so much disagree with your colleagues
in the Afenifere until you took up this appointment to serve under
President Obasanjo?
Actually, the truth of the matter is that there is no fundamental
disagreement. I can tell you that. Some people are playing games but
there is no fundamental disagreement in the leadership of Afenifere.
What is this game about?
I don't want to talk about it
Let's talk about the Alliance for Democracy I remember some time
ago you told TEMPO in an interview to ask Chief Ayo Adebanjo if he
could call Ambassador Yusuf his party leader. Today, AD has had a
convention and a new leadership under Musa Abdulkadir. People want to
know from you if you could refer to him, an alleged ex-convict, as
your leader?
You are the ones who said so because if what you say is libellous,
it's you who have said it. I haven't said so. I think, with very
great respect, Alhaji Abdulkadir will call me his leader.
But he is the National Chairman of your party and you're believed
to have installed him.
Do you install somebody who is your leader?
But is he the leader of your party?
My dear friend, I thought that is a simple thing to understand.
It seems that you doubt the story TEMPO did on Abdulkadir
I have not read the story
I'll get you a copy. Recently, your friends, some of your old
friends in the progressive camp met and they said they wanted to form
a new party of the progressives and we were surprised that you were
not in the meeting. Why were you not there?
Quite frankly, I didn't receive a notice to the meeting until
after it had taken place because I was outside the country. I saw a
letter but the letter did not indicate that people were going to meet
to form a new party. Quite frankly, I didn't know what they met for.
People saw the move as part of the realignment of political forces
you have talked about.
I strongly believe that there will be a realignment of forces this
time. And I pray to God that it should happen within the next 18
months. There must be a realignment of forces. What shape it will
take will depend on those who are going to bring these about but
there must be a realignment of forces. If you know the structure of
each of the three parties, you would know that although the AD is the
best of the three parties, there's no doubt about it.
Whatever internal quarrel the AD has, it is the most focused. It is
the one that people expect to be ideologically correct, it is the one
that people respond to very genuinely.
As for the two other parties, they want to exercise power, that's
all. And that's why you find birds of unidentical plummages, if I can
use a plural for plumage, gather together. But I am praying to God
that within the next 18 months, there should be a realignment of
forces. But the realignment of forces does not mean people who have
already predetermined what they want. No, people should come together
and decide, look what do we want to make of Nigeria. The realignment
of forces that I'm thinking of is the type that will position Nigeria
as a truly great country on the basis of a truly federal set-up which
will be able to bring about the best of Nigerians in which Nigerians,
whatever region or zone or state they belong to will know that the
goal for the greatest good of the greatest number is the one that is
important. Let me give you an example. You probably read in the last
few days that of the six hundred and something thousand who to took
JAMB and WAEC examinations, I think Gombe has fewer than 700
candidates. Lagos had more than a hundred thousand followed by Oyo,
followed by Rivers. In fact, probably all those who wrote the exams
for JAMB from Gombe State were not as many as four secondary schools
in the South West Local Government of Ibadan: Ibadan Grammar School,
St. Anne's School, Yejide Grammar School, and Ibadan People's Girls
School. Now the realignment of forces must be such that could say
while we will not stop people who want to grow, we are going to make
people who are on the floor grow and fast too. And therefore the
mechanism for creating a revolutionary educational situation in the
educationally backward places because Nigeria cannot go on without
serious trouble when you have a state who will send less than 1,000
children for JAMB. And that state probably takes more money from the
Federation account than Osun State which probably has about 20,000.
And in the realignment of forces who will agree that this is the type
of life we want for our people wherever they live. This is the type
of government we want. There must be a realignment of forces,
political forces.
You played a major role in the setting up of the three political
parties, the PDP, APP and AD. Are we to expect another major role
from you in this realignment of forces.
I pray God to use me in any way he wants. One thing I don't do is
to predetermine for myself what activity I engage in politically.
Those who know me know that I don't do that. I accept jobs as they're
given to me. 'Oh, Uncle Bola, we want you to do this' and I will do
it to the best of my ability. I certainly intend to campaign and to
work for a realignment of forces in Nigeria because that is the thing
that can help our country develop.
Talking about realignment of forces and how it affects the
politics of this country in 2003 one of the things everybody has been
talking about is the candidacy of President Olusegun Obasanjo and
Atiku for a second term.
Quite frankly I think the newspapers just want to fill their
newspapers with something that they like to salivate on. I have no
reaction to the thing yet because President Obasanjo and I have not
discussed anything about 2003.
Based on his performance so far, do you think he's ready for a
second term?
You know I don't answer hypothetical questions. I never do.
Let's talk about Osun State before we come back to Nigeria. The
problem in the state between the governor and his deputy, some people
think the problems are compounded by your personality, that you've
refused to sit the governor down and talk to him
What truth do you want me to tell the governor?
That he retrenched workers in disregard of the AD manifesto to
provide employment for all?
Now, is that a crime?
And these are people that have not been absorbed elsewhere. The
economy is not strong enough to absorb them and the state is trailing
other states of the federation in all areas.
Do you really know that? Can you honestly swear to God that you
know? You see, the truth of the matter is, when Osun State was carved
out of the old Oyo State, about 60 per cent of the workforce went to
Osun State. Is that not a problem? Secondly, Osun State is the
poorest state financially in federal allocations, is that not a
problem? Thirdly, because people moved into Osun State and
particularly into Osogbo, the lives of civil servants, all of them
congregated there. Some of them, their qualification is BA Yoruba, BA
Home Economics. And you had situations like in a school of 15
teachers, 9 had qualifications in religious studies. If you are the
governor, would you like that situation to continue? I always ask
people, what would they do if they were in Bisi Akande's position.
Nobody has ever answered that. What would they do? Here is a man,
nobody has accused him of mismanagement. Nobody has accused him of
dishonesty. Nobody has accused him of abuse of office. Nobody has
ever challenged his integrity. Does anybody think he's just a callous
person that he just wants to sack people. By the time of the
retrenchment, Osun State was spending 75 per cent of its revenue in
paying about 23,000 civil servants and teachers. It was when it was
to go up to 89 per cent that he took the action that he took.
Does it sound reasonable for anyone in government to spend 89 per
cent of the revenue of the state in paying 23,000 people? In a state
of over two million? Now, these are some of the things you the
younger ones must sit down... because sometimes I think you just
enjoy being in opposition and criticizing those in government whereas
you could be governor of Osun State tomorrow. If you are the governor
of Osun State and you have to employ 89 per cent of the revenue of
year state on paying salaries of 23,000, are you going to go on?
I'll like to ask you a question arising from your analysis. You
said Osun State receives so little from the Federation account, and I
suppose that many states in Nigeria especially in the South-South
have these complaints...
Please, don't talk about South-South. There is no governor in the
South-South who will complain about the money they take home.
I'll tell you why they complain, sir. Given what they contribute
and given the kind of challenges they have in their state, they have
to complain...
Agreed. Now you're talking but don't compare the two. What I think
you should ask is, the man who has 22,000 writing JAMB exam and the
man who has only 700.
It's all part of this national issue we're talking about but...
What I'm saying is... I'm sorry my dear young people, are some of
the things that we in the past when we wrote for newspapers, we
brought facts from all parts of Nigeria. You just don't focus on
something about which you have very little information. I don't know
what has happened to journalists in this country. People will just
take something and fight over it and you don't look at the total
picture.
Now, the situation in Osun, I think Governor Bisi Akande had said a
new beginning had begun in Osun State in which Osun State is moving
from poverty to prosperity.
And I want to say, and I have no apologies to make, I don't think any
governor in Nigeria can claim to be more focused, to know what he
wants to do and to have experience of doing it as Bisi Akande. And
that's why whatever people say, they've not been able to challenge
his integrity. Right now, I know for example that he's building 330
blocks of classrooms. I told him a couple of months ago that part of
his problem is that he does not have sufficiently good relationship
with the press. If you go to Osun and you find out what has happened
as far as education is concerned, and the amount of money Bisi Akande
is putting into educational development, and health development,
you'll be amazed. But he thought that well, 'if I do my best, the
press will appreciate it.' He does not know that these days, you' ve
got to blow your own trumpet and I've told him that he should change
his style and be more journalists proactive.
My original question is this sir, what is to be done about states
like Osun which are not the least contributors to the Federation
account but receive so little in return.
That's part of why I say there must be a restructuring of Nigeria.
The issue of derivation must be in the front burner, nobody can doubt
that. He who does not work shall not eat, simple. And those who
produce must eat a lot. I'm an unrepentant supporter of the cause of
the oil producing states because God who put oil there knows that
there must be devastation, there must be ecological problems, the
people there don't have good portable water to drink, they have no
land to produce food, they have no water to breed and eat fish.
Therefore, God has given them that oil, the money from there they
should be able to go to Abakaliki and buy big yams, go to Plateau and
buy Swan water to drink and import food with the money which God has
allowed them to have from oil. There's no way we can run away from
these things.
From these oil issue you are talking about, up to barely few weeks
ago, you were seen as a hero but with these whole thing about going
to court the situation seem to be changing...
Don't let us go back to that. I have asked you two intelligent
people, the writ that I have taken to court, what is in it that
denies the people of the oil producing states what belongs to them?
What have I done?
The problem between Akande and his deputy has continued to get
worse. We want to know what you, as a senior citizen of the state
have done in respect of it?
I think you better ask the two of them, not me
You should tell us, you're a senior citizen in that state
Yes, you see...
And it is being claimed that because you brought Bisi Akande to
power therefore you must take responsibility for his actions.
I suppose the people who think that way are entitled to their own
opinion. It's like saying... no I don't want to go into it. I have no
apologies to make for the support I gave to Bisi Akande and continue
to give to him because I am satisfied that he's a honest leader, he's
a hardworking administrator. He has had problems about finance but
nobody has been able to impinge his integrity. And If I am said to
support such a person, I thank God.
And you know, talking about that, has anybody pointed to a PDP
governor whom any of the AD states should emulate? You know when they
talk about PDP wanting to take over in Yorubaland, I said the
simplest question a Yoruba man will ask is in the whole of Nigeria,
which PDP governor are you going to point to, to say this is the type
of governor that we want. And I ask you two gentlemen, is there such
a PDP governor?
What of Chief James Ibori of Delta State.
When we're talking serious things, you're talking such things.
Lets talk about deregulation. There is fuel crisis and the federal
government is insisting on deregulation. People are suffering...
I know. I know about the suffering of the people.
Why isn't it possible to get the government to forego its plans of
deregulation.
I'm not the person you should ask that. I think it's President
Obasanjo you should ask that.
But you're in government
Yes, The opinion that I will give, I will not tell you. It will be
irresponsible to tell you.
But deregulation is it a policy thrust you support?
I am a left wing socialist and I make no bones about it. You see,
when you people talk about wholesome competition and how you want to
open Nigeria to capitalism, once you accept capitalism, you must
accept capitalism, you must accept everything about it. You can't eat
your cake and have it. If you want Nigeria to go capitalist which is
what all of you are clamouring for, you cannot run away from
deregulation. Deregulation is one of the planks of capitalism. I have
never accepted capitalism and it's too late in the day for me to
accept it.
Have you made your opinion known to President Obasanjo?
There is nobody in Nigeria who doesn't know my ideological views.
If you want capitalism, you must have deregulation, you must.
So what is to be done, not on the part of the government but on
the part of the people, what's to be done?
An ideological orientation. Nigerians must decide to take their
own destinies in their own hands but you can't do that in a
capitalist economy.
Deregulation, is the best option for the government?
Why are you asking me? Somebody who does not accept capitalism.
You want me to be making a case for capitalism? You're wasting time.
How do you solve this problem of the oil industry?
Me, I'm not in the oil industry
But you are in Nigeria and you buy petrol too.
Yes.
Of course, as a Minister now you don't queue but members of your
family, you friends they all queue.
That's true. I also know that Nigerian oil is being sold as far as
Burkina Faso and I hear Mauritania, all over.
Isn't it the duty of the government...
It is what capitalism spurns. The people, the Mafia, that manage
to get our oil out of the country, do you think they are people like
me and you?
They may not be but these are human beings that any government
with the police would deal with.
If you want a capitalist economy, do you think of socialists
methods...
In other words, we are right to conclude that President Obasanjo
has failed the nation because...
I have not said so. I have not said so.
As it is now, one is tempted to come to the conclusion that
President Obasanjo has failed, nothing is working anymore.
I think you are very unfair. If General Obasanjo has failed, I
won't be in that government. In fairness to him I don't think there's
any other Nigerian who works harder, who's more committed, who is
truly committed about what should happen for the good of this
country. He loves this country to the marrow of his bones and he is
doing everything humanly possible to succeed.
I can also say that in many ways, he has succeeded. What has happened
is that there are one or two areas which there is compounding of
things but General Obasanjo has done very well considering that he's
only been there for 21 months. He has done very well and I can tell
you that if he didn't, I wouldn't be in this government.
It is difficult to appreciate that with so many problems like fuel
scarcity, NEPA and all these social infrastructure that are
collapsing.
Now, in fairness let me give you an example. The amount of road
work that has been done in the last 18 months, nobody has done it in
the last 40 years. Nobody.
Where is the evidence, I don't see it.
The roads are being made all over the country. I also know about
the infrastructure that is being put in place in regard to power.
Even this year alone, over N60 billion is going to be used for the
improvement of the power situation. You may say that we are not half
done but there's no doubt that a lot of work is being done. I also
know that, take for example on the human rights angle, a lot is being
done and in the next month or two, a unique bill is going to the
National Assembly for children and women. It's going to be a
revolutionary bill in the history of Nigeria particularly given the
various interfaces in Nigeria.
I agree with you or the issue of fuel scarcity. I know that President
Obasanjo himself is extremely worried about this. I sincerely hope
that he and those who are in charge will do something about it but I
tell you he himself is very much concerned about it. And in regard to
power, he has promised that by the end of this year, that will be a
thing of the past. And I know that everything is being done to see
that the promise is fulfilled. May I also add that when it comes to a
vigorous desire to move Nigeria in the capitalist way, a lot of
effort is being put in place.
A lot of people are not happy with the Obasanjo economic outlook
especially his romance with key Western financial institutions like
the IMF and the World Bank, There is genuine fear that he is about to
mortgage Nigeria's sovereignty.
I know him enough to say that he loves Nigeria sufficiently that
he will never want to mortgage the future of Nigeria. But I also know
that his party and the leader of the party in government are
committed to a capitalist tag for Nigeria and frankly, just as during
the socialist hey days in which people were complaining that there
are traumas, difficulties about the socialist structures. I guess
when they also went to try capitalist structures, people are bound to
suffer. But I try as much as possible to be fair because I have never
beern satisfied that Nigeria must follow a capitalist path.
A few months ago, my very good friend and colleague, Adamu Ciroma,
said that I was a loss to the socialists cause because when I was in
NEPA, it had to be privatised, that's the policy of the government.
Let us face it, after Gorbachev betrayed socialism, the world became
a unipolar world with the United States bestriding it like a
colossus. And the whole world now has to tow the line that the United
States plot and Nigeria is an underdeveloped country. We want to
catch up. The socialist countries, because of internal betrayals, are
no longer in a position to be shown to the world as examples. So,
we've got to face this thing pragmatically. And I want to say that
Nigerians will have to be patient. When I see the actions of students
and the actions of labour movements, these organisations act as if we
are in a socialist society. We're not. We're a neo-capitalist society
and the earlier we realise that the better. May be what we should do
is to gather our heads together and say okay since we're doing
capitalism, how do we do it reasonably, I mean properly. Maybe that
is a matter which should come up at the constitution of a national
conference.
I was surprised when you mentioned the achievements of Obasanjo.
You failed to mention the anti-corruption campaign.
Because you were asking me about physical things. As for the
anti-corruption campaign, General Obasanjo and many of us have set
ourselves against corruption. It is the worst cancer that has
destroyed the human fabric of Nigerian society and it is something
that has to be fought very vigorously. But you see, corruption comes
in different forms. When there are incompetent people who ought to be
thrown out of their jobs and you want them kept, that is corruption.
People seldom look at it from that point of view. You see that this
thing is not good and you go ahead and go on with it, it's
corruption.
I was going to ask about corruption in the federal executive
council because you were quoted recently in the media that you were
not appointed a Minister to come and eat.
I never said that. Again, I'm surprised that you would be
misquoting me in my presence.
That was what we read in the papers, that you were appointed to
serve. Are there people who are not serving?
No, I never said such a thing. You must have read it wrongly.
That you were in the federal cabinet to serve and not to eat?
Yes, because Chief Afolabi said that the PDP invited me into
government to come and eat. And I said General Obasanjo did not
invite me to come and eat, he invited me to come and serve. And that
there is nothing in any government for me to eat. That's what I said.
But people are insinuating that your government presupposes that
there are cabinet ministers who are just eating.
They must be stupid, very stupid. Because people know me. I don't
insinuate, I say what I want to say.

Original_Search_Title: This Dan is Cool
Headline: Nigeria: This Dan is Cool
Body_Text:
Dan Foster is Cool FM's biggest on-air personality. The African
American has been setting new standards for radio presentation since
he arrived Nigeria early last year. He took Yinka Ijabiyi through his
life and work
The voice is deep and rich. Though delivered in the true Southern
American accent of its owner, the voice commands the attention of the
listener. And you only have to listen just once, to be hooked.
Behind the deep-throated voice is the intelligence of a man with the
crafty style of a master storyteller. Every weekday beginning at 6
a.m, the masculine voice of Dan Foster helps thousands of listeners
start their day. He does so with plenty of jokes and good music.
Indeed, Dan has managed to carve a niche for himself as breakfast
radio's best host. His delivery is impeccable. He may not speak
Queen's English but his American accented English has millions of
fans.
Dan has a natural style which is as smooth as silk. He puts on no
airs on air. Or off it for that matter. Around Dan, the Big Dog, as
he calls himself, there is never a dull moment. Rib-cracking jokes
and stories that would make you laugh until you shed tears are
familiar elements of his company. He can tell a good story and he
knows it. He has known it since his days as a sound engineer with
Kathy Hughes in the United States. Kathy Hughes, he says, is a black
sister who owned three radio stations in Washington DC back in 1992.
Today, Kathy also presented a live talk show on her stations. Then
Dan was her sound engineer. He communicated with her via a little
microphone in her ear. He'd tell her to take calls, pause for
commercials and even remind her of her appointments when she got off
air. He recalls that he would say things like: "You'll probably want
to take caller number four because he's really an asshole. You'll
have a good time with him," into the tiny mic in her ear. So good was
he with Kathy that she would continually thank him for doing a great
job for her even while she was on air.
Off air, she still showered him with praises. Because of this
familiarity, Dan said he soon learnt to relax in her presence rather
than be shifty anytime she was around like the other workers. He got
so familiar with her that he called her Kathy even though she always
insisted he called her Miss Hughes like the other employees.
It is to Kathy that he owes his success in the radio world today.
According to him, Kathy had told him that she wanted a special
package for February which is the Black History month in the United
States. "So I came up with these creative stories, black stories to
tell. I did some research and came up with some stories, added some
music and sound effects to it and people loved it," he remembers.
It was for one hour. At the end of that hour, people were so
enthralled that they asked for more.
For his one hour of storytelling, he won the Broadcaster of the Year
award.
That was the beginning of his career as an on-air personality (OAP).
Dan is one great storyteller. As he tells his stories, one tumbles
into the other creating a perfect story with a great ending.
For instance, as he's telling the story of how he started out in
radio, he smoothly incorporates the story of how Kathy's devotional
period on radio provided him with the idea of dedicating some time to
God on his show every morning. While telling this story, he continues
to counsel his teenage nephew back in the States about the evil of
giving up to the devil for any reason.
Dan continues the story of his big break. "Somebody else heard me (on
radio) and they started getting me to go tell stories for children of
all ages in libraries, big library though, the Washington National
Library.
I was there and everybody got off to come and see these little kids
gather round me with this little wired microphone. 'Hi kids! How's
everybody? They gave me five hundred dollars just to tell a story for
Halloween. It was an amazing story.
I was telling them a story about this little boy that changes into a
little monster or something and the lights were a little low. And I
said: "They say it's in my family too. And I don't know when it can
come back. It can just... It's getting cold, has anyone seen my
jacket? I'm really getting cold. Where's my jacket? Anyone seen my
jacket?' And they said: 'There's your jacket right there' And I said:
'Okay. I got ta put it on, I'm cold.' I'm putting the jacket on and I
put this ugly ass mask on and I said: 'Oh, there's something
overcoming me. Could someone please pull the jacket.' No one said
anything. And I said: 'Pull the jacket.' They didn't know I had this
ugly, ugly mask on. And the little kids came and said: 'We'll pull it
off.' And when they did, they were so scared they were tumbling over
themselves trying to get away, even their teachers.'
The newspapers made a big story out of this great performance at the
library the next day. On air that morning, Dan says Kathy wanted to
know how much he was paid for the job. And I said, 'you gonna ask me
that on air live? What If I told you it's none of your business?" And
she says: "Oh, none of my business? I'm the one that pays your
check,' she says "Nigger, Please! You will meet me in my office when
you get off the air."
The argument got bigger off air and it strained their working
relationship.
It was about this time that he got a job offer in the Virgin Islands.
A new radio station had just been opened by a young man and he
offered Dan the morning belt. It was called 'The Dan Foster Breakfast
Jam.' Kathy asked him not to go but he chose to anyhow.
Two years later though, he almost had cause to regret his decision. A
hurricane swept away the radio station. Not wanting to return to the
US directly, Dan got a job to coach American Football at a Catholic
School. Again, Dan has a couple of wonderful stories to tell about
his time at the catholic school. He says for instance that, the
catholic school was filled with children from very rich homes whose
parents were only too glad to hand them over to the priests at the
school so they could help train them because the kids had become
spoilt brats. "These were some of the worst kids in the world and
their parents knew it. And of all things, this catholic school was
the one that could beat the public school in fight," Dan says.
Dan says he had a hard wonderful time working with the kids. He was
also given an opportunity to teach at the school even though he
didn't have a teaching degree. He describes teaching as one of the
best things he'd like to go back to sometime. While at the school,
Dan also had the opportunity to write, direct and produce a nativity
play. This play and the funeral of one of his best students are
memories which he continues to cherish.
"The play I wrote for the school was about abortions. So many kids
were having abortions. And this was supposed to be a Christmas play.
The principal was like, 'it's Christmas time. Why do you want to talk
about abortions at Christmas time.' I I said 'don't worry, I got the
theme, it's a heavy theme.' She says 'I hope you're good with this,
don't forget this is a catholic school.' Oh, it was a rough play from
the beginning. It opened up with a scene with a brother coming down
and saying, 'Eh, Sharon, how is it doing?' And she says, 'there's
something I got to tell you, I'm pregnant.' He was like, 'What? It
ain't mine. It must be John's. I know John wanted to kiss you. Oh, it
must be Mike. I heard you hooked up with Mike. So don't come blaming
me.' And she says, "How dare you' and they go on into a fight and at
the end of that, his shirt is torn."
For Dan, acting is about reacting. He explains that his success with
the nativity drama then was helped by the fact that he simply chose
the right personalities in the school for the right roles. He says he
painted a scenario and asked the students to react to it and thus
they got their parts. And this, he says, was what was lacking in the
home video, Face of a Liar in which he acted a part.
"I'm supposed to be an African. A Nigerian that left Nigeria when he
was six and then I come back, what, thirty-eight years later?! I'm no
longer an African, I'm an American. I've been in America for
thirty-two years. So, when I come back and somebody says, 'oh, by the
way, I ain't your father,' I'm pissed.
I say, 'what you mean you ain't my father.
He's like saying, 'No, no Dan, don't get so emotional.' I say 'what,
I'm not an African.' He says 'Don't do that, this is Nigeria.' I say
I need to be me, if someone tells me that right now, I'm going to
react to that situation. I'm like 'yo, big dog's back. What's up Dad?
Thanks for sending me to America. I know I was in Nigeria but, eh,
I've been to America, I've been in DC. You not my father? Eh, what
the hell you mean you not my father? Well who is? You mean you've
been lying to me all these years sending me money and stuff? Who's
the man that abandoned me? Damn it. I wanna know who the hell he is.'
And then when I see him, 'What the hell you abandoned me for?' I want
to be aggressive with the role and do it this way." But he doesn't
get his way and so as far as he's concerned, the film was certainly
not to his taste.
Whether to his taste or not though, Dan says the film sold at least
eighty thousand copies just because he was in it. Right now, he has
vowed to write his own home video because he thinks the Nigerian
market is right for it. All he needs right now, he says, is a good
story.
Dan loves Nigerian dishes. His best Nigerian meal is, wait for it,
pounded yam. Yes, pounded yam and very peppery soup. He loves pounded
yam because "I just love the way they pound the hell out of the yam,"
he says. And as for peppery soup, he says he loves eating the soup
hot and having sweat breaking out all over him courtesy of the
pepper.
His favourite colour is sky blue. He loves a chapman and won't say no
to a chilled bottle of Star lager beer. Though not an avid night
crawler, he says he has begun to be a regular face at Incognito,
Towers and Tiberias.

difficulties.
After he left the Virgin Islands in 1997, Dan said he headed back to
the United States. The only reason he left the Caribbean where he was
enjoying himself so much was because the girl he was dating at the
time claimed she was pregnant for him. An American like him, she said
she wanted to go back to the States and that if he did not follow
her, he would never see the baby. Not wanting to miss being a
first-time father, Dan said he threw in the towel in the Caribbean
and headed back to Washington. In Washington, he was well received at
the radio station where he formerly worked.

dropped a tape of his voice, Dan says he approached another radio
station, Mix 106.5, for a job. Though a white station, he was offered
a job immediately.

called him from the airport that she had lost the baby.

devastated but he knew he had to get on with his life and this he
did.

move again when he walked into the station one day and saw everybody
filling out their resumes. When he asked why, he was informed that
the radio station had been sold. The implication was that most of the
staff would be replaced by new people. Nobody wanted to wait for such
a day. His next job was as a credit control officer with a credit
firm. The firm helped people with debt problems work out their
credits and even counselled them. It was while on this job that he
saw the Cool FM job on the internet. He applied on-line and he got
the job. At his office then, there were like four Nigerians working
with him. Three of them had sung the praise of Nigeria to high
heavens.
The last one had painted a dismal picture of a Nigeria filled with
crime and death. On the United States official internet site, Dan had
been advised, after he wrote in to seek information on Nigeria, not
to even think of travelling down here. Stories of death in the hands
of the police, armed robbery, and other such violent crimes were used
to discourage him. Dan said that despite all these, he chose to come
to Nigeria anyhow because he loved broadcasting and he, a former
marine, could not be scared into hiding by stories of violence. Dan's
father too was not too enthusiastic about his first child's desire to
go to the 'jungle of Africa' to work. The prevalent mentality in the
United States is of Africa as a continent of highly backward people.
Dan's other siblings, a brother and two sisters, would also probably
have stopped Dan from making the trip had he not been the oldest. Dan
recalls that when his younger brother who is a medical doctor called
him at the station recently and was put on hold with the music of
Britney Spears playing on the station, he could not believe a
Nigerian station could be playing Britney Spears' latest music!
Dan's younger sisters, a nurse and a lawyer, have largely come to
understand he's doing a job he is enjoying.
One year on, he's not had cause to regret his decision to come down
here. He describes the time he has had here so far as fun. He's fully
settled and thanks to the free hand he's given to innovate and create
on his five-hour morning programme. Dan is having a ball. Dan is not
married yet but this is a situation he might remedy in a very short
while. Courtesy of the love he's found right here in Nigeria. Dan's
life has certainly taken on a new kind of fun. He won't allow her
name in the papers yet because he doesn't want to pre-empt or
pre-spoil anything. He has found love and she's a Nigerian.

Original_Search_Title: Ex-Government Accused Of Neglecting Sene
Headline: Ghana: Ex-Government Accused Of Neglecting Sene
Body_Text:
Accra — The people of Kwame Danso, capital of Sene District, have accused the
discredited NDC government of total neglect of the district.
"It is one of the poorest and worst districts in Ghana," they
claimed, adding that it was a complete sabotage by the NDC
government. The road from Atebubu to the area is in a very deplorable
state, compelling passengers to depend on tractors instead of
vehicles during the rainy season. Speaking to newsmen at Kwame Danso,
Mr. S. P. Sarkwa, NPP constituency chairman, said many projects of
the assembly have been abandoned for lack of funds.
Conducting newsmen round some of the abandoned projects, Mr. Fred Nii
Paterson, the Senior Executive Officer of the district, denied
reports of the commission of the district education office. He said
the contract, which was awarded to Alhaji Salisu, NDC chairman of the
constituency, at the cost of ¢161 million, believed to be the highest
contract, has been abandoned by the contractor for almost six months
and cannot be commissioned.
Other abandoned projects include a 17-unit office complex for the
Ministry of Agric.
According to Dr. Kofi Afakye, district agric officer, the project was
abandoned almost one-and-a-half years ago with the contractor nowhere
to be found. He said he knew the contractor to be one Mr. Frimpong
but it was awarded to him in Accra.
All attempts to trace him through AESL, who are the consultants at
Sunyani office, have proved unsuccessful. It was also alleged that
there had been a theatre and a 40-bed capacity ward built but it was
only a theatre, which is now under construction. Dr Nafuli Peter
Vuuri, the acting District Health Director, confirmed the allegation
and said personnel from the head office of the Ministry of Health
came to inspect the projects last week before this reporter's visit
but met a ghost ward.
They have appealed to the NPP government to come to their aid by
tackling their roads for them so that Kwame Danso, an agric town,
famous for yam, cowpea, rice and groundnut, can help the nation by
transporting food to the entire nation.

It is to Kathy that he owes his success in the radio world today.

Original_Search_Title: Home-made Bomb Goes Off In Gayaza
Headline: Uganda: Home-made Bomb Goes Off In Gayaza
Body_Text:
Kampala — A "highly lethal" homemade bomb was yesterday detonated by police in
Kasangati-Kyankima village, off Kampala-Gayaza road.
Terrified residents scampered for cover when the bomb, which had been
concealed in an aluminium Britannia biscuit container, was detonated
by the Police Bomb Squad at about 8.00am.
Two police dogs were used to sniff and confirm that there were
explosives.
The police at Kasangati described the bomb as "highly lethal". They
confirmed it was "planted" in the home of a Kampala businessman,
Emanuel Kavuma.
Kavuma's house, on Nakigoye Road, is surrounded by a short mistle-toe
hedge without a gate.
A visibly-shaken Kavuma, a trader in the city's Nabugabo business
area, said he returned home with his wife at about 10.00pm and packed
their car beside their bedroom window.
"I woke up at 5.00am to check on the car which was packed just
outside our bedroom window. I saw a tin near the car. The tin was
covered with part of the hedge, yams and black plastic bags. At first
I thought a mad person or a nightdancer had visited us," Kavuma said.
"I rang the Nangabo LC3 chairman, Kizza Seninde, my brother David and
the police. There were wires leading to the car. My wife said it
might be a bomb.
The police brought dogs. I suspect it was meant to explode if I
turned on the car's ignition," he added.
On the motive of planting the bomb at his home, Kavuma said, "The
chairman (Seninde) and I are some of President Museveni's supporters.
This bomb might be a result of political differences, may be here or
at Nabugabo where I work.
But we leave it to the police to investigate and tell us."
Meanwhile, the anti-terrorism unit detonated another home-made bomb
at Kampala Casino last night, report Davis Weddi and A. G Musamali.
The bomb was discovered by the special-hire drivers who park in front
of the casino on Kimathi Avenue. The bomb was hidden in a tree on the
pavement.
The drivers informed the security at the casino, whose managing
director, Bob Kabonero, called in the anti-terrorism unit based at
the Chieftaincy of Military Intelligence. The unit came with a robot
to detonate the bomb that went off in a big explosion at about
8:00pm.
The police said the bomb had a timing device and they suspected it
had been set to go off later that night during the busier hours.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Medicine Men Invade Street Pavements
Headline: Zimbabwe: Traditional Medicine Men Invade Street Pavements
Body_Text:
Harare, Zimbabwe — Zimbabwe's traditional medicine
men are seldom far from controversy.
They are at it again, this time claiming they have
discovered an effective AIDS drug, the African potato.
As if the claim is not controversial enough, the medicine
men have invaded major cities in the country, vending the drug on
street pavements.
Street corners and shop fronts in the cities, particularly
the capital Harare, have been turned into open air pharmacies,
much to the annoyance of medical authorities who think the drug
is fake and perhaps harmful.
The African potato, a yam-like tuber whose botanical name is
hypoxis, first hit town in January this year, and has since
spread throughout the country.
The medicine men claim it cures many ailments, such as
cancer and AIDS, which conventional medicine is not yet able to.
With a quarter of Zimbabwe's 12.5 million people estimated
to be suffering from AIDS, the drug has found a ready market.
Officially, 5,000 people are thought to be dying from the
incurable disease weekly in the country, a figure many say is a
gross under-estimate.
"During the first few days, we used to sell about five
pieces (tubers) but now we are selling much more," said Kurauone
Phineas, an African potato vendor.
"I am not disclosing how much because there are many people
who are fighting us," he said, evidently excited about the
booming business.
Cost is another major attraction of the drug to sufferers.
It costs between Z$30 and Z$50 per tuber, depending on size. The
vendors say this is enough for one person for a month.
Conventional retro-viral drugs, by comparison, cost hundreds
of times much more, and are beyond the reach of most Zimbabweans.
But, as in similar cases in the past, the traditional
medicine men are not having it easy with medical authorities with
their latest discovery.
Conventional doctors, including Minister of Health Timothy
Stamps, are warning people against using the African potato which
they say could be harmful as it has not yet been subjected to
laboratory tests.
"Purchasing the African potato from the street is very
dangerous. These herbs are toxic and can kill people on the spot
or damage their kidneys permanently," said David Mabaya, a
director of government pharmacies.
Stamps agreed: "There are eight different types of African
potato classified as poisonous and medicinal herbs, implying that
whoever prescribes it must know what he is doing as it can be
poisonous."
"The herb has a strong action in the human body. An overdose
can lead to poisoning. In fact, it does not really cure or
reverse any illness. What it does is that it makes someone feel
different," he added.
But the traditional medicine men say this is yet another
ploy by modern medical practitioners to under-play their
discovery, in order to dominate the medical industry in the
country.
"Some of them are buying (the African potato) from us and
then reselling to their clients at very high prices. They are
charging patients in excess of Z$3,000 for a stomach ailment but
we charge Z$100 or less for the same concoction," said Phineas.
Patients, for now, seem to support the traditional medicine
men who have frequently locked horns with the country's medical
authorities for dismissing most of their medicines as fake.
"I was in a serious condition when I came to this clinic (of
a traditional medicine man) a month ago. But Mr Ncube prescribed
for me among other things, the African potato. I am now feeling
much better and I have re-gained my weight," said an AIDS
sufferer.
In spite of the medical authorities' skepticism of the
African potato's medicinal value, AIDS sufferers in Zimbabwer are
flocking to the traditional medicine men's pavement pharmacies,
including tourists.
"There are many whites flocking into this market looking for
the African potato. Some are tourists while some are locals,"
said Lizwe Baloyi, who operates a thriving pharmacy mainly
specializing in the drug.

Original_Search_Title: Prof. Nkrumah Recalls Accra Of The '50s
Headline: Ghana: Prof. Nkrumah Recalls Accra Of The '50s
Body_Text:
Accra —  Pof. Joe Nkrumah of the Ghana National Museums and Monuments Board
had part of his childhood days in the city of Accra in the then Gold
Coast. He recalls those good old days with nostalgia in a chat with
the Accra Mail in his office.
SOCIAL LIFE IN The CITY
Accra in the 50s was an interesting and hectic place especially in
the central part, the Orgle Street area or Faase as the Gas called
it. Faase means behind the stream. This is an implication that there
was at a certain period of time a body of water or a stream at the
place. There were various cultural activities in Accra because of the
different ethnic groups existing in the city. The half mile square
radius area around the present day Bank of Ghana and the Parliament
House were scenes of many social activities at weekends and even
during week days. The present day Ghana Commercial Bank was the site
of the famous Rodgers Club, a social centre built by the one-time
colonial governor of the Gold Coast, Governor Rodgers. This spot was
open to all races unlike the European Club, which was an exclusive
all-whites club. Across the road in front of the old Parliament
Building was the Accra Town Hall built by the then Anglican Bishop of
Accra during the tenure of Governor Hodgson. The old Polo Ground
where the Kwame Nkrumah Mausoleum stands today used to be the Accra
Community Centre. There was also the Baden-Powell Memorial Hall,
which also served as a social centre. When the aforementioned centres
were occupied the Bishop's Girls' School could also be used for
social functions.
CINEMA HOUSES
The elite of society had their own cinemas and these were the four Rs
cinemas - Rex, Royal, Roxy and Regal, which were owned by Captan. The
Temani Brothers also built the Cinema Palace, which was the first to
show daytime programmes at 12.15 not forgetting the 8.15pm shows.
Then the Opera Cinema came on board to compete with Cinema Palace. It
cost 6pence to watch a show. As kids in those days we raised money to
enable us go for these shows by selling newspapers like Graphic,
Evening News, Daily Echo and Morning Post. This venture could fetch
us some 5shillings a day.
GROWING UP IN THE CITY
As kids we slept little waking up at 3am because we had to engage in
a number of ventures like selling newspapers and helping the
fisherfolks to earn some money and at the same time go to school.
This was a time when discipline was at its highest peak. When fish
was in season we went to the beach through a stairway near the Ussher
Fort to help the fishermen cart their catch home. We created a sort
of balloon with a cloth and this we used to hold some of the fish we
carried for the fishermen. We carried with us a basket each. We added
the few fish we were able to get to the ones the fishermen gave us
and sold them. This done we went to collect the morning newspapers
which we sold before going to school.
SCHOOLS
As a kid I lived at Osu RE initially and it was at this place that I
started schooling. While there I sold cut yams for my mother. The
pieces of yam were arranged in a pan and to keep them fresh I carried
water in the pan for watering.
In 1951 the Gold Coast District was situated at Nima junction and it
was responsible for primary education. Giffard Camp was for Middle
School education and this was where I had my middle school education.
The late General Kotoka was one of my teachers at Giffard Camp. At
that time all the teachers in the garrison schools for kids of
soldiers like myself had teachers from the military's Education
Corps. My father was a soldier in the Gold Coast Regiment fighting in
both India and Burma. At the end of the war he re-enlisted as a
teacher.
I was not strictly a barracks boy because my father was part of the
administrative and professional class in the military who lived
outside the barracks. I can not remember the feeling I had when my
father was leaving for the war because I was too small.
CHILDHOOD DAYS IN AKWAPIM MAMPONG
I had part of my childhood days in Akwapim Mampong and it was
interesting watching soldiers engage in military exercises during
which they used live ammunition. After the exercises we took the
unused bullets and the empty cases of the spent rounds with which we
played with. We opened the brass shell and emptied the powder and
made it to generate a fine smell. Another thing we did was create
holes on a plank and place the live rounds in them. We then supported
this between two stones and employed a certain trick to do target
practice.
BARRACKS LIFE
The only time that I experienced life was in Kumasi. In Accra in the
40s administrative and professional staff lived outside the barracks.
It was only the infantry men and bands men who lived in the barracks.
SUPERMARKETS
The Kingsway stores was next door to the Ussher Fort in those days
and the building still stands today. It occupied two floors. The
shops were later moved to the new Kingsway when the place was
finished. Other shops were the CFAO, A.G. LEVENTIS, SCOA, UTC, SAT,
G.B. OLLIVANT, John Holt. The Unilever House was built by UAC.
ACCRA IN THE '50s
Places like Abossey Okai, Mamprobi and others were seen as being
outside Accra. From Abossey Okai one had to pass through Fadama,
which was a reclaimed land and the Owusu Memorial Park where the yam
market is. The city had two cemeteries but churches like the
Methodist Church and others had their own churchyards.

Original_Search_Title: PJ Dines At The Ghanaian Village
Headline: Ghana: PJ Dines At The Ghanaian Village
Body_Text:
Accra — La-Palm Royal Beach Hotel has recently opened another restaurant.
Two, as a matter of fact, but it's The Ghanaian Village, which serve
local dishes that thrilled me on my visit there for lunch the other
day. I am welcomed to its very airy sheds with an optional calabash
of sweet palm wine, whose first taste on my tongue is quite
refreshing.
The main meal on the menu for today is Banku and Tilapia, and Chicken
khebab with an assortment of accompaniment, including yam balls,
fried potatoes and Jollof rice in small portions. I opt for the
khebab dish. I am not about to leave La-Palm this afternoon with an
unsavoury smell on my hands. I, like many Ghanaians I know, devour
tilapia, and this is neither the place nor the time to engage in a
plate of the delicate fish, best eaten with the hands, like many
local dishes! A chicken dish is always a civilised one. I could use
my cutlery and behave civilly.
The restaurant is set in the gardens of the hotel, quite near the
coastline. A gangway of some sort leads to its entrance with
different colourful plants lining its side. The ocean roars nearby as
you eat, and there is certain serenity about this place, which makes
you relax without even trying. The decor adds immensely to all this.
It's all thatch for the roof, calabash for lampshades, trunk beams
for pillars, bamboo here, and cane there, with a ceramic and earth
colour scheme. It's simply beautiful, the way it's done.
The waiters are eager to please, and seem to be at the 'beck and
call', ready to provide any item one asks for. I want hot pepper, I
tell one, and in an instant, it is provided.
The seats are cane, wide and with comfortable cushions. When the food
arrives, two tall khebabs on one plate, nicely garnished and
accompaniment in another on the side, I mumble something in my head
about the portions. They seem small, but fill me up somewhat, I
realise by the time I'm through. The presentation is most impressive.
The starter was coconut salad, which was a mix of lettuce and onions
with slices of real coconut fare. Very tropical! There is creme
caramel for dessert, which I pass.
Eating here is an experience. There's quite an ambience, and the
prices are high. But you get to be fussed over. Most credit cards are
accepted here.
The Ghanaian Village is inside La-Palm Royal Beach Hotel at La. Call
711700 to make reservations.

Original_Search_Title: Issues: the $5.8 Billion External Debt
Headline: Ghana: Issues: the $5.8 Billion External Debt
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's external debt makes a mouthful of zeros behind the main
figures. Dressed, we simply call it ¢31.7 Trillion just wondering how
many zeros fill our imagination. The loan itself is not as seriously
high as the length of the actual figure expressed in cedis if it
comes to be written. Why do Ghanaians want to ridicule their nation
this way?
The only comments available on the internet concerning the Ghanaian
currency is about the story of a foreigner who was going to receive
money transferred to him through one of our banks. He said he saw
people in the queue going away with filled black plastic bags. It was
when his thousand dollars were packed into a sizeable BLACK PLASTIC
BAG - a good seven million cedis of fourteen bundles that he started
trembling. He had nowhere to hide the trash!
POCKET MONEY
One tends to carry his pocket money in his wallet and not on his head
to the glare of all thieves and armed robbers. And exactly that is
how heavy our small loan of $5.8 Billion has become! Indeed to some
of us it only goes to show that we are not credit worthy enough as a
nation.
To think of it this same amount would have been around seven trillion
cedis just three years ago. Our cedi was then around ¢2,300 to one
dollar. We have chosen the path of making the same amount of $5.8
Billion cedis sound 31.7 Trillion. This is more than four times what
the Ghanaian tax payer is being asked to pay for that same amount of
dollars!
Meanwhile, for those three years the best many employers have been
able to do has been to increase the yearly salary by a maximum of 25%
per annum. This has in only a few cases ended up in a 100% increase.
Petrol prices got about 25% increase each year to at most 100%. Beer,
soft drinks and other taxable products have not had their prices
increased much. Yet these tax payers are being asked to pay three
times the cedis for the same amount of dollars. This does not make
any economic sense.
If that foreigner were to carry that bag of seven million cedis
exchanged for his one thousand dollars back to New York NO BANK WOULD
EXCHANGE The LOAD TO GIVE HIM EVEN ONE DOLLAR FOR HIS BUS JOURNEY
HOME!
Which means that the cedi is globally worthless no matter how low we
sink it in relation to the Dollar.
On the other hand if that stranger took twenty thousand of that load
of cedis and bought two tubers of yam, he could sell it to some
Ghanaian couple he or she knows in the same New York for some $ 5 or
even $10 to get a good meal. This goes to show how we are just
burdening ourselves to try to cook a price for our cedi. It is
worthless outside.
If we do not wise-up about this fact we shall be putting a useless
yoke on the tax payer for nothing. It is only the products we put on
the international market that can help us to pay the external debt.
And many of these debts have only one digit interest rate and many
years of moratoriums!
The worth of the cedi should therefore not be allowed to stifle the
very process of production! Anyone who borrowed dollars for any means
of production three years ago can simply not pay for it.! Many may
have to abandon their dream some have gone back to Europe or America.
The wonder story of the ex-ministers' cars are all a part of this
problem. ¢38 million price of the car was around $16,000 three years
ago. One quarter of that amount is now $4,000 after 3 years. This is
today ¢28 million.
Why do those who operated that kind of cedi depreciation of the
economy not understand the simple mathematics of it and pay ¢28
million? Ghana listens to any foreigner who comes along as an
economic expert to talk the book knowledge we seen too much to
understand and adore with no ability to re-shape before applying. Yet
we claim to be better educated than most African countries.
The reader should just open page 33 of the latest West Africa
magazine.
On this page is listed the worth of each African country's currency
to the dollar. Even Somalia without a government manages around 2,680
of her currency to one dollar. We are only next to Mozambique whose
floods have shattered her economy.
There is a lot of carelessness in our micro and macro fiscal
management within the various zeros behind our currency figures.
Normally the small child would have asked for a unit figure, one
cedi, to buy herself something. We have trained and are still
training our children to ask for one thousand cedis, five hundred
cedis or the least two hundred! It is the same training we are giving
to our economic managers. It is one point or three point something.
Our cedi is worth nothing! We can as at now never get an
international worth for it! The Minister of Finance would himself
recognise how many pebbles would clear of his eyes and the eyes of
all Ghanaians if we were to say today that our new currency is now
called so so and so,.That it is worth 70 to the dollar and worth
¢100. This means the cedis become like our pesewas. This would not
change our external debt much.
BURDEN
Even more courageously the minister can give a new worth to our own
cedis. Seven hundred cedis to one dollar shall never change the
dollar. It shall change the necessary artificial burden we have given
ourselves. It shall make us able to produce more without the tension
of fear!
Surely our internal debt burden can be reduced through that
corruption promoting good for nothing so called market forces induced
high interest rate. There are more than enough computer experts in
the transparent market of the USA. Yet only a few days ago when the
interest rate was going to be changed BY The NEW ADMINISTRATION no
one knew whether it would go up or come down. No one could guess by
how much.
Republicans lower interest rates. Democrats increase it. Both do so
for particular purposes. It was increased by half of one per cent.
This was never done through market forces!
Which market forces in Ghana then are supposed to determine the
interest rate? The sale of cocoyam leaves and snails in the
hinterland? We should take a radical care of the worth of the cedi to
the dollar. If we are able to add a radical cut even a surgical
amputation to the level of the interest rate, this country would see
wonders! People are prepared to borrow, produce and market!
Those who want to remain at their desk in the office should watch us
produce! Artificial manipulation of interest rate and the worth of
the cedis through supposed market forces is no capitalism. It is just
a lazy manipulation.

Original_Search_Title: Bekwai Youth Bare Teeth
Headline: Ghana: Bekwai Youth Bare Teeth
Body_Text:
Accra — A meeting of the Bekwai Traditional Council was last Monday disrupted
when a delegation of the Abusuapanin, Nana Kwame Osei Tutu decided to
meet the council on issues bordering on the proposed destoolment of
the Omanhene of Bekwai, Nana Appau Karikari.
A group of concerned citizens had earlier called on the Omanhene to
abdicate the Ayeboafo stool within two weeks.
Hardly had the delegation led by the spokesman, Opanin Akwasi Poku
briefed the council on their mission that the Queenmother, Nana Adwoa
Pramang III, prompted the council to close the meeting to avoid the
delegation.
The delegation which had been prompted into action following concerns
raised by the concerned citizens wanted to know the reaction of the
council to the destoolment charges against the chief.
Nana Appau Karikari is said to be ineligible because he is not a
member of the Oyoko royal stock.
He is also reported to have impregnated an SSS1 graduate of the local
SDA Secondary School causing her to drop out of school. The said
student (name withheld) is presently married to the chief.
Present at the said meeting were the Krontihene, Nana Okofo Asiedu,
Akwamuhene Nana Ntiamoah Amankuo III, Twafohene Nana Kwabena Gu,
Gyasehene Nana Kwabena Asante III and Akyeamehene, Nana Asante.
In an interview with the Chronicle, Twafohene Nana Kwabena Gu said
the Abusuapanin's delegation came at the time the meeting had closed
having exhausted the agenda.
Nana Gu admitted that the Omanhene had duly been served with a letter
calling for his abdication and that neither he nor the council could
react to the said letter because the Omanhene had travelled outside
the country.
Abusuapanin Nana Osei Tutu and some kingmakers have already initiated
moves to prefer destoolment charges against the Omanhene before the
Asantehene.
Manhyia sources have confided in the Chronicle that the Asantehene's
senior linguist, Nana Boakye Yam has summoned the Abusuapanin of
Bekwai, Nana Kwame Osei Tutu and Opanin Akwasi Poku to a meeting to
this effect.

Original_Search_Title: We Must Talk - Emeka Ojukwu
Headline: Nigeria: We Must Talk - Emeka Ojukwu
Body_Text:
Q: Let us start off with your title which has been expanded from Eze
Ndi Igbo to Eze Ndi Igbo Gburugburu. What is its significance, given
the "Republican" nature of the Igbo nation and that leadership among
the Igbos is considered non- monolithic?
A: Let me seize this opportunity to thank you for according me the
honour of addressing the vast readership of your magazine. Your
magazine, TheNEWS, has been involved in the long struggle for
democracy, which we appear to be winning. I remember that TheNEWS was
pushed aside initially, but today I am confident that people who are
interested in Nigerian politics read TheNEWS. I want to wish your
readers a prosperous year. I want to assure them also that, like all
of them, I look very much forward to peace and prosperity. I will
like to add 'equity' in Nigeria also.
Q: Thank you very much for the compliments. Now your expanded
title...
A: You asked me an interesting question actually, particularly when
you added the 'Republican' nature of Igbo society. First, my title
Eze Igbo is different from Eze Ndi Igbo. I am Eze Igbo by virtue of
the general will of our people. There are people who don't understand
the significance of Eze Igbo. There are many people who, sometimes,
betray ignorance of the language even when they try to make some form
of translation. Eze in Igbo is not just a king as you would have Oba
or Emir. Eze is a champion. If you are a good boxer, you become (in
boxing) an Eze. If you are a yam farmer, the same thing-an Eze. If
you take some prestigious titles, you become an Eze of something. I
take pains to explain to people that my own is far more on the
conscience of Ndigbo.
Q: How?
A: If you are really looking for a comparison, you talk about the
College of Cardinals in the Roman Catholic Church. They are the
princes of the church. But they are not really princes, except that
where they are gives them a quasi princely presence. So, you find
that an Eze is without territory or territorial pretensions. I don't
tax anybody. I am just somebody who is just at the pinnacle of the
Igbo conscience. When you get a Bishop and you say "My lord," he is
not a lord, he just happens to be addressed in that way. So, I am Eze
Igbo because the people have been going around in different areas,
saying it. Even when I arrived from exile, I was given the title
Ikemba, I was again given Eze Igbo dike di oramma (the leader of the
Igbo who has found favour with his people). Later then, the Eze Igbo
became more prominent.
Q: That title has been extended with an additional word,
gburugburu....
A: Actually, there is no official extension. People, again of their
own volition, have started saying gburugburu to differentiate my
position in the heart of Ndigbo from the position of the local Eze
Ndigbos that you find in various areas. Gburugburu only indicates the
totality of the Igbo nation. Why that? I am flattered, of course,
when people say so. But when you say Alayeluwa (owner of the world in
Yoruba) to an Oba, he does not own aye (the world). So generally,
these titles that you add is to create an impression. When they say
uku akpolokpolo in Benin and all that, it is to create impression.
The point is that our people have chosen to separate my position by
making sure that this Eze Igbo is gburugburu. That is, 'to
everybody'.
Q: How do we relate that to the Republican situation here?
A: The Igbos are not Republican! They have never been. You don't take
pride in the break-down of society! The essence of society is that
the group has, within it, a hierarchy. People are quite mischievous
when they say we are 'Republican,' to the extent that they deny us
any form of political organisation. When you say the Igbos are
Republican, it is another word for saying that the Igbos are without
order. No society can thrive without order, you know it. Because we
have been forced into reading and speaking in English, our
explanations and positions are usually, at best, approximated.
'Republicanism'? No. Otherwise we would not have in our language, a
word for the Eze phenomenon. It is our language and right through the
ages, it has remained a word for some form of excellence. If you tell
me that our society has a lot of freedom in it, no problem. Tell me
that we will not dobale (prostrate) in our society, yes we don't
dobale. But we do bow to the Eze and to certain people. That,
however, is not evidence of 'Republicanism,' There is a title in Igbo
land which says Okwoto Ekene Eze (he who stands up to greet a king).
You can imagine what that signifies that this person is such a
personality that in a community, he alone could greet a king standing
up. What do the others do then? They bow to the king. So you see, the
concept of 'kingship' is there. All societies produce kings, leaders,
presidents... It is wrong to think that because we are 'Republican,'
that we lack order. No, we have order and we have heirarchy in our
society. I hope I have touched the three major parts of your
question.
Q: There was a renewed crack-down on the Movement for the
Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB) and Ralph
Uwazuruike, its leader. What, in your view, could be responsible for
that?
A: I don't know what was responsible for that. What I can tell you is
that people are getting panicky over nothing. I deplore the
crackdown. I deplore the attack. I abhor the killings attendant upon
the attempt. Why? This is an organisation of young, non-violent
people. The young people have remained non-violent in everything they
have done up till today. The police at no point said that these
people shot at them. For some unknown reasons, they decided to
attack. Why did they attack? Have they invited them to the police
station for questioning and they refused? I think we have to curb the
excesses of everybody in Nigeria that we, the tax payers, pay for and
give arms to. I abhor the rampant situation in Nigeria where, anytime
you get the police on duty, they tell you it is 'accidental
discharge.' Damn it! The time has come to make sure that there are no
accidents, because those people killed are Nigerian citizens that
(the Police) are supposed to protect. The Police would say 'we shot
five, we shot 10.' No. Shooting, even one, is wrong, very wrong. I
will, therefore, pray that the Federal Government reins in its
coercive agents and, certainly, check the level of command that can
unleash death on the citizenry of Nigeria.
Q: What do you want the government to do about the incident?
A: I will like to see an inquiry into the excess at Okigwe. I will
like to see a situation where, if the police were found to be wrong,
compensation is paid. I don't mean money will pay for the deaths, no
amount of money would do that. But I want it done, symbolically, to
tell the whole of Nigeria, one, that the life of every Nigerian is
worth something, two, that we don't pay with our taxes, assassins in
Nigeria.
Q: The police explained that 'MASSOB Police' shot at their men. That
brings up the issue of insecurity and the advent of ethnic militias .
What, in your opinion, could be done to bring about security in the
land?
A: A lot of things in Nigeria have degenerated. The Police, (I am not
saying anything new), have, on various occasions, failed Nigerians.
The police, at various times, have been highhanded, inefficient. But
there is no doubt in my mind that the security of our people is such
an important matter that cannot be abandoned to individual self-help.
Therefore, if police have to ensure our security, it is then
necessary we look again into their recruitment, training, deployment
and service conditions. There is no doubt in my mind that at each
stage of what I have enunciated, our government has failed. I have an
orderly here whose uniform remains at half mast! That is here (he
touches his shin, that front part below the knee). Why should that
be? The police should never look ridiculous. I know, for a fact, that
the police would go for three or four months without salaries. That
is very wrong, because if you don't pay the police, you are inviting
danger. This area should be looked into. It is very clear to most of
us that in cases of accidental discharges, there is something wrong
with the training. This is because properly trained police personnel
should not discharge accidentally. I am talking with the experience
of a senior army officer. If the police were properly trained, but
you have accidental discharge, then the weapon is bad. So, the
question of equipment comes in. Not only that the society of
yesterday is not the society of today, a country like Nigeria that
has gone through a war is not the pre-war Nigeria. There will be a
lot of arms more than before in our society. I look and I see the
police, over the years, they are being forced to a retreat. Very soon
the police may not ever go out of its barracks because it is
dangerous out there and the government hasn't made it less dangerous
for them. Bullet proof vests and things like that should be normal
part of their equipment. I think the communications system between
their policemen on the street is so basic it doesn't exist. There are
riot situations that you know that guns are being freely used, there
is no point sending the police in soft-skinned vehicles, because
again, they would be killed. Above all, let me ask: Does their salary
reflect the danger they are expected to operate within? These are
things we have to do.
Q: What should the commission of inquiries do about the MASSOB
incident?
A: One, to know really whether MASSOB actually shot at the police. It
is so easy to say 'you shot at us, we shot back!' It is also
significant to tell you that up till today, nobody has told you how
many policemen were injured. It is also significant to know that
nobody has told you how much damage their shooting caused. Don't
forget, it is not every time that you hear pam that you start firing.
There are many stages before you actually shoot to kill. Sometimes
police shoot...But we need to know more about what happened. It is
deplorable that a non-violent organisation would be treated with such
violence. The police are meant to take care of us the citizens. I
thank you for asking me this question and I take this opportunity to
point that the most effective police anywhere in the world is the
police that invest themselves in the community they are defending. I
am not accusing anybody. But when you send a Sokoto policeman to
maintain order in Nnewi, you are asking for trouble. There are other
things he can do in Nnewi, but he is not going to invest his life in
Nnewi. And if you are part of the community, you would know before
anything where danger is likely to come from. You would be more
efficient. The policeman in Nnewi, at least, should be able to speak
Igbo to be fully useful. When you get an Igbo policeman operating in
Okitipupa for example, well, he doesn't know what is going on around
him. Even when people are gathering and people are talking about it
in Okitipupa market, he doesn't know because he doesn't understand
the language. The only thing he can do actually is, 'show me your
particulars' and at the very first sound of danger, he runs. 'Save
your life and not save the polity.'
Q: What should the government do?
A: I will urge the government not to dismiss off- handedly, the
various suggestions to create local police force. What government
should look into is that possibility of regulating it and presenting
it in such a way that it does not threaten the existence of the
Federation. Let us look back. The Scotland Yard is actually the
police for the whole of Great Britain. Every other person you see in
uniform is local police. There are other examples. I know there must
be fears in other areas. You can do a lot of studies to find that
point where you draw the line. And we should know that the most
effective police is that police that can mount a patrol, either on
foot, or bicycle, but within contact with the people of the area he
is taking care of.
Q: What is your reaction to what critics have dubbed the 'rabble
rousing' nature of Governor Orji Uzor Kalu of Abia State?
A: We have a tendency in Nigeria to call people names. Who would dare
say that Governor Orji Uzor Kalu is rabble-rousing? Who are the
rabble that he has been rousing? Governor Kalu is the most effective
governor that we have in the East today. Certainly when he speaks on
Igbo matters, Gov. Kalu speaks the heart of the people he is
governing. And you cannot dismiss him by just giving him a name,
'rabble rousing.' Now we are moving further into democratic practice.
Among so many other things, we must be tolerant of people who have
different opinions from what we have. It is not democratic to dismiss
everybody else's opinion. There are aspects of governance that Gov.
Kalu keeps pointing at. In a certain way, he doesn't agree with the
president who, incidentally, is my very good friend. We trained
together, we served together and I see him regularly. But he has not
become a God yet! If he does not play the game properly, the duty of
his best friend is to criticize him. So, to the question (I am sorry
that I sort of pointed at a person) as an Igboman, I don't see
Governor Kalu as a rabble-rouser. In fact, I applaud so many things
that he has been saying. Newspapers and magazines, (like yours) would
be better advised to take each point he raised and carry out
research. That is the way to categorise things. At the end of the
day, you may find out that he is the most truthful governor, not
rabble-rousing. And finally, what 'rabble' has he roused? I hope you
are not saying that the Igbo public are rabbles? You can't say that
in front of me and go free!
Q: I asked that question because of what people have been saying.
This is because journalists act as the devil's advocates on many
occasions...
A: Yes, yes, I understand ....
Q: With what Governor Kalu is saying, that Igbo leaders like you,
Chief Ojukwu....
A: I don't like being called 'Chief,' I am 'dim' (d,i,m), because
chief rhymes so much with 'thief' that I don't want anybody mistaking
me for a 'thief.'
Q: I am sorry....
A: That's all right.
Q: The question is, with Governor Kalu's outspokenness of late,
observers reason that Igbo leaders like you, Dr. Alex Ekwueme, Prof.
Benjamin Nwabueze and others might have ordained him (Kalu) as Igbo
leader...
A: I don't know about that. What I know is that he stood election in
Abia state and won. So, Abia State 'ordained' him governor of Abia.
He is doing his work. At the end, we will see how far he has
succeeded and to what extent he has failed. The governor has not been
ordained anything beyond governor to my knowledge. Let me take this
question of Igbo leadership. Actually, why are people so anxious to
impose a leadership on our people? Who is the Yoruba leader? Who is
Hausa leader? Efik? Ibibio? Edo? Look at them, who are their leaders?
Why must only the Igbo produce, at this stage, a universally
acclaimed leader? Why, why, why? I think that is mischief making! It
is either you are running away from a leader or insisting on this
leadership so as to have a target! Igbo leader? Don't worry. At any
stage in Nigeria's interraction within itself, there will always be
an Igbo leader, that I can promise you. We can decide to hide our
leader. Why shouldn't we? What we have seen in Nigeria does not
encourage us to put forward our leader. I think the media should
think again. We are no different from the other ethnic groups in
Nigeria. And the day you find leaders and point them out for all the
