others, and the Igbos are found wanting in that, then you have a
point to make about Igbo leadership. I repeat, who is the Yoruba
leader today?
Q: You once called some Igbo leaders 'hirelings and job seekers.' Do
you want to modify that statement given the protest it attracted? Who
are these hirelings?
A: I haven't seen any protest. First, everybody knows me well in
Igboland. I said that the so-called Igbo summit was something put
together by a clique. And it was that clique that I called 'job
seekers and hirelings.' I am quite prepared (should those individuals
raise their voices) to tell them when they were hired. I am prepared
to tell them what jobs they sought. I can go ahead to tell them what
jobs some of them got. The authenticity of what I am saying has not
been lost to any Igboman. I am prepared to tell them that as far as
the Igbo leadership is concerned, I will only be part of an
organisation for Ndi Igbo where the leadership (whoever is part of
the leadership) would be deemed to have resigned (if he is in the
executive or appointed by the Federal Government). Because really,
the struggle of trying to get the Ndi Igbo into the centre of Nigeria
is raging. You have to decide where you are to be able to lead the
struggle. I don't want to be misunderstood. That struggle is not to
tear Nigeria apart. No. In fact, it will make Nigeria very very
strong. That is why it is a cardinal point (and it should remain
such) of our struggle that somehow we mount sufficient pressure to
get the government of Nigeria to accept a national conference. To set
Nigeria right, we have to understand the things of Nigerianism. We
are adults. Let nobody spoonfeed us. There is nothing more arrogant
than anyone saying 'I know the problems, I can solve them.' Absolute
nonsense! You can't. Let us, around the table, discuss these problems
and let nobody say 'these are no-go areas.' Every citizen of Nigeria
is a sovereign of Nigeria. It is the citizenry that makes up the
sovereignty of Nigeria, not individuals. We are the ones to decide
our future. Nigerians have never yet written a constitution.
Q: How can we get a proper constitution because the one in use now is
heavily criticised.
A: I am one of the few, perhaps, that believe that until Nigerians
have a document fully accented to by the Nigerian peoples, that
begins with the wonderful sentence: 'We the people of Nigeria
agree....' Other countries have it. That is the basis of the
documents that bind those nations together. Not that the British
handed us an independent constitution. Not that the military wrote
this 'nineteen what ever it is.' No. Every creation of state is
illegal and remains illegal. This is because it was not accented to
by the sovereigns that agreed to the Nigeria union after the British.
Even that constitution they gave to us told us how to create states.
We created the Mid West (which was the only legal exercise). The
others have been puniture. We cannot do much in Nigeria if we build
on illegality. It is one of the reasons I always make a clean line,
urging us to consider a national conference and not a sovereign
national conference.
Q: Why don't you like a 'sovereign' national conference?
A: I go away from that because the disruption that it will bring will
be beyond control. There can be no two sovereigns in a state. If it
is sovereign national, it is that conference that assumes sovereighty
while it is on and it may result in anarchy (in fact I would prefer
certain oppression to anarchy and this is my personal opinion. Others
might have theirs). So, the sovereign national might frighten those
in various offices too. Many would say 'frighten them' is not
important, it is just practical, if you want to get on, you must
accept that they are there for the time being. I believe, however,
that we should sit round the table. All the nations of Nigeria must
sit round that table. I believe then that the nations should use
their own resources to negotiate their own positions within the
federation. It is for that, that I support fully people of the
South-South in their belief that they must have resource control.
What they are saying is that Nigeria does not exist today as such.
But for them to be part of Nigeria, they must control their
resources. There is nothing wrong with that, let us debate it. We
might decide also that the rest of us, in order to protect you, would
require a certain percentage of whatever it is you have. You can't
have it both ways. So, there is always give and take in negotiations.
We should start from somewhere. And you cannot say we need to control
this and at the same time be part of telling me to leave my resources
as national reserve. No. There are many things you can discuss round
the table.
Q: The South-South is very much concerned about resource control,
among other issues. What does the Igbo nation want?
A: Let me take this opportunity to enlighten some people that as long
as the pain of the war remains, we Ndigbo will continue to question
our participation in this organisation that causes us so much pain. I
would think that: One, the start point of wisdom for Nigeria is total
reconciliation. And you can tell me that it is 'costly and it doesn't
matter!' Wealth is made for people not people for wealth. We can sit
round and decide. This problem we keep having in Nigeria is too much.
We have wasted so many years trying to patch so many things. Let us
reconcile. And when I talk of total reconciliation, I am not just
saying Igbos and the rest of Nigeria. No, it should be for all of us.
The North claims that they are marginalised . Ok, there is nothing
wrong with the claim...
Q: Can that argument be sustained?
A: When we sit round the table, we will evaluate that claim. That is
the way things are done. What is everybody afraid of? People of the
West are my brothers and sisters and I believe that they look upon me
as their brother. We talk about how to get ourselves moving together.
It is significant that it is the big government that is at fault.
Okafor can Marry Binta and they will live very well in their homes.
So there is nothing really in itself bad about Okafor marrying a
Binta. Femi will marry Ngozi and they will live well. It is when you
are trying to be Lord Almighty over so much that the problems come.
Let us put away our pride. Pride of governing a people you have never
conquered anyway. It is ridiculous. It is bad enough to be conquered,
but to govern me without first conquering me, I think there is
something wrong with that.
Q: There was that slogan, 'no victor no vanquished....'
A: Let nobody point at the war as a conquest. Because for years, for
centuries and, perhaps millenniums, the debate will continue raging
who won and who lost. I went to war to save the Igbo nation. Whenever
I go to Lagos and Kano, I see the fruit of my victory, not the fruit
of my defeat. That is the truth. The other truth is that even at the
point of victory, the Nigerian policy was 'no victor no vanquished.'
My own position is let us stop lying to ourselves. If there were no
victor no vanquished, there should be no bar to total reconciliation.
Finally, I want to point out that very many people are still
suffering as a result of dismissals wrongly given as a result of the
war. So many people are suffering as a result of enforced penury at
the end of the war. So many people are suffering as a result of
wounds inflicted during the war. The catalogue is long and that is
what we should be discussing. I believe that with open minds, we
should be able to organise a Nigeria that can accommodate all of us.
Q: Apart from the pains of the war, what are other areas of Igbo
marginalisation?
A: There is no way you can reassure me about the safety of Nigeria if
you deliberately bar me from the security of Nigeria. It is as simple
as that. You can also extend this to certain areas of governance.
When I feel that for some unknown reasons, the likes of myself are
not ever allowed to take part, this is marginalisation.
Marginalisation comes up when you have a set of rules, either setting
out deliberately to favour somebody and disfavour me, or set up a
universal application and whenever it affects me, different reasons
are adduced. What I am trying to say about marginalisation is when
you bring me as a citizen under different rules because of where I
come from. There is marginalisation when you say other states should
adopt Sharia as the religion or system of law in that area where,
clearly because we are a nation, people from other areas must be
living. And in a situation where our constitution is quite clear that
you cannot adopt a religion as a state religion. There are so many
instances of marginalisation. And my own concept of justice and
fairness is also that whatever I produce as evidence of
marginalisation, I agree that other people would also have their own
catalogue of marginalisation. The only way to resolve it is to sit
round the table. What are we afraid of? Come with your baggage of
marginalisation . Put it on that conference table. It doesn't matter
if it is three years, four or five. We cannot continue running a
colonial left-over and call it Nigeria. Nigerians must not accept
this pattern for their future. Finally, I think I am in a better
position than most to say what I am saying. I have been the one who
talked about the handshake across the Niger. I have been the one
telling you that in Geography class at school, I learnt that the
opposite of north is south, that the opposite of the north has never
been east and west. I have so much personal experience of those
areas.
Q: Handshake across the Niger notwithstanding, there was a report
that you would be General Ibrahim Babangida's running mate. What is
your reaction?
A: I don't work under my junior. At school, educationally,
administratively ( I was an administrative officer of some repute),
militarily , experience- wise, I can even say (I don't know what
anybody might have outside), but money -wise too, you know where I
come from! So generally, when I say things, I would implore Nigerians
to look deeper and think again. I am not flattered by Nigerians'
coming 40 years after and saying 'Ah Ojukwu was right anyway.' That
is not it, if only people would think twice, leave the immediate and
think beyond. Realising that I was right 40 years ago would mean 40
years wasted. There are many things that I can put to Nigerians. For
some reasons, they thought I was a rebel some years back, they should
realise that I have been proved right. And, therefore, they should
listen.
Q: The Enugu Port-Harcourt expressway was awarded to a relatively
unknown contractor, instead of Julius Berger or Strabag. Is this
another form of not taking the South East seriously?
A: I have to confess that it is this type of question that I dread. I
would like Igbo contractors. I would like Nigerian contractors. I am
a bit reticent because I don't want to pronounce 'give me a white man
instead of black man.' I find it difficult. I know why it is so. All
we are saying is that we want an internationally accepted good road
from Enugu to Port Harcourt. It is necessary. Get us the best
contractors. There is the other side of course that you can say that
Julius Berger is Nigerian. I don't want anybody to say I am a
hypocrite on this matter. I, myself, went to the late General Sani
Abacha and said 'give us our share of Julius Berger as well.' I said
so. You understand where I am coming from. I will like as many
Nigerians in this, because this way we grow. But there are certain
things we cannot show capacity for just yet. I am of the opinion that
if Nigeria wanted to establish a nuclear energy plant, I will support
Nigeria using an expatriate firm, a firm from countries that have the
renown and experience for producing such. We have gone in circles. I
would, in this particular matter, like a competent firm ( I will
hesitate to pronounce for one company or the other, there are many
competent firms). Let us use the best we can in Nigeria.
Q: Another area of marginalisation, according to the Igbo, is
appointments, especially in the armed forces. How would you assess
President Olusegun Obasanjo's handling of this situation given what
critics said about the Igbos losing their 'military' opportunities
during Biafra and their refusal to join the armed forces?
A: There are certain lies that when they are told so often, they
become truth! And this is one of them. There has never yet been a
recruitment drive that the Igbos have been lacking in producing
candidates. None. That cannot justify what I can say is a deliberate
attempt not to accept Igbos. This is what I look at primarily when I
talk about total reconciliation. As a human being yes, anybody that
went through those years, 1966-1970, which way you look at it, there
must be certain fears. My point is that the fears have lasted too
long in this area and that if we must move together as an entity,
then the fears should be taken away and we should all look upon
ourselves as one. For every fear that you have for Oyen- Igbo, I can
count six reasons I, as Oyen-Igbo, should have fears about you, the
group that fought against us during the war, raped our women after
the war, condemned us to penury by making all our wealth, for which
we are known, £20 each! I should be more afraid for our houses that
are standing under different ownerships for which we were not
compensated. Oh, the catalogue is long! But in 2001, for something
that ended 1970, I am anxious to see a way to clear these things. Let
me ask Nigerians. What does it cost to pay for certain buildings?
Pay! Sit down, think about it. 'Oh, well we cannot be changing back,
forth, this that or whatever it is', many would say. But let's do
something equitable. It is part of our expenses for the greatest
present we can look for: peace in Nigeria. I keep telling people for
as long as these things stand ...
Q: What do you think should be done about the £20 issue you raised?
A: Everyone that got £20 knew what he had in the bank. What we should
now say, is 'look, it's over, bring your documents. Afterall this is
your contract with your bank, not with the Federal Government. Go and
claim your money from the bank.' What is wrong with that? What is
wrong with that other than the so-called 'victory'. No, if Nigeria
must march forward, these are things we must rectify.
Q: Are you ready to pursue the abandoned property and the £20 issue
further?
A: Oh yes and even abandoned posts. Can you ever say that the Igbos
were not threatened outside Igboland? When they are threatened and
everybody knows that they were threatened, should they sit down and
wait for their heads to be cut off? There are ways to do these
things. Have sympathy for your brother so that your brother can
remain your brother. That is all I am saying.
Q: There is the issue of federal industrial presence in the Igbo
nation. The Federal Coal Corporation was established in 1916. There
was an agreement between the National Council of Nigerian Citizens,
NCNC and Northern People 's Congress, NPC, about the establishment of
Kainji Dam in the North and a steel company in Onitsha (which was
never done).
A: My reaction is that we can solve all these problems on the table.
We can also, at that table, make appointments reflect more of
national needs. These are evidence for anybody to see for the need
for a national corporation.
Q: There seems to be an irony in the fact that the Igbos seem to be
down there, despite their political gains. Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe was
president; Nwafor Orizu, Senate president; Alex Ekwueme, Vice
President... Or is it that the Igbo leaders are actually guilty of
politics of expediency?
A: Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe was, at independence, cheated. There is no
doubt in my mind that the leader of Nigeria, before independence, was
Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe. With all due respect, there were Chief Obafemi
Awolowo, the Sardauna, (Sir Ahmadu Bello), but the uncontested leader
was Nnamdi Azikiwe. At independence, what happened actually was that
the whole English habit/solution to many problems was applied to Zik.
He was 'kicked upstairs'. He was given the presidency without powers.
His irrelevance to Nigeria's future was symbolised by his being first
appointed the representative of Her Majesty the Queen. Nwafor Orizu,
president of the senate, reflected the power configuration of the
time when we had three regions. Then Alex Akwueme... You are the ones
now cheating, not Nigeria! Forgive me for saying that, because you
know damn well that no vice president has power. You quietly moved
away from power and now first talking about symbolism, ceremony. This
is because one of the problems Alex Ekwueme has actually today is
that he does not feature in the power structure of Nigeria. Do you
know that the whole war, even though the Igbo fought the war, was
essentially for Yoruba benefit?
Q: How? Please explain.
A: The war which we fought was because of a coup that was designed to
give the premiership of Nigeria to Chief Awolowo. When it failed, I
found myself obliged to oppose Gowon and Murtala Mohammed on the
basis that without Aguiyi Ironsi, Ogundipe should be anointed. He
fled. And I changed and said that in the absence of Ogundipe, it
should be Adeyinka Adebayo. That was my break with Nigeria. It wasn't
for Ojukwu to go to Lagos. No. There are other things. I think a way
to answer you is to say that over the years Ndigbo have appeared to
be too honest for Nigeria.
Q: Still on Igbo marginalisation and Igbo leadership. There is this
criticism that part of the Igbo problem is that their leaders cannot
withstand sustained heat for their followers. Critics would readily
cite Uwazuruike who escaped on a motorcycle and that you travelled to
Cote d' Ivoire, leaving Biafrans behind....
A: Tell me what Yoruba leader has committed suicide. Tell me any!
When even a Yoruba leader would fly into exile at pure suspicion.
What are they talking about? Uwazuruike was able to deceive the
powers of oppression and got away on a motorcycle. What was the
essence of the fame of the scarlet pimpernel? How many times did he
go to the French to commit suicide? Today I am talking to you. And
you have seen (you have to accept ) that I have been vindicated. I
don't know what it would have been if I had been dead. You wouldn't
have this opportunity. Opposition of Nigeria to Ndigbo is that we
refuse to die! We have the skills. Has anybody in Nigeria shown who
(you know sometimes it is flattering when these things are said,
because you may think we are gods) you will compare. My good friend
Wole (Soyinka). Who threatened him into exile? He is still my friend
and I respect him a great deal. Who? Let's face it. We are not
talking about toy guns now, but real guns. We are not talking about
symbolic take-over of a radio station. No, we are talking about real
war. Now come of it! I even believe that one of the problems we had
during the war was the fact that Ikene was so directly under Gowon's
rule and that Ibadan was under his military. But man-to-man, I still
honour very much Pa Obafemi Awolowo, because I know what I discussed
with him...
Q: What did you discuss?
A: Suffice it to say that I knew that had things been different, he
would have gone differently.
Q: What is your view about the Federal Government's dragging of the
state governments to court over resource control?
A: Whose court by the way?
Q: Federal, I mean supreme court.
A: O tan (that's all).
Q: The question concerns the disappointment of Nigerians with Chief
Bola Ige who used to have a pro-true federalism persona. Now he has
dragged the constituent units to court over a fundamental federal
issue.
A: I don't like judging people. Because this is the type of question,
really, that you should ask Bola. Very often, men find themselves in
contradictory conditions. And this could be one of those occasions.
For one reason or the other, the Federal Government thinks that the
courts should decide on a matter of constitution. But I would have
thought that this should have come the other way round, that is the
state government seeking clarifications, because the government has
it within its right to do one thing or the other. Why do we choose
the long way? If we are around the table, it was there their own
percentage was lifted from one per cent to 13. One sitting! If we go
through a national conference, there is nothing stopping them. They
might end up with 50 or 60 per cent. The position of Chief Bola Ige,
I don't know. It is, perhaps, because of this that I am not in any
position, because I like freedom. I like to act totally as I feel
inside me.
Q: You are a member of the All People's Party, APP. People wonder why
you have not also joined the exodus to the People's Democratic Party,
PDP...?
A: The reason I am not joining the PDP is the reason I did not join
in the first place. What have they done? Because they've won, does
that make them a better party? No, it doesn't actually, particularly
in the Nigerian context. I am a member of the APP and, in the
Nigerian context, I am proud to remain a member. I am totally
satisfied with APP because I have been brought up in a school that
says that parties should distinguish only by ideology. Ideologically,
the APP, PDP and the Alliance for Democracy, AD, have nothing much
separating them. If that is so, why then the difference in the first
place? You would ask me. I will tell you. I prefer the people I am
working with in APP to the people I have to work with if I were in
PDP.
Q: There was this innuendo that you did not join the PDP because you
hate President Olusegun Obasanjo?
A: I call him Omoba(Prince). He is my good friend. I can work with
him anywhere. In freedom and captivity, I can work with him. Today,
if I pick up my phone, we will talk. I phone him always. There is no
time I go to Abuja that I don't have a meal with him. He is my
friend.
Q: What are the chances of APP in the 2003 presidential election?
A: You should ask the Secretary General that question. There are so
many things we don't know. First, I think you should not register
parties and therefore parties should spring up. I believe that
passionately, not because we happen to have slidden to the little gap
of APP, PDP that you should block others who want parties. Two, I
believe if we are really a democratic country, that independent
candidature should be allowed. This is because that is the only one
that blows the whistle on conspiracies. I will like to see minority
interest reflect more on the configuration of political parties. I
will like somebody to form a party of those who like eating chicken
on Sunday! It is part of our human life.
Q: You were once quoted as saying you would play a 'significant role'
in 2003. If APP wants you to play the 'significant' role of being its
presidential flagbearer, would you accept?
A: It is easy to answer it by just cliches. But I believe that it is
an important one. I remember watching the Cable News Network (CNN)
and listening to Ehud Barak, discountenancing the possibility of
cooperation and collaboration with Ariel Sharon. Today, we wake up
with a 'Hurray' that he is not only going to collaborate with Sharon
but that his group would hold defence and foreign affairs. So, don't
let us be childish. The facts are these: I am 67. That, to me, means
retirement, with more need to train younger ones than to maintain the
leadership position. Secondly, I feel that my name, having come up as
the Head of State of Biafra, I have to be very careful and be
absolutely certain that whatever post I go into should not look like
opportunism. One other thing that worries me is that I have a sense
of duty. Honestly, I have given my life fighting the cause of Ndigbo
here. Only because I feel they are oppressed. If they insist and wish
me to go, but particularly if the people of Nigeria want me to, God
knows, if the circumstances are right, one might consider. But I
haven't seen the possibility yet. But my preference, today is to help
bring up a breed of Nigerians who believe that politics is a service,
not recompense. A people who seek their success in the hearts of
their people not in the size of their own pocket. I will like to see
a new group which knows when to come in and knows when to get out. I
will like to see a people living in honesty with one another. That is
my choice.
Q: What is your view about the purported military pact between the
United States and Nigeria?
A: Well I don't know, because I have not seen the details. I don't
know if it is true. And in any case, in the context of how it applies
is what one worries about. There is no doubt in my mind that our
military, as it is today, is not certainly what we will wish for. The
propensity for coup making is too much. Everybody in the barracks
wakes up thinking 'how do I?' And the most famous sentence in the
Army today is 'fellow countrymen and women'. I think, therefore,
something drastic has to be done. The same way I have talked about
the police, I have to talk about the Army. They should be retrained.
If it is a training pact, there is nothing wrong in that. But if the
idea is to put an umbrella over exploiters of the people, oppressors
of the people, give them longer tenure of office, I would oppose it.
It would be very, very wrong. Nigerians should be given the
opportunity to choose their own leadership.
Q: There are agitations that President Obasanjo should go for a
second term. What is your view?
A: President Olusegun Obasanjo, in my mind, is a human being. He has
every right to want it. There is nothing wrong with it, he has every
right. We should learn not to oppose people like this. His work will
tell, if at the end of the first term, he is successful, why
shouldn't he? Constitutionally he can. We have refused to to put
rotation into our constitution and, therefore, have not designed a
country where a rotating presidency will be existing. Now, all the
arguments against running is based on rotation. This country has not
been designed for that. If you want to constitute a national
conference, I agree that it should be rotatory. Then you can then use
those arguments. Let Obasanjo do, as a citizen of Nigeria, what the
constitution permits him to do. Let him not hinder anybody else so
permitted to do the same thing.
Q: Chief Ojo Maduekwe, Minister of Transport, reportedly called those
calling for Igbo presidency 'idiots'. What is your reaction to that?
A: I called for Igbo presidency in 2003. If I am an idiot in his
view, he has every right to say so. I have nothing against him if he
said so. Am I an idiot? It sounds odd only because I haven't told you
what I think of him. Do you understand? We shouldn't bother about the
use of words. I don't really believe that he believes we are idiots
anyway. It was a manner of speech, a bit loose, sometimes excessive.
We have our own quirks too. Ojo that I know is not somebody who can
be written off easily. You might not agree with everything he does (I
don't agree with everything he does). But whenever we think of the
Igbos as a nation, I will want him around and will certainly want him
on my side. I cannot just judge him on one statement. If he said so,
I wouldn't say so. If he said so, he has every right.
Q: If the marginalisation of the Igbos continues, will there be a
recurrence of Biafra? In other words, would you carry arms?
A: If we woke up and there has been a landslide that wipes out the
whole of the north and a flood that wipes out the whole of the west,
will the Igbos remain? If, if, is what everybody is saying. It is
really so hypothetical that it is difficult to give a good answer.
But I put it slightly different for you. If your intention is to
continue marginalising the Ndigbo, you would be foolish to expect
Ndigbo to stay. That's all.

Original_Search_Title: Hop On The Jazz Train
Headline: South Africa: Hop On The Jazz Train
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — It's a weekend of celebration as local jazz musicians bring out four
new discs. In the past week, it has been difficult to talk to anyone
in the music industry without mentioning the name Moses Taiwa
Molelekwa. His tragic death has cast a haunting shadow on the scene.
This weekend four artists and two groups feature in two concerts that
will no doubt lift the gloom. The range of talent on offer is broad "
from the mesmerising, eclectic sound of Zimbabwean Oliver Mtukudzi to
the lilting timbre of Gloria Bosman. It'll be a fitting, albeit
unofficial, tribute to the late pianist.
Mtukudzi, the legendary native from our beleaguered neighbour, has
practically made South Africa his second home, or rather one of many
as his stature in world music grows with every album. He flies in
from the Perth International Arts Festival to present material from
his latest album, Bvuma (tolerance).
For this appearance he will be joined by a long-time collaborator, as
well as a more recent musical acquaintance. Steve Dyer was musical
director of the ensemble Mahube, of which Mtukudzi was head honcho,
and has been Mtukudzi's producer for five albums over the past four
years. He has found time to put together a solo album, Down South in
Africa, and will undoubtedly take pleasure in sharing the stage with
a man he has been friends with for a decade.
Around the middle of last year, Mtukudzi happened upon a recording of
Ringo Madlingozi's Into Yam from his latest offering Buyisa. The
track grabbed Mtukudzi so much he composed a Shona translation for
himself. The recording, now a firm favourite at Kaya Fm, is said to
be the first that combines Shona and a Nguni language, in this case
Xhosa. The reception has been mind-blowing.
On a recent tour of Zimbabwe by Mtukudzi and Madlingozi, crowds of
10?000 " in Harare and Bulawayo " formed mass choirs to sing along.
The people in the capital accompanied the lagging Shona section while
Madlingozi led in Xhosa.
In the Ndebele-dominated Bulawayo, Madlingozi found strong
accompaniment in Xhosa while Mtukudzi followed with his enchanting
tenor. This weekend, South Africans get their turn to savour the
magic for the first time live on stage.
In a second and equally thrilling concert the country's progressive
jazz label, Sheer Sound, unveils new offerings from an established
exponent and two new finds.
Gloria Bosman has practically had everything fall into place since
the release of her debut album, Tranquillity. She scooped the South
African Music Award for best new artist. She then crossed the great
divide to collaborate with kwaito artist S'bu in the latter's Abantu
Abazwani.
She's collaborated with serious music divas Sibongile Khumalo and
Sylvia Mdunyelwa and has been joined on stage by Bongo Maffin's
Thandswa Mazwai and the celebrated Busi Mhlongo.
Bosman's intended tour with Mhlongo " a phenomenal idea " is unlikely
to happen since it was the brainchild of musical director Molelekwa
and his late wife, Florence Mthoba.
For her new album, The Many Faces of Gloria Bosman, she was supposed
to combine covers with original material. "I decided against using
the tried and tested to support the originals because I felt the
songs could stand on their own," she says of the album, which she
produced, keeping her hand in all 10 tracks.
She describes the process of taking creative control of her work as
gratifying and alludes to being a control freak. "If it turns out to
be a mess, I want to have myself to blame," she says.
Her last album exudes remarkable longevity, with roughly six of the
nine tracks enjoying airplay. Her attempt at repeating her debut feat
begins on Saturday, in the company of the group Voices, who she knows
well, and Heavy Spirits, Sheer Sound's latest find.
Voices are the low-key version of the Sheer All Stars, only these
stars have not recorded solo projects. For their debut, Quintet
Legacy, they bring together experience spanning decades and a heavy
academic influence.
Acclaimed drummer Lulu Gontsana leads a quintet of respected session
musicians. University of Natal-educated pianist, Andile Yenana, notes
how Gontsana "holds us together. He is our link to what is happening
in the country jazz-wise."
Bass guitarist Herbie Tsoaeli's name has been in a number of Sheer's
projects, most notably with that of McCoy Mrubata. Together with
Yenana, they are the less-mentioned reasons for the success of
Mahube. On saxophone, the mean five " as Bosman calls them " have
Sydney Mnisi, a product of Pretoria Technikon who, since 1998, has
frequently performed in Holland. To round off, there is Marcus Wyatt
on trumpet and flugelhorn. Wyatt has extended his talents to arrange
for and produce Heavy Spirits, a new group that's also featured on
the bill.
Heavy Spirits hail from Atteridgeville in Pretoria and have been
together for just more than a year. They got their break at a
performance in Silverton. "We have a loose approach to our
composition and like to experiment" says tenor saxophonist Paul
Vranas. Their approach has yielded a fairly standard bass guitar and
saxophone blues set.
On their first major performance in Johannesburg, the group expects
"to put on a good show, but more importantly, to make people give the
album more than one listen".
The company on stage is likely to give them butterflies in the
stomach. But the irony of how, as they hit the big time, another
artist has just bowed out will not be lost to them.
Oliver Mtukudzi and others are performing on February 23 at Mega
Music Warehouse. For more info contact Sheer Sound on Tel: (011)
444?1818. Gloria Bosman is live in concert on February 24 at the
Civic Theatre from 7.30pm.
Tickets available at Ticketweb, Tel: 082?1400?500

Original_Search_Title: Join Fight Against Bush Burning - Christian Leaders Told
Headline: Ghana: Join Fight Against Bush Burning - Christian Leaders Told
Body_Text:
Accra — Men of God throughout the country have been admonished to preach
against the rampant menace of bush burning and its attendant havoc
instead of preparing the minds of their congregation towards greener
pastures somewhere beyond the blues.
About 95% of the Volta Region has been destroyed by bush fires in
recent months making it the first on the National Bush Fire Map. The
Public Relations Officer of the Regional Fire Service, Division
Officer Joy Agbleze reminded Christians and other church-goers of the
sinful nature of the destruction of the vegetation God has commanded
them to keep and to replenish.
At Tokokoe and Adaklu Kpodzi in the Ho District, six acres of cassava
farm and another six acres of oil palm plantation of 1,200 palm trees
were consumed by bush fire on Valentine Day while a farmer, Anas
Tsigbey of Tanyigbe lost 600 tubers of yam on the same day.
D.O. II Agbleze commended Togbe Noagbesenu of Awate in the Kpandu
District and the chiefs of Lolobi, Baiku, Ayoma and Lukpe in the
Hohoe and Jasikan districts for their strict observance of the
anti-bush fire laws.
He made a suggestion to the government to empower the fire service to
prosecute fire offenders at the law courts since the police appeared
not to attach any importance to fire cases.
Agbleze intends to translate series of poetry he has written on bush
fires into the various Ghanaian languages in order to whip up
awareness of the danger inherent in bush burning.

Original_Search_Title: Getting Out Of Hands
Headline: Nigeria: Getting Out Of Hands
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerians struggle to survive as the prices of commodities rise
beyond the reach of the average man. The spiralling cost of luxury
and basic goods have remained up, even after two months into the new
year. The price increases started gradually in November last year
when a bag of 100 kg yellow garri which hitherto sold for N1,500 went
up to N2,000. Refilling a 12.5 kg gas cylinder also jumped from N600
to 1,000. At the time, the rising cost was attributed to the approach
of the yuletide season, with buyers hoping that the situation would
return to normal. But that is not to be. Instead, there has been a
consistent increase in the prices of almost every commodity.
The prices of basic food items like eggs, bread, garri, yam and
beans, among others, have also gone up. A survey of prominent markets
and supermarkets in the Lagos metropolis shows that the price
increase cuts across almost every item. "A big crate of 30 eggs which
was N240 last year, sells for N270 now while a crate of the
medium-sized eggs which used to cost N220 is now N250," an egg
seller, Mrs. C. Dada, said. Bread, a favourite for breakfasts is
gradually disappearing from the menu of most people because of its
high cost which bakers attributed to the hike in the prices of baking
ingredients. At UAC Foods, a chief baker explained that the basic
materials such as flour, butter, sugar and yeast have all attracted
higher prices. " A 50 kg bag of Golden Penny flour which previously
sold for N1,900 is now N2,300; 50kg sugar has gone up from N2,100 to
N2,600 and a tin of 10-litre butter of N1,700 now sells for N2,000"
he said. This has resulted in about 20 per cent increase in the price
of bread. A big loaf of bread of brands such as Splendid, UAC, UTC
and Leventis that previously sold for N80 now sells for between N100
and N120.
"A 100kg of red garri which went from N1,700 mid-last year to N2,000
in December is now N2,500" explained Tony Nwocha, a big time grain
trader. This translates to about N450 for a tin of red garri and N400
for the same quantity of white garri.
Garri Ijebu of the same quantity now sells for N2,800 instead of the
former N2,000 while 100kg of drum beans which was N4,000 now attracts
additional N4,600. Oloyin beans of the same quantity has risen from
N3,400 to N3,800.
At the Mile 12 market, Ketu, Lagos, popular for its reasonable
pricing of foodstuff, Consumer Watch noted that the smallest tuber of
yam sells for N60. The same size of yam was bought for N30 last year.
A survey of major supermarkets like Masco Super Market, Ikeja; The
Source Supermarket, Surulere, Leventis and UAC stores, revealed that
one of the areas most hit is food. The managing director of "The
Source Supermarket," Mr. Chike Durujo, noted that virtually all
canned baby foods have attracted price increases. For instance; 300g
Frisocream milk cereal is N300 as against N285; 450g Frisolac infant
powder milk is N320 as against the November price of N295, while SMA
Gold jumped from N700 to N765. The same fate befell Similac and other
baby foods.
Drugs and other pharmaceuticals are also not left out. Three months
ago, a sachet of Chemiron blood tonic capsule sold for N40, now it
sells for N60. Female toiletries and cosmetics did not escape the
hike. For instance, Clear Essence body lotion sells for N800 as
against N500.
At the Balogun market, Lagos and Tejuosho market Yaba, there was a
major price increase in the cost of fabrics and fashion accessories
from the Far East and U.S. Cod and Coupion laces that sold for N5,000
two months ago, now go for about N9,000. George wrappers, mostly
imported from India, and hair accessories, most of which come from
Taiwan and China also attracted additional prices. In all the
markets, buyers sighed and cursed as they tried in vain to buy at old
prices.
Ready-made dresses imported from the United Arab Emirate are almost
out of the reach of the average working Nigerian. The dresses which
previously cost N3,000 for trouser and skirt suits; N1,000 for
blouses and about N2,000 for gowns have almost doubled in prices.
With the emergence of these wears in Nigerian markets about five
years ago, a lot of ladies who wanted 'Ready Made' dresses but could
not afford the ones imported from US and European markets heaved a
sigh of relief. However, with this increase in prices, it is like
going back to square one.
Right now, it costs an average of N2,220 to refill a 12.5kg gas
cylinder. Early last year, it went for N500 but gradually went up to
about N1,300 in December.
Newspaper houses and printing companies are also not left out as the
cost of newsprint and other printing materials have been affected. A
ton of newsprint which sold for N65,000 early last year, went up to
N140,000. Naturally, the price has translated to increases in the
cover prices of newspapers and magazines.
Diverse reasons have been adduced for the steady increase in prices.
While Garri sellers attribute it to the heavy rainfall of last year;
beans sellers said they have not harvested their produce. Yam
traders, however, said that the increase in the cost of transporting
their produce to the market is to blame.
Major importers, like the chief executive officer of Wilport Nigeria
Ltd, Mr. Sina Williams, said the price hike was as a result of
'Domino effects.' "With fuel scarcity, lack of infrastructures and
the dwindling value of the naira, how do you expect us to record any
meaningful progress in the economy." Continuing, he noted that "a
situation where you have NITEL and most other establishments relying
on NEPA to function shows that no industry or company will work to
its maximum capacity as NEPA is inefficient."
Lamenting the increase in the price of goods, an industrialist, Paul
Okeke, the CEO of Hollifield Industries, called on the Federal
Government to stop the devaluation of the naira. As he said "the
devaluation is to blame for the price increases. I spend almost
double what I used to spend on importation as the cost of foreign
exchange transactions has gone up tremendously."
He also noted that the poor performance of NEPA has caused
manufacturers to transfer the overhead of running generators to the
price of goods. "We all know how expensive diesel is," adding that
the traders who pay more on transportation will also pass the buck to
the consumer.

Original_Search_Title: Federal Government Secures $23 Million Loan For Root Crops Production
Headline: Nigeria: Federal Government Secures $23 Million Loan For Root Crops Production
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has secured a concessional loan of 23.05
million United States dollars from the International Fund for
Agricultural Development (IFAD) to finance root corps production in
the country.
Enugu State coordinator of the National Agricultural Co-ordinating
Committee (NACC), Chief Alfred Eneh, told newsmen in Enugu that those
who would benefit from the loan scheme were farmers in yam, cassava,
potatoes and coco-yam production.
He revealed that Cocoa Growers Association of Nigeria had been given
an initial release of N90 million for their members. Chief Eneh who
had just come back from Abuja after attending a meeting of joint
representatives of the Project Co-ordinating Unit (PCU) and leaders
of 47 commodity association, revealed that both Dr.
Perim Saint Arige and Dr. Jean Delion from the World Bank, had
confirmed that IFAD assisted cassava multiplication project would
also lay emphasis in processing, utilisation and marketing of crops.
He stated that a six-man committee had been inaugurated to study
farmers draft constitution recently submitted to NACC by Professor
Emeka Opara led constitution drafting committee. Eneh who also is the
National Chairman, Cashew Growers Association, said that the Farmers
Draft Constitution Review Committee led by Chief Sylvanus Ekeanyanwu,
would complete its job within the stipulated time to "create enabling
environment for the second all farmer's summit" due to hold this
month.

Original_Search_Title: Yam, NYC, Youth Groups Organize Cleansing Exercise
Headline: Gambia: Yam, NYC, Youth Groups Organize Cleansing Exercise
Body_Text:
Banjul — Last Wednesday volunteers from youth groups such as Lend A Hand
society and the Red Cross in collaboration with Yam, the National
Youth Council organised a cleaning exercise coined "set-settal" at
the Banjul Ferry terminal. The purpose of the exercise was to help
the country and part of Yam's initiative.
Mr. Gibba President of the NYC, said Yam and his council felt it was
a "fantastic idea" to work together in a cleansing exercise that
brought together many youth.
Yam consists of 21 students from Denver, Colorado in the U.S.A. Mr.
Gibba said volunteers cleaned the whole area and painted the
building.
Sam Periannan who is the leader based in The Gambia aid he was
travelling by the terminal when he saw that the whole place was
dirty, and felt that it needed some cleaning. He talked it over with
Raymond Gibba and Pastor Forbes and others who endorsed the idea ad
received support from various administrators. He said Yam is the
mother of five Mercyships and that they have 160 places and employ
14,000 people full time. They have five mercyships. He said they are
a Christian organisation made up of youths and adults. They travel
from country to country and clean places.
Ousman Njie the Maine Operations Managers said that the cleansing
exercise was a welcome idea and has been done at the right time. He
further went on to say that volunteers appreciate the job they have
done.

Original_Search_Title: Doctor Raises Alarm Over Food Poison
Headline: Nigeria: Doctor Raises Alarm Over Food Poison
Body_Text:
Lagos — A medical doctor and the pioneer/executive chairman of Market of
Convenience, Dr. Bayo Kumolu-Johnson has raised an alarm over the existence
of adulterated and poisonous foods in Nigerian market.  Speaking during a
courtesy call on P.M. News recently, Dr.  Kumolu-Johnson said about 21
varieties of essential foods are in circulation and are prone to being
poisoned.
Some of the food listed are rice (imported only), beans (variety from
Maiduguri called Olo), sugar (granulated, cube and variety in sachet), milk
(liquid and powder), egg (large, medium and small sizes), palm oil, soup
condiment, salt, vegetable oil, groundnut oil, gari, fresh fish, stock fish,
frozen chicken, frozen turkey, yam tubers, potatoes, semovita (small packs,
plastic containers, poly bags) and fruits in the season.
Dr. Kumolu - Johnson said the foods are being adultrated or poisoned due
to the market environment and the greed of most manufacturers to make money
at the expense of the consumers. He said "palm oil sellers store the product
for a longer period than necessary, worse still, pap and potash are being
added to it to make fake vegetable oil from used oil, bleach palm oil for
more money as well as sell groundnut mixed with sawdust as ground pepper
among others".
Continuing, he said most of the ailments in the country which are being
diagnosed like typhoid fever and some other general sicknesses are caused by
food poisoning. To avert this, he said his organisation has established an
alternative market called Oja Irorun (Market of Convenience) in the
neighbourhood which sells to people in schools, offices, banks, churches. He
revealed that plans are in the pipeline to establish markets in all the
local governments in Lagos State first before moving to other states.

Original_Search_Title: I've Never Been Bothered About My Health - Obiageli Molobe
Headline: Nigeria: I've Never Been Bothered About My Health - Obiageli Molobe
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Pioneer of the Nigerian home video industry, Obiageli Molobe has
never ceased to show her talents and mastery in the artical arts. Now
a grandmother, she is not in the habit of disclosing her age, but she
admits she's has "many" children and has been around for "a while".
Besides featuring in countless movie productions, Obiageli spends
time doing radio drama on Radio Nigeria. She also junkets several
other studios doing voice overs for radio commercial jingles all in
the struggle to keep body and soul together. She however admits that
all too often, the spirit may be willing but the body too weak to
comply. So how does she keep it all together healthwise? Hear her...
"I wake up in the morning at about 7:00 am on a typical day that I
don't have to move out very early. Even then, I don't usually get to
bed later than midnight.
If NEPA allows it, I'm inclined to watch the late night movies
otherwise I read or switch on my battery-operated radio for some
music. Just before retiring to bed, I check my schedule for the next
day. My schedule usually determines whether I'd have to wait a little
bit more before hopping into bed. But I must be in bed by 12:15 am at
the latest.
I have a body system that makes sleep almost impossible whenever I
stay up till about 1:00 am or thereabout. I always leave home at
about 9:00 am. However, I'm in the habit of doing something before
leaving home.
After listening to the morning news, I do my daily chores. I'm a
grandmother now and do not have to cope with suckling babies anymore.
As a result, my morning chore is quite different.
Soon after, I step into the bathroom for a cold shower water which I
have breakfast which must be balanced like tea, bread and eggs.
Sometimes it is ogi (pap) and akara.
I hate taking heavy meals in the morning because they make me feel
heavy and unable to do my theartherical and other duties comfortably.
Lunch comes in the form of rice or eba. This is my real meal. I eat
heavily at this period mostly because it is the time for real work
and one needs to be "solid". For supper, depending on how I feel. I
may skip it altogether. On the other hand, I could make do with bread
and tea, a cup of beverage or boiled yam. When I'm hungry, I eat as
the weight of my hunger determines. When my body begins to tell me
its time for rest, I do not hesitate to rest. As soon as I feel any
form of fatigue, I rest and try to avoid undue stress.
I've never really been bothered about my health system, really
because I do not have that kind of consciousness.
My kind of work is stressful enough. I'm on the move most of the time
shunting here and there. Sometimes it could be all night in case of
the over night productions. Like I said, once my body begins to show
signs of fatigue, I relax.
As a rule, Fridays and Sundays are for relaxation. I try to remain
indoors throughout during these periods. It is at such times that I'm
able to read all the novels within my reach. My favourite authors
include Danielle Steel, Barbara Tyler and Bradford. They thrill me
any day. I love mystery novels. Sydney Sheldon's Bloodline
particularly thrills me. Novels that have been made into movies
generally give me great joy. I love I also watch any home movie that
may be around at that time. I could take a trip to the beach to rest
with anyone of my children who wants to accompany me.
What you would find me doing on a typical rest day is window-
shopping. just go down to Cash & Carry and while away the time
comparing the prices of various items on display. Believe it or not,
this helps to relax my tensed nerves."

Original_Search_Title: A Panel's Thorny Path To Peace
Headline: Nigeria: A Panel's Thorny Path To Peace
Body_Text:
Lagos — Apparently worried and in a bid to find a lasting solution to several
re- occurrences of bloody ethnic clashes which have resulted into
wanton destruction of lives and property, the Lagos State government
on December 1, 2000 inaugurated the tribunal of inquiry into the
civil disturbances in the state between December 1999 and October
2000.
The tribunal is headed by a retired High Court judge, Justice Victor
Famakinwa with other members drawn from various professions.
There is no doubt that the ugly incidents in the state termed, "Lagos
Mayhem" or "The dark days" are still fresh in the minds of many
Lagosians who were caught unawares by the bloody violence in October
last year allegedly unleashed on the state by members of the Oodua
Peoples Congress (OPC) and the Arewa Forum representing Northern
interest.
For many Lagosians, the prayer was 'never again' to such incidents
which had led to a colossal loss of lives and property worth millions
of Naira. Utter confusion, and a feeling of insecurity amidst
wailings were the order of the day during the mayhem.
Although Lagos State is the smallest state in the federation, yet it
has the highest population due to its cosmopolitan nature. The state
which was formerly the country's capital is still regarded as the
commercial nerve centre of Nigeria. Its population is over five
percent of the national estimate comprising various ethnic groups.
According to the 1991 national census, the state has a population of
5,725,116 out of a national estimate of 88,992,220. However, based on
a United Nations (UN) study, the state is estimated to have about 8.5
percent of the total state population with a land mass of 37 percent.
In a recent UN study however, the city is expected to hit the 24
million population mark and thus be among the ten most populous
cities in the world by the year 2015.
No doubt, the population explosion coupled with the high rate of
unemployment of the youths in the state, has in no small measure
contributed to the hydra-headed re- occurrences of ethnic clashes.
In his speech at the inauguration, Lagos State governor, Senator Bola
Tinubu had stated thus: "I am sure that the chairman and members of
the tribunal will apply themselves diligently to this arduous and
challenging task which will assist the state in putting an end to the
gruesome and senseless killings of innocent citizens under the guise
of ethnicity and bring succour to the common man. I am certain that
you will bring to bear your well known reputation for integrity and
diligence."
Though the tribunal was set up mainly as a fact- finding mission into
the crises, the following are also its terms of reference:
* To inquire into the remote and immediate causes of the civil
disturbances which occurred.
* To ascertain the circumstances surrounding the incidents.
* To determine the number and identities of casualties in the course
of the disturbances.
* To identify persons or groups responsible for the civil
disturbances.
* To determine the extent of loss of lives and damage to properties
occasioned by the disturbances.
* To inquire into the other matters incidental to the above which may
assist the state government in coming to a fair and just conclusion
on the subject matter of the inquiry.
* To make recommendations on this and any other matters incidental to
the above which in the opinion of the Tribunal would assist
government in preventing future occurrence.
According to Justice Famakinwa, "a total of 64 witnesses have been
heard, while over 300 memoranda / petitions have also been received
with more entries pouring in daily." In pursuance of its objectives
and in an attempt to have a stronger view and under-standing of the
situation at the affected areas, the Panel, therefore, had on January
23, 2001 embarked on a tour to make an on the spot assessment of the
different local government areas which were mostly affected by the
mayhem.
Among the local government areas visited are Kosofe, Ifelodun, Ojo,
Agege, Ifako- Ijaiye and Mushin.
The visits, however, have been evoking mixed feelings among Lagosians
particularly from the Hausa communities in Lagos who have at
different occasions protested the rationale of the Tribunal visit,
accusing it of discrimination as most of their (Hausa) affected areas
were allegedly intentionally over looked during the visit. In fact, a
brawl almost resulted at Ajegunle when some Hausa leaders had minor
misunderstanding among themselves on who should be the spokesman.
However, Justice Famakinwa rose in stout defence of his action.
Debunking the allegation, he said, "it is not true that we
intentionally left out some of their affected areas as claimed. As
human beings, there is a limit to the areas we can cover per day. We
cannot be everywhere in a day. It is not possible. But I am assuring
them that all areas shall be visited. All they have to do is to send
in their memoranda and we shall act appropriately."
Justice Famakinwa added that "I don't want our people to see this
Panel as a means to victimise them or to apportion blames, but rather
as a fact- finding body seeking first hand information in order to
arrive at a logical conclusion in its recommendation to the state
government."
Throughout the visits to all the council areas, two questions kept
re-occurring . These bordered on the issue of the Oodua Peoples
Congress (OPC) and the clashes between the three major ethnic groups
in the country. Counter-accusations were the order of the day as each
group tried to absolve itself of blame on who started the mayhem.
Different versions of how and what started it were freely traded.
The burnt Akanimodo and Ajelogo markets at the popular Mile 12 in
Kosofe area were the first point of call. It was reportedly burnt
down by some miscreants believed to be members of the OPC employed by
two warring parties belonging to the Sukura Yam Sellers association.
Power tussle within this association was said to have led to the
bloody clash last October. According to one of the many eye
witnesses, Sukura Market is predominantly composed of Hausas who call
the shots there. One of the stories of how it started has it that the
Yorubas in the area apparently irked by the dominance of the Hausas
decided to call their bluff by attempting to take control of the
market. This subsequently led to a bloody clash between the two
ethnic groups with the OPC featuring prominently.
Another version said the power tussle among the incumbent executives
at the Sukura market was responsible for the problem. It was said
that the executive refused to leave the post after their normal
tenure. This Vanguard Midweek Features (VMF) gathered did not go down
well with the other party which was accused of using OPC members to
threaten the executive. And the exco largely made of Hausas decided
to eject the Yorubas from the market after earlier asking them to
leave the place as their continued stay in the market constituted
insecurity to their lives and property.
Also miscreants mainly from Ajelogo and Akanimodo were said to have
been used for the attack rather than the wide spread speculation that
OPC members carried out the attack.
The council boss who expressed deep concern over the illegal
acquisition of the market place by touts and hoodlums described the
place as the "robbers den" where no human being except criminals dare
go into.
Barrister Braimoh said, all efforts by both the local and state
governments to eject them proved abortive as they kept coming back.
The Ajelogo and Akanimodo market was said to have been initially
planned to accommodate Kosefe High School, Health Centre and Police
Station by the then Shomolu Local Government before it came to be
under Kosofe Local Government.
According to one Mrs Yetunde Ogunleye, the coordinator of the market
(Mile -12) "the land belongs to the defunct Marketing Board and later
bought over by Kosofe Local Government before being hijacked by these
hoodlums.
The land span then was over 77.5 hectares of land".
The panel later moved to Ajegunle in Ajeromi Ifelodun area from where
the crisis was said to have spread to other areas in the state. It
was led by the council boss Prince Rabiu Adio Oluwa, who took the
panel members first to Achakpo and Alayabiagba where the crisis
recorded its highest casualties.
The case was however not different from that of Mile-12. The clashes
recorded here were also purportedly executed by miscreants who seized
the opportunity to unleash their criminal intent.
The council boss, described Achakpo area as a predominant Hausa
settlement, bedevilled with all kinds of problematic and provocative
lay abouts. He described Ajegunle as a model of Nigeria where you can
find all shades of tribes that make up Nigeria. "We have the Yorubas,
Hausas, Igbos, Efik, Deltalites, Ijaw, Fulani and so on," he said.
Prince Oluwa partly blamed the Police for their slow response to the
crisis and therefore called for a more efficient Police Force, with
adequate men on ground to combat crime.
Other areas also visited were the Lagos State Abattoir and Lairage,
Ayokunnu Bakery and Kampala Way, all in Agege area.
Others included Ajangbadi, Alaba International and PPL Street all in
Ojo area.
The last places visited were Alasatu, Oluwatosin Street and the
notorious Alaka Street, all in Mushin area.
Testifying at one of the sittings of the tribunal, the Commissioner
of Police, Lagos State, Mr. Mike Okiro described the emergence of
militia groups as a child of circumstances brought about by
repressive military regimes and also by the ailing economy of the
country. He said many of these hoodlums are able-bodied men who find
themselves in crimes due to unemployment and lack of proper
upbringing on the part of parents and guardians. He added that "lack
of confidence in the police can be anchored on our degenerating legal
system; it is not because we are inefficient."
Remarkably, the tribunal chairman, local government chairmen and
other petitioners seem to hold similar opinions as to how to check
further crisis apart from identifying similar traits as the causes of
the crises.
According to many of the complaints from petitioners, community
leaders, local chiefs and opinion leaders are always in the centre of
the crises. They are said to be guilty of making inciting statements
that often lead to bloody clashes and also fuel the crises beyond
imagination.
One of such allegations was made against the Seriki Hausawa of
Ajegunle, one Alhaji Watago. He was alleged to have urged his kinsmen
to take up arms against the Yorubas in the area which sparked off the
gruesome killings in October last year.
A cross-section of Lagosians also had opportunity to express their
views on the crises and what should be done to stem the ugly trend.
Apart from calling for a restructuring of the Police, they also said
that the panel would only remain relevant if it focuses on its terms
of reference and not mortgage its integrity. They said the panel's
terms of reference should also include justice and adequate
compensation for victims of the mayhem.
It was also suggested that government must also ensure that the
panel's findings and recommendations must not be swept under the
carpet, as was the case with this similar investigation panels set-up
in the past. Government was also advised not to tamper with the
panel's report before it is made public. If government retrains from
doing this they argued, the public will be encouraged to accept the
panel's findings and recommendations.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Records Increase In Non-traditional Exports
Headline: Ghana Records Increase In Non-traditional Exports
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's exports of non-traditional
products garnered 214.47 million dollars in the first half of
last year, up 19.41 percent from the 179.6 million dollars earned
during the same period in 1999, the Ghana Export Promotion
Council (GEPC) said on Monday.
The performance analysis of the GEPC said the sub-sectors of
agriculture, manufacturing and handicraft dominated the export
drive.
Major export destinations are the United Kingdom, the
Netherlands, Italy, France, Germany, Spain, Belgium, Greece and
the United States. Ghana's traditional exports are gold, cocoa
and timber.
Figures for the second half are still being compiled.
"The volume of exports was made possible by 2,275 exporters
of 253 items compared to 1,740 exporters of 240 items within the
same period of 1999," it said.
Of the total earnings, the manufacturing sub-sector
contributed 167.495 million dollars (136.456 in 1999),
agriculture 43.464 million dollars (40.095 million in 1999) and
handicrafts 3.5 million dollars (3.06 in 1999).
Wood and wood products led the manufacturing sub-sector,
earning 60.96 million dollars.
Prepared foods and beverages brought in 36.8 million
dollars, followed by cocoa cake, liquor and butter with 29.4
million dollars, plywood 17.672 million dollars, sliced veneer
15.324 million dollars and builders woodwork 11.84 million
dollars.
The rest are aluminium products (5.486 million dollars),
palm oil (4.83 million dollars) while furniture and furniture
parts fetched 4.2 million dollars, and common salt 1.875 million
dollars.
Horticultural products brought in 14.369 million dollars,
from fresh pineapples, banana, papaw and fresh yams. Fish and
seafood earned 9.865 million dollars, sheanut 2.62 million
dollars, robusta coffee 2.742 million dollars and cocoa waste 1.1
million dollars.
The handicraft sub-sector contributed 3.516 million dollars
during the period made up of assorted handicrafts and basket-
ware, the report said.

Original_Search_Title: Action Now!
Headline: Ghana: Action Now!
Body_Text:
Accra — The need for some form of forum for truth and reconciliation keeps
beckoning. The pains of the past keep crying for healing.
The government of President Kufuor is being looked upon to midwife a
new era of healing which will hopefully help the nation put behind it
the pains of June 4 and December 31. Many people were willfully
damaged by the system, some with flimsy excuses that they had
children with other people's spouses.
One of the early victims of the Rawlings coup of December 31, 1981
was a mild mannered civil servant called Mr. Bentum-Eshun. His crime
was that he had fathered children out of wedlock and allegedly not
taking care of his wife.
He was vilified and hounded by the powers that be, with the
connivance of the editor of the Ghanaian Times, who wrote scathing
editorials against the man and commissioned articles from a
mysterious "Correspondent of the 31st Movement" to do the man "pasa
pasa."
The Ghanaian Times and the Daily Graphic were used to clobber any
individual or group that were perceived to be either personal enemies
of the powers that be, or enemies of the "revolution". The editor of
Ghanaian Times, who today sits on the National Media Commission,
thanks to Rawlings, surpassed himself and was considered by many
people as a terrorist of sorts. As a result, the circulation of the
Ghanaian Times under his editorship fell to its lowest point. Both
state-owned papers became vehicles for planted letters from the
Castle to destroy the integrity of people who had neither the chance
of answering back, nor seeking redress in the courts.
It is needless to add that after the terror of the 31st DWM and
Ghanaian Times, Bentum-Eshun eventually lost his job and suffered
terrible humiliation and penury until his death in 1999. He has gone
to his grave with no chance of defending himself. His final home call
was with the humiliation he suffered from the Rawlings "revolution".
It did not end there, the lady "guilty" of having a child with him,
was herself hounded and vilified by the 31st December Women's
hierarchy. She also lost her job and with it went her confidence to
face life. A promising civil servant and sports administrator
herself, she fell into tough times and has since been reduced to
selling fried yams to take care of herself and her child. The
following is her own story of how state power can be misused to flout
the human rights of others. Why was this poor woman treated the way
she was by the 31st December Women's Movement? An honest answer to
this simple question can begin the healing process of this poor woman
and her lonely child.
I fell in love with one Mr. Kofi Bentum-Eshun, a Principal Secretary
at Ministry of Trade and had a child with him.
There was a publication in the Ghanaian Times of 11th June 1984 about
Mr. Bentum-Eshun titled, 'The fall of a respectable man' and an
editorial, 'Pillars of society'. With that publication, Mr. Bentum-
Eshun was retired from the Civil Service and then they [the
leadership of 31st DWM] started on me.
I wrote to Christian Aggrey [Editor of Ghanaian Times] about the
newspaper article, wanting to know who the 31st December
Correspondent was so that I could seek redress. Mr. Aggrey told me
that the orders were that he shouldn't counteract anything about the
said publication. I felt I had been cheated by them using the
state-owned media to victimise Mr. Bentum-Eshun and me.
In 1988 I went on an ILO course in Harare and when I came back I was
arrested and sent to BNI (Bureau of National Investigations). At the
BNI, they made me sit down from 8 in the morning to 8 in the evening.
Anytime I got up to enquire why I was being kept there - because I
was nursing a baby at the time - they asked me to sit down. Later on,
one Mr. Annor Kumi and one other interviewed me and they said I was
employing people to become dissidents. I disagreed saying I didn't
know anything about what they were saying. After the interrogation, I
was allowed to go.
Later in the year, around November, I went to officiate a match at
Abuja. When I came back, I was again arrested.
They said I had collected some monies from people telling them I was
taking them to Israel.
That was a frame-up and I told them it was not true. I was sent to
James Fort for two months and then from there to the one of the
public tribunals which operated as courts.
Another Mr. Aggrey was the Tribunal Chairman and my lawyer was Lawyer
Arthur. Unfortunately for me, Lawyer Arthur had the Bitar Timber
case, so he transferred the case to Mr. Kofi Bosumpem. The case went
on for four years.
After that, I was at the Accra Sports Stadium officiating a match
when I was arrested again. They didn't even allow me to see my
lawyer. They said for the past four years they had been looking for
me and couldn't find me. That wasn't true because I hadn't travelled
outside Accra - even to my hometown within that period. It was a
Saturday; they took me to Korle Bu Police Station and on Monday
morning, I was taken to Nsawam for 18 months with no paper on me. The
Police told them they were just asked to bring me there, so later
they would bring the paper. The paper never came and I was there for
18 months only to be told that somebody had gone to pay ¢3 million on
my behalf before I was released.
When I came out I tried to get in touch with people I had written
petitions to. There was a man called Akrasi Sarpong at the Castle and
I wrote a petition to him. He was supposed to take the case to P.V.
Obeng, but later I was told that the case wouldn't get anywhere
because it involved 31st December Women's Movement. All these years I
have been writing petitions and getting nowhere. The last petition I
wrote was to CHRAJ a copy of which I gave Mr. Bebaako Mensah who was
became Secretary to the Cabinet of the former government. I was
assured that they would go through the matter; but nothing happened
and it has been lying there ever since.
To take the matter up myself, I tried to get copies of the tribunal
proceedings and was directed to a court at the seaside. They told me
they couldn't find any documents because the tribunal had stopped
functioning. I was again directed to Ho where the clerk in charge of
the case, one Ofori, was, but I couldn't get anything from him
either. Unfortunately for me, my lawyer, Mr. Kofi Bosumpem, also
died. I tried to get in touch with the Chairman of that tribunal, but
I couldn't find him.
At the moment I don't have any job and it has become like a stigma
because I went to Nsawam [jail]. But there is no paper, nothing on
me. I feel I have been cheated and I have been treated poorly. With
my background and education I can't get a job because of that and I
am seeking redress. Because of the stigma, the victimisation, I am
even afraid to come out because anytime I wrote applications for
jobs, someone would go and say I had been sent to Nsawam before.
I rose through the ranks to become a Labour Officer and I am as good
as any other woman who holds public office. Even though I am
qualified to even be a Minister with my educational background, I now
fry yams for a living. The daughter I had with Mr. Bentum is now in
secondary form two and since Mr. Bentum is now dead, I'm finding it
very difficult to pay her school fees.
I have been victimised and need assistance. I am taking it upon
myself to bring this out in the papers so that the case can be
recalled. I think this was somebody's private affair. It wasn't the
first time a man had taken two wives, nor will it be the last time.
Now that there is a chance for me, I have to take my matter up again
to seek redress. CHRAJ couldn't help me much; I am therefore
appealing to the Minister for Women's Affairs and the Attorney
General to help me get a lawyer to take my matter up.

Original_Search_Title: Why Ghai Should Visit My Republic
Headline: Kenya: Why Ghai Should Visit My Republic
Body_Text:
Nairobi — If there is a Prof Yash Ghai out there without a job, I have one for
him.
I would like him to reconcile the various factions in the Whis
Republic, which I also call home.
The prime minister of the republic, her majesty Thatcher, leads one
faction. The girl who calls herself Pajey leads the second. Her real
name is the Investment alias the Pajero. She also goes by other names
including pre-woman, girl-child and Ka-Beijing.
At one time, she could not decide whether she was a young man or a
young woman but now she has decided that she is a pre-woman. That is,
she is neither a girl nor a woman, but is the source of many
troubles.
The third faction is led by the character called Whispers Junior
alias Ras Whispero, alias Man Whispero. He is the same character who
recently converted to Mungiki. That is why he is causing all the
trouble that is cooking in my house. I say trouble is cooking in my
house because it all ends up in the kitchen and not because Thatcher
is at times given to talking to pots and pans rather loudly when she
should be talking to me.
Trouble ends up in the kitchen because each faction wants to rule in
that place. Let's start with the Investment. She has this idea that
when women met in Beijing some years ago to decide that they, too,
had a right to roast and eat meat, they met to talk about her. So she
has this thing that she calls the "Beijing Declaration" on the
girl-child and pre-women, meaning girls of her age.
I don't know whether the declaration says anything about what fathers
should be called. All I know is that she no longer calls me dad. She
calls me Mum's slave-driver or the resident Kabila. By now, I hope
you know that Kabila was very famous for dispatching a few fellows to
the past whenever he had something bad for breakfast.
I won't commit suicide for being called all those things. What will
most likely lead me to an early death is that I am supposed to obey
the Beijing Declaration on the girl-child with my wallet. As a
result, my wallet is weeping every day. But there are other reasons
behind my weeping.
Take, for example, her claim that the Beijing Declaration gives the
pre- woman, meaning her kind, the right to express themselves. She
thinks that the right includes bursting her vocal chords and lungs in
song when I am trying to reconcile the various factions in my head
created by what I had been drinking the previous night.
I perhaps might not mind her singing but I get mad when she disturbs
my peace by barking like a dog in the name of singing. Here I am
trying to make the warring factions in my head understand that I need
peace when she suddenly starts singing something like, "Who let the
dogs out?" Then she goes, "Wo! Wo! Wo!" like a mad dog.
I hear that the barking is supposed to be a song by somebody who has
been rewarded for doing it. I can assure that daughter of mine that I
will reward her for her barking with a total ban from living in the
house for which I pay rent.
Let us now go to what she cites as another clause in that
declaration. She alleges that the clause says a woman should have
total control over her body. The Investment says this right means
that a pre-woman should have a right to ask from her father for
things that will make her body "look right". Looking right to her
means looking like that insect called a praying mantis.
If you have never seen the insect, it has a very thin waist. In fact
it looks as if its waist is being strangled permanently. It has a
rather long neck and indeed its entire body suggests that it feeds on
air. The investment wants to look like that insect. In other words,
she wants to be in what is called "shape".
She wants to look like one of those pre-women called Spice Girls.
Unlike the praying mantis, she does not want to feed on air. She
wants to feed on things that have never been heard of in my house Ð
or in my clan for that matter Ð and expects me to pay for them.
In an effort to turn herself into that relative of a mosquito, the
Investment is demanding that I buy items that have never appeared in
the dictionary of the Nyaituga clan to which she belongs. She is
demanding that I buy her things called "Portobello mushrooms". The
only mushrooms I knew of were those that looked like umbrellas. They
used to grow up on the Slopes where I was looking after goats as I
grew up. Something happened and they vanished. Even then, those
plants were not for eating. They were for kicking about like a
football.
They were soft on our toes that had been chewed up by jiggers and we
really enjoyed kicking them around. If you ask me, those "portobello"
mushrooms might as well be things that are given to European goats to
expel worms from their tummies. Why the Investment is demanding them
is beyond my thinking. She says they are supposed to provide the kind
of calories that will make her waist as slim as that of a mosquito.
She also demands an item called "Lemon meringue cake". She insists
the "meningitis" cake is the right stuff for a pre-woman who has
control over her body. That lemon "meningitis" cake is supposed to
strengthen her hair. If she does not eat it, her hair will suffer
stress. When that happens, she says, she too will suffer from stress
and might have to see a psychiatrist.
The Investment, alias Pajey, is not taking any chances with her
efforts to ensure that her waist resembles that of a praying mantis.
That is why she has forced me to buy what should be found only in a
butchery. She has forced me to buy a weighing scale so that she can
watch her weight.
The first thing she does in the morning is to step on that weighing
machine and look at its needle very suspiciously. She wants to ensure
that she has not gained any weight in the night. It is as if sleeping
can make her gain weight. Come 10 am, she is on that weighing machine
again.
She spends five minutes looking at its needle to make sure that the
strungi (strong tea) she took with lemon in the morning has not made
her gain weight. At 4 pm, she climbs onto it again, this time to
ensure that what she had for lunch has not changed her weight. All
she took for lunch is half a carrot and a suspicious looking leaf
whose name I cannot memorise.
All I know is that the leaf costs real money at a green grocer where
those who visit don't use coughing cars like mine. They go there in
cars that say that it is bad manners for a car to sneeze.
Let us leave that girl and her Beijing Declaration and turn to her
brother Whispers Junior alias Ras Whispero and recently converted to
Mungiki. Ever since his conversion, the fellow talks very little.
Instead his nose is very busy receiving snuff according to the rules
of his sect.
He keeps loading that stuff into his nose as if his brain, like a car
engine, receives fuel. Apart from banning himself from talking too
much, the fellow has also outlawed himself from using underwear. The
fellow says that his sect considers that item to be designed for
women and he is not one. Instead, he is now wearing very strange
items.
He cuts off his old trousers at the knees and uses them as inner
wear. He calls them "boxer shorts" and claims that is what real men
should wear. I don't mind a young man consuming the stuff that his
ancestors used to partake of, but the things that he is demanding for
his mouth are worrying me.
The fellow has declared that his sect does not allow what he calls
European food. He claims his sect demands that he only eat the food
of his ancestors. That is why he demands boiled arrow roots, sweet
potatoes, roast yams and bananas as well as boiled green vegetables
which can only be found in the valleys of Mount Kenya.
The last time I tried to make the character understand that he cannot
