force us to eat the food of his ancestors, he clogged his nostrils
with tobacco and, after sneezing a number of times like an angry
bull, looked towards Mount Kenya. He said a prolonged prayer to the
God of Kirinyaga, the "Owner of the Ostrich". He asked the Owner of
the Ostrich to give his hands the strength of Dedan Kimathi, he who
put terror in the hearts of the white men and made them flee from
Kenya.
He prayed that he needed that strength so that he could fight the
enemies of the Owner of the Ostrich now resident in the same house
with him. He called the house Babylon and asked the Owner of the
Ostrich to help him get out of slavery where he is being forced to
eat the food of slaves instead of that of his ancestors. I did not
need to look very far to know whom he referred to as "the emperor of
Babylon".
The character was talking so negatively about me that I decided to
give him enough money to go and buy the food of his ancestors. Just
at that moment, Thatcher stretched her hand. She wanted money for her
own food. She, too, had her special menu. It happened that a few
weeks ago, her sect of Many Waters got a new bishop. The bishop came
with new rules. One of the rules is that no food that has been cooked
with fat shall pass the mouth of any of the sect of Many Waters
member unless she or he wants to burn in hell.
Thatcher does not want to suffer in hell where she says sinners are
fed with hot tar in the morning for breakfast. So she has been eating
boiled cabbages, boiled beans, boiled rice, boiled potatoes and such
stuff. From the look of things, she will soon ask for boiled air.
What all this means is that there are three cooking pots in the
house. There is one for the relative of the praying mantis, another
to make the food of the ancestors and the third to make what is
called holy food or the dish for the mouths of those who wish to go
to heaven.
It means is that when I get home in the evening, I find outstretched
hands asking for money to buy items for crazy menus. When I ask where
my food is, I get looks that suggest that I should cook for myself.
If Prof Ghai you're out there reading this third-rate column today, I
need your help please. Tell me how I can cook a solution to this
kitchen problem on wis@mitsuminet.com

Original_Search_Title: Heroes Of The 20th Century: Alhaji Audu Bako
Headline: Nigeria: Heroes Of The 20th Century: Alhaji Audu Bako
Body_Text:
Lagos — And he gave it for his opinion, that whoever can make two
ears of corn. . . grow upon a spot of ground where only one grew
before would ... do more essential service to his country, than the
whole race of politicians put together, Jonathan Swift 1667-1745.
In which part of Nigeria were the following grown most in 1966: round
pepper (rodo), tatase, yams, tomatoes, rice, cassava, okro,
vegetable? Where are they most grown in Nigeria today? Which state of
Nigeria is today most nearly self-sufficient in food production?
Virtually all the food products listed in the first question were
produced in southern states and the Middle Belt area; the core north
produced very little except millet, maize, sorghum and alligator
pepper as well as groundnuts. Surprisingly the answer to the second
and third questions is: Kano State. Kano State the state which made
Nigeria famous with its groundnut pyramids rivalling in height those
pyramids of Egyptian rulers from Cleopa (2650 - 2630 BC) the first
builder, has now become Nigeria's food basket and within one
generation has been transformed into the state which is most nearly
self-sufficient in food. Name it, cassava, yams, rice, yams, rice,
tomatoes, onions, okro, vegetables, millet, maize, sorghum, wheat,
potatoes (including sweet potatoes) cabbage, lettuce, carrots as well
as a wide variety of livestock (goats, cows, fish, poultry) are
available in this state far in excess of the quantity that the
state's nearly seven million population can produce.
If ever a state can be said to have benefitted from the wave of
global green revolution which has eluded most other parts of Nigeria,
then that state is Kano. And the father of Kano's green revolution
was Alhaji Audu Bako, the governor of Kano State from 1966 to 1975
during the Gowon administration and the only northern governor who
escaped indictment for corrupt self-enrichment when Gowon's
government was overthrown. In fact, Alhaji Audu Bako was perhaps the
only one of two persons who achieved the status of state governor in
Nigeria and still died a poor man relatively.
The other was Pa Adekunle Ajasin. And it was not because of
squandermania or on account of investments which went bad. He just
didn't have the money. He was so poor, when they were booted out of
office, he just moved into a modest rented house and later moved to a
building purchased for him through donations by friends and former
subordinates when he was governor.
In 1966, according to a close associate and a top official in
Government House Kano at the time, Audu Bako, for reasons still
unclear had decided that his administration would make one great
achievement and that is food self-sufficiency for the people of Kano
State.
Perhaps his resolve to ensure this might have been prompted by the
experience of food shortage in Kano during the civil war when
suppliers from the South, especially yams from Biafra became
irregular. Audu according to the source called a meeting and vowed
that his people would never again depend on others for food and he
set about working with the Federal Government, the World Bank and
other institutions to establish irrigation systems and to acquire
farming equipment to help farmers clear land and prepare them for
planting. He simplified land acquisition, established Kano State
Agricultural Company (KASCO) with branches in several parts of Kano
State providing seeds, fertilizers and pesticides at subsidized rates
to farmers. And he strengthened the Ministry of Agriculture by
employing people capable of providing farmers with training and other
forms of support.
By the time Audu Bako left office, the groundnut pyramids due to no
fault of his own were already disappearing from the Kano landscape as
imported vegetable oil induced by sharply increased crude oil revenue
has started to make local groundnut oil uncompetitive and groundnut
cultivation unprofitable. In fact, Kano State's agriculture might
have been totally devastated and the peasants in rural areas
depending on farming might have been in deep trouble if Audu Bako has
not diversified the scope of agricultural output making it possible
for farmers to switch from one product to another in response to
market demand.
Audu Bako had also targeted Lagos State and its huge population but
small landmass coupled with what he rightly perceived as the
Lagosians distaste for farming. If his people could grow abundant
food, they could "export" to Lagos on a large scale. At the time,
nobody ever imagined that the federal capital will ever move from
Lagos to Abuja.
Today, Audu's target market had multiplied; Nigeria has three
capitals all hungry for food from the north. Lagos is the commercial
capital; Abuja the political capital and Port Harcourt the
fossil-fuel capital of Nigeria.
If the man were alive now, he could not help feeling satisfied that
his dream had come true because other northern states soon emulated
Kano and now the south is dependent on t he north for food. In fact
if the south were to hang on to its crude oil and the north its food
in a war of mutual embrago, it is likely that the south will cave in
first despite ability to import. And all these had taken place within
a generation after Audu Bako placed food at the top of the northern
agenda. And if ever the history of the 20th century Nigeria is ever
written, then the name Audu Bako can never be excluded from the
standpoint of lasting achievement.
Prayer House needs paint
Massey Morning Daily Praying Band, the nation's oldest morning
multi-denominational prayer house 63 years old this year ;needs
repainting. Colours selected are: bluish-grey textured for exterior
and white gloss for interior. Those wishing to donate paint or assist
with money should contact me or send their gifts to 2, Massey Street,
Lagos Island.
The Lord blesses a cheerful giver.
In defence of OPC
"It ain't what people don't know that causes all the problems; it's
what they think they know that just ain't so."
I have on these pages once asked my brother Dr Faseun to disband the
OPC and to start all over again chiefly because the OPC like the
Frankenstein monster had grown out of control of its inventor. And
secondly because private militia dispensing jungle justice
constituting themselves into accuser; prosecutor, judge and
executioner eventually will constitute worse dangers to society than
the menace they set out to eradicate in the first place. Injustice,
in the end will be multiplied.
Those views remain strong even today. Paradoxically, however, I find
a lot of the attacks on OPC not only stemming from ignorance but also
often induced by meddlers tribalism. The 19 northern state governors
and a few w Igbo leaders as well as commentators give the impression
that OPC attacks and kills non-Yorubas indiscriminately.
Nothing could be further from the truth. In reality, the OPC has
eliminated more Yorubas suspected to be armed robbers than any other
tribal group Within a week of blind rage in Lagos Island last year
the OPC summarily executed and burnt nothing less than twenty armed
robbers; everyone was a Yoruba. Their war with armed gangs in the
Akala area of Mushin previously a notorious den of robbers when, the
police could not dislodge yielded over 40 dead in body count;
virtually all Yorubas. Nobody raised an eye-brow at the time.
It was when OPC moved to Ajegunle, heavily populated by non-Yorubas
that the first charges of tribalism were made; one suspects by
erudite robbers and their barons. The truth remains that the easiest
way to get anybody killed in Lagos by OPC or any mob is to shout
"Ole" (thief!) and the unfortunate victim can only escape cruel fate
by divine intervention. The uproar in Ajegunle which spread to other
parts of Lagos last year started with that dreaded word "Ole". The
selected victim just happened to have been a northerner and before
anybody knew it an inter-tribal war was underway. Had the victims
been a Yoruba, nobody would today be calling for the prosecution of
Faseun or the proscription of the OPC.
Furthermore 20 per cent of the houses burnt in the jungle city were
owned by Yorubas and other southerners. Clearly, the OPC, if it were
bent on attacking only northerners could not be rendering Yorubas
homeless.

Original_Search_Title: Robbers Den Uncovered at Kosofe
Headline: Nigeria: Robbers Den Uncovered at Kosofe
Body_Text:
Lagos — A den of robbers has been discovered at the popular Akanimodo market,
Mile 12 in Kosofe local governments area. Making this disclosure,
chairman of Kosofe local government, Barrister Tunde Braimoh said the
den is located inside the market.
He told the Justice Victor Famakinwa panel of inquiry investigating
civil disturbances in the state when the members visited the local
government area that the market covers over 77.5 hectares of land. He
said the market is supposed to be yielding millions of Naira to both
the state and local government but that it is being inhabited by
people of questionable characters.
The panel was told that some parts of the popular Mile 12 market
especially, some of the uncompleted stalls are being used as
residence by those criminals.
The panel also gathered that the occupants of the sheds neither pay
any form of levy or revenue to the local government nor the state as
they are being shielded by some market leaders.
Mr. Essien Emmanuel Umoren the District police officer of Kosofe
local government told P.M.News in an exclusive chat that the police
is having a tough time flushing out the criminals.
"You see that there are many exits. It is not easy raiding this area
during surveillance," the police boss disclosed. Barrister Buraimoh
prayed tribunal to include the area in its recommendation to the
state government as the place was inherited from the old Shomolu
local government. He said the market contained displaced traders from
Jankara market in central Lagos.
During the visit the panel visited Ajelogo market, Mile 12, Sulewa
yam market and other areas of the local government.

Original_Search_Title: Passage: Otse Agilla Goes Hunting
Headline: Nigeria: Passage: Otse Agilla Goes Hunting
Body_Text:
Lagos — The king is dead. Long live, the king. Joy and fanfare
characterise the burial of Philip Agbese Onazi, district head of
Agilla and father of Dan Agbese, Newswatch editor-in-Chief
Ordinarily, every death evokes sympathy and sorrow. But in Idomaland
of Benue State, that of a king elicits joy and happiness. When the
people of Agilla in Ado local government area of Benue State gathered
January 4, at Apa, a village in the town, it was with joy and funfare
as they bid farewell to Philip Agbese Onazi, the Otse Agilla and
their district head, who joined his ancestors December 22.
Of course, it is a popular belief in the town that a chief does not
die. He is said to have gone to the bush for treatment. "Yes," said
Lawrence Eza, a retired army sergeant and head chief of the Osiroko
clan, "a chief in our place doesn't die, we say he has gone to the
bush to take medicine. Today is declared a day that everybody knows
he has gone to the bush,"
"The King never dies. He goes to hunting until whenever he comes
back," intoned Michael Onazi, the king's first son and heir-apparent.
It was the thinking that their king was alive that the people went
into celebrative mood on the 14th day after his passage. Nobody
except the king makers saw his corpse. The visitors from far and near
had gathered at the expansive square outside the king's palace from
early in the morning. Drums started beating very early in the morning
with occasional cannon shots booming into the air. The traditional
chiefs who have been sleeping in the compound for seven days appeared
in their best flowing robes with equally flowing Idoma traditional
caps to add colour to it. It was a carnival type of occasion which
every Idoma male, female, old and young appeared in his or her
"Christmas" dresses full of anticipation for the events which were
billed to start in the afternoon.
Inside the compound, kegs of palm wine made rounds amongst the male
folks while the women and young ladies busied themselves with the
preparations of the various delicacies to be served later. "It is a
special day, it is not every time that a king travels. If an ordinary
person had died, we would have been wailing by now," said a woman who
was supervising other women preparing pounded yam.
In a small house painted all white was an object dressed in a
chieftaincy regalia and cap sitting on a royal chair. On a casual
look, passers-bye would think that the king is seated on his throne.
The villagers and visitors came to the palace to pay homage to the
king for a good life lived before he travelled. That included his
generosity, bravery, care for the entire subjects and his ability to
manage his large family up to the time of his death without crises or
squabbles. Onazi is survived by about nine wives and more than forty
children. An elderly woman in her seventies who came from Igumale, a
neighbouring town, just walked up to the house, looking at the
"sitting king" and praised him for his kindness to her in times of
trouble.
Speaking in Idoma, she gave testimonies of how the king came to her
rescue in times of financial crises, and his good judgment in the
cases he delivered. She dropped N40.00 into a tray in front of the
house to bid the king farewell. She was amongst hundreds of visitors
and natives who came to pay homage to the king and praise him for his
good lifestyle as well as bid him farewell. The farewell by the
various clan chiefs, men, women and youths alike were done with good
words accompanied by donations.
Masquerades and various dance groups were also present. They came
from the various neighbouring towns where the Agilla head was known.
Ekpe masquerade came all the way from Enahumu in Ebonyi State.
Unaloko, a king masquerade in Agilla also made an appearance. It went
straight to the little house, with its knees on the ground, bowed
down and paid its own homage to the king. It then went to the square
to entertain the already fascinated guests.
When, however, the Egwuaja, a masquerade, from Idah in Kogi State,
appeared on the stage from the surrounding bush, the crowd went wild
with jubilation amidst scampering of feet among women and children.
Patricia Oluma, a 46 year-old worker in the fisheries department of
the ministry of agriculture in Ado local government, who jumped up
when she saw the masquerade, told Newswatch it was the second time in
her life time that she was seeing the masquerade.
"It does not come out often. I saw it last when my father, who was
the Adochie of Agilla died many years back," she said. Probably
enjoying the reverence attached to it, the masquerade, with two bells
in each hand ran like a possessed man to the sitting king and
prostrated. It then headed to the square to the admiration of the
king makers who by mid-afternoon, had exhausted several gourds of
palmwine. The natives who were then animated, danced and sang praises
as each masquerade or dance group appeared.
The masquerades, which the women saw during the day was at best all
they could see. They were not allowed to see the masquerades that
performed in the night. Those ones were strictly for the men and the
"initiated." When the owu masquerade from Igumale made its entry late
the previous night, women ran for their dear lives. It is part of the
culture of the Agilla people that women should not see certain
masquerades. The women themselves have accepted it as part of them.
Said Regina Agbese, a daughter-in-law and director of the National
Youth Service Corps, NYSC, Plateau State: "We don't argue with our
men. They give us freedom to do whatever thing we want but when they
say we should not do any thing, we simply obey them."
Newswatch gathered that the joyous mood in the town was as a result
of the elaborate funeral rites which the family organised to bid
farewell to the deceased. Impressed with the funeral arrangements,
villagers were seen pumping hands with Michael, the king's first son
and showered him with praises. He later explained to Newswatch that
it was compliment for the ability of his family to organise the
burial successfully. "It is believed that the king lived in enjoyment
all through his life. So they are thanking me for sending my father
away with the whole rites," he said.
Michael also said the late Onazi Agbese was an "embodiment of every
thing to me and my brothers." Said he: "He never went to school but
he did his best to send us to school, knowing fully well that
education would pay him better than farming." Regina described the
late king as a very loving father, a fine gentleman who gave enough
time to his children. "He was a great farmer, hunter and warrior,"
she told Newswatch.
Onazi's fame in farming, hunting and family life earned him the
kingship of Agilla on December 16, 1984. Eza told Newswatch that he
(Onazi) was "a popular and brave man," who commanded the respect of
almost everybody in the town. "He is a very generous man," he added.
The late Onazi was the father of Dan Agbese, editor-in-chief of
Newswatch magazine.

Original_Search_Title: The Man From Yendi
Headline: Ghana: The Man From Yendi
Body_Text:
Accra — Alhaji Alhassan was named after his grandfather, a renowned
Islamic scholar in Yendi. In Dagomba tradition names of important
personalities in families are hallowed. They are not mentioned but
are instead replaced by expressions like Alfatogma, which means the
namesake of the teacher or Baba, which also means father.
He loves history very much so when the interview lasted he frequently
sent me back to the Gold Coast of the 1930s and sometimes things he
heard from some of his relations. I would take a break from
contemporary issues for a visit to the good old days of the Northern
Territories of the '30s which Alhaji Afatogma relishes recounting.
District Councils
Gaining employment in the then District Councils was a cherished
dream of many a young educated man in those days. Such councils were
responsible for the day to day provision of local government services
and educational matters. In those days clerks of councils were highly
revered. They were responsible for the provision of bursary
scholarships to students from that part of the country and could
determine whether an applicant actually hailed from the region and
thus qualified to enjoy the facility or otherwise.
In the early fifties one Abdulai Musa otherwise called Abdulai Cralwi
was in charge of the Yendi District Council and he was later replaced
by Abdulai Nantong. It was common in those days for people to adopt
the names of their towns of origin as their surnames, a trait shared
by the Hausa of Northern Nigeria. Nantong is the name of an important
town in Dagbon.
Mr. J.H. Allassani, one of Ghana's first crop of indigenous ministers
from Dagbon, showed interest in the affairs of the council in Yendi
where a lot of malfeasance was going on at the time.
He found in one Mr. Harruna-Atta the person to reverse the
unacceptable trend at the local council. Harruna-Atta was the father
of the Managing Editor of The Accra Mail.
His arrival in Yendi sent worrying signals to the status quo who saw
him as a man who had come to disturb their old ways of doing things.
They resisted his efforts, as he remained unwavering in his resolve
to stamp out institutional misdemeanor in the Yendi District Council.
A royal whose uncle is the Gushenaa, B.A. Yakubu, one-time IGP, the
late Harruna Atta was born and raised in Kumasi and so his Dagbani
was with an accent.
This led cynical Yendi indigenes to refer to him as the Fulani man
especially since he was tall and lanky. Adamant in his resolve he
soon streamlined the operations of the council.
He was known to have refused overt attempts at bribing him with gifts
of tubers of yam and guinea fowls to the satisfaction of J.H.
Allassani and others who were instrumental in bringing him to the
north.
So peeved were the indigenes especially beneficiaries of the
malfeasance that they used party political machinery to have him
banished to a place in Trans-Volta.
Southerners In Dagbon
There were quite a number of southerners in Yendi and indeed the
whole of Dagbon many of who were civil servants and traders. In the
case of the former they were given special allowance to entice them
to go and work in the NTs. For those who had never traveled to the
north before, there were many strange and false stories about that
part of the country especially the temperature. I can recall names
like Mr. Amoah, who was the chief clerk in the District Office who
was a Fanti from Saltpond.
There was also one Mr. Turkson who I remember lived in Yendi in about
1949. His brother later became the Commissioner of Income Tax. Mr.
Tamakloe whose descendants are still living in Tamale was also a
staff of the District Council and one of the houses of Tamale
Secondary School was named after him. The Veterinary Services
Department had personalities like Mr. Binka and Olympio.
The Police Service in Yendi at the time under review had an Inspector
Agyiri as the commander. He had under him one Sgt. Timbilla whose
prowess at checking rioting Konkombas was well known. Konkombas were
notorious land poll or 'lampoo' resisters and when they did it was
violent with the bow and arrow, their hallmark. It was Sgt. Timbilla
who was charged with the task of controlling them.
First Aircraft In Dagbon
In 1936, my uncle the late Alhaji Amadu Hussein took me to Tamale
where an announcement had gone round about the arrival of an aircraft
to the town. Chiefs and other persons from far and near converged on
the town to catch a glimpse of the aeroplane, a first time experience
for most people. I had to be lifted to see the 'yigrigu' which means
the flying object.
The Early  Drivers
One had to serve as a driver's mate for a long time before learning
how to drive. The service meant taking care of the 'master's' laundry
and other miscellaneous chores. After sometime the master introduces
the obedient mate to the rudiments of driving. Then comes the task of
facing the testing officer who could fail or pass a candidate. The
passing of the driving test in those days was special because it was
not automatic.
Most of the drivers in Dagbon in my youthful days were Gas and they
included personalities like Ataa Boi, Abiaduka, Tetteh Blankson.
Labadi produced some of the early commercial drivers of the Gold
Coast. The early Dagomba drivers were Mumuni Driver who was the
father of Alhaji Yakubu Ka1suli the Chief Executive Officer of the
Gbewaa Civil Engineers Ltd., Abudu and Bankparma among others.
Mail Delivery
It was difficult getting mail delivered to Tamale and vice versa.
Commercial drivers were therefore used for this purpose but this too
proved unreliable, as the drivers were reluctant in carrying out this
assignment. The authorities therefore devised a method of dealing
with the situation.
Drivers who came to Yendi to discharge goods and passengers were
obliged to deposit their licences with the Police. When they came to
recover these on their way out of town they were given the available
mail for delivery to Tamale. Licenses were so important in those days
that it was unimaginable for drivers to operate without them. Drivers
were obliged to hang numbered driving licenses around their necks
which were in form of metal plates.
The reliance on motorized transport for mail delivery had been
preceded by the use of bicycle for the over fifty mile trip to
Tamale. This task was ably performed by the late Mahaman Massenga a
Yendi indigene whose children are now holding important positions in
the country.
They include Mr. Amadu Mahama who was a one-time senior staff with
the Northern Electricity Department of the VRA, Dr. Ramatu Alhassan,
wife of the CPP chairman and a veterinary surgeon, Dr. Charles
Mahama.
Colonial District Copmmissioners
Most of the DCs as they were then known were military officers
working for the Colonial Service. I can recall names like
Silminkpibga and Mr. Cook. The former was so nicknamed because the
indigenes did not find in him the harshness associated with other DCs
like Captain Armstrong who was nicknamed Halilana or the
temperamental man. His wife too had a share of the nicknaming with
the tag Bugumdia, which means the fire, has gutted. The story about
Silminkpibga was narrated to me by my mother. Silminkpibga was
regarded by the indigenes as an orphan. He was fond of greeting the
people and returning their compliments. One day he sought to know the
meaning of the nickname they had given him. When one day when no less
a person than the Zohenaa, an important chief in Yendi called him by
that name he had him arrested and sent to the Police station. It took
the intercession of the then Yaa Naa Abudu to have the chief
released. The chief explained that he was not an orphan as his
parents were alive in England.
Silminkpibga was also given a name, Bugumdirkugle, which means stone
catches fire. This implied that even the DC's people could be
punished for wrongdoing. Some DC's servants because of their
closeness to the corridor of power used the unauthorized derived
authority to make illegal demands at the marketplaces and others.
Some cooks in particular were fond of asking for guineafowls and eggs
on behalf of their bosses. When this came to the notice of the DCs
they took swift action to stem the thievery.
Contemporary Issues
Asked why he campaigned for the NPP during the recent elections since
he was originally a CPP man who is now a PNC man he had this to say:
'When a lion chases you and you make it to the top of a tree where
you cannot afford to die from starvation you avail yourself of every
available opportunity to survive. In this case joining forces with
the NPP to oust the old order was just the right thing to do.'
On how he feels now that the NDC has been defeated he said, 'I can
now sleep soundly. I had relief only when I was reading the Holy
Quran at night.'
On the Save Dagbon Mission to the North he said 'it was not an easy
task because on one occasion my grand daughter whose husband is an
NDC man rebuked me for being engaged in a campaign for the NPP when
younger men could have done that. It was an uphill task attempting to
turn the scale at a place awash with people contaminated with NDC
goodies. Many of the people had been compelled to swear on oath that
they would vote for that party after receiving assortment of gifts
like torch lights, kettles and money. Eventually however they
responded to our pleas to change their position and the result was
what happened on December 28 for which we are grateful to God.'
On how he felt when the NDC boasted that it was going to win the
polls he said 'I felt that they were going to kill to achieve their
objective and I would have been one of the victims since I would not
have stopped pointing at the ills of that party.'
Alhaji Afatogma said he has met President Kufuor only twice and on
the first occasion his impression of the man was that he is a
God-fearing person. To NPP supporters he said they must be wary of
unguarded statements like 'Ya gye ye man' which does not help the
image of the party. The recent electoral victory he went is not for
one party but for all Ghanaians and he has this message for the
President: ' Beware of sycophants who are capable of derailing your
good intentions for the nation and be guided by the fear of God in
whatever you do.'
To his fellow citizens, he says, 'We should be tolerant of one
another and contribute towards the building of our battered country
irrespective of our party affiliations. For now, what the President
should concern himself with is the economy in which all of us should
assist. Issues like probes should be left for the time being.'
He has travelled widely outside Ghana and has been to countries like
Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Lebanon, Italy and Britain.

Original_Search_Title: Wrestling With Destiny
Headline: Nigeria: Wrestling With Destiny
Body_Text:
Lagos — After a celebrated year of firsts, few might think there
remains more to accomplish for Canada's first-ever world and Olympic
free style wrestling champion.
But there is one thing, something deep in the soul of Nigerian-born
Daniel Igali, that a maple leaf flag, an Olympic gold medal in
Sydney, Australia, and all the international recognition as Canada's
reigning athlete of the year just will not reconcile.
Since exiling himself from his central African roots following his
defection to Canada after attending the Commonwealth Games in
Victoria in 1994, Igali has embraced the Canadian way with ardour.
The picture of the 27-year-old Igali, a new Canadian and now Olympic
champion draped in the national flag of his adopted country is well
documented.
So too is his affection for a Burnaby woman, Maureen Matheny, who
welcomed him as a son into her family when he was new to the country.
He later dedicated his first world championship gold medal to her.
She died five days after that historic event.
The homecoming Igali returned to his homeland for a second time last
winter, after a six-year exile.
There, inside the crude mud and thatched roof huts of his village, he
saw his likeness in the wide, dark, staring eyes of the admiring
Nigerian children. He was welcomed as their hero. He was followed by
swarms of shirtless youngsters, up and down the sandy streets,
everywhere, everyday, through seas of tiny searching hands -
outstretched - all wanting closer, to feel and touch the skin of
their real-life idol.
"It was a great feeling to go and it's more what you see in the eyes
of the kids, everyone wants to be like Daniel Igali".
But while poor. shoeless children dream of breaking free of society's
tight hold and becoming like their hero, Igali dreams of returning
one day to the grasslands and the dirt fields of his youth where as a
youngster he first grappled with the sport that would ultimately
change his life.
While in Nigeria, Igali was constantly asked on local talk shows if
he could ever have achieved his status as the best in the world as an
African.
He bluntly answered, "No", to every suggestion, putting him on the
wrong side of civil authorities again. "Never, and that was an issue
with the authorities. In one sense it was not good but it was also
true".
He says his remarks raised resentment from outside his family and
sport. Some said Igali was likely Nigeria's only chance to ever
embrace a world champion, that he was a traitor to his people and
doing it just for the money. "I tried to make them understand, that I
am happy to be doing it for my adopted country Canada".
But there is still so much pride in being African that Igali can no
longer hide it under the huge maple leaf he so exuberantly draped
over his muscled torso after winning his first world gold medal in
Turkey.
It's in the blood He says returning home was how he expected it would
be - a paradox. A love unrequited and in many ways still unfulfilled.
An ongoing struggle between the political and the moral. His values
against the rule of authority.
"I kind of think they (Nigeria) don't deserve it", he said of their
recognition for his world title. "One thing in my mind says it would
be nice to have a little Nigerian flag, it would be for them, I

learned to wrestle there. If I come to the conclusion it is right,
for sure, I'll probably do it, but I won't do it to satisfy any
administration anywhere".
In the end, it was his adopted country of Canada that received his
outpouring of affection and the ultimate glory. For that, he has no
regrets whatever.
Igali says winning an Olympic medal in Sydney as a Nigerian national
would have been a trade off that an elevated standard of living in an
unsure environment could ever justify.
To give up the general level of respect and human rights that
Canadians enjoy would be too big a price to pay, he says.
But some unseen hand still tugs at Igali's singlet. A grip so tight
and so strong that even the best 69 kilogram wrestler in the world
cannot slip its gut- wrenching hold.
Igali is being called home, home to Eniwari town on the South-East
coast of Nigeria 600 kilometres from the nearest large city Port
Harcourt, where the mighty Niger River meets the Atlantic Ocean.
He desires to one day become a traditional wrestling champion of his
village.
"It's a cultural thing," he explains. "When I grew up all my heroes
were these people - champions of their villages. Unfortunately, I did
not have the chance to do it before I retire".
That chance has now come. Igali is leaving for his homeland Dec. 9,
starting a journey back to his tribal roots - with all its mystery
and tradition.
A high price to pay Both Igali's grandfathers were village champions.
His father's father took line of champions. In fact, Igali's
grandfather was so good he did not have to pay a dowry when he
married.
Igaly says village champions win only prestige, not chattels, and
many are the most wretched in the village by the time they reach old
age.
Villagers could care less if their wrestlers lose 10 matches on the
day to visitors as long as their best defeated their neighbour's
champion just once.
It's a question of community pride and everything is riding on the
success of your champion - including voodoo.
"It does happen", says Igali, "Voodoo is big time in village
wrestling. If I want to wrestle I would go to a voodoo man to protect
me".
But the price paid by village champions is a dear one adds Igali. He
describes the wrestlers as the poorest of the poor. Many are left
without children in their old age, and to a man their lives have been
shadowed by ill fortune in life and money matters every step of the
way.
He say these broken men live off the goodwill of villagers and
admirers like himself to give them money so they might eat and drink.
Igali makes a point of always visiting these men first when he
returns home.
"But those are the guys you want to pray for you when you get home
and then it's up to you to do something for them. It's very
powerful".
Talking drums Igali sees the semi-naked men in white ankle-face and
torsos, muscles burstling and oiled from sweating in the hot sun.
One drum, the seventh and largest - the talking drum - speaks louder
than all the rest. It calls his spirit and muscle to the test. The
wrestler answers, asking the hare skin stretched over the hollow
wooden cylinder to invoke the spirits of his wrestling ancestors to
give him the power.
"I can listen to a talking drum when I'm wrestling. It's like a coach
saying, 'get his leg, get his leg'. You can hear that with a talking
drum. 'your opponent is getting tired. He's getting tired, you see
it's time to attack now".
The gods have spoken Before a match is sanctioned, the two village
camps descend on an open field of coarse grasses, stiffened by the
dying sun when harmattan time sends a cool wind from the north
creating a drying pattern after the harvest of cassava, plantain,
bananas and yams.
Supporters size up protective opponents from among the neighbouring
wrestlers and when a suitable match is found, ask permission of the
referee from a neutral village, before engaging in combat.
Stripped to the waist and clad only in a pair of short pants, the two
combatants grapple, each trying to throw the other to the ground.
Much betting and drinking take place as the village atmosphere
explodes into song -tunes made up on the spur of the moment from old
rhythms - all through their man's prowess over that of their
neighbour.
In a traditional match, the handlers tell the wrestlers the right
time to fight.
It is essential the wrestler be in an aroused state, totally consumed
by the unseen spirits conjured up by the elders.
"It's sort of like saying the gods have spoken", says Igali.
"It is a different kind of emotion. It's like you're so pumped you're
at the highest point of power you can get unexploded power".
Igali describes the feeling as being the very opposite to the
outpouring of emotions he felt after realizing he had won the Olympic
gold medal in Sydney two months ago.
"It's in my head even when I have success now. It's always in my head
all the time.".
"It's in my youth, I remember how they celebrate. It's something you
just want to be".
A dream full circle The combatants fight barefoot on the baked grass
- shoeless- and under clear skies with the searing sun beating down
on their broad backs.
It can be very slippery too in Bayelsa State because the fields are
surrounded by swamps and you don't have to dig more than two feet
into the ground to find water there.
If you slip and fall to your knees, it doesn't matter, it is still a
strike against you.
Winning is not guaranteed in Eniwari, not even for a world and
Olympic free style champion.
There are no divisions in the village fields and Igali will give away
almost 40 kilograms in weight to his opponents.
One slip could cost a wrestler everything.
A fighter can go to one knee but no farther.
"It will be a conclusion of sorts", Igali says. "You don't know how
excited I am to get to wrestle in front of my village. Wrestling for
the glory of the village is the biggest thing I can do for them. My
village would value that more than a gold medal".
When the time comes for him to wrestle, Igali knows he will be ready.
"The talking drum will call me out and ask me, 'Are you ready to take
prisoners here?' "The elders will call on my ancestors - my great,
great grandfather - to come at this time, on this day of war and back
up his son. 'Give me all your power to wrestle'.
"I will kneel down to the elders and pray, while alcohol poured onto
the dry earth is drunk in by the spirits of past champions".
Igali's name in the Ijaw language is Ibrikp-ikpi (pronounced Ebree
bee bee) 'Thundering Storm' and he has returned home again to bring
Eniwari honour.

Original_Search_Title: New Chapter In Ghana-Togo Relations
Headline: Ghana: New Chapter In Ghana-Togo Relations
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's new President, Mr. John Agyekum Kufuor's visit to the
Republic of Togo is seen by many political analysts as a shift of the
country's foreign policy that will create more business and economic
opportunities for the citizens of the two West African states.
In the past, relations between Ghana and Togo were very sour with
pulsating cross border clashes and dissident activities that
destabilised the two countries and destroyed the trading link and
good neighbourliness that existed in the early years of independence.
In those years, Togo's President Gnassingbe Eyadema had to contend
with dissident activities by Togolese exiles in Ghana. The Togolese
authorities reciprocated the unwelcome gesture by habouring Ghanaian
exiles, who also launched similar attacks on Ghanaian territory in a
bid to take over power.
The two countries have now changed their foreign policies from
antagonism to friendship and mutual economic cooperation. This
development effectively closes the dark pages of history and the bad
blood of antagonism. President Eyadema set the stage for good
neigbourliness by honouring an invitation to attend the inauguration
of President Kufuor.
President Kufuor's political opponents and the Togolese opposition
consider his visit last weekend to Togo as unacceptable in the face
of the perceived political nature of the call. However, some economic
analysts see the historic visit as a tactical political move that
will bring considerable economic gains and the dividends of peace to
Ghana and Togo in the spirit of Economic Community of West African
States (ECOWAS). Incidentally, President Eyadema is the Chairman of
OAU.
The inclusion of Alhaji Asuma Banda and Mr. B.A Mensah, both business
tycoons in President Kufuor's delegation stresses the willingness of
the new government to lead a crusade to open a channel for business
among the two neigbours.
Togo's Natural Resources
Togo is rich in mineral resources. The country offers a strong
mineral potential and has large reserves of phosphate, in addition to
limestone and marble quarries. Its reserves of iron ore, manganese,
limestone and pure dolomite are genuine assets for industrial and
economic development. The leading manufactures include cement, flour,
palm oil, cotton textiles, beverages, and soap.
The country is a leading world producer of phosphate, which is by far
its most significant mineral product. In the early 1990s, about 3.7
million metric tonnes of phosphate rock was mined annually.
Togo's phosphate is of a high grade that allows making fertilizers
highly concentrated in phosphoric compounds. This phosphate is very
much appreciated for its production of phosphoric acid a chemical
important for use in research laboratories and industry. Ghana's
match factory at Kade may be revived if phosphate, which is the raw
material for the production of matches, is imported from Togo.
The iron ore reserves of Togo are estimated at more than 500 million
tonnes. According to the latest research findings, there is a genuine
ferruginous basin as well as substantial mixed mineral reserves
including iron titanium.
Togo also possesses large clinker reserves that have been exploited
during the 1980s by Cements of West Africa jointly owned by Ghana,
Togo, and Ghana's western neigbour, Cote d'Ivoire. Despite the
large-scale exploitation of high-grade clinker for the manufacture of
cement, important reserves remain to be exploited in the near future.
The main manufacturer of cement in Togo is Company of Togo's Cements
(CIMTOGO), which is operating in partnership with Norwegian
interests. As Ghana and Togo enter partnership to broaden bilateral
trade between them, Ghana Cement (GHACEM) could take advantage of the
open doors and import cheap clinker for its manufacturing plant at
Tema.
Cross-border Trade
The country's food industry revolves around the processing of local
agricultural commodities. There is a sugar refinery named Anie Sugar
Factor that has an annual capacity of 60,000 tonnes of refined sugar
and 6,000 litres of medical alcohol per day. There is also a
flourishing industry for palm oil and groundnut oil extraction,
coffee roasting, and cassava processing.
Togolese and Ghanaians along the two countries' border stand to
benefit from increased cross-border trade in agricultural produce and
this would have a positive impact on the Anglophone and Francophone
economies who are foes- turned-friends.
The large numbers of Ghanaian traders who shuttle between Accra and
Lome hardly cross into Ghana without the usual cargo of wax prints
and fine jewellery. The textile industry in Togo is much alive to the
demand of customers beyond the borders of that country and this can
be measured with ease when you consider the high value that Ghanaians
place on such goods.
This recent industry is controlled by Togotex International, which
manages two privately owned factories at Kara and Dadja. As trade
between Ghana and Togo increases, the Ghanaian textile industry will
have to raise standards to be able to meet the challenges in the
context of ECOWAS globalisation.
Lome, Togo's chief port and the country's principal administrative,
transportation, and economic centre has an interesting political
history that dates back from the 18th century. Lome is the country's
major port; the port of Kpeme handles phosphate shipments. An
international airport is located near the capital.
Most of Togo's international trade passes through the Lome port,
which was enlarged and deepened in 1968 to accommodate ocean-going
vessels. Most of Togo's exports pass through the nearby port of
Kpeme. Railroads extend inland to Palime, Atakpame, and Sokode and
along the coast to Anecho. Lome is the seat of the University of
Benin (1965) and the National School of Administration (1958).
Lome grew after 1897 as the capital of German Togoland and as a port
exporting raw materials. It passed to Anglo-French control
(1914-1922) and to French control (1922-1960), before becoming the
capital of independent Togo in 1960.
Togo has 37 ethnic groups and a population of about 6 million. The
principal groups in the south are Ewe-speaking peoples; in the north,
the Kabre (or Kabye) and Gurma are most numerous.
Agriculture
Although fertile land is scarce in Togo, more than three-quarters of
the country's labour force work in agriculture. Most of these
agricultural workers are sharecroppers and subsistence farmers on
small plots of land. In many cases, groups of families own farmland
collectively and cultivate corn, millet, yams, sorghum, and cassava
for direct consumption.
About 78% of Togo's labour force is engaged in agriculture. These
farmers cultivate about 26% of the country's land. Togo is generally
self-sufficient in basic food production. The principal food crops
are cassava, yams, corn, millet, and sorghum.
The leading export crops are coffee (13,000 metric tonnes produced
annually in the early 1990s), cotton (41,000), and cocoa (7000).
Livestock raising is important on the northern plateau. In the early
1990s, the country had a total of about 2 million pigs, 1.5 million
sheep, 800,000 goats, and 320,000 cattle. The fishing industry had a
yearly catch in the 1990s of some 12,500 metric tonnes.
Foreign Trade
Togo's yearly foreign trade usually shows a deficit. In the early
1990s, annual exports totalled $512 million and imports totalled $583
million. The leading exports were phosphates (which accounted for
about half of the total), raw cotton, cocoa, coffee, cement, karite
nuts, and palm kernels.
The principal imports were food and food products, textiles,
machinery, electrical equipment, construction materials, and
transportation equipment. Togo's chief trading partners for exports
are Canada, France, Spain, and Italy; principal partners for imports
are France, the Netherlands, Germany, and the United States.
Togo has more than 7,800 km (about 4,850 miles) of roads, about
one-fifth of which are paved. A northern-southern road system links
the port of Lome with Burkina Faso. A 537-km (about 334-miles)
railroad network that includes three northern-southern lines serves
the country.
Political History
According to historians most of the ethnic groups inhabiting Togo are
descended from peoples driven into the area during the 18th and 19th
centuries, except for the Ewe, who left the Niger River area for Togo
some time between the 11th and 16th centuries. During the 18th and
19th centuries, the area was under pressure from the Akwamu
Confederacy and the Ashanti Kingdom to the west and from the Kingdom
of Dahomey to the east.
Togo was a part of the Slave Coast, a primary area of European
slaving activities. Small slave posts were established in that region
in the 17th century, but most of the slave trade was carried on in
Dahomey (now Benin) and the Gold Coast (now Ghana).
The German protectorate of Togoland was established in 1884, when the
rulers of the region signed a treaty granting sovereignty to the
Germans. From 1887 to 1889, Germany, Great Britain, and France fixed
the territorial limits of the protectorate.
The Germans created the port of Lome and developed the resources of
the region. During August 1914, the first month of World War I, the
Germans surrendered the region after an invasion by French and
British forces.
In 1920 the final division of the area between the two countries took
place, and Lome and the entire coastline were assigned to French Togo
in exchange for an enlarged British territory in the interior. In
1922, the League of Nations granted both nations mandates over their
respective territories.
UN Plebiscite
On December 13, 1946, the United Nations (UN) granted France and
Great Britain trusteeships over Togo to supersede the mandates
established by the League of Nations. Because of a plebiscite held in
1956, the British territory became part of the Gold Coast and was
later incorporated into Ghana.
In another UN-supervised plebiscite in 1958, a majority of the votes
in the French territory was gained by the National Union Party, which
favoured complete independence. Sylvanus Olympio, head of the party,
became the premier. In February 1960, Olympio rejected the suggestion
advanced by President Kwame Nkrumah of Ghana that the two countries
be united. Togo achieved independence on April 27, 1960, and was
admitted to the UN in September.
President Olympio was assassinated in January 1963, during a military
coup. The army selected Nicolas Grunitzky to form a provisional
government, and he subsequently assumed the office of president. A
new constitution was approved by a national referendum. In January
1967, the army staged another coup, installing Gnassingbe Eyadema,
then a Lieutenant Colonel and the Army Chief of Staff, as head of
government.
The constitution was abrogated and the legislative body dissolved. In
April, Eyadema assumed the offices of President and Defence Minister.
A new party, the Togolese People's Assembly, was founded in 1969. In
1970, a plot to overthrow President Eyadema was foiled. In early
1975, a treaty between 46 developing nations and the European Union
was signed in Lome.
The Togolese president promulgated a new constitution in December
1979, under which he was unanimously re-elected to office. In January
1980, the 13th anniversary of his coup, he proclaimed the Third
Togolese Republic. France sent troops to Togo in September 1986 to
help suppress a coup attempt.
He was re-elected to another seven-year term in December 1986.
President Eyadema apparently bowed to popular pressure in August 1991
and yielded power to a transitional government, for democratic
elections that he won for another term. In subsequent months,
however, troops loyal to Eyadema repeatedly tried to overthrow the
interim regime.
President Eyadema's position as former Chairman of ECOWAS made him a
major political player in the West African sub-region. He is among
one of the longest serving political leaders in Africa and the world
and many political analysts credit him with his ability to create
stability in his country. This probably explains the reason why he
was elected as the Chairman of OAU.

Original_Search_Title: Indians Open Free Hospital At Hastings
Headline: Sierra Leone: Indians Open Free Hospital At Hastings
Body_Text:
Freetown — As part of its ongoing humanitarian assistance to Sierra
Leone, the Indian contingent of UNAMSIL has opened a Medical Aid Camp
at the Police Training School at Hasting.
The camp was inaugurated yesterday by the Vice President, Albert Joe
Demby.
The opening of the camp comes as a big relief to the civilian
population especially thousands of displaced persons in the area.
Thousands of civilians from Kossoh Town,Yams Farm and Devil Hole also
stand to benefit from the camp.
The camp will provide a wide range of medical facilities including
surgical dressing, gynaecology, ophthalmology, paediatric, dental and
a dispensary for free medicine.
Vice President Dr. Albert Demby said the opening of the camp at
Hastings could not have come at a better time as all the surrounding
villages are now occupied by vulnerable displaced people and refugee
returnees who require urgent medical attention.
"It is indeed no surprise that such a great gesture should be
undertaken by Indian medical personnel whose exemplary performance in
the country like in Daru, Mile 91 etc have helped in no small measure
to give hope to our traumatized people" the VP said.
Station Commander, Indian Aviation Unit, Hasting, Group Captain B. S.
Siwach said the camp is a special gesture to provide medical
facilities to the people of Sierra Leone.
" The best of Indian Armed Doctors will be here for the next three
days from 9am to 4:30pm to offer medical consultation, medicines and
treatment" Siwach said. Senior members of the NIBATT contingent at
the ceremony were the Commanding Officer of the Choithram Hospital,
Lt. Col. Dayanand and Commanding Officer of NIBATT 2 Col Kushal
Thakur.
This program was facilitated by three other units, the Mariners
Company, the Artillery Unit and the mechanise Company.
The Force Commander is expected to visit the camp.
The first patients to be treated were police detectives who suffered
from cramping hands and malaria.

Original_Search_Title: Fiscal Policy: Textile Manufacturers Still In The Woods
Headline: Nigeria: Fiscal Policy: Textile Manufacturers Still In The Woods
Body_Text:
Lagos — Despite the slash of various import duties on textile raw
materials in the tariff amendment and fiscal policy measures for year
2001 released by the federal government recently, operators in the
textile sub-sector of the economy are not happy. Their grouse is that
the measures fell short of their expectations, particularly as they
pertain to dumping of woven fabrics and second hand materials.
Although the Federal Government had, in a bid to improve the
industrial climate and stimulate higher capacity utilisation, and
provide the level playing field to investors, reduced import duties
on major inputs for manufacturing sector, the textile operators said
they expected higher duties on finished products than the government
wanted.
Going by the provisions of the new guidelines, textile raw materials,
whose duties were slashed, are synthetic mono-filament from 30
percent to 15 percent; cotton, not corded or combed from 30 percent
to five percent; jute or other textile bast fibres from 10 percent to
five percent.
Other inputs whose import duties were reduced are textile flock and
dust and will neps (H.S.code 5402. 1000-6900) from35 percent to 15
percent, and synthetic filament yam from 30 percent to 10 percent.
On the other hand, the federal government increased the duty on woven
fabrics from 35 percent to 45 percent, operators say that the 45
percent increment is not enough deterrent to smugglers who are having
field day at the nations porous borders in the east, north and
western parts of the country.
For instance, Mr. Ibukun Adeogun, the Senior Manager, (Corporate
affairs) of Churchgate group, the largest conglomerate in the
country, said that what the government gave with the right hand,
through import duty reductions, it took away with the blanket opening
of the borders to all manners of goods.
Although government removal of import prohibition is in line with the
principles of the world trade organisation regarding trade
liberalisation except gypsum whose importation will attract a duty of
65 percent.
Adeogun argues that, Nigeria signed the WTO principles of
liberalisation in a hurry. Specifically he argued that if it were not
the obsequious tendencies of the Abacha administration, which signed
the agreement because it was looking for international friends at the
time when the international community, ostracised Nigeria, the
authorities would have signed it too early. At least, the Government
ought to have waited until the country's infrastructure improves
before signing the WTO agreement.
The implication of the agreement is that Nigeria's trade policy has
opened the borders to well established foreign companies to compete
with local industries. He argued that since the government did not
effectively tackle the myriads of problems that weigh down the local
industries the competition is now skewed against operators of textile
industries.
Specifically, he complained that the textile companies are still
weighed down by the effects of sticky wages and deteriorating
infrastructure, the overall performance of the textile sector is
nothing to write home about as they are still weighed down by unsold
stock.
Other constraints, which contributed to the anaemic growth of the
sector, include high cost of inputs due to weakening naira, weak
purchasing power, unfavourable taxes and levies which favour
importation coupled with the predilection of Nigerians to go for
imported goods rather than made in Nigeria fabrics.
As the organised private sector, put it in their pre-budget 2001 memo
to the federal government, the long-standing constraints to
manufacturing activities have not abated. The sector is still
characterised by the applying effects of globalisation, arising,
principally, from unfair competitive pressures, triggered off by the
problem of smuggling and dumping.
Apart from that, the capacity utilisation of the industry is
currently estimated at below 30 percent.
Indeed, it was for these reason that the OPS comprising the
Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), the Nigerian Association
of Chambers of Commerce, Industry, Mines and Agriculture (NACCIMA)
and the Nigerian Employers Consultative Association (NECA) called on
the federal government to impose 100 percent duty on imported textile
and strictly enforce the ban on second hand clothing.
The OPS also advocated that the 50 percent import duty on polyster
staple fibre and polyster filament yarn be increased to 75 percent.
However, the government met the expectations of the operators half
way, for political and diplomatic reasons more than economic
expediency.
According to Mr. Adeogun, all the African Prints Manufactured by the
Aba textile mills, the United Nigerian textile mills Kaduna, Aswani
textiles, Afprints, are not selling because of the influx of foreign
brands.
While, the Aba textile mills suffer from the activities of importers
east of the Niger, the companies located in Kaduna, Kano and other
states suffer from the crushing weight of dumped products from the
Niger Republic, Chad and Cameroun. Commenting on the stiff
competition between foreign and local brands, he said that while the
made in Nigeria wrappers also known as African Prints matches their
foreign counterparts in terms of quality, the foreign brands under
cutting the local brands.
While the African Prints sale for an average of N460, the foreign
brands sale for N380, thus making it extremely difficult for Nigerian
companies to break even. But if the government had imposed 100
percent import duty as advocated by the OPS, it would have tided the
industry over pending the improvement of the nation's infrastructure
.
Accordingly, despite the reduction of the price of inputs, the
textile industry is not totally out of the woods. Unless the
government revisits the tariff and tax amendment measures, textile
operators will have to contend with the problem of unsold stock.
As it appears, the government favours the stance of the world trade
organisation at the expense of the textiles industries.
But the reason is not far fetched. To continue to gain international
confidence, Nigeria's trade policy is expected to emphasise
consistency, transparency and predictability.
The textile industries are therefore left to either device new ways
of survival or die.
But the only escape route market watchers say is for the industries
to explore the African Growth Opportunities Act (AGOA), passed by the
US government.
According to Chief (Mrs) Priscilla Kuye, President of the
Nigerian-American Chamber of Commerce, the textile companies should
come together to explore the opportunities.
In her words, "I know about 20 textile companies folded up, not too
long ago. But what I believe the textile companies should do, is that
they should organise themselves into co-operatives, so that they can
be formidable.
If they organise themselves, I think they will be able to take
advantage of the provisions of AGOA. I don't think one company can do
it."
The president, Institute of Directors of Nigeria shared the same
view.
According to him, although the government did not grant all that the
OPS recommended, tariff amendment is an on-going process.
He argued that Nigerian companies should warm up to the competition.
Barring the long-standing problems of the sector, the textile
industry should have been able to absorb the shocks of unfair
competition.

Original_Search_Title: For The Love of Christ
Headline: Nigeria: For The Love of Christ
Body_Text:
Lagos — The aged, the poor, the widows and the orphans in St.
Timothy's Catholic parish, Ojodu, a Lagos suburb, had no reason to
worry about what they would eat or wear on last Christmas day. The
Justice Development and Peace Commission and St. Vincent De Paul
Society took care of their needs.
On December 21, the societies distributed new clothings, live
chickens, rice, yams and toiletries to the needy at the church
premises along Otunba Street after they were sumptuously fed at a
get-together party three days earlier. The party began with a
thanksgiving mass at 6:30 p.m. followed with Christmas carols sang by
various church groups which included the senior and junior choirs,
Catholic Women's Organisation, the singing ministry, the block
rosary, the parish brigade and the Catholic Youths of Nigeria.
The idea of the get-together party was first mooted by Ethelbert
Ifeanyi Iwu, the priest in charge of the parish, early in the year.
It could still have remained a dream but for the generosity of his
friend whom he simply referred to as Mr. V. T. Iwu told Newswatch: "I
have been thinking of how or what to do to help the poor in my parish
until one of my friends came around. He refused to allow me mention
his name but I prefer to call him Mr. V. T. He gave me a large sum of
money and told me to do something for the poor and the widows in my
parish. So it was like a dream come true when he gave me the money."
The Catholic priest said he decided to spend all the money on the
poor in order to give them a sense of belonging during the Christmas
celebration. "I am happy that many people were sponsored to celebrate
the festivities for the love of Christ and they were very happy," he
said.
The parish priest said there would be a better arrangement next year
for the get-together for the poor. Said he: "I didn't even campaign
for it this year until I got the money. By the grace of God, I will
start in time next year. I will go to my friends and some of the
companies that produce the materials that would be given to the poor.
I got the money and bought those things this year. I will get the
materials and meet more people. The poor will have more things to
enjoy from my friends next year."
Apart from the Christmas party, Iwu said the parish was also working
on a poverty alleviation programme. He said the man who gave him
money for the party had earlier donated money which he used to start
a parish clinic. He had, in addition asked him to come up with a
project idea which would help widows in the parish.
"I am still preparing the proposal. Before the middle of next year,
we shall set up something permanent that will benefit the widows. We
have more plans for the widows, the poor, the aged and orphans in the
parish," he assured.

Original_Search_Title: "They Are Killing Us"
Headline: Nigeria: "They Are Killing Us"
Body_Text:
Lagos — William Oketa, chairman of the committee set up by the
Erovie community in Ozoro, Isoko north local government, is now very
sick.
Oketa, once energetic and an outspoken community activist, now looks
gaunt and feeds on drips and injections to keep life going. Newswatch
was told that Oketa's doctors could not identify the cause of the
strange ailment which has defied medical treatment.
But the villagers and some toxic experts have linked Oketa's poor
health to the alleged dumping of suspected substances into well 3,
Isoko deep field in June last year by Shell Petroleum Development
Company Limited, SPDC. About 1,200 litres of the waste substances
were said to have been injected into the land through the well before
the community stopped the project in July last year. But it appeared
the havoc they feared had already been done.
Oketa had contact with the substances in July last year when he was
given three samples of the waste which he later took to the Benin
Owena River Basin/University of Benin Joint analytical research
laboratory. The substances were found to be toxic. Thirty days after
doing that, he fell sick and his health has deteriorated ever since.
The toxic waste is now a major threat to the 50,000 inhabitants of
Erovie community in Ozoro. Their farmlands have been ravaged by the
chemical components of the dumped toxic waste and people are
reporting strange ailments.
Odhegolor Lucky Abikelegba, a biochemist and the national leader of
Erovie community in Ozoro, told Newswatch that between April and June
this year, about 93 people, including pregnant women and school-age
children who must have had direct or indirect contact with the dumped
waste had died. "Strange infections in Ozoro which doctors have
confirmed their existence here include vomiting, lung and skin
diseases, heart attack, dysentery and typhoid fever," he said.
Some Erovie people who are sick were said to have had indirect or
direct contact with the toxic waste through farming, fishing or
eating fruits or root- crops harvested from the areas allegedly
polluted by the waste. "I know of two members of our committee who
are having problems with their legs because of the toxic waste. The
cells of their legs have been killed by the substance," said
Abikelegba. Frederick Ogbo, the traditional ruler of Erovie
community, told the story of a family of five, which died recently
after eating yam tubers harvested from the polluted farmland. Ogbo
also said that a farmer from the community almost lost his sight
after washing his face with water from a polluted pond adding;
"pregnant women also had pre-mature babies."
Farming is the main occupation of Erovie and, indeed, the Isoko
people. This has been put on hold for now because of the alleged
pollution of their farmland. Ogbo said: "We have no other job than
farming. SPDC has polluted our soil and driven every farmer away from
our land. Many of us had farm there. I have six farms on that land,
many of us left our crops unharvested last year. None to my knowledge
has gone back to farm there till today. Herdsmen have lost a great
deal of their animals; fishermen and farmers are relocating."
The department of geology, University of Nigeria, Nsukka, UNN, which
conducted the environmental assessment of the waste samples on behalf
of Isoko north local government early last year had advised the
farmers to "halt further development of the land in the vicinity of
the dump in order to locate, isolate and comprehensively characterise
the waste and co-ordinate its removal and proper disposal in
accordance with existing environmental guidelines."
UNN also advised government to "conduct intensive and detailed
hydro-chemical and soil studies to identify and isolate areas of
potential soil and groundwater contamination" because the
environmental assessment of the dump showed clearly that it is toxic.
All the three samples which Emmanuel Edevor, chairman, Isoko north
local government, took to UNN for analysis were confirmed to be
toxic. The university said the concentrations of chemical parameters
in the analysed samples suggested that "a good number are enriched up
to and above the limits recommended for use." UNN analysis showed
that "the samples are toxic to both human beings/animals and plants."
It showed that some of the parameters tested such as lead (pd), zinc
(zn) and mercury (Hg) "are poisonous even at concentrations far below
that observed in the samples."
UNN said if a material containing these samples was dumped on any
part of Isoko land without adequate preparation of the site to
control the movement and spread of the leachate arising from the
material, it would constitute an environmental hazard. As such, it
said, "the soil and groundwater systems would be endangered. The
shallow groundwater system which is the major source of water to the
rural inhabitants, could be damaged beyond use. Such a damage could
be difficult, even expensive, to rectify."
UNN experts also analysed the chemical contents of the dumped
substance. After analysing the waste's electric conductivity which is
a measure of the concentration of chemical materials in the soil or
groundwater, the university said the values it obtained range from
11,650 us/cm to about 19,650 us/cm. "The normal soil conductivity in
the Isoko area ranges from less than 1,000 us/cm for sandy loams to
less than 2,000 us/cm for clay/salty soils. These natural values are
much less than the values obtained in the samples. In fact, the three
samples have conductivity values more than 10 times the normal soil
values and in this regard, the samples are polluted," the report
said. (see table for details of UNN report)
The university took particular note of the concentration levels of
sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, mercury and chlorides in the
samples tested and declared that soils with any concentration of
chlorides are "saline and are useless for any crop production." It
said the mercury chemicals it found in the tested samples, though in
small concentrations, "are usually toxic and poisonous even at very
small concentrations."
UNN study also found grease in the tested samples. "Because of low
solubility, grease separates from water adhering to the interior of
soil grains, pipes and tank walls. They thus reduce the productivity
of soils and even the biological treatability of the waste and
produce greasy sludge that is difficult to process and degrade," the
university said.
On the environmental impact of the waste dumped, the report said the
groundwater of Isoko community is vulnerable to pollution "unless
artificially protected." The university said: "Relatively fast
groundwater movement is also suggested by the sandy nature of the top
soil which implies good permeability. Any land-based pollution
sources, is thus a serious threat to the quality of the groundwater
in Isoko area. Dug wells, especially those in individual homes used
for domestic supplies would be the first to be contaminated and
subsequently polluted."
The findings of UNN tally somewhat with the test results obtained by
Erovie community from the University of Benin, UNIBEN. The
university, in a joint report with the Benin-Owena River Basin, said
the waste contained heavy chemical metals which are inimical to human
beings, animals and plants, (see box).
The test result of UNN and UNIBEN confirmed the findings of the
committee constituted, August 4, last year, by James Ibori, Delta
State governor, to look into the toxic issue. The five-man committee
which was headed by L. T. Popo, acknowledged "the presence of heavy
metals at above acceptable limits and the unusually high
concentration of ions which make the substance to be toxic." The
committee therefore warned that "if these substances were to
infiltrate into the underground water or acquifer, it would have
serious environmental and health implications."
Up till today, SPDC has not come out to reveal how the toxic waste
got to Isoko land. But the Popo-led committee fingered Dowell
Schlumberger, a prominent agent of SPDC. The committee also said that
the various ailments presented by the people could be "directly
attributed to the effect of contact with the substances."
But the Federal Ministry of Environment said in its "full and final
report" published in The Guardian of October 30, that the people's
ailments were not associated with the waste. "A medical appraisal of
the site and detailed medical review of seven members of the
community who had close contact with the samples was carried out by
Scotchville Industrial Consortium, Warri. The medical report
submitted, indicated no conclusive evidence to link the suspected
harmful substances with any real or perceived negative health impact
on the Ozoro community or its environs," said Imeh Okopido, minister
of state for environment.
Those on which medical examinations were carried out following
complaints of strange ailments were A. Cheranu, M. Ebiri, G. Egelbe,
J. Atagbagba, Joseph Adakpo and William Oketa. Most of the people,
especially Adakpa and Oketa are at the throes of death. The ministry
insisted that the dumped wastes were not toxic. Okopido said that the
"substances had no obvious significant harmful impact on human and
the immediate environment," because they were injected "at a depth of
5,300-5,400 ft. while the fresh water depth in Delta is 1,560 ft
beyond which is brine."
"Considering the depth at which the substances were injected," says
Okopido, "it is most unlikely that the injected substances would have
any negative effect on the flora, fauna and acquirer" which is the
water level. He said the samples of the injected substances were
tested by 13 science-based firms. They include Jawura Environmental
Services Limited, FM Environmental Reference Laboratory, Lagos, Shell
Petroleum Development Company, Laladson Limited, Jebeco Envirotech
Limited, and Scotchville Industrial Consortium, Warri.
There is already a raging controversy on the membership of the joint
investigative panel set up by the federal government to probe the
alleged dumping of the toxic substances in Ozoro.
Names of Ozoro representatives on the panel, were given as Emmanuel
Edevor, chairman, Isoko north local government area; Christopher
Imoga, an Ozoro high chief, Abikelegba, national leader of Erovie
community in Ozoro and Fred Obe, president-general, Isoko National
Youth Movement. All the people were surprised to find their names on
the list of the panel.
Said Edevor: "There was no day I was invited to the meeting of the
panel. Look at the list of firms whose reports were considered by the
ministry in deciding this case, my report from UNN is not there. I
only saw my name in the publication. I did not participate in any way
in the panel." Said Abikelegba: "I knew nothing about the meetings of
the panel until they published their contractors' findings as the
full and final results." Imoga: "I was not party to the decision.
Shell may have bribed some people to kill us and our children." Obe:
"Nobody in his right senses could have said I was party to that
report. I think what the ministry was saying was that I was present
when it gave samples of the substances to interested members of the
public to test and report to it."
Angered by the seeming helplessness of Erovie people in the face of
the ministry's description of its report as "full and final," Felix
Morka, executive director of the Social and Economic Rights Action
Centre, SERAC, a non- governmental organisation, has taken up the
fight on behalf of the people. At a press conference in Lagos,
December 14, he disagreed with the conclusion of the panel that "no
evidence had yet been found of any immediate or discernible threat to
lives and property."
Said Morka: "This conclusion was reached without a full, and careful
review of all available evidence so far developed on the matter."
Morka cited a laboratory test carried out jointly by the Benin-Owena
River Basin and the University of Benin, saying the result showed
that the injected substances contained very high and dangerous levels
of toxicity. He said another scientific analysis of the substances
carried out by the department of geology, University of Nigeria,
Nsukka, similarly showed that the substances injected by Shell into
well 3, Ibo bush was highly toxic. "Both tests confirmed that the
substances contained dangerous high levels of heavy metals and that
most of the parameters were far in excess of DPR/FEPA limits."
The tests further confirmed that the substances contained unnatural
hydrocarbon and a very high degree of conductivity."
Morka alleged that tests carried out by the ministry were sponsored
by Shell to escape being indicted. "Curiously enough, little weight
was given by the panel and the federal ministry of environment to
aspects of another Shell- sponsored tests conducted by Peak
Engineering which concluded on page seven paragraph four of its
report that "the re-injection project at Ibo bush, Ozoro is likely to
remain environmentally friendly if spills of materials are completely
avoided," Morka observed.
He wondered why the panel conveniently ignored the "veiled warning
contained in that report of the inherent danger to human life and
environment." Morka then called on President Olusegun Obasanjo to
reject the panel's conclusion and to institute an independent
judicial inquiry into the alleged toxic waste dump by Shell.
The realities in Ozoro and the palpable fears and anxieties expressed
by the people over the alleged toxic waste dump in their community
clearly makes the need for an independent inquiry necessary. There is
the urgent need now to determine the environmental impact of the
substances injected into Ibo bush.
When Newswatch visited the oil well into which the substances were
allegedly injected last week, most economic trees around the well
appeared, affected by the substances. For instance, the mango tree
close to the well reportedly bore no fruit this year. Banana stalks
bore withering seeds and there appeared to be some obvious problems
with the ecology of the place.
The problems now being experienced by the people do not allow them to
trust the government's report that the suspected harmful substances
were injected very deep into the ground. "It's all a lie. They are
saying that to make us feel safe. At least some of us can feel that
way even if it meant that the future of our children is doomed.
Earthquake often brings up anything hidden underground. Shell knows
that," Imoga said.
Virtually all ailments in the community are being attributed to the
suspected harmful substances. An Ozoro-based medical doctor who only
spoke to Newswatch on condition of anonymity, blamed SPDC for what
the people are passing through now. "The people have sufficient
reasons to believe that SPDC is out to kill them. One, SPDC violated
known procedures for re-claiming an oil well they abandoned many
