years ago," he said. He said SPDC usually obtains the consent of
immediate land-owners before entering their land for oil exploration.
"In this case, SPDC sidetracked Erovie people who owned the land they
were entering. The people are saying that if SPDC had come to Ozoro
to do clean business, they would have contacted the right people, the
Erovie community. Again, the fears of the people have been upheld by
the reports of UNN and UNIBEN. Even if the suspected substances have
no immediate human and environmental effects, the state of fears and
anxieties which the people now live in has thrown up psychosomatic
problems," the doctor said. He added: "people are reporting strange
ailments not totally because of the problems emitted by the
substances but because of the great injury which the breach of trust
by SPDC had inflicted on the people's minds. On the increase now in
the community are skin and heart diseases, typhoid fever and
dysentery. Yes, some people have died. The only psychotherapy which I
know of is immediate evacuation of the suspected substances and
offering of due apology to the people in form of compensation."
Problems started in June last year when members of Erovie community
in Ozoro found SPDC operating on their land without obtaining their
consent. Erovie, a community of 50,000 people is said to be a
separate entity in Ozoro clan. "We sought to know how they came in
there in defiance of section 47, subsection 2(m) of our constitution
and their own policy which we stoutly relied on. We also sought to
know what manner of work they were to do in a supposedly dry well. We
were, however, informed by Ozoro clan community organisation leaders
that SPDC met with them and sought permission to re-enter our Erovie
community," Joseph Adakpo, chairman, Erovie community told Newswatch.
Surprised by the breach of protocol by SPDC, Adakpo said the company
was asked to suspend operation "pending the determination of the true
nature of the chemicals being injected into the well. But within 24
hours, to our greatest surprise and consternation, the company went
to the site and dismantled their equipment-hastily and started
packing away hurriedly. On hearing this, we raised an alarm and the
vehicle (truck) with registration number AA 997 KLK that came to
remove the two giant tanks being their last trip was abandoned as
they fled." At the site when Newswatch visited last week were a truck
and the two tanks. Forty loads of such tanks were said to have been
injected into the area before the community heard about the
activities.
The community, through its leaders, quickly reported the development
to the presidency and the National Assembly. Based on the people's
outcry, the federal ministry of environment set up a joint
investigation panel, last year. The panel headed by Ikemefuna
Onyekwelu, head of the Lagos office of the ministry, released its
preliminary report in April this year. The panel said then that the
substances had no "any immediate or discernible threat to lives and
property."
Abikelegba said last week that federal government was being misled on
this matter by Onyekwelu who had come in April to say the substances
were non-toxic. "We believe that the honourable minister was misled
by information and manipulated results presented to him by the panel
headed by Onyekwelu who did not hear from us and did not collect any
analytical result from us before informing the honourable minister of
state of their findings," Abikelegba said.
SPDC said in Lagos last week that it stood by the report of
Onyekwelu-led panel on the matter. Bisi Ojediran, head, corporate
affairs, Shell Lagos told Newswatch on phone: "We stand by the
ministry's report. We believe it is credible." He said what was being
disposed of by SPDC in Ibo bush were drilling muds and not toxic
substances as alleged by the community.
On why a dried oil well in Isoko was considered for such exercise,
Ojediran said the well 3, Ibo bush, was nearest to where the mud was
generated. "Proximity," he said, "informed the choice of the well for
the exercise."
In any case, Isoko people do not believe any explanation given by
SPDC. Most of them are standing firm on the laboratory findings of
UNN and UNIBEN. "I disagree that it is not toxic substance. Federal
government is being fed by SPDC as their budgets depend on SPDC
revenue that is why they have supported the company to say the
substance is not toxic. We stand by UNN and UNIBEN decisions and we
are depending on the media and the court of law to get justice in
this case," said Patrick Egerueme Ogblioni, an Ozoro high chief.

Original_Search_Title: Ringo's Long Hard Road To Stardom
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ringo's Long Hard Road To Stardom
Body_Text:
Harare — On the stage, he is the embodiment of enchantment and off it
he is polite, soft-spoken and an unrepentant charmer.
Born Sindile Brian Madlingozi 36 years ago in Gugulethu township just
outside Cape Town, South Africa, he has travelled the hard road to
musical stardom.
He has traversed through the jungle of imitating such Reggae gurus as
the late Bob Marley and has also flirted with musical styles of the
R& clique.
After years in the musical wilderness, the artist now popularly known
as Ringo met Chris Black - well, a producer of Island Records and
Marley's music, who advised him to sing in his mother tongue, Xhosa.
"I had tried to impress Blackwell with my Reggae songs so when he
told me to sing in my language and stick to my culture I thought he
was playing a cruel joke on me," Madlingozi told the Financial
Gazette in Harare last week.
"Eventually I saw the light and took Blackwell's advice. In a very
big way he was right because as soon as I started singing in my own
language about issues I was more conversant with, my popularity
soared." The artist, who laughs and grins a lot throughout the
interview, adds: "On reflection now, I realise I could have sounded
quite funny singing about Harlem and Jamaica which I didn't even
know. I wasn't even sure the things I was singing about were the way
I assumed they were."
Madlingozi was in the capital to launch his latest album Buyisa and
also to perform at two end-of-year shows with local music maestro
Oliver Mtukudzi. The two artists have a duet on the new album: a
re-mix of Madlingozi's Into Yam.
He grins widely as he talks fondly of growing up in Cape Town,
kissing his mother goodbye each morning before leaving for school and
going about his life without a care.
"Cape Town that time was very quiet and even when I went to school at
times without anything in my lunch box, I never for once thought
things in the country were not stable. It was only after my Matric
and I began toying with the idea of going to university that my
political awareness was raised," he said.
He said although at that early stage his passion was music, he still
felt that he had to have a good education but eventually that was
overtaken by his love for music. He joined up with friends and came
up with a band called Peto, which is township lingua for friend.
By 1986 he was still trying to find his feet and he says his road to
fame was via participation in a road show that saw him gain
recognition. Soon after, he formed another band, Gecko Moon.
Madlingozi began to churn out sizzling hits starting off with the
album Vukani. From then on the road became smoother with his lyrical
prowess showing in the production of Sondelani, an album that was
made popular by the song Sondela.
And more recently he unleashed on the unsuspecting entertainment
world Into Yam, a gem of a song. This album has re-mixes of songs
like Khaya and Isipithi Pithi. Now with the album Buyisa he has
firmly planted his feet on the world of music giants.
He says his music, which focuses mostly on love, has done very well
in Africa and more especially in southern Africa.
"In five years' time I see my music on a higher level. I will also
continue to explore working with other musicians like I have just
done with Oliver (Mtukudzi). Collaboration with other artists is
enriching because it produces a unique product," says Madlingozi.
He says the idea of doing a duet with Mtukudzi was something that
just came up without any prior consideration. He said Mtukudzi was in
Johannesburg recently for just eight hours and within those few hours
they re-worked Into Yam.
"Oliver is amazing. With his direction we came up with a beautiful
piece of music and he also worked with my backing female vocalists
for the Shona version of the song," says the South African artist.
While he waxes lyrical about his music, mother, father, his
friendship with Mtukudzi and food, Madlingozi however wants to remain
an enigma when it comes to his personal life.
"There are things that must remain within me. There is something that
I call a personal cabinet where some things about me remain locked
up," he says with a boyish grin.
Using his wicked sense of humour, the artist evades any question that
seems to point towards his personal life and speaks of no special
woman in his life or anything outside show business.
As he prepares to jump onto the stage with Mtukudzi to perform the
famous duet, he looks around him and with his trademark grin says
that his vocation is to make people happy and to churn out the kind
of music that people can relate to.
